topics.title: Properties of Fluid: Specific Gravity, Specific Weight, Specific Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fluid: Specific Gravity, Specific Weight, Specific Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Specific Gravity (Relative Density)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Specific Gravity (denoted as **G** or **S** or **SG**) of a fluid is defined as the **ratio of the density (or specific weight) of the given fluid to the density (or specific weight) of a standard reference fluid**, which is usually **water** for liquids and **air** for gases, both taken at standard temperature and pressure conditions.

$$G = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{\gamma_{fluid}}{\gamma_{water}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Examination Note:** Specific Gravity is a **dimensionless** quantity. It is also called **Relative Density (R.D.)**. Always mention the standard reference temperature (typically **4°C for water**, where its density is maximum at **1000 kg/m³**).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine you have two identical buckets — one filled with **honey** and one filled with **water**. If you try to lift them, the honey bucket feels noticeably heavier. Specific Gravity simply tells you *"how many times heavier the fluid is compared to water, for the same volume."* 

- Honey has **G ≈ 1.42** → A bucket of honey is 1.42 times heavier than the same bucket of water.
- Mercury has **G ≈ 13.6** → Mercury is 13.6 times heavier than water.
- Most oils float on water because they have **G < 1**.

---

## 1.2 Specific Weight (Weight Density)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Specific Weight (denoted as **γ** or **w**) is defined as the **weight of the fluid per unit volume**. It represents the gravitational force exerted by a unit volume of the fluid.

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \rho \cdot g$$

where:
- **W** = Weight of the fluid (in **N** or **kgf**)
- **V** = Volume of the fluid (in **m³** or **cm³**)
- **ρ** = Mass density of the fluid
- **g** = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

**Standard SI Unit:** **N/m³** (Newton per cubic meter) or **kN/m³**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Think of Specific Weight as *"how heavy a fixed box of fluid feels when you hold it."* If you fill a 1 m³ box with water, it weighs about **9810 N** (≈ 1000 kgf). If you fill the same box with mercury, it weighs about **13.6 times more**! 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use Specific Weight and not Density?**  
> In fluid statics and hydrostatics problems (like pressure calculations at depth, manometer problems, dam design), we frequently deal with **force per unit volume** (weight) rather than mass. Specific Weight directly appears in pressure-head equations like **P = γ·h**, making calculations more straightforward.

---

## 1.3 Specific Volume

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Specific Volume (denoted as **v**) is defined as the **volume of the fluid occupied per unit mass**. It is the **reciprocal of mass density**.

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Standard SI Unit:** **m³/kg** (cubic meter per kilogram)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine you buy **1 kg** of different fluids at a market:
- **1 kg of water** occupies **0.001 m³** (= 1 litre) — pretty compact.
- **1 kg of gasoline** occupies a slightly larger volume (gasoline is less dense).
- **1 kg of air** (a gas) occupies a **huge volume** — about **0.83 m³** at room conditions!

Specific Volume essentially answers: *"How much space does just 1 kg of this fluid take up?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas vs. Liquid Behavior — KTU High-Yield Point:**  
> - For **liquids** (incompressible fluids), specific volume is essentially **constant** with pressure.  
> - For **gases** (compressible fluids), specific volume **changes significantly** with temperature and pressure (governed by ideal gas law: **Pv = RT**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Interconnected Web of Fluid Properties

All three properties discussed here are **not independent** — they are mathematically linked through density (ρ) and gravity (g). Understanding these relationships is essential for solving KTU numerical problems.

### Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown:

**Step 1 — Foundation: Mass Density (ρ)**
The fundamental property is **mass density**, defined as mass per unit volume. Every other property on this topic is derived from it.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3]$$

**Step 2 — Deriving Specific Weight (γ)**
Multiply density by gravity to convert *mass* into *weight*:

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

This is the most important transformation in fluid statics because pressure at a depth *h* becomes **P = γ·h** (instead of ρ·g·h).

**Step 3 — Deriving Specific Volume (v)**
Take the reciprocal of density to get volume per unit mass:

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Deriving Specific Gravity (G)**
Form a dimensionless ratio by comparing with water's density at **4°C (1000 kg/m³)**:

$$G = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water,\,4°C}} = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{1000}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 4°C for water?**  
> Water has a unique property — its density is **maximum at 4°C** (1000 kg/m³). This is why KTU and IS standards fix the reference temperature at **4°C**, eliminating temperature as a variable for water's density reference.

**Step 5 — Combined Master Relationship**
The single most useful relationship combining all three properties:

$$\gamma_{fluid} = G \cdot \gamma_{water} = G \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g = G \cdot 9810 \quad [\text{N/m}^3]$$

This single equation lets you compute specific weight of **any** fluid in **one step** if you know its specific gravity.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

| **Property** | **Symbol** | **Defining Equation** | **SI Unit** | **Alternate Form** | **Standard Value for Water (4°C)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass Density | ρ | $\rho = m/V$ | **kg/m³** | $\rho = G \cdot 1000$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific Weight | γ | $\gamma = W/V$ | **N/m³** | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | **9810 N/m³ (or 9.81 kN/m³)** |
| Specific Volume | v | $v = V/m$ | **m³/kg** | $v = 1/\rho$ | **0.001 m³/kg** |
| Specific Gravity | G | $G = \rho_{fluid}/\rho_{water}$ | **Dimensionless** | $G = \gamma_{fluid}/\gamma_{water}$ | **1 (for water itself)** |
| Dynamic Viscosity | μ | $\mu = \tau \cdot (dy/du)^{-1}$ | **N·s/m² (Pa·s)** | — | **0.001 N·s/m² (at 20°C)** |
| Kinematic Viscosity | ν | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | **m²/s** | — | **10⁻⁶ m²/s (at 20°C)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners often check whether you write the **unit** alongside the numerical answer. Always include units in your final answer to gain that extra half-mark / step mark.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏗️

| **Engineering Field** | **Why These Properties Matter** |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Engineering (Dams, Spillways)** | Specific weight γ is used to compute hydrostatic thrust on dam faces: **F = ½·γ·h²·b** |
| **Petroleum Engineering** | API gravity (related to specific gravity) classifies crude oils; oil-water separator design uses density differences |
| **Aerospace / Aviation** | Air density and specific weight vary with altitude — critical for aircraft performance and lift calculations |
| **HVAC & Refrigeration** | Specific volume of refrigerants determines compressor sizing (suction line volume) |
| **Chemical Process Industry** | Specific gravity of process fluids is measured using hydrometers for quality control |
| **Pipe Flow Design** | Pressure drop calculations begin with ρ (Darcy-Weisbach equation needs ρ) |
| **Ship & Submarine Design** | Archimedes' principle (buoyancy) directly uses specific weight to determine floating/sinking |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Master Relationship Between G, γ, and ρ

### Starting Point
We begin with the **definition of Specific Gravity** as a ratio of two densities:

$$G = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$$

### Step 1: Cross-Multiply to Isolate ρ_fluid
Multiply both sides of the equation by ρ_water to isolate the fluid's density on one side:

$$\rho_{fluid} = G \cdot \rho_{water}$$

**Logic Explanation:** This step is simply algebraic rearrangement. The "G times water's density" gives us the actual density of any fluid whose specific gravity is known. This is a **time-saving shortcut** in numerical problems.

### Step 2: Substitute into the Specific Weight Definition
Recall the definition: $\gamma_{fluid} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g$

Replace ρ_fluid with the expression from Step 1:

$$\gamma_{fluid} = (G \cdot \rho_{water}) \cdot g$$

**Logic Explanation:** We are substituting the known expression for fluid density into the weight-density formula. The right-hand side now contains only *known quantities* (G is given, ρ_water is a standard constant, g is the gravitational acceleration).

### Step 3: Group ρ_water and g Together
Recognize that $\rho_{water} \cdot g = \gamma_{water}$ (the specific weight of water):

$$\gamma_{fluid} = G \cdot (\rho_{water} \cdot g) = G \cdot \gamma_{water}$$

**Logic Explanation:** This compact form is elegant — it tells us that the *specific weight of any fluid* equals its *specific gravity multiplied by the specific weight of water*. This is the most useful single equation on this topic.

### Step 4: Insert Standard Numerical Values
Substitute $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$\gamma_{fluid} = G \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81 = G \cdot 9810 \quad \text{N/m}^3$$

**Final Master Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = 9810 \cdot G \quad [\text{N/m}^3]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reciprocal Relationship Between Specific Volume and Density

### Starting Point
By definition:

$$v = \frac{V}{m}$$

### Step 1: Compare with Density
Recall that density is defined as:

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

**Logic Explanation:** Notice that v and ρ are **mathematically reciprocals** — v has V in the numerator (m in denominator), while ρ has m in the numerator (V in denominator).

### Step 2: Multiply the Two Equations

$$\rho \cdot v = \frac{m}{V} \cdot \frac{V}{m}$$

**Logic Explanation:** The mass terms cancel (m/m = 1), and the volume terms cancel (V/V = 1).

### Step 3: Simplify

$$\rho \cdot v = 1$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{v = \frac{1}{\rho} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \rho = \frac{1}{v}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Typical KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement:**  
A liquid has a specific gravity of **0.85**. The liquid is stored in a tank of cross-sectional area **2 m²** to a depth of **3 m**. Calculate:
1. Specific weight of the liquid (in kN/m³)
2. Density of the liquid (in kg/m³)
3. Specific volume of the liquid (in m³/kg)
4. Total weight of the liquid in the tank (in kN)

**Given:** $G = 0.85$, $A = 2 \text{ m}^2$, $h = 3 \text{ m}$

**Constants:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

---

### Part (i): Specific Weight of the Liquid

Using the master relationship:

$$\gamma_{fluid} = G \cdot \gamma_{water}$$

$$\gamma_{fluid} = 0.85 \times 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3 = 8.3385 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Logic:** We multiplied specific gravity (0.85) by the standard specific weight of water (9810 N/m³). Since 0.85 < 1, the result is less than water's specific weight, as expected for a lighter-than-water liquid (like oil or petrol).

---

### Part (ii): Density of the Liquid

Using $G = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$:

$$\rho_{fluid} = G \cdot \rho_{water} = 0.85 \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{fluid} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Logic:** 0.85 times 1000 kg/m³ gives 850 kg/m³, a typical value for fuels like diesel or gasoline.

---

### Part (iii): Specific Volume of the Liquid

Using $v = 1/\rho$:

$$v = \frac{1}{850 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$\boxed{v = 1.176 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

**Logic:** The reciprocal of 850 is approximately 0.001176. This is a small number, consistent with a liquid (liquids are dense, so 1 kg occupies very little volume).

---

### Part (iv): Total Weight of Liquid in the Tank

First, find the volume of liquid:

$$V = A \times h = 2 \text{ m}^2 \times 3 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ m}^3$$

Then compute the total weight:

$$W = \gamma_{fluid} \times V = 8.3385 \text{ kN/m}^3 \times 6 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{W = 50.031 \text{ kN}}$$

**Logic:** Total weight = specific weight × volume. The result ≈ 50 kN is the total gravitational force exerted by the liquid on the tank base.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Relationship Between Fluid Properties

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **logical connections** between the three fluid properties covered in this topic, plus the foundational concept of mass density.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["MASS DENSITY rho (kg per m cube)"] --> B["SPECIFIC WEIGHT gamma (N per m cube)"]
    A --> C["SPECIFIC VOLUME v (m cube per kg)"]
    A --> D["SPECIFIC GRAVITY G (dimensionless)"]

    B --> E["Hydrostatic Pressure P equals gamma h"]
    B --> F["Dam Thrust Force F equals half gamma h squared b"]
    B --> G["Buoyancy and Archimedes Principle"]

    C --> H["Ideal Gas Law P v equals R T"]
    C --> I["Compressor and Turbine Sizing"]

    D --> J["Hydrometer Reading (Quality Control)"]
    D --> K["API Gravity for Petroleum Classification"]
    D --> L["Fluid Comparison Charts"]

    A --> M["MASS m (kg)"]
    A --> N["VOLUME V (m cube)"]

    M --> A
    N --> A

    D --> B
    B -. uses .-> D
```

**Diagram Interpretation Guide for Students:**

- **Center node A (Mass Density ρ)** acts as the *parent property* — all other properties are derived from it.
- The arrows leaving A show the three transformation directions:
  - **A → B:** Multiplying by **g** converts density to **specific weight**.
  - **A → C:** Taking the **reciprocal** gives **specific volume**.
  - **A → D:** Dividing by **ρ_water** (and comparing) gives **dimensionless specific gravity**.
- The **dashed arrow from B to D** indicates the *alternate* path: G = γ_fluid / γ_water (you can compute G using specific weight, not just density).
- The right-side blocks (E through L) show the **engineering applications** that consume each property.

---

## 4.2 Comparative Property Matrix for Common Fluids

The following reference table consolidates standard values for common fluids you will encounter in KTU numerical problems. **Memorize these — they appear repeatedly in past papers.**

| **Fluid** | **Density ρ (kg/m³)** | **Specific Weight γ (kN/m³)** | **Specific Gravity G** | **Specific Volume v (m³/kg)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water (at 4°C) | 1000 | 9.81 | 1.000 | 0.001000 |
| Sea Water | 1025 | 10.06 | 1.025 | 0.000976 |
| Mercury (Hg) | 13600 | 133.4 | 13.60 | 0.0000735 |
| Standard Air (15°C) | 1.225 | 0.01201 | 0.001225 | 0.816 |
| Diesel Fuel | 850 | 8.34 | 0.85 | 0.001176 |
| Engine Oil (SAE 30) | 920 | 9.03 | 0.92 | 0.001087 |
| Glycerine | 1260 | 12.36 | 1.26 | 0.000794 |
| Petrol/Gasoline | 750 | 7.36 | 0.75 | 0.001333 |
| Blood (Human) | 1060 | 10.40 | 1.06 | 0.000943 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Memorization Strategy:** Note that specific weight and specific gravity differ only by a factor of **9.81**. So if you know G, just multiply by 9.81 to get γ in kN/m³. This mental math trick saves time in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Specific Gravity and Specific Volume of a fluid. Why is water chosen as the reference fluid for measuring specific gravity of liquids?**

**Model Answer:**

**Specific Gravity (Relative Density):** It is defined as the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a standard reference fluid (water, at 4°C). It is a **dimensionless** quantity.

$$G = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water,\,4°C}}$$

**Specific Volume:** It is defined as the volume of a fluid per unit mass. It is the **reciprocal of density** and has SI units of **m³/kg**.

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Why water as reference?**
1. Water is **abundant, easily available, and chemically stable**.
2. Water has its **maximum density (1000 kg/m³) at 4°C**, which provides a unique, temperature-independent reference value.
3. Water is a **universal solvent** and a **common reference** in engineering practice, making it the international standard (IS/ISO/ASTM).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A liquid has a specific gravity of 1.2. Find its specific weight, density, and specific volume. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $G = 1.2$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Specific Weight:**

$$\gamma = G \cdot \gamma_{water} = 1.2 \times 9810 = 11772 \text{ N/m}^3 = 11.772 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Density:**

$$\rho = G \cdot \rho_{water} = 1.2 \times 1000 = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Specific Volume:**

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{1200} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Loses 1 Mark Each):**
> - Forgetting to convert **9810 N/m³ to 9.81 kN/m³** when the question asks in kN.
> - Writing specific volume as **0.000833** instead of the scientific notation **8.33 × 10⁻⁴ m³/kg**.
> - Using g = 9.8 or 10 instead of 9.81 m/s². The KTU answer key usually accepts any of these, but the **standard is 9.81**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Define the following fluid properties with their SI units: (i) Specific Weight, (ii) Specific Volume, (iii) Specific Gravity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A storage tank contains **5000 litres** of a petroleum product with specific gravity **0.82**. Calculate:
   (i) The mass of the liquid in kg
   (ii) The specific weight of the liquid in N/m³
   (iii) The weight of the liquid in kN
   
   Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Definitions (7 Marks)

**(i) Specific Weight (γ):**  
The specific weight of a fluid is defined as the **weight of the fluid per unit volume**. It is the product of mass density and acceleration due to gravity.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = \frac{W}{V} \quad [\text{N/m}^3 \text{ or kN/m}^3]$$

**(ii) Specific Volume (v):**  
The specific volume of a fluid is defined as the **volume of the fluid per unit mass**. It is the reciprocal of mass density.

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{kg}]$$

**(iii) Specific Gravity (G):**  
The specific gravity (or relative density) of a fluid is defined as the **ratio of the density of the fluid to the density of water at 4°C** (1000 kg/m³). It is a **dimensionless** quantity.

$$G = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water,\,4°C}} \quad [\text{Dimensionless}]$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Volume, $V = 5000 \text{ litres} = 5 \text{ m}^3$ (since 1000 litres = 1 m³)
- Specific gravity, $G = 0.82$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Mass of the liquid:**

First, find density:

$$\rho_{fluid} = G \cdot \rho_{water} = 0.82 \times 1000 = 820 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Then find mass:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 820 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 5 \text{ m}^3 = 4100 \text{ kg}$$

**∴ Mass = 4100 kg** ✓ (1 mark for density, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer with unit)

**(ii) Specific weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 820 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 8044.2 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**∴ Specific weight = 8044.2 N/m³ (or 8.044 kN/m³)** ✓ (1.5 marks)

**(iii) Total weight of liquid:**

$$W = \gamma \cdot V = 8044.2 \text{ N/m}^3 \times 5 \text{ m}^3 = 40221 \text{ N} = 40.221 \text{ kN}$$

**∴ Weight = 40.221 kN** ✓ (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfalls:**  
> - **Most common error:** Students forget to convert **5000 litres to 5 m³**. This single mistake cascades through all three sub-parts, leading to wrong mass, wrong weight, and **loss of up to 5 marks**.  
> - **Second most common error:** Using $g = 10$ m/s² throughout instead of 9.81, which gives a slightly different numerical answer. Always use **9.81** unless explicitly told otherwise.  
> - **Step mark deduction:** Even if the final numerical answer is correct, the examiner awards step marks only if you show the **intermediate formula and substitution**. Don't jump directly to the final answer.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Distinguish between **mass density** and **specific weight** of a fluid. A fluid has a mass density of **1.5 × 10³ kg/m³**. Find its specific weight in (i) N/m³ and (ii) kN/m³. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The specific volume of air at standard atmospheric conditions is given as **0.84 m³/kg**. Determine:
   (i) The mass density of air in kg/m³
   (ii) The specific weight of air in N/m³
   (iii) The specific gravity of air with respect to water (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³)  
   Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Distinction + Numerical (7 Marks)

**Distinction Table:**

| **Basis** | **Mass Density (ρ)** | **Specific Weight (γ)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Mass per unit volume | Weight per unit volume |
| Formula | $\rho = m/V$ | $\gamma = W/V = \rho \cdot g$ |
| SI Unit | kg/m³ | N/m³ or kN/m³ |
| Depends on g? | **No** | **Yes** (varies with location) |
| Nature | Intrinsic property of material | Varies with gravitational field |

**Given:** $\rho = 1.5 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Specific weight in N/m³:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(ii) Specific weight in kN/m³:**

$$\gamma = \frac{14715}{1000} = 14.715 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

✓ (2 marks for distinction table, 2.5 marks for numerical in N/m³, 2.5 marks for conversion to kN/m³)

---

#### Part (b) — Air Properties (7 Marks)

**Given:** $v_{air} = 0.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Mass density of air:**

Since $v = 1/\rho$:

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{0.84} = 1.190 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

✓ (2 marks)

**(ii) Specific weight of air:**

$$\gamma_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot g = 1.190 \times 9.81 = 11.68 \text{ N/m}^3$$

✓ (2.5 marks)

**(iii) Specific gravity of air:**

$$G_{air} = \frac{\rho_{air}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{1.190}{1000} = 0.001190 = 1.190 \times 10^{-3}$$

✓ (2.5 marks — including the scientific notation requirement)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b) Air Properties:**  
> - Students often confuse **specific volume of air** (≈ 0.84 m³/kg) with **specific weight of air** (≈ 11.68 N/m³) — these are **completely different properties**.  
> - A common mistake: writing **G = 1.190** instead of **1.190 × 10⁻³**. The specific gravity of air is a very small number (about 1/800th of water), so the **10⁻³ factor is essential**.  
> - Examiners deduct **½ mark** if you forget to write the **unitless / dimensionless** annotation for specific gravity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

Use this **high-density checklist** for last-minute revision before your KTU Module 1 exam:

### Core Definitions (Memorize Word-for-Word)
- ✅ **Mass Density (ρ):** Mass per unit volume, units = **kg/m³**.
- ✅ **Specific Weight (γ):** Weight per unit volume, units = **N/m³** or **kN/m³**. **Includes gravity** in its definition.
- ✅ **Specific Volume (v):** Volume per unit mass, units = **m³/kg**. **Reciprocal of density.**
- ✅ **Specific Gravity (G):** Dimensionless ratio to water at **4°C** (1000 kg/m³). **Reciprocal relationship with density.**

### Master Equation to Memorize
- ✅ $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ — The single most important equation in this topic.
- ✅ $\gamma_{fluid} = 9810 \cdot G$ (N/m³) or $= 9.81 \cdot G$ (kN/m³) — Quick computation formula.
- ✅ $v = 1/\rho$ — Reciprocal relationship.

### Standard Reference Values (No Excuse to Forget)
- ✅ $\rho_{water,\,4°C} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- ✅ $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- ✅ $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- ✅ $1 \text{ litre} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$

### Conceptual Distinctions (Frequent Question)
- ✅ **Density vs. Specific Weight:** Density is independent of g; specific weight depends on g.
- ✅ **Specific Volume vs. Specific Weight:** Specific volume has mass in denominator; specific weight has volume in denominator.
- ✅ **Specific Gravity of water = 1** (by definition).

### Numerical Problem-Solving Checklist
- ✅ Always **state given data** with units at the start.
- ✅ Always **show the formula** before substituting values (earns step marks).
- ✅ Always **include units** in the final answer.
- ✅ Always **check reasonableness** — e.g., if G < 1, the fluid should be lighter than water (oil, petrol, air).
- ✅ Always convert **litres → m³** by dividing by 1000 (common conversion error).

### Connections to Future Modules
- ✅ Module 1 (this one) — properties used as **input data** for problems.
- ✅ Module 2 (Fluid Statics) — uses **γ** in $P = \gamma h$ and **G** in manometer problems.
- ✅ Module 3 (Fluid Kinematics) — uses **ρ** in Reynolds number calculations.
- ✅ Module 4 (Fluid Dynamics) — uses **ρ** in Bernoulli's equation energy per unit volume form.
- ✅ Module 5 (Hydraulic Machines) — uses **ρ and γ** for power calculations ($P = \rho g Q H$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Phenomenon of Viscosity

**Viscosity** is the physical property of a fluid that quantifies its internal resistance to deformation (or flow) when subjected to an external shear force. It is the fluid's analogue to friction in solids — every fluid "sticks" to varying degrees when its layers slide past one another.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCSFT303, viscosity is treated as a **transport property** of fluids, alongside compressibility and surface tension. It directly governs the magnitude of internal shear stress ($\tau$) developed within a moving fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> The student is expected to clearly distinguish between **dynamic (absolute) viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity**, state their respective **SI and CGS units**, **dimensions**, and understand the **physical significance** of each in real engineering problems.

---

## 1.2 Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute Viscosity) — Formal Definition

**Dynamic viscosity**, denoted by the Greek letter **$\mu$ (mu)**, is defined as the **shear stress** ($\tau$) developed per unit **velocity gradient** (or rate of shear strain, $\frac{du}{dy}$) in a flowing fluid.

Mathematically, it emerges directly from **Sir Isaac Newton's Law of Viscosity (1687)**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress developed in the fluid (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $\mu$:**
> A fluid with a **high $\mu$** (e.g., honey, tar, glycerine) flows sluggishly and resists motion strongly. A fluid with a **low $\mu$** (e.g., water, air, alcohol) flows easily. Dynamic viscosity is the direct measure of this "stickiness" between fluid layers.

---

## 1.3 Kinematic Viscosity — Formal Definition

**Kinematic viscosity**, denoted by the Greek letter **$\nu$ (nu)**, is defined as the **ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid**:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity (m²/s)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s = kg/m·s)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Kinematic"?**
> The word *kinematic* refers to motion. Because $\nu$ arises from the ratio $\frac{\mu}{\rho}$ (force-based quantity ÷ mass-based quantity), it depends **only on motion and geometry**, not on the force causing the flow. This makes $\nu$ the natural parameter in momentum-diffusion problems and dimensionless numbers like the **Reynolds Number ($Re = \frac{VL}{\nu}$)**.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy — The "Stack of Cards" Model

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** held in your palm:

- If you push the top card sideways, it slides smoothly over the next card, and so on, until the bottom card (fixed by your palm) barely moves.
- Each layer of "fluid" moves at a slightly different velocity, and the **friction between adjacent layers** is what viscosity represents.
- **Honey** = the cards are extremely "grippy" → high $\mu$ → high resistance to sliding.
- **Water** = the cards slide over each other easily → low $\mu$ → low resistance.
- **Air** = nearly frictionless cards → very low $\mu$.

The **kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ can be intuitively viewed as the *fluidity* of a fluid *relative to its own inertia* — a light fluid (low $\rho$) and a moderately viscous fluid can still have a large $\nu$ because $\nu$ amplifies with decreasing density.

---

## 1.5 Standard Reference Values (At 20 °C and 1 atm)

| Fluid | Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) | Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$ (m²/s) |
|---|---|---|
| Water | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Air | $1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ | $1.51 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| Glycerine | $1.49$ | $1.19 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Mercury | $1.55 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.14 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| SAE 30 Oil | $\approx 0.29$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{-4}$ |

---

## 1.6 The Two Major Classification Flags

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newtonian Fluids** — Fluids that strictly obey $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ with a **constant $\mu$** (independent of the rate of shear). Examples: Water, air, gasoline, alcohol, thin oils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Newtonian Fluids** — Fluids whose apparent viscosity changes with the rate of shear. Examples: Blood, toothpaste, paint, ketchup, slurries. The 2024 KTU scheme restricts the viscosity study in Module 1 to **Newtonian fluids**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Bedrock Equation

When a fluid flows over a solid surface, the layer of fluid in **direct molecular contact with the surface adheres to it (no-slip condition)** and has zero velocity. Successive layers above this move with progressively higher velocities, generating a **velocity profile** $u(y)$.

The relative motion between adjacent layers produces a **shear stress** $\tau$ proportional to the local **velocity gradient** $\frac{du}{dy}$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}}$$

This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. The proportionality constant $\mu$ is the **dynamic (absolute) viscosity**.

### 2.1.1 Why does this law exist physically?
- Fluid molecules in faster-moving layers possess **higher momentum** than those in slower layers.
- Due to random molecular motion (Brownian-like), fast molecules continually migrate into slow layers and vice versa.
- This **momentum exchange** is the microscopic origin of the macroscopic shear stress $\tau$.
- The constant $\mu$ encapsulates the ease or difficulty of this molecular momentum transfer.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of $\mu$ from Newton's Law

Rearranging the fundamental equation:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)} = \frac{\text{Shear stress}}{\text{Velocity gradient}}$$

**Interpretation:** Dynamic viscosity equals the shear stress required to produce a **unit velocity gradient** in the fluid.

---

## 2.3 Kinematic Viscosity — The Derived Quantity

The term "kinematic" arises because $\nu$ contains only the kinematic variables (length and time) — no force term.

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- $\nu$ is the **diffusivity of momentum** in the fluid.
- It governs the rate at which momentum disturbances (e.g., a vortex) spread through the fluid.
- It is the natural variable in the **Navier-Stokes equations** when written in non-conservative form.

---

## 2.4 Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Viscosity

### 2.4.1 Temperature Effect

| Fluid Type | Behaviour with Increasing Temperature | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquids** (e.g., water, oil) | $\mu$ **decreases** | Intermolecular cohesive forces weaken; molecules slip past each other more easily. |
| **Gases** (e.g., air, N₂) | $\mu$ **increases** | Molecular momentum exchange increases with temperature. |

A useful empirical relation for liquids is the **Andrade's equation**:

$$\mu = A \cdot e^{\frac{B}{T}}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are fluid-specific constants and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

### 2.4.2 Pressure Effect
- For **liquids**, increasing pressure slightly **increases** $\mu$ (compression brings molecules closer).
- For **gases**, pressure has **negligible effect** on $\mu$ over a wide range (at constant temperature).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **high-yield formulas** for Module 1 viscosity problems. Memorize the units and dimensions — they appear in almost every KTU exam paper.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | — | — | — |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity (definition) | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$ | **poise (P)** | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | **stoke (St)** | $L^2 T^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Centipoise → Pa·s | $1\,\text{cP} = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | — | — | — |
| 5 | Centistoke → m²/s | $1\,\text{cSt} = 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | — | — | — |
| 6 | Reynolds Number (uses $\nu$) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | dimensionless | $M^0 L^0 T^0$ |
| 7 | Specific Weight relation | $\rho = \dfrac{w}{g}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| 8 | Dynamic viscosity via $\nu$ | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | poise | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Traps to Avoid (Examiner Hot-Spot):**
> - $1$ **poise** $= 1$ **g/(cm·s)** $= 0.1$ **Pa·s** $= 1$ **dyne·s/cm²**
> - $1$ **centipoise (cP)** $= 0.01$ **poise** $= 0.001$ **Pa·s** (water at 20 °C is **1 cP**)
> - $1$ **stoke (St)** $= 1$ **cm²/s** $= 10^{-4}$ **m²/s**
> - $1$ **centistoke (cSt)** $= 10^{-6}$ **m²/s**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pipeline Design** — Darcy-Weisbach friction factor depends on $Re$, which uses $\nu$. Correct viscosity input determines pumping power.
2. **Lubrication Engineering** — Bearing design uses dynamic viscosity $\mu$ of the lubricant to compute the shear stress in journal bearings.
3. **Hydraulic Machines** — Selection of turbines and pumps depends on the viscosity of the working fluid.
4. **Meteorology & Oceanography** — Air and water viscosities govern atmospheric boundary layer dynamics and ocean current friction.
5. **Polymer & Food Industry** — Viscosity measurements are quality-control parameters (Non-Newtonian context — beyond Module 1).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dimensions of Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$)

We begin with the defining equation (Newton's Law of Viscosity):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Rearranging to isolate $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)}$$

### Step 1: Dimensions of Shear Stress ($\tau$)

Shear stress is force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

The dimensions of force are $[M L T^{-2}]$, and area is $[L^2]$. Therefore:

$$[\tau] = \frac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$$

### Step 2: Dimensions of Velocity Gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$)

Velocity $u$ has dimensions $[L T^{-1}]$. Distance $y$ has dimensions $[L]$. Thus:

$$\left[\frac{du}{dy}\right] = \frac{[L T^{-1}]}{[L]} = [T^{-1}]$$

### Step 3: Combine to Obtain $[\mu]$

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[du/dy]} = \frac{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]}$$

$$\boxed{[\mu] = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]}$$

This is the **dimensional formula** for dynamic viscosity. In SI base units, it is $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dimensions of Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)

We use the defining relation:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

### Step 1: Dimensions of Density ($\rho$)

Density is mass per unit volume:

$$[\rho] = \frac{[M]}{[L^3]} = [M L^{-3}]$$

### Step 2: Divide $[\mu]$ by $[\rho]$

$$[\nu] = \frac{[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]}{[M L^{-3}]} = [M^{1-1} \cdot L^{-1-(-3)} \cdot T^{-1}]$$

$$\boxed{[\nu] = [L^2 T^{-1}]}$$

This confirms that kinematic viscosity has dimensions of **length² / time** — exactly the dimensions of any *diffusivity* (thermal, mass, or momentum).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the SI Unit of Dynamic Viscosity

The SI unit of $\mu$ is **Pa·s** (Pascal-second). Let us verify by dimensional analysis:

$$1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 1\,\frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} \cdot \text{s} = 1\,\frac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}/\text{s}^2}{\text{m}^2}\cdot\text{s}$$

Simplifying step by step:

$$= 1\,\frac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}}{\text{s}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2}\cdot\text{s} = 1\,\frac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{s}}{\text{s}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2}$$

$$= 1\,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}\cdot\text{m}} = \text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

This matches the dimensions $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$ derived above. **Proved.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Conversion between Poise and Pa·s

A poise is the CGS unit of dynamic viscosity:

$$1\,\text{P} = 1\,\frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}\cdot\text{s}}$$

Convert each base unit to SI:
- $1\,\text{g} = 10^{-3}\,\text{kg}$
- $1\,\text{cm} = 10^{-2}\,\text{m}$
- $1\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{s}$

Substitute:

$$1\,\text{P} = \frac{10^{-3}\,\text{kg}}{(10^{-2}\,\text{m})\cdot(1\,\text{s})} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-2}}\,\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}\cdot\text{s}}$$

$$1\,\text{P} = 10^{-1}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{1\,\text{poise} = 0.1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}}$$

And conversely:

$$1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 10\,\text{poise}$$

Similarly for centipoise:

$$1\,\text{cP} = 10^{-2}\,\text{P} = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Compute $\mu$ and $\nu$ of a given fluid

**Problem:** A liquid of density $850\,\text{kg/m}^3$ flows between two parallel plates $5\,\text{mm}$ apart. The bottom plate is fixed; the top plate moves at $0.4\,\text{m/s}$ under a shear stress of $0.68\,\text{Pa}$. Compute $\mu$ and $\nu$.

### Step 1: Identify the velocity gradient
Linear velocity profile (Newtonian assumption):

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.4 - 0}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.4}{0.005} = 80\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's law to get $\mu$

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{0.68}{80} = 8.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Compute $\nu$

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\mu = 8.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} \quad ; \quad \nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

This fluid behaves like a **light lubricating oil**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Engineering Scenario | Use $\mu$ (Dynamic) When... | Use $\nu$ (Kinematic) When... |
|---|---|---|
| Computing shear stress $\tau$ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Reynolds number $Re$ | ❌ (use $\nu$) | ✅ |
| Bearing / lubrication analysis | ✅ | ❌ |
| Pipe flow / boundary layer | ❌ (use $\nu$) | ✅ |
| Viscous force $F = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Stokes' law $F_D = 6\pi\mu r v$ | ✅ | ❌ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity: Conceptual Block Schematic

The schematic below illustrates how velocity varies across a fluid layer bounded by a fixed plate (bottom) and a moving plate (top), and how shear stress arises.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Force F applied on top plate] --> B[Top plate moves with velocity U]
    B --> C[Fluid layer thickness h between two parallel plates]
    C --> D[Linear velocity profile u_y = U times y by h for Newtonian fluid]
    D --> E[Shear stress tau generated on fluid layer]
    E --> F[Tau equals mu times du by dy]
    F --> G[Newton Law of Viscosity: mu is dynamic viscosity]
    G --> H[Kinematic viscosity nu equals mu divided by rho]
    H --> I[Used in Reynolds number and pipe flow analysis]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Classification of Fluids by Viscosity Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fluids] --> B{Obey Newton Law of Viscosity?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Newtonian Fluids]
    B -- No --> D[Non Newtonian Fluids]

    C --> C1[Water]
    C --> C2[Air]
    C --> C3[Thin Oils]
    C --> C4[Glycerine]
    C --> C5[Constant mu independent of shear rate]

    D --> D1[Pseudoplastic example: paint blood]
    D --> D2[Dilatant example: cornstarch slurry]
    D --> D3[Bingham plastic example: toothpaste]
    D --> D4[Thixotropic example: ketchup]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture of Viscosity Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Input Parameters]
        I1[Shear stress tau in Pa]
        I2[Velocity gradient du by dy in per second]
        I3[Fluid density rho in kg per cubic meter]
    end

    subgraph S2[Core Engine: Viscosity Laws]
        P1[Newton Law: tau equals mu times du by dy]
        P2[Definition: mu equals tau divided by du by dy]
        P3[Kinematic relation: nu equals mu divided by rho]
    end

    subgraph S3[Output Quantities]
        O1[Dynamic viscosity mu in Pa times s]
        O2[Kinematic viscosity nu in square meter per second]
        O3[Reynolds number Re equals V times L divided by nu]
        O4[Shear force F equals mu times A times du by dy]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P1 --> O4
    P3 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Temperature Increase] --> B{Type of Fluid}
    B -- Liquid --> C[Dynamic viscosity mu DECREASES]
    B -- Gas --> D[Dynamic viscosity mu INCREASES]
    C --> E[Empirical: mu equals A times exp of B over T - Andrade Equation]
    D --> F[Molecular momentum exchange increases with T]
    C --> G[Engineering consequence: oil thins in summer]
    D --> H[Engineering consequence: hot air boundary layer thickens]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]`
**Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the deformation (or shearing) of the fluid. Mathematically, it is the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$):

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$$

- **SI Unit:** $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$
- **CGS Unit:** poise (P); 1 cP = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- **Dimensions:** $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- **SI Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **CGS Unit:** stoke (St); 1 cSt = $10^{-6}$ m²/s
- **Dimensions:** $[L^2 T^{-1}]$

> [!NOTE]
> *Awarded marks: 1 mark for definition of $\mu$, 1 mark for $\nu$, 1 mark for units and dimensions combined.*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]`
**Distinguish between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Obeys Newton's law | Yes, strictly | No |
| Dynamic viscosity | Constant | Apparent viscosity changes with shear rate |
| Velocity profile in pipe | Parabolic (laminar) | Modified (plug flow, etc.) |
| Examples | Water, air, gasoline, thin oils | Blood, paint, toothpaste, ketchup |
| Shear stress vs rate of shear strain | Linear relationship | Non-linear |

In Newtonian fluids, the plot of $\tau$ vs $\frac{du}{dy}$ is a **straight line through the origin** with slope $\mu$. In non-Newtonian fluids, this plot is **curved** or even exhibits a yield stress.

*Awarded 3 marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for the contrast, 1 for examples.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **internal choice**. You must attempt **one** of the two options. Each option below has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question B — Option A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A) A fluid of density $900\,\text{kg/m}^3$ is contained between two horizontal parallel plates $4\,\text{mm}$ apart. The lower plate is fixed and the upper plate moves at $0.6\,\text{m/s}$. The shear stress observed is $1.08\,\text{Pa}$.**

**(a)** Determine the **dynamic viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity** of the fluid.  
**(b)** If the temperature rises and the dynamic viscosity drops to $0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, calculate the new shear stress and the kinematic viscosity. Comment on the type of fluid.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Velocity gradient (assuming linear profile, Newtonian):

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.6}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 150\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Dynamic viscosity from Newton's law:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{1.08}{150} = 7.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Kinematic viscosity:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{900} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answers for (a):**

$$\boxed{\mu = 7.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} \quad ; \quad \nu = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for $\frac{du}{dy}$ + 3 marks for $\mu$ + 2 marks for $\nu$.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\mu_{\text{new}} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

**Step 1:** New kinematic viscosity:

$$\nu_{\text{new}} = \frac{\mu_{\text{new}}}{\rho} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}{900} = 8.89 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** New shear stress (velocity gradient unchanged at $150\,\text{s}^{-1}$):

$$\tau_{\text{new}} = \mu_{\text{new}} \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = (8.0 \times 10^{-4})(150) = 0.12\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Comment on type of fluid.

Since dynamic viscosity **decreased with rising temperature**, the fluid behaves as a **liquid** (e.g., a lubricating oil). The values of $\mu$ and $\nu$ fall in the range typical of light machine oils (SAE 10 grade), confirming it is a **Newtonian liquid**.

**Final Answers for (b):**

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{new}} = 0.12\,\text{Pa} \quad ; \quad \nu_{\text{new}} = 8.89 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for $\nu_{\text{new}}$ + 2 marks for $\tau_{\text{new}}$ + 3 marks for the comment.

---

### Question B — Option B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern]`

**B) (a)** Derive the **dimensional formula** for both dynamic and kinematic viscosity. A fluid has a dynamic viscosity of $0.85\,\text{P}$ and density $0.86\,\text{g/cm}^3$. Express both in **SI units**.

**(b)** A thin film of oil of viscosity $0.1\,\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$ is sandwiched between a fixed plate and a plate of area $0.5\,\text{m}^2$ moving at $0.05\,\text{m/s}$. If the film thickness is $2\,\text{mm}$, find the **shear stress** and the **force required** to maintain the motion.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Derivation of dimensions:**

Starting with $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$:

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[du/dy]} = \frac{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$$

For $\nu$:

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]}{[M L^{-3}]} = [L^2 T^{-1}]$$

**Unit conversions:**

Dynamic viscosity:
$$\mu = 0.85\,\text{P} = 0.85 \times 0.1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 0.085\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

Density:
$$\rho = 0.86\,\text{g/cm}^3 = 860\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

Kinematic viscosity in SI:
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.085}{860} = 9.88 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answers for (a):**

$$\boxed{[\mu] = M L^{-1} T^{-1} \quad ; \quad [\nu] = L^2 T^{-1} \quad ; \quad \mu = 0.085\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} \quad ; \quad \nu = 9.88 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> **Mark Split:** 3 marks for dimensional derivation, 2 marks for unit conversion of $\mu$, 2 marks for $\nu$ calculation.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given data:**
- $\mu = 0.1\,\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2 = 0.1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $V = 0.05\,\text{m/s}$
- $A = 0.5\,\text{m}^2$
- $h = 2\,\text{mm} = 0.002\,\text{m}$

**Step 1:** Velocity gradient:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{V}{h} = \frac{0.05}{0.002} = 25\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Shear stress:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.1 \times 25 = 2.5\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Shear force required:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 2.5 \times 0.5 = 1.25\,\text{N}$$

**Final Answers for (b):**

$$\boxed{\tau = 2.5\,\text{Pa} \quad ; \quad F = 1.25\,\text{N}}$$

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for velocity gradient, 2 marks for $\tau$, 3 marks for $F$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 1 Viscosity Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Mixing SI and CGS units in a single calculation is the **#1 mark-loss trigger**. If $\mu$ is in poise, all other quantities must be in CGS. If $\rho$ is in kg/m³, $\mu$ must be in Pa·s. **Pick a system and stay there.**
> 2. **Forgetting the $10^{-3}$** — Converting mm to m: a $5\,\text{mm}$ gap is $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, **not** $5$ m. Many students lose 2 marks on this silly slip.
> 3. **Confusing $\mu$ with $\nu$** — The Reynolds number uses $\nu$, NOT $\mu$. A direct substitution of $\mu$ into $Re = VL/\mu$ is incorrect; it must be divided by $\rho$ first (or use $Re = VL/\nu$).
> 4. **Forgetting to state units and dimensions** — In KTU theory answers, you must **always** quote both the SI unit and the dimensional formula. A correct number without units fetches **partial credit only**.
> 5. **Misclassifying fluids as non-Newtonian** — The Module 1 syllabus strictly deals with Newtonian fluids. Mentioning "non-Newtonian behaviour" in a $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ problem loses you a mark.
> 6. **Sign error in $\frac{du}{dy}$** — Always use the magnitude (or specify direction). Negative gradient values without sign convention confuse the examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Dynamic & Kinematic Viscosity):**
>
> - [x] **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$
> - [x] **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** SI = $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, CGS = poise (P), Dimensions = $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$
> - [x] **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$, SI = $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, CGS = stoke (St), Dimensions = $L^2 T^{-1}$
> - [x] **1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s ; 1 cP = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s ; 1 stoke = $10^{-4}$ m²/s ; 1 cSt = $10^{-6}$ m²/s**
> - [x] **Liquids:** $\mu$ decreases with temperature; **Gases:** $\mu$ increases with temperature.
> - [x] **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian:** Module 1 is restricted to **Newtonian** fluids.
> - [x] **Reynolds Number** uses $\nu$: $Re = \dfrac{VL}{\nu} = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$
> - [x] **Shear Force on a plate:** $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy} = \tau A$
> - [x] **No-slip condition:** Fluid at the wall has zero velocity relative to the wall.
> - [x] **Water at 20 °C:** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (memorize for sanity checks).
> - [x] **Stokes' Law (viscous drag on a sphere):** $F_D = 6\pi\mu r v$
> - [x] **Always state units and dimensions explicitly** in KTU theory answers.
> - [x] **Conversion traps:** $1\,\text{Pa} = 1\,\text{N/m}^2 = 10\,\text{dyne/cm}^2$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fluid Mechanics - Types of Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Fluid Mechanics - Types of Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fluid Mechanics — The Formal Definition

**Fluid Mechanics** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the behavior of fluids (liquids and gases) at rest (**Fluid Statics / Hydrostatics**) and in motion (**Fluid Dynamics / Hydrodynamics**), including the study of the forces, energy transfers, and interactions between the fluid and its boundaries.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the subject is divided into foundational theory (Module 1: Properties of Fluids & Fluid Statics) followed by kinematics, dynamics, and hydraulic machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *fluid* is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** under the action of an applied **shear stress** (tangential force), no matter how infinitesimally small that stress may be. This is the single most important defining property of a fluid — its inability to sustain a static shear stress.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine stacking **five solid wooden blocks** on a table. Push the top block sideways with your finger. The blocks will resist and may *slide as a rigid pack*, but they will **not continuously deform**. The wood has internal structure (molecular bonds) that resists tangential force up to a yield point.

Now take a glass of water and tilt it slightly. The water *flows* and continuously changes shape. It offers **no resistance to static shear stress** — the moment you apply a tangential force (gravity component along the slope), the fluid layers slide past one another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Anchor:** A solid **resists** shear stress elastically up to a limit. A fluid **yields** to shear stress continuously. This is the conceptual cornerstone of Module 1.

## 1.3 Why Study Fluids?

Fluid mechanics underpins nearly every engineering domain:

- **Civil Engineering** — design of dams, spillways, water supply networks, open channel flow, sewer systems.
- **Mechanical Engineering** — design of turbines, pumps, hydraulic presses, lubrication systems, HVAC.
- **Aerospace Engineering** — aerodynamics, jet propulsion, wind tunnel testing.
- **Chemical Engineering** — pipeline transport, mixing, reaction engineering.
- **Biomedical Engineering** — blood flow in arteries, drug delivery.

## 1.4 The Continuum Hypothesis

At the atomic scale, matter is **discrete** — it consists of molecules separated by empty space. However, dealing with individual molecular motion in engineering problems is computationally impossible and physically unnecessary for typical macroscopic volumes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuum Hypothesis:** Fluid properties (density, pressure, velocity, temperature) are assumed to vary **continuously** in space and time, and the fluid is treated as a **continuous distribution of matter** rather than a collection of discrete molecules.

**Valid when:** The smallest length scale of interest (e.g., pipe diameter, body length) is at least **1000 times larger** than the molecular mean free path. For air at atmospheric conditions (mean free path ≈ 0.066 μm), any engineering geometry larger than about **0.1 mm** qualifies.

## 1.5 Units and Standard Metrics (SI System)

| Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Mass | kilogram | **kg** |
| Length | meter | **m** |
| Time | second | **s** |
| Force | newton | **N** (= kg·m/s²) |
| Pressure / Stress | pascal | **Pa** (= N/m²) |
| Dynamic Viscosity | pascal-second | **Pa·s** |
| Kinematic Viscosity | square meter per second | **m²/s** |

Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard value used throughout KTU problems).
Standard atmospheric pressure: **p_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa**.
Standard water density at 4 °C: **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**.
Standard air density at 15 °C: **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physical Properties of Fluids

### (A) Mass Density (ρ)

**Definition:** Mass per unit volume of the fluid.

$$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to \delta V} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V}$$

where $\delta V$ is the **infinitesimal volume** at which the continuum assumption still holds. **SI Unit: kg/m³**.

### (B) Specific Weight (γ)

**Definition:** Weight per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

**SI Unit: N/m³**. For water at standard conditions, $\gamma_{water} = 9810$ N/m³.

### (C) Specific Gravity (S) or Relative Density (RD)

**Definition:** Ratio of the density of a fluid to the density of a standard reference fluid (water for liquids, air for gases, both at standard temperature — usually 4 °C for water).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} \quad \text{(for liquids)}, \qquad S = \frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}} \quad \text{(for gases)}$$

**It is dimensionless** and is simply a number (e.g., mercury S = 13.6).

### (D) Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Core Constitutive Equation

When a fluid flows, internal friction arises between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. This friction manifests as a **shear stress (τ)** proportional to the **velocity gradient (du/dy)** perpendicular to the flow direction.

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = **dynamic (absolute) viscosity** (Pa·s)
- $du/dy$ = **velocity gradient** perpendicular to flow (1/s) — also called **shear strain rate** $\dot{\gamma}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign in some texts?** Some authors write $\tau = -\mu (du/dy)$ to indicate that shear stress on a fluid layer acts **opposite** to the relative motion of the adjacent layer (Newton's third law pair). The magnitude form $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ is used in KTU problems — be careful with sign conventions.

### (E) Dynamic Viscosity (μ) and Kinematic Viscosity (ν)

The ratio of dynamic to kinematic viscosity gives the **density**:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Units:** μ → Pa·s (= kg/m·s); ν → m²/s. For water at 20 °C: μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, ν ≈ 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s. For air at 20 °C: μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, ν ≈ 1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity of a fluid decreases with temperature (for liquids), and increases with temperature (for gases).** This counter-intuitive behavior is a favorite KTU question topic.

### (F) Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)

**Definition:** A fluid's resistance to **uniform compression** under external pressure. It is the ratio of change in pressure to the **volumetric strain (relative change in volume)**.

$$K = -V \frac{dp}{dV} = \rho \frac{dp}{d\rho}$$

**SI Unit: Pa** (often expressed in GPa for water: K_water ≈ 2.2 GPa).

### (G) Surface Tension (σ)

**Definition:** The force per unit length acting along a fluid surface, tending to minimize surface area. Caused by cohesive molecular attraction at the free surface.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L}$$

**SI Unit: N/m**. For water–air at 20 °C: σ = 0.0728 N/m. The phenomenon explains **capillary rise**, **capillary depression**, and the **spherical shape of droplets**.

### (H) Capillarity (h)

The rise or fall of a liquid in a small-diameter tube:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

where $\theta$ = contact angle, $d$ = tube diameter. Water rises in glass ($\theta < 90°$, $\cos\theta > 0$); mercury depresses ($\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta < 0$).

### (I) Vapor Pressure (p_v)

The pressure exerted by a fluid's vapor when in equilibrium with its liquid at a given temperature. **Cavitation** occurs in pumps/turbines when local pressure falls below p_v.

## 2.2 Classification of Fluids — The Master Taxonomy

Fluids are classified based on the **relationship between shear stress and the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient)**.

### 2.2.1 Ideal Fluid vs Real Fluid

| Property | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity (μ) | **Zero** | **Non-zero** |
| Compressibility | **Zero** (incompressible) | Generally compressible |
| Surface Tension | **Zero** | **Non-zero** |
| Exists in nature? | **Imaginary** | **Yes** |
| Engineering use? | Simplifies analysis (Bernoulli's eq.) | Actual working fluids |

### 2.2.2 Classification Based on Shear–Strain-Rate Behavior (Rheological Classification)

This is the **most important classification for KTU Module 1** — frequently asked in Part A and Part B.

| Fluid Type | Shear Stress vs Strain Rate | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | Linear: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$, passes through origin | Water, air, thin oils, alcohol, gasoline |
| **Ideal (Inviscid)** | $\tau = 0$ for all strain rates | Hypothetical |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Linear **after** yield stress: $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p(du/dy)$ | Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge |
| **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** | Apparent viscosity **decreases** with strain rate; curve concave down | Blood, paint, ketchup, milk, paper pulp |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | Apparent viscosity **increases** with strain rate; curve concave up | Sugar–water suspensions, starch solutions, wet sand |
| **Rheopectic** | Viscosity **increases with time** under constant shear | Gypsum paste, printer's ink (some types) |
| **Thixotropic** | Viscosity **decreases with time** under constant shear | Yogurt, honey, certain paints, clays |

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity holds strictly only for Newtonian fluids.** For non-Newtonian fluids, the ratio $\tau / (du/dy)$ is the **apparent viscosity (μ_a)**, which is a function of the strain rate.

### 2.2.3 Compressible vs Incompressible Fluids

- **Incompressible:** Density remains essentially constant regardless of pressure. **All liquids** (water, oil, mercury) are treated as incompressible in most engineering problems.
- **Compressible:** Density varies significantly with pressure. **Gases** at high speeds (M > 0.3) are compressible; at low speeds, they may be treated as incompressible.

### 2.2.4 Other Sub-Categories
- **Single-phase fluid:** Only one phase (liquid or gas) — most common in KTU problems.
- **Multiphase fluid:** Mixture (e.g., water + steam in boilers, oil + gas in pipelines).
- **Real fluid classification by vapor pressure:** Sub-cooled liquid, saturated liquid, saturated mixture, saturated vapor, superheated vapor.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property / Concept | Formula | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass Density | $\rho = m / V$ | kg/m³ |
| 2 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ |
| 3 | Specific Gravity | $S = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ | Pa |
| 5 | Dynamic ↔ Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s |
| 6 | Bulk Modulus | $K = -V (dp/dV)$ | Pa |
| 7 | Surface Tension Force | $F = \sigma L$ | N |
| 8 | Capillary Rise | $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta / (\rho g d)$ | m |
| 9 | Continuity (1-D, steady) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | m³/s |
| 10 | Vapor Pressure (Cavitation index) | $\sigma_c = (p - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V^2)$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Pressure at Depth | $p = \rho g h$ | Pa |
| 12 | Absolute vs Gauge Pressure | $p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$ | Pa |

**Real-World Utility of Newton's Law of Viscosity:**
- Design of pipelines (head loss calculations — Darcy–Weisbach equation).
- Selection of lubricants in bearings.
- Determination of blood-flow resistance in arteries.
- Calibration of viscometers (capillary, rotational, falling-ball).
- Quality control in food, paint, polymer, and oil-refining industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from First Principles

### Statement of the Problem
Consider a fluid element trapped between two parallel plates separated by a small distance $dy$. The lower plate is **stationary**, the upper plate moves with a velocity $U$ in the $x$-direction. The fluid adheres to both plates (no-slip condition). A force $F$ must be applied to the upper plate to maintain its motion at constant velocity.

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between the applied shear stress and the resulting velocity gradient.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Experimental Setup Definition**

Let the contact area between the fluid and each plate be $A$. The **shear force** required to drag the upper plate is $F$. Define the **shear stress** as force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Observation of Linear Velocity Profile**

Experimental evidence (and the no-slip boundary condition) shows that for small gaps and steady flow, the velocity varies **linearly** from 0 at the lower plate to $U$ at the upper plate. Hence the velocity gradient is constant:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy}$$

Therefore the velocity at any height $y$ above the stationary plate is:

$$u(y) = \frac{U}{dy} \cdot y$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Observation**

Sir Isaac Newton postulated (and countless experiments confirmed) that the **shear stress** in a fluid is **directly proportional** to the **velocity gradient** for most common fluids (Newtonian fluids):

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Introduction of the Proportionality Constant**

Introduce the constant of proportionality $\mu$, the **dynamic (absolute) viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Vector Form and Dimensional Check**

In 3-D vector form (for completeness — not always required by KTU):

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left( \nabla \mathbf{V} + \nabla \mathbf{V}^T \right)$$

**Dimensional analysis of $\mu$:**

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[du/dy]} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{(m/s)/m} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{1/\text{s}} = \text{N·s/m}^2 = \text{Pa·s} \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation**

$\mu$ is a measure of the fluid's **internal resistance to flow**. A high $\mu$ (e.g., honey) flows sluggishly; a low $\mu$ (e.g., water) flows easily. This completes the derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** The relation $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ is also called the **constitutive equation** of a Newtonian fluid. It is **empirical**, not derived from first principles of physics. It is an observed law that holds for a specific class of fluids.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Step 1 — Start from the definitions:**

Dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ has units Pa·s = kg/(m·s).
Kinematic viscosity: $\nu$ has units m²/s.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $\nu / \mu$ and simplify:**

$$\frac{\nu}{\mu} = \frac{\text{m}^2/\text{s}}{\text{kg/(m·s)}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \cdot \frac{\text{m·s}}{\text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the inverse of density:**

By definition, mass density $\rho = m/V$ has units kg/m³. Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve:**

$$\frac{\nu}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\rho} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capillary Rise in a Small Tube

**Step 1 — Force balance on the column of liquid in the tube:**

Three vertical forces act on the raised liquid column of height $h$:
1. **Upward surface tension force** along the contact perimeter: $F_\sigma = \sigma \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \cos\theta$ (vertical component of the meniscus force).
2. **Downward weight of the column:** $W = (\pi d^2/4) \cdot h \cdot \rho g$.
3. The pressure at the top of the meniscus equals atmospheric; pressure at the bottom of the column (just below the flat free surface outside) also equals atmospheric. Thus **no net pressure force** acts on the column — they cancel.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition (steady state, no acceleration):**

$$F_\sigma = W$$

$$\sigma \cdot \pi d \cdot \cos\theta = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h \cdot \rho g$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h$:**

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

This is the classical **Jurin's Law** for capillary rise.

**Step 4 — Sign convention:**

If $\theta < 90°$ (e.g., water on clean glass), $\cos\theta > 0 \Rightarrow h > 0$: liquid **rises**.
If $\theta > 90°$ (e.g., mercury on glass), $\cos\theta < 0 \Rightarrow h < 0$: liquid **depresses** below the free surface.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Calculation

**Problem:** A liquid of density 850 kg/m³ flows between two parallel plates 5 mm apart. The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate moves at 1.5 m/s. If the dynamic viscosity is 0.105 Pa·s, find (a) the shear stress on the upper plate, (b) the shear stress at the mid-plane, and (c) the kinematic viscosity.

### Solution

**(a) Shear stress on the upper plate:**

The velocity profile is linear (Newtonian, fully developed):

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{1.5 \text{ m/s}}{0.005 \text{ m}} = 300 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.105 \times 300 = 31.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**(b) Shear stress at the mid-plane (y = 2.5 mm):**

The velocity gradient is constant throughout (linear profile):

$$\frac{du}{dy}\bigg|_{mid} = 300 \text{ s}^{-1} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau_{mid} = 0.105 \times 300 = 31.5 \text{ Pa}$$

The shear stress is uniform throughout the gap — this is a unique property of Newtonian fluids in plane Couette flow.

**(c) Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.105 \text{ Pa·s}}{850 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.235 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (a) τ = **31.5 Pa**, (b) τ_mid = **31.5 Pa**, (c) ν = **1.235 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Non-Newtonian Fluid Behaviors

| Fluid Type | $\tau$ vs $du/dy$ Curve | Apparent Viscosity Trend | KTU Favourite Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | Straight line through origin | **Constant** (μ fixed) | Water, air |
| Pseudoplastic | Concave-down, through origin | **Decreases** with $du/dy$ | Blood, paint |
| Dilatant | Concave-up, through origin | **Increases** with $du/dy$ | Starch slurry, wet sand |
| Bingham Plastic | Straight line, **yields** at $\tau_y$ | Constant (after yield) | Toothpaste, drilling mud |
| Thixotropic | Hysteresis — viscosity drops over time | Time-dependent decrease | Yogurt, printing ink |
| Rheopectic | Hysteresis — viscosity rises over time | Time-dependent increase | Gypsum paste, some lubricants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Fluids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID SUBSTANCE]:::root --> B[Liquid]
    A --> C[Gas]
    B --> D[Classification by Viscosity Behavior]:::mid
    C --> D
    D --> E[Newtonian Fluids]:::newt
    D --> F[Non-Newtonian Fluids]:::nonnewt
    E --> E1[Water]
    E --> E2[Air]
    E --> E3[Thin Oils]
    E --> E4[Glycerin]
    F --> F1[Pseudoplastic - Shear Thinning]:::nonnewt
    F --> F2[Dilatant - Shear Thickening]:::nonnewt
    F --> F3[Bingham Plastic - Yield Stress]:::nonnewt
    F --> F4[Thixotropic - Time Dependent Drop]:::nonnewt
    F --> F5[Rheopectic - Time Dependent Rise]:::nonnewt
    F1 --> F1a[Blood]
    F1 --> F1b[Paint]
    F1 --> F1c[Ketchup]
    F2 --> F2a[Starch Slurry]
    F2 --> F2b[Wet Sand]
    F2 --> F2c[Quicksand]
    F3 --> F3a[Toothpaste]
    F3 --> F3b[Drilling Mud]
    F3 --> F3c[Sewage Sludge]
    F4 --> F4a[Yogurt]
    F4 --> F4b[Honey]
    F5 --> F5a[Gypsum Paste]
    B --> G[Classification by Compressibility]:::mid
    C --> G
    G --> H[Incompressible - Liquids Generally]
    G --> I[Compressible - Gases at High Mach]
    A --> J[Continuum Assumption]:::mid
    J --> J1[Kn > 1000 Knudsen Check]
    J --> J2[Treat Fluid as Continuous Medium]
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#fde68a,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef newt fill:#bbf7d0,color:#064e3b,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef nonnewt fill:#fecaca,color:#7f1d1d,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Newton's Law of Viscosity — Plane Couette Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stationary Bottom Plate y equals zero]:::plate --> B[Fluid Layer Moving at Velocity u of y]:::layer
    B --> C[Top Plate Moves at Velocity U]:::plate
    C --> D[Linear Velocity Profile u of y equals U times y over h]:::profile
    B --> E[Shear Stress tau equals mu times du by dy]:::stress
    E --> F[Constant Velocity Gradient Equals U over h]:::gradient
    F --> G[mu of Liquid Determined Experimentally]:::mu
    classDef plate fill:#cbd5e1,color:#0f172a,stroke:#475569,stroke-width:1px
    classDef layer fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1px
    classDef profile fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef stress fill:#fce7f3,color:#831843,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef gradient fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mu fill:#e9d5ff,color:#581c87,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Property–Behavior Mapping of Common Fluids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FL[FLUID PROPERTY SPACE]:::root --> P1[Density rho]:::node
    FL --> P2[Viscosity mu]:::node
    FL --> P3[Compressibility K]:::node
    FL --> P4[Surface Tension sigma]:::node
    FL --> P5[Vapor Pressure pv]:::node
    P1 --> P1a[Mass per unit volume]:::sub
    P1 --> P1b[Specific Weight gamma equals rho g]:::sub
    P1 --> P1c[Specific Gravity S equals rho over rho water]:::sub
    P2 --> P2a[Dynamic mu in Pa s]:::sub
    P2 --> P2b[Kinematic nu equals mu over rho]:::sub
    P2 --> P2c[Newtonian or Non Newtonian]:::sub
    P3 --> P3a[Bulk Modulus K in Pa]:::sub
    P3 --> P3b[Incompressible if K Infinity]:::sub
    P4 --> P4a[Force per unit length N per m]:::sub
    P4 --> P4b[Capillary rise via Jurin]:::sub
    P5 --> P5a[Causes cavitation if exceeded]:::sub
    P5 --> P5b[Function of temperature]:::sub
    classDef root fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f766e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef node fill:#fde047,color:#713f12,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:1px

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following fluid properties, stating their SI units:**
(a) Specific weight, (b) Dynamic viscosity, (c) Kinematic viscosity.

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Specific Weight (γ):** The weight of a fluid per unit volume. $\gamma = \rho g$. **SI Unit: N/m³**.

(b) **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** The proportionality constant in Newton's law of viscosity, $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$, representing the fluid's internal resistance to flow. **SI Unit: Pa·s** (also N·s/m²).

(c) **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. **SI Unit: m²/s**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish clearly between a Newtonian fluid and a Non-Newtonian fluid, giving one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian |
|---|---|---|
| Relation between $\tau$ and $du/dy$ | **Linear**, passes through origin | **Non-linear** (or has yield stress) |
| Viscosity | **Constant** (independent of strain rate) | **Variable** (strain-rate or time dependent) |
| Examples | Water, air, thin oils, alcohol | Blood, paint, toothpaste, starch slurry |

A **Newtonian fluid** obeys $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ strictly. A **Non-Newtonian fluid** does not obey this linear law; its apparent viscosity changes with strain rate or time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 3-Mark Question:** Students often give vague answers like "Non-Newtonian fluids are thicker" (true sometimes, but not the definition). Always cite the **constitutive equation** difference and the **apparent viscosity** concept for full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A flat plate of area 0.8 m² is being pulled through a thin layer of oil (μ = 0.6 Pa·s, ρ = 900 kg/m³) at a velocity of 0.25 m/s. The oil film thickness is 1.2 mm. Assuming linear velocity distribution (Newtonian behavior), determine:
  (i) The shear stress developed in the oil.
  (ii) The total viscous drag force on the plate.
  (iii) The kinematic viscosity of the oil.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a clear sketch the **rheological classification of fluids**, distinguishing at least **five** categories based on the shear stress vs. shear strain-rate relationship. Give one industrial example for each category.

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** A = 0.8 m², μ = 0.6 Pa·s, ρ = 900 kg/m³, U = 0.25 m/s, h = 1.2 mm = 0.0012 m.

**(i) Shear stress:**

The velocity gradient for a linear profile is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{0.25}{0.0012} = 208.33 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Applying Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.6 \times 208.33 = 125.0 \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) Viscous drag force:**

$$F = \tau \times A = 125.0 \times 0.8 = 100.0 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.6}{900} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (i) τ = **125 Pa**, (ii) F = **100 N**, (iii) ν = **6.667 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s**.

#### Part (b) — Theoretical Solution

The five categories of fluids based on the $\tau$ vs $du/dy$ relationship are:

1. **Newtonian Fluid** — Linear relationship $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$ through the origin. *Example: water, air, gasoline.*
2. **Ideal Fluid** — Shear stress is zero for all strain rates. *Example: hypothetical — used in theoretical Bernoulli analysis.*
3. **Bingham Plastic Fluid** — Has a yield stress $\tau_y$ before flow begins, then behaves linearly. *Example: toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge.*
4. **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) Fluid** — Apparent viscosity decreases as strain rate increases; curve concave down. *Example: blood, paint, ketchup, polymer melts.*
5. **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) Fluid** — Apparent viscosity increases as strain rate increases; curve concave up. *Example: starch slurry, wet sand, quicksand.*

(Students can also mention Thixotropic and Rheopectic for full enrichment marks.)

The standard plot of $\tau$ (y-axis) vs. $du/dy$ (x-axis) shows each category as a distinct curve:
- Newtonian: straight line through origin.
- Bingham: straight line starting at $\tau_y$ on the y-axis.
- Pseudoplastic: concave-down curve through origin.
- Dilatant: concave-up curve through origin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 7-Mark Part (b):** Most students draw the **τ vs μ plot** (which is a single straight line — useless). The KTU-correct plot is **τ vs du/dy (shear strain rate)**, where each fluid type has a distinct curve. Drawing the wrong axes costs 2 marks.
