Also: each example must **match** its category — blood is pseudoplastic, not Newtonian; this is where careless answers lose marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A capillary tube of diameter 2.5 mm is inserted into a liquid of surface tension 0.073 N/m, density 1000 kg/m³, and contact angle 30°. Calculate the capillary rise. If the same tube is inserted into mercury (σ = 0.51 N/m, ρ = 13600 kg/m³, θ = 140°), find the capillary depression. Comment on the sign of your answers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **bulk modulus of elasticity** of a fluid. A fluid has a volume of 0.05 m³ at 1 MPa pressure. When the pressure is increased to 4 MPa, the volume decreases by 0.0001 m³. Calculate: (i) The bulk modulus, (ii) The compressibility, (iii) Identify whether the fluid behaves more like a liquid or a gas based on the result.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Capillary Rise and Depression

**Given (water-like liquid):** d = 2.5 mm = 0.0025 m, σ = 0.073 N/m, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, θ = 30°.

Using Jurin's law:

$$h = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d} = \frac{4 \times 0.073 \times \cos 30°}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0025}$$

$$\cos 30° = 0.8660$$

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.073 \times 0.8660}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0025} = \frac{0.2529}{24.525} = 1.031 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 10.31 \text{ mm}$$

**Given (mercury):** σ = 0.51 N/m, ρ = 13600 kg/m³, θ = 140°.

$$\cos 140° = -0.7660$$

$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.51 \times (-0.7660)}{13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.0025} = \frac{-1.5626}{333.51} = -4.685 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = -4.69 \text{ mm}$$

**Sign Commentary:** Positive $h$ ⇒ **capillary rise** (water-like, $\theta < 90°$, $\cos\theta > 0$). Negative $h$ ⇒ **capillary depression** (mercury-like, $\theta > 90°$, $\cos\theta < 0$). Mercury does not "wet" glass — its cohesive forces are stronger than the adhesive forces with the glass wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** Water-like liquid: $h = +10.31$ mm (rises). Mercury: $h = -4.69$ mm (depresses).

#### Part (b) — Bulk Modulus Calculation

**Given:** V₁ = 0.05 m³ at p₁ = 1 MPa = 1 × 10⁶ Pa, V₂ = 0.05 − 0.0001 = 0.0499 m³ at p₂ = 4 MPa = 4 × 10⁶ Pa.

**Definition (worth 2 marks):** The bulk modulus of elasticity $K$ of a fluid is the ratio of change in pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain (relative change in volume). It measures the fluid's resistance to uniform compression:

$$K = -\frac{dp}{dV/V} = -V \frac{dp}{dV}$$

**Units: Pa** (or N/m²).

**(i) Bulk Modulus:**

$$K = -V_1 \cdot \frac{p_2 - p_1}{V_2 - V_1} = -0.05 \times \frac{(4 - 1) \times 10^6}{(-0.0001)}$$

$$K = -0.05 \times \frac{3 \times 10^6}{-10^{-4}} = -0.05 \times (-3 \times 10^{10}) = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 1.5 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Compressibility (β):**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^9} = 6.667 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Identification:**

Water at standard conditions has $K \approx 2.2$ GPa. Air at atmospheric conditions has $K \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa (about **10,000 times smaller**). Our calculated value of **1.5 GPa** is close to that of water and **vastly larger** than that of air. Therefore the fluid behaves like a **liquid** (very nearly incompressible, with very low compressibility).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Bulk Modulus:** Students often **forget the negative sign** in the definition and get $K = -1.5$ GPa (physically meaningless). The minus sign makes $K$ positive because $dV$ is negative when $dp$ is positive (compression reduces volume). Also: do not confuse **compressibility (β)** with **bulk modulus (K)** — they are reciprocals.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Fluid Mechanics & Types of Fluids**

- **Fluid definition:** A substance that **continuously deforms** under applied shear stress, no matter how small.
- **Continuum hypothesis:** Valid when the characteristic length $\gg$ molecular mean free path (Knudsen number $Kn \ll 1$).
- **Mass density** $\rho = m/V$ in **kg/m³**.
- **Specific weight** $\gamma = \rho g$ in **N/m³**.
- **Specific gravity** $S = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$ is **dimensionless**.
- **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — holds **only for Newtonian fluids**.
- **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ in **Pa·s**; **kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu/\rho$ in **m²/s**.
- Viscosity of **liquids decreases** with temperature; viscosity of **gases increases** with temperature.
- **Ideal fluid:** $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 0$, incompressible — a theoretical construct.
- **Real fluid:** All fluids in nature (viscous, compressible, finite surface tension).
- **Newtonian fluids:** water, air, alcohol, gasoline, thin oils.
- **Non-Newtonian categories:** Pseudoplastic (paint, blood), Dilatant (starch, sand slurry), Bingham Plastic (toothpaste, drilling mud), Thixotropic (yogurt, ink), Rheopectic (gypsum paste).
- **Bulk modulus** $K = -V(dp/dV)$ in **Pa**; **compressibility** $\beta = 1/K$ in **Pa⁻¹**.
- Liquids: high $K$ (GPa range) → nearly incompressible. Gases: low $K$ (kPa range) → highly compressible.
- **Surface tension** $\sigma$ in **N/m**; capillary rise $h = 4\sigma\cos\theta / (\rho g d)$ (Jurin's Law).
- **Capillary rise** when $\theta < 90°$ (wetting fluids); **depression** when $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting, e.g., mercury).
- **Absolute pressure** = **gauge pressure** + **atmospheric pressure**; vacuum pressure = **atmospheric** − **absolute**.
- **Cavitation** occurs in pumps/turbines when local pressure falls below **vapor pressure**.
- Standard values to memorize: $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\gamma_{water} = 9810$ N/m³, $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- A **no-slip condition** means fluid in contact with a solid boundary has the same velocity as that boundary.
- For the KTU exam: always **state units** with numerical answers, **draw labelled diagrams** for theoretical questions, and **mention the continuum assumption** whenever deriving from a control volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Questions on Types of Fluids in KTU 2024 are usually worth 7–14 marks, and the most common sub-questions are (1) classify a given fluid, (2) derive or apply Newton's law of viscosity, (3) compute capillary rise/depression, and (4) calculate bulk modulus or compressibility. Memorizing the formula sheet in Section 2.3 is sufficient to score full marks on the numerical portions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's Law of Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition):**
> The shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a real flowing fluid is directly proportional to the **rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient) at that point, provided the fluid is Newtonian, the flow is laminar, and thermodynamic conditions (especially temperature) remain constant.
> 
> Mathematically expressed as:
> $$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

This proportionality is what distinguishes a **Newtonian fluid** (e.g., water, air, thin oils) from a **Non-Newtonian fluid** (e.g., blood, toothpaste, paint, ketchup). The constant of proportionality is called the **Coefficient of Dynamic Viscosity** (or simply **Viscosity**), denoted as $\mu$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Honey-Spoon Test 🍯**
> 
> Imagine you spread a thin layer of **honey** on a slice of bread. If you try to slide the top layer horizontally with a spoon, the spoon barely moves. The honey resists the motion. That internal "stickiness" or resistance to flow is **viscosity**.
> 
> Now imagine doing the same with **water** on a plate. The water slides easily. Water has very low viscosity.
> 
> **Why does this happen?** A flowing fluid can be thought of as multiple infinitesimally thin layers sliding over each other. The layer in direct contact with the fixed boundary (e.g., the bread surface) is stationary due to the **no-slip condition**. The next layer slides a little, the next one a little more, until the topmost layer moves fastest. This relative sliding between adjacent layers generates internal friction — and that internal friction is what Newton's Law quantifies.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **standard value of dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C** is approximately $\mu_{water} \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ **N·s/m²** (or **Pa·s**).
- The **kinematic viscosity** of water at 20 °C is $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ **m²/s**.
- The **dynamic viscosity of air** at standard atmospheric conditions is $\mu_{air} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ **N·s/m²**.
- The **kinematic viscosity of mercury** at 20 °C is approximately $\nu \approx 1.157 \times 10^{-7}$ **m²/s**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Mnemonic:** Viscosity of liquids **decreases** with temperature (honey flows faster when heated), but viscosity of gases **increases** with temperature. This is a favorite 2-mark question in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Breaking Down the Concept

The complete logical flow of Newton's Law of Viscosity can be understood through the following structured points:

- **Step 1 — Fluid as a Continuum:**
  Real fluids are made of molecules. However, for engineering analysis, we treat the fluid as a **continuum**, meaning physical properties like velocity, density, and viscosity are defined at every mathematical point in the flow domain.

- **Step 2 — Velocity Profile Assumption:**
  Consider a fluid flowing over a stationary flat plate. Due to the no-slip condition, the fluid layer touching the plate has zero velocity. As we move perpendicular to the plate (in the $y$-direction), velocity increases. This creates a **velocity gradient**, $du/dy$.

- **Step 3 — Newton's Postulate:**
  Sir Isaac Newton hypothesized that the shear stress between adjacent fluid layers is proportional to this velocity gradient. The proportionality constant is the **dynamic viscosity**, $\mu$.
  $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

- **Step 4 — Why the "du/dy" and not just "du":**
  The shear strain rate matters, not the velocity itself. If two layers move at the same speed, there is no relative motion, and thus no shear stress, regardless of how fast they move. Only the *rate of change* of velocity across the layer thickness creates shear.

- **Step 5 — Newton's Fluid vs. Non-Newtonian Fluid:**
  A **Newtonian fluid** obeys the linear relationship $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ where $\mu$ is constant. If the relationship is non-linear or $\mu$ depends on the shear rate itself, the fluid is **non-Newtonian** (pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, thixotropic, rheopectic).

- **Step 6 — Local vs. Average Viscosity:**
  Viscosity is a **point property**. Its value can vary from point to point in a flow field (especially in non-uniform temperature fields), even for a Newtonian fluid.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas for Module 1 related to Newton's Law of Viscosity. All symbols, units, and dimensions are SI-standard.

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | Description | Standard SI Unit | Dimensional Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear stress in a flowing Newtonian fluid | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) | $M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-2}$ |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity (definition) | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | Ratio of shear stress to shear strain rate | $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or **Pa·s** | $M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $L^2\,T^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Stokes' Relation (theoretical) | $\mu \propto \rho\, v\, \ell$ | From kinetic theory ($\ell$ = mean free path) | — | — |
| 5 | Newton's Postulate (force form) | $F = \mu\, A\, \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Tangential force between two fluid layers | N | $M\,L\,T^{-2}$ |
| 6 | Shear strain rate | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Rate of change of shear strain | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | $T^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\, V\, D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V\, D}{\nu}$ | Inertial forces vs. viscous forces | Dimensionless | Dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Machine Design:** Viscosity determines the **power loss** in pumps, turbines, and pipelines. Engineers must size bearings, select lubricants (oils of specific $\mu$), and compute the **head loss** in pipe flow (Darcy-Weisbach equation) using viscosity.
- **Lubrication Engineering:** The **film theory of lubrication** is built entirely on Newton's law. The shear stress in the thin oil film between journal and bearing determines friction torque and heat generation.
- **Aerospace & Meteorology:** Viscosity of air governs the **boundary layer** thickness on aircraft wings, which in turn affects lift, drag, and stall behavior.
- **Food and Pharma Industry:** Pumping of viscous fluids (syrups, blood, oils) requires selection of pumps based on viscosity values.
- **Civil Engineering:** Sediment transport in rivers, flood modeling, and groundwater flow all depend on the viscosity of water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (No-Slip Condition):**
> At a solid boundary, the fluid velocity equals the boundary velocity.
> If the boundary is stationary: $u(y=0) = 0$.
> If the boundary moves with velocity $U$: $u(y=0) = U$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shear Force Between Two Parallel Fluid Layers

Let us consider a fluid element between two parallel layers separated by a small vertical distance $dy$.

**Step 1 — Setup the Geometry:**
Assume the lower layer (Layer 1) is at a fixed boundary and the upper layer (Layer 2) is at a small height $dy$ above it. The velocity of the lower layer is $u$. The velocity of the upper layer is $u + du$.

**Step 2 — Define the Velocity Gradient:**
The rate of change of velocity with respect to the perpendicular distance $y$ is given by:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Hypothesis:**
According to Newton's Law of Viscosity, the shear stress $\tau$ is directly proportional to this velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Constant of Proportionality:**
Let the constant of proportionality be $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity). Then:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Convert Stress to Force:**
Shear stress is force per unit area. For a fluid element with contact area $A$ between the two layers, the tangential (shear) force $F$ required to maintain the relative motion is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form:**

$$F = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

> This equation is the **force form of Newton's Law of Viscosity** and is the basis of all viscous-flow analyses.

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Step 1 — Definition of Dynamic Viscosity:**
From Newton's Law:
$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Density:**
Density is mass per unit volume:
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \rho V$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Kinematic Viscosity:**
Kinematic viscosity is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to mass density of the fluid:
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Dimensional Check:**

Let us verify the dimensions of kinematic viscosity $\nu$.

$$\text{SI unit of } \mu = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} \cdot \text{s} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$

$$\text{SI unit of } \rho = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Unit of } \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\text{kg}/(\text{m}\cdot\text{s})}{\text{kg}/\text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional Formula Check:**

Using dimensional analysis with $[M] = M$, $[L] = L$, $[T] = T$:

$$[\mu] = M\, L^{-1}\, T^{-1}$$

$$[\rho] = M\, L^{-3}$$

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-1}}{M\,L^{-3}} = L^2\,T^{-1}$$

This confirms that $\nu$ has the dimensions of **length²/time**, which represents the diffusion of momentum — exactly the same dimensions as thermal diffusivity and mass diffusivity.

### 3.3 Derivation: Dimensions of Dynamic Viscosity Using $[\tau] / [du/dy]$

We can independently derive the dimensions of $\mu$ from the definition $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$.

$$\text{Dimensions of shear stress: } [\tau] = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{M\,L\,T^{-2}}{L^2} = M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-2}$$

$$\text{Dimensions of velocity gradient: } \left[\frac{du}{dy}\right] = \frac{L\,T^{-1}}{L} = T^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[du/dy]} = \frac{M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-2}}{T^{-1}} = M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-1}$$

**Conclusion:** The dimensional formula of dynamic viscosity is $M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-1}$, and its SI unit is **Pa·s** (Pascal-second) or equivalently **N·s/m²** or **kg/(m·s)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **CGS system**, the unit of dynamic viscosity is the **Poise (P)**, named after Jean Léonard Marie Poiseuille.
> 1 Pa·s = 10 Poise = 1000 centiPoise (cP).
> For example, water at 20 °C has $\mu \approx 1.002$ cP, which is why water viscosity is so easy to remember in cP units.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Newton's Fluid vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part A questions.

| Property / Behavior | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ relationship | Linear, passes through origin | Non-linear or does not pass through origin |
| Viscosity $\mu$ | Constant at given T, P | Varies with shear rate |
| Examples | Water, air, alcohol, gasoline, thin oils | Blood, paint, toothpaste, ketchup, slurry |
| Bingham Plastic case | — | Behaves as solid until yield stress $\tau_0$ is exceeded, then flows with linear $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ |
| Pseudoplastic | — | Viscosity decreases with shear rate (shear-thinning), e.g., blood, polymer solutions |
| Dilatant | — | Viscosity increases with shear rate (shear-thickening), e.g., cornstarch in water |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Velocity Profile Between Two Parallel Plates

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the velocity profile of a Newtonian fluid confined between a fixed lower plate and a moving upper plate. The linear variation of velocity with height ($y$) is the hallmark of **Couette flow** — the simplest physical scenario in which Newton's Law of Viscosity is directly applied.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fixed Lower Plate at y equals 0] --> B[No-Slip Condition: u equals 0 at boundary]
    B --> C[Fluid Layer 1: y equals dy by 4]
    C --> D[Fluid Layer 2: y equals dy by 2]
    D --> E[Fluid Layer 3: y equals 3dy by 4]
    E --> F[Moving Upper Plate at y equals dy with velocity U]
    F --> G[Velocity Gradient du by dy equals U by dy]
    G --> H[Shear Stress tau equals mu times du by dy]
    H --> I[Force F equals mu times A times U by dy]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Newton's Law

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Input: Fluid Type Identification] --> P2{Fluid is Newtonian?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Apply Linear Law: tau equals mu du by dy]
    P2 -- No --> P4[Apply Non-Newtonian Model: Power Law, Bingham, etc.]
    P3 --> P5[Input: Velocity Profile u of y from Governing Equation]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Compute du by dy at required point]
    P6 --> P7[Compute tau equals mu times du by dy]
    P7 --> P8[Output: Shear Stress Distribution and Viscous Force]
    P8 --> P9[Engineering Application: Head Loss, Bearing Design, Pump Power]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Relative Sliding of Fluid Layers (ASCII-Equivalent Block Diagram)

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ← Upper Plate (Velocity = U)
        │  Layer N:   Velocity = U           │     ↑
        │  Layer N-1: Velocity = U - ΔU     │     │ dy
        │  Layer N-2: Velocity = U - 2ΔU     │     │ (total gap)
        │  ...                               │     │
        │  Layer 1:   Velocity = ΔU          │     ↓
        └─────────────────────────────┘  ← Lower Plate (Velocity = 0)
                  ↓ Shear Force F = μ·A·(U/dy)
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The arrows on the right represent the **velocity gradient** $du/dy$.
- The shear force $F$ at the bottom is the **resistive force** the fluid exerts on the lower plate.
- By Newton's third law, an equal and opposite force is needed to drag the upper plate at velocity $U$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Visual Mnemonic:** The faster the top layer moves (higher $U$) and the thinner the gap ($dy$ smaller), the **larger** the velocity gradient and hence the **larger** the shear stress. This is why very thin oil films in machinery create enormous shear stress.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Newton's Law of Viscosity. Mention its limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):**
The shear stress developed in a flowing fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient), provided the fluid is Newtonian and the flow is laminar.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Definition of $\mu$ (1 Mark):**
The constant of proportionality $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, defined as the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient in the fluid.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
1. It applies only to **Newtonian fluids** and not to non-Newtonian fluids like blood, paint, etc.
2. It is valid for **laminar flow** conditions; in turbulent flow, additional stresses (Reynolds stresses) appear.
3. The viscosity $\mu$ is assumed constant, but in reality, it varies with **temperature** (significantly for liquids and gases).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$ (1 Mark):**
It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to deformation (or flow) under an applied shear stress. Its SI unit is **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

**Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$ (1 Mark):**
It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid. It represents the **diffusivity of momentum**. Its SI unit is **m²/s**.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Relationship (1 Mark):**
The two are related through density: $\mu = \rho \nu$ or equivalently $\nu = \mu / \rho$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Penalty (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often confuse **Pa·s** (unit of $\mu$) with **Pa** (unit of $\tau$) or with **m²/s** (unit of $\nu$). Always state units explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Proportionality Constant (–1 mark):** When stating Newton's Law, many students write $\tau = du/dy$ instead of $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. The constant $\mu$ **must** be included.
> 
> 3. **No-Slip Condition (–1 mark):** In derivation-based questions on Couette flow, students often forget to apply the no-slip boundary condition. Always write $u(0) = 0$ and $u(dy) = U$ explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Temperature Variation of Viscosity (–1 mark):** For liquids, $\mu$ decreases with T; for gases, $\mu$ increases with T. Stating the wrong trend costs a mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme – ESE Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive Newton's Law of Viscosity from first principles, clearly defining all terms and stating its limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plate of area $1.5 \, \text{m}^2$ is separated from a fixed plate by a film of oil of thickness $1.2 \, \text{mm}$. The oil has a dynamic viscosity of $0.85 \, \text{Pa·s}$. Determine the force required to drag the upper plate at a velocity of $0.30 \, \text{m/s}$. Also, calculate the kinematic viscosity if the density of the oil is $920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept of velocity gradient (1 Mark):**
Consider two adjacent fluid layers separated by a perpendicular distance $dy$. The lower layer moves with velocity $u$, and the upper layer moves with velocity $u + du$.

**Step 2 — Definition of velocity gradient (1 Mark):**
The rate of change of velocity with respect to the perpendicular distance is:
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}$$

**Step 3 — Newton's hypothesis (1 Mark):**
Newton postulated that the shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient:
$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Introduction of viscosity constant (1 Mark):**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Force form (1 Mark):**
Multiplying by contact area $A$ gives the viscous force:
$$F = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 6 — Limitations (2 Marks):**
- Applies only to Newtonian fluids and laminar flow.
- Assumes constant $\mu$ (true only at constant T and P).
- Does not account for the no-slip condition being violated in rare cases (e.g., rarefied gas flows).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Area: $A = 1.5 \, \text{m}^2$
- Film thickness: $dy = 1.2 \, \text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.85 \, \text{Pa·s}$
- Plate velocity: $U = 0.30 \, \text{m/s}$
- Density: $\rho = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Velocity gradient (1 Mark):**
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy} = \frac{0.30}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 250 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress (1 Mark):**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.85 \times 250 = 212.5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Viscous force (1 Mark):**
$$F = \tau \cdot A = 212.5 \times 1.5 = 318.75 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic viscosity (2 Marks):**
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.85}{920} = 9.239 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer with units (2 Marks):**
$$\boxed{F = 318.75 \, \text{N}, \quad \nu = 9.239 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with a labeled stress vs. strain-rate diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of diameter $150 \, \text{mm}$ rotates at $1800 \, \text{rpm}$ inside a journal bearing. The radial clearance (oil film thickness) is $0.20 \, \text{mm}$, and the length of the bearing is $200 \, \text{mm}$. The oil used has a dynamic viscosity of $0.045 \, \text{Pa·s}$. Calculate: **(i)** the velocity gradient, **(ii)** the shear stress, and **(iii)** the torque required to overcome the viscous resistance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Differentiation (7 Marks):**

**Newtonian Fluids (3 Marks):**
- The relationship between shear stress and strain rate is **linear** and passes through the origin.
- Viscosity $\mu$ is **constant** (does not depend on shear rate).
- Examples: water, air, alcohol, kerosene, mercury.

**Non-Newtonian Fluids (3 Marks):**
- The relationship is **non-linear** or has a yield stress.
- Viscosity is **apparent** and depends on shear rate.
- Types: pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, thixotropic, rheopectic.
- Examples: blood, paint, toothpaste, slurry, mud.

**Sketch (1 Mark):**
- Linear straight line through origin for Newtonian.
- Curved (concave) for pseudoplastic, curved (convex) for dilatant, straight line with yield offset for Bingham plastic.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Shaft diameter: $D = 150 \, \text{mm}$, radius $R = 75 \, \text{mm} = 0.075 \, \text{m}$
- Rotational speed: $N = 1800 \, \text{rpm}$
- Oil film thickness (clearance): $dy = 0.20 \, \text{mm} = 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- Bearing length: $L = 200 \, \text{mm} = 0.20 \, \text{m}$
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.045 \, \text{Pa·s}$

**Step 1 — Angular velocity (1 Mark):**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 60\pi \approx 188.496 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Surface velocity of shaft (1 Mark):**
$$U = \omega R = 188.496 \times 0.075 = 14.137 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity gradient (1 Mark):**
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy} = \frac{14.137}{0.20 \times 10^{-3}} = 70{,}685 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Shear stress (1 Mark):**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.045 \times 70{,}685 = 3180.83 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Contact area of oil film (1 Mark):**
$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.150 \times 0.20 = 0.09425 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Viscous force and torque (2 Marks):**
$$F = \tau \cdot A = 3180.83 \times 0.09425 = 299.79 \, \text{N}$$

$$T = F \cdot R = 299.79 \times 0.075 = 22.48 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\frac{du}{dy} = 70{,}685 \, \text{s}^{-1}, \quad \tau = 3180.83 \, \text{Pa}, \quad T = 22.48 \, \text{N·m}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — the cornerstone equation of viscous flow.
- **Dynamic viscosity $\mu$:** Resistance to shearing; SI unit = **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**; CGS unit = **Poise** (1 Pa·s = 10 P).
- **Kinematic viscosity $\nu$:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$; SI unit = **m²/s**; represents **diffusivity of momentum**.
- **Dimensional formula of $\mu$:** $M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-1}$. **Dimensional formula of $\nu$:** $L^2\,T^{-1}$.
- **Newtonian fluids** (water, air, oils) have a constant $\mu$. **Non-Newtonian fluids** (blood, paint, toothpaste) do not.
- **No-slip condition:** Fluid in contact with a solid surface has the same velocity as that surface — always apply this in boundary conditions.
- **Temperature effect on viscosity:**
  - **Liquids:** $\mu$ **decreases** as T increases (molecules move apart, friction reduces).
  - **Gases:** $\mu$ **increases** as T increases (momentum exchange increases with molecular speed).
- **Velocity gradient** is the heart of the law — it is $du/dy$, not just $u$ or $du$ alone.
- **Reynolds Number $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$** — links viscosity to flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent).
- **Engineering applications:** Bearing design, lubricant selection, pipe flow losses, pump/turbine power, boundary layer analysis, lubrication theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Measurement: Fluid Pressure, Pressure Head, Types of Pressures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Measurement: Fluid Pressure, Pressure Head, Types of Pressures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fluid Pressure — The Fundamental Concept

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Fluid pressure (also called *hydrostatic pressure* when the fluid is at rest) is defined as the **normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area** on a surface in contact with it. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

where:
- $P$ = Fluid pressure $[\text{N/m}^2]$ or $[\text{Pa}]$
- $F$ = Normal force exerted by the fluid $[\text{N}]$
- $A$ = Area of contact $[\text{m}^2]$

The **SI unit** of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)** where $1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^2$. Because a Pascal is extremely small, the **kilopascal (kPa)** and **megapascal (MPa)** are widely used in hydraulic engineering. In older texts, **bar**, **atm**, and **psi** are also seen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property — Pascal's Principle:** Fluid pressure at a point acts **equally in all directions** and always **perpendicular to any surface** with which it is in contact. This is independent of the orientation of the surface, as long as the fluid is static (at rest).

## 1.2 Pressure Head — Translating Pressure into a Column of Fluid

**Pressure Head** is a convenient engineering concept that converts a pressure value into an equivalent **height (head) of a fluid column**. For a liquid of specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$, the pressure head at a point is:

$$h = \frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$

The head is measured in **metres (m)** of the fluid. For example, a pressure of $9810 \text{ N/m}^2$ of water equals a pressure head of **1 m of water**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use pressure head?** In fluid statics and hydraulics, head is a more intuitive representation of pressure energy. It allows direct comparison of energy levels between two points without worrying about the fluid's density. This concept is foundational for the **Bernoulli's equation** and **hydraulic machine** analysis in later modules.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are underwater in a swimming pool. The deeper you dive, the more your ears hurt. This is because the **weight of the water column above you** is pressing down on every square metre of your body. The pressure you feel increases with depth — this is fluid pressure. If you stand on one foot versus lying flat, the *force distribution* changes, but the *pressure at a given depth* does not. That depth, converted to a height of water, is the **pressure head**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on the relationship between absolute, gauge, and vacuum pressures. Mastering this triad is essential for solving manometer and barometer problems that appear frequently in Part B questions.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants for Pressure

| Constant | Value (SI) | Value (Common Units) |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $101325 \text{ Pa}$ | $1 \text{ atm} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$ |
| Density of water at $4^\circ\text{C}$ | $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | $1.94 \text{ slug/ft}^3$ |
| Specific weight of water | $9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ | $62.4 \text{ lb/ft}^3$ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ | $32.2 \text{ ft/s}^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — Logical Steps

The fundamental equation that governs fluid pressure variation with depth is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Consider a fluid element at rest.**
Take an infinitesimal fluid parcel of cross-sectional area $dA$ and vertical height $dz$, with the top face at depth $h_1$ and bottom face at depth $h_2$.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's equilibrium condition.**
For the fluid to remain static, the sum of vertical forces must be zero. The forces acting on the parcel are:
- Pressure on top face: $P_1 \cdot dA$ (acting downward)
- Pressure on bottom face: $P_2 \cdot dA$ (acting upward)
- Weight of the element: $\rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz$ (acting downward)

**Step 3 — Write the equilibrium equation.**

$$P_2 \cdot dA - P_1 \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and rearrange.**

$$dP = -\rho g \, dz$$

**Step 5 — Integrate for an incompressible fluid** ($\rho$ = constant):

$$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) = \rho g (h_1 - h_2)$$

If point 1 is the free surface where $P_1 = P_{\text{atm}}$ and depth is measured downward as $h$:

$$\boxed{P = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h}$$

## 2.2 The Three Types of Pressures — Complete Analysis

### 2.2.1 Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{\text{atm}}$)
The pressure exerted by the **weight of the Earth's atmosphere** on all surfaces. It is measured using a **barometer** and varies with altitude and weather. At sea level, it is approximately **$101.325 \text{ kPa}$**.

### 2.2.2 Absolute Pressure ($P_{\text{abs}}$)
Pressure measured with respect to a **perfect vacuum** (true zero pressure). It is the **total pressure** at a point and is always positive.

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$$

### 2.2.3 Gauge Pressure ($P_{\text{gauge}}$)
Pressure measured **relative to local atmospheric pressure**. Most pressure gauges (Bourdon, diaphragm) read gauge pressure by default.

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$$

### 2.2.4 Vacuum Pressure ($P_{\text{vac}}$)
When absolute pressure is **less than atmospheric**, the difference is called vacuum (or negative gauge) pressure. It represents a *suction* condition.

$$P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "AGA" Rule:** **A**bsolute = **G**auge + **A**tmospheric. If gauge is negative (vacuum), subtract it: $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|------|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Fundamental definition of pressure | Pa |
| 2 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure due to depth | Pa |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure head (height of fluid column) | m |
| 4 | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{gauge}}$ | Absolute–Gauge relationship | Pa |
| 5 | $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$ | Vacuum pressure definition | Pa |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ | Hydrostatic pressure gradient (general) | Pa/m |
| 7 | $P_A + \rho_1 g h_1 = P_B + \rho_2 g h_2$ | Manometer equation (incompressible) | Pa |
| 8 | $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Differential manometer equation | Pa |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Hydraulic Presses & Lifts:** Use Pascal's principle to multiply force using fluid pressure.
- **Aircraft Altimeters:** Use atmospheric pressure variation to determine altitude.
- **Blood Pressure Measurement:** Sphygmomanometers measure gauge pressure of blood in mmHg.
- **Submarines & Diving:** Hulls are designed to withstand hydrostatic pressure that increases with depth.
- **Boiler Safety:** Pressure gauges (always gauge type) monitor steam pressure above atmospheric.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Variation with Depth

### Setup
Consider a static fluid of density $\rho$ contained in a vessel open to atmosphere. Let point 1 be at the free surface (depth $= 0$) and point 2 be at depth $h$ below the surface.

### Derivation

Starting from the equilibrium of an elementary fluid column of base area $dA$ and height $dh$:

**Step (i) — Identify all vertical forces:**

$$F_{\text{down}} = P_1 \cdot dA + \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh$$
$$F_{\text{up}} = P_2 \cdot dA$$

**Step (ii) — Apply static equilibrium** ($\sum F_z = 0$):

$$P_2 \cdot dA - P_1 \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh = 0$$

**Step (iii) — Divide by $dA$:**

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho g \, dh$$

**Step (iv) — Set $P_1 = P_{\text{atm}}$ at the free surface and $P_2 = P$ at depth $h$:**

$$P - P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h$$

**Step (v) — Rearrange for absolute pressure:**

$$\boxed{P = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h}$$

**Step (vi) — Rearrange for gauge pressure:**

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = P - P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above derivation confirms that **pressure increases linearly with depth** in an incompressible static fluid. The pressure is independent of the shape of the container (this property is known as the **hydrostatic paradox**).

## 3.2 Derivation: Simple U-Tube Manometer Equation

### Setup
A U-tube manometer is connected between two points A and B in a pipeline. The manometric fluid (typically mercury, density $\rho_m$) is heavier than the flowing fluid (density $\rho_f$). The manometer reading (difference in levels of the manometric fluid) is $h$.

### Derivation

**Step (i) — Start at point A and trace the pressure changes to point B:**

At point A: $P_A$

Moving down through the flowing fluid by a height $h_1$: pressure increases by $\rho_f g h_1$.

**Step (ii) — Cross the manometric fluid interface (going up):**

At the level of the lower mercury meniscus: $P_A + \rho_f g h_1$

Moving up through mercury by height $h$: pressure decreases by $\rho_m g h$.

At the upper mercury meniscus: $P_A + \rho_f g h_1 - \rho_m g h$

**Step (iii) — Continue tracing up through the flowing fluid to point B:**

Moving up through the flowing fluid by height $h_2$: pressure decreases by $\rho_f g h_2$.

At point B: $P_A + \rho_f g h_1 - \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_2 = P_B$

**Step (iv) — Simplify using geometry** ($h_1 - h_2 = z$ where $z$ is the elevation difference):

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g (h_1 - h_2)$$

**Step (v) — If A and B are at the same elevation** ($h_1 = h_2$):

$$\boxed{\Delta P = P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}$$

This is the **differential manometer equation** used in virtually all KTU numerical problems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pressure Types at a Glance

| Condition | $P_{\text{abs}}$ vs $P_{\text{atm}}$ | $P_{\text{gauge}}$ | $P_{\text{vac}}$ | Example |
|-----------|----------------------------------|------------------|------------------|---------|
| Pressure above atmospheric | $P_{\text{abs}} > P_{\text{atm}}$ | Positive | Zero (or undefined) | Tyre pressure, water tap |
| Atmospheric equilibrium | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}}$ | Zero | Zero | Open vessel at sea level |
| Pressure below atmospheric (partial vacuum) | $0 < P_{\text{abs}} < P_{\text{atm}}$ | Negative | Positive | Condenser of steam plant, suction of a pump |
| Perfect vacuum (theoretical) | $P_{\text{abs}} = 0$ | $-P_{\text{atm}}$ | $P_{\text{atm}}$ | Outer space, top of atmosphere |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board problems, **never** confuse gauge and absolute pressures. The student must explicitly state which one the answer refers to. A typical error is to write $P = \rho g h$ without specifying that this is the *gauge* pressure relative to the free surface.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem:** A tank contains oil of specific gravity $0.85$ to a depth of $6 \text{ m}$. Above the oil is a layer of compressed air at $50 \text{ kPa}$ (absolute). Find: (i) the absolute pressure at the bottom of the tank, and (ii) the gauge pressure at the bottom.

**Solution:**

Given: $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $h = 6 \text{ m}$, $P_{\text{air}} = 50 \text{ kPa (abs)}$.

**Step (i) — Compute hydrostatic pressure due to oil column:**

$$P_{\text{oil}} = \rho_{\text{oil}} \cdot g \cdot h = 850 \times 9.81 \times 6$$

$$P_{\text{oil}} = 50,\!031 \text{ Pa} \approx 50.031 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step (ii) — Compute absolute pressure at the bottom:**

$$P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = P_{\text{air}} + P_{\text{oil}}$$

$$P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = 50 + 50.031 = 100.031 \text{ kPa (abs)}$$

**Step (iii) — Compute gauge pressure at the bottom:**

$$P_{\text{bottom,gauge}} = P_{\text{bottom,abs}} - P_{\text{atm}} = 100.031 - 101.325$$

$$P_{\text{bottom,gauge}} = -1.294 \text{ kPa}$$

The negative sign indicates the pressure at the bottom is **below atmospheric** (a partial vacuum condition).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pressure Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID PRESSURE P] --> B[Reference Frame Selection]
    B --> C[Absolute Pressure Pabs]
    B --> D[Gauge Pressure Pgage]
    B --> E[Vacuum Pressure Pvac]
    C --> F[Reference: Perfect Vacuum]
    C --> G[Pabs = Patm + Pgage]
    C --> H[Pabs = Patm minus Pvac]
    D --> I[Reference: Local Atmosphere]
    D --> J[Pgage = Pabs minus Patm]
    D --> K[Always positive when above atm]
    E --> L[Reference: Local Atmosphere]
    E --> M[Pvac = Patm minus Pabs]
    E --> N[Exists when Pabs less than Patm]
    F --> O[Measurement Device: Barometer]
    I --> P[Measurement Device: Bourdon Gauge]
    L --> Q[Measurement Device: Vacuum Gauge]
    O --> R[Final Output: Pressure Value in Pa]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Simple U-Tube Manometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pipe A with pressure PA] --> B[Fluid in pipe density rho_f]
    B --> C[Falling column height h1]
    C --> D[Lower mercury interface]
    D --> E[Mercury column of height h]
    E --> F[Upper mercury interface]
    F --> G[Rising column height h2]
    G --> H[Pipe B with pressure PB]
    D --> I[rho_m x g x h]
    H --> J[Pressure difference Delta P]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Result: P A minus P B equals rho_m minus rho_f times g times h]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pressure Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Physical Source: Static Fluid Column or Vessel] --> S2[Sensing Element: Diaphragm, Bourdon Tube, or Manometer]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conversion: Mechanical deflection or Fluid column height]
    S3 --> S4[Calibrated Scale: Reads in Pa, bar, mmHg, or m of fluid]
    S4 --> S5[Output Display: Absolute, Gauge, or Vacuum reading]
    S5 --> S6[Engineering Decision: Pump design, Pipe rating, Tank safety]
    note1[Atmospheric Compensation Applied Here] -.-> S5
    note2[Reference Vacuum Applied Here] -.-> S5
    style S1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S6 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The above diagrams are functional architecture block diagrams. They map the *flow of information and pressure signal* from physical source to engineering decision, mirroring how real pressure measurement systems operate in industrial settings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**Define the following terms with SI units: (a) Fluid Pressure, (b) Pressure Head, (c) Atmospheric Pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Fluid Pressure:** It is the normal force per unit area exerted by a fluid on a surface in contact with it. $P = F/A$. SI unit: **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**.

**(b) Pressure Head:** It is the height of a fluid column that produces a given pressure at its base. $h = P/\rho g$. SI unit: **metre (m)**.

**(c) Atmospheric Pressure:** The pressure exerted by the weight of the Earth's atmospheric column on a unit area at sea level. Standard value: $P_{\text{atm}} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$. SI unit: **Pascal (Pa)**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish clearly between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. Show their relationship using a neat sketch description.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Absolute Pressure** is measured from a perfect vacuum (true zero).
- **Gauge Pressure** is measured from local atmospheric pressure as the reference. $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$.
- **Vacuum Pressure** is the magnitude by which absolute pressure falls below atmospheric. $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$.

**Relationship diagram (textual):**

```
Perfect Vacuum (0) ---- Pabs ---- Patm (101.325 kPa) ---- Pvac = 0
                                    |
                                    |--- Pgage = 0 at atm
                                    |
                          Pabs less than Patm
                                    |
                              Pvac positive
                              Pgage negative
```

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q.A. (a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h$ for a static incompressible fluid. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider an elementary fluid column of base area $dA$ and height $dh$ at depth $h$ below the free surface.

**Step 2:** Identify the forces:
- Pressure force on top face (downward): $P \cdot dA$
- Pressure force on bottom face (upward): $(P + dP) \cdot dA$
- Weight of the element (downward): $\rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh$

**Step 3:** Apply static equilibrium:

$$(P + dP) \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dh = 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$dP = \rho g \, dh$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from free surface ($P = P_{\text{atm}}$ at $h = 0$) to depth $h$ (pressure $P$):

$$\int_{P_{\text{atm}}}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh$$

$$P - P_{\text{atm}} = \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{P = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h}$$

**Step 6:** Stating the physical meaning — pressure increases linearly with depth; depends only on the vertical height, not the container shape (hydrostatic paradox).

---

**Q.A. (b)** A U-tube manometer containing mercury (specific gravity 13.6) is connected to a pipe carrying oil (specific gravity 0.85). The manometer reads a difference of $40 \text{ cm}$ of mercury. If the two points in the pipe are at the same elevation, find the pressure difference between them. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $h = 0.40 \text{ m}$, $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1:** Apply the differential manometer equation:

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\Delta P = (13600 - 850) \times 9.81 \times 0.40$$

$$\Delta P = 12750 \times 9.81 \times 0.40$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$\Delta P = 50,\!031 \text{ Pa} \approx 50.03 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** The pressure difference between the two points is **$50.03 \text{ kPa}$**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the working of a simple barometer and a Bourdon tube pressure gauge with neat descriptions. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

**Simple Barometer (Mercury Barometer):**
A glass tube of length greater than $76 \text{ cm}$, closed at one end and filled completely with mercury, is inverted into a mercury reservoir. The atmospheric pressure acting on the reservoir surface supports a mercury column in the tube. At standard atmosphere, this column is **$760 \text{ mm}$ of Hg**. The space above the mercury in the tube is a near-perfect vacuum (Torricellian vacuum). Thus:

$$P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_{\text{Hg}} \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{Hg}}$$

**Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge:**
It consists of a curved metallic tube (elliptical cross-section) connected to the pressure source. When pressure enters, the tube tends to straighten due to internal pressure, and the free end moves. This motion is amplified through a gear mechanism and indicated on a calibrated dial. The gauge reads **gauge pressure** directly.

---

**Q.B. (b)** A closed tank contains water to a depth of $4 \text{ m}$. The air space above the water is pressurised to $0.8 \text{ bar}$ (gauge). Find: (i) The absolute pressure at the bottom of the tank, (ii) The pressure head at the bottom in metres of water, (iii) The pressure in metres of mercury. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $h = 4 \text{ m}$, $P_{\text{air,gauge}} = 0.8 \text{ bar} = 80,\!000 \text{ Pa}$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101,\!325 \text{ Pa}$.

**(i) Absolute pressure at the bottom:**

Hydrostatic pressure due to water: $P_w = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4 = 39,\!240 \text{ Pa}$.

Absolute pressure at top of water: $P_{\text{top,abs}} = P_{\text{air,gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}} = 80,\!000 + 101,\!325 = 181,\!325 \text{ Pa}$.

$$P_{\text{bottom,abs}} = 181,\!325 + 39,\!240 = 220,\!565 \text{ Pa} \approx 220.57 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Pressure head in metres of water:**

$$h_w = \frac{P_{\text{bottom,gauge}}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{\text{bottom,abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}}{\rho g}$$

$$P_{\text{bottom,gauge}} = 220,\!565 - 101,\!325 = 119,\!240 \text{ Pa}$$

$$h_w = \frac{119,\!240}{1000 \times 9.81} = 12.15 \text{ m of water}$$

**(iii) Pressure in metres of mercury:**

$$h_{\text{Hg}} = \frac{P_{\text{bottom,gauge}}}{\rho_{\text{Hg}} \cdot g} = \frac{119,\!240}{13,\!600 \times 9.81} = 0.894 \text{ m of Hg}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state the reference** for pressure (absolute, gauge, or vacuum). Marks are deducted if the student simply writes "P = ..." without specifying.
> 2. **Units matter.** KTU expects SI units (Pa or kPa) in final answers. Mixing bar, mmHg, and Pa without conversion is a common error.
> 3. **Manometer sign convention:** If point A is at a higher elevation than point B, add the term $\rho_f g (z_A - z_B)$ carefully. Many students miss this when both points are at different heights.
> 4. **Vacuum pressure is never negative.** It is defined as $P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$ and is always $\geq 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pressure ($P = F/A$)** is a scalar quantity with SI unit **Pascal (Pa)**.
- **Pressure head** $h = P/\rho g$ converts pressure to an equivalent fluid column height in **metres**.
- **Hydrostatic equation:** $P = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h$ — pressure increases linearly with depth.
- **Three pressure types** (always remember the AGA relationship):
  - $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$
  - $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$
- **Pascal's Law:** Pressure at a point in a static fluid is the same in all directions.
- **Manometer equation:** $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ — essential for all differential pressure problems.
- **Bourdon gauge** reads gauge pressure; **barometer** reads atmospheric pressure; **absolute pressure** requires a vacuum reference.
- **Standard atmosphere:** $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$.
- **Hydrostatic paradox:** Pressure at the bottom depends only on fluid depth, not on container shape or volume.
- **For KTU numericals:** Always show free-body diagrams, state the assumption of static equilibrium, and convert all units to SI before substituting.
- **Vacuum pressure cannot exceed atmospheric pressure** under normal terrestrial conditions.
- **Diaphragm gauges** are preferred for low pressures and corrosive fluids; **Bourdon tubes** for high pressures.
- **Piezoelectric transducers** are used for dynamic (rapidly varying) pressure measurement, not static pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezometer, Simple and Differential Manometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piezometer, Simple and Differential Manometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Piezometer

### Formal Definition
A **piezometer** is the simplest form of manometer used to measure the **gauge pressure** of a liquid at a specific point within a pipe or container. It consists of a single vertical, open-ended transparent tube (typically glass) connected directly to the point where pressure measurement is desired. The liquid rises inside the tube to a height proportional to the pressure head at that point.

The basic working principle is **hydrostatic equilibrium**: the pressure exerted by the fluid column inside the tube must exactly balance the pressure of the fluid at the connection point.

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- **$P$** = Gauge pressure at the point ($\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa)
- **$\rho$** = Density of the liquid being measured ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- **$g$** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 $\text{m/s}^2$**
- **$h$** = Height of the liquid column above the point (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A piezometer can ONLY measure the pressure of liquids. It CANNOT be used for gases because the density of gas is too low to produce a measurable liquid column. Furthermore, it only measures **gauge pressure** (pressure above atmospheric), not absolute pressure.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine drinking through a straw submerged in water. The water rises inside the straw because of the pressure pushing it up. Now, if you poke a thin, transparent vertical tube into a water pipe, the water will rise inside that tube. The **higher the pressure inside the pipe, the higher the water climbs** in that tube. That vertical tube is essentially a piezometer — it translates the invisible "push" of pressure into a visible "height" of liquid.

### Limitations of Piezometer
- Cannot measure **negative gauge pressure** (vacuum or suction pressure) effectively — the liquid column would just fall back.
- Cannot measure pressure of **gases** (very low density).
- Cannot measure **high pressures** practically — would require impractically tall tubes (e.g., 1 MPa of water pressure would need a 102 m tall tube).
- **Cannot** measure pressure difference between two points directly.

---

## 1.2 Simple Manometer (U-Tube Manometer)

### Formal Definition
A **simple manometer** is a U-shaped tube containing a **heavier, immiscible manometric fluid** (typically **mercury, Hg** with density $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, or carbon tetrachloride, or other heavy liquids). One end is connected to the point where pressure is to be measured, while the other end is either open to the atmosphere (giving gauge pressure) or sealed (giving absolute pressure).

It overcomes the major limitations of the piezometer by using a **dense manometric fluid** so that only small column heights are needed to measure large pressures.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a U-shaped transparent tube (like a curved drinking straw) partly filled with mercury. If you gently push on one end with high-pressure water and leave the other end open, the mercury level on the high-pressure side gets pushed **down**, while it rises on the open side. By measuring the **difference** in mercury levels between the two arms, you can figure out exactly how much pressure was pushing down on that side. It's like a balance — instead of measuring pressure against gravity with water (which needs tall tubes), you're using mercury as a heavy counterweight to keep things compact.

> [!NOTE]
> The key property of mercury that makes it ideal: it has a **very high density** (13.6 times that of water) and is **immiscible** with most liquids, so it doesn't mix and gives a clear interface.

---

## 1.3 Differential Manometer

### Formal Definition
A **differential manometer** is a device used to measure the **difference in pressure between two points** in a system (typically in a pipeline or between two containers). It does not measure absolute or gauge pressure at any single point, but rather the **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_A - P_B$ between two locations.

It consists of a U-tube containing a heavier (or lighter) manometric fluid, with both limbs connected to the two points of interest through tubing filled with the working fluid.

### Types of Differential Manometers
1. **U-tube Differential Manometer** — uses a heavier manometric fluid (mercury).
2. **Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer** — uses a lighter manometric fluid (e.g., oil or air) and is preferred when the working fluid is heavier than mercury is impractical, or for measuring pressure differences in gas pipelines or water systems where a small differential is needed.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two balloons connected by a U-tube filled with colored oil. If one balloon is squeezed harder than the other, the oil gets pushed from the high-pressure side to the low-pressure side. By looking at how much higher the oil sits on the "easier" side, you can determine the difference in how hard each balloon was being squeezed. A differential manometer works exactly this way — it doesn't care about the absolute pressure, only the **difference** between two points, which is critical for measuring pipe friction losses, flow through orifices, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why measure pressure differences?** In fluid mechanics, we are often more interested in the *pressure drop* along a pipe (for calculating friction losses, head loss across an orifice meter, etc.) than in the absolute pressure at any single point. Differential manometers are central to flow measurement devices (Pitot tubes, Venturimeters, orifice meters) and in determining minor losses in pipe networks.

---

## 1.4 Standard Constants and Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants and Properties to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Density of water: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of mercury: $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Specific gravity of mercury: $S_{Hg} = 13.6$
> - Specific weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
> - Specific weight of mercury: $\gamma_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 9810 = 133{,}416 \text{ N/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle: Hydrostatic Equation

The foundation of all manometric measurement is the **hydrostatic pressure equation**:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho \cdot g$$

This means that as we go **upward** in a static fluid (z increases), the pressure **decreases** at a rate equal to the specific weight of the fluid.

For a finite height difference, integrating gives:

$$P_1 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \rho g z_2$$

This is the **modified Bernoulli equation for static fluids** and is the principle behind every piezometer and manometer derivation.

---

## 2.2 Operating Logic of a Piezometer

### Why it works (step-by-step logic):
1. The tube is open at the top, so the pressure at the top of the liquid column equals **atmospheric pressure** ($P_{atm}$).
2. The tube is connected at a point where pressure $P$ is to be measured.
3. Liquid rises in the tube to a height $h$ above the connection point until hydrostatic equilibrium is reached.
4. At the connection point (at the bottom of the liquid column in the tube), pressure from the tube side = pressure from the pipe side.
5. Therefore: $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$

### Result:
- **Gauge pressure** = $P - P_{atm} = \rho g h$ (this is what piezometer reads)
- **Absolute pressure** = $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners specifically test whether you write **"gauge pressure"** vs **"absolute pressure"**. By default, a piezometer reads gauge pressure unless stated otherwise.

---

## 2.3 Operating Logic of a Simple Manometer

### Why it works (step-by-step logic):
1. The U-tube contains a manometric fluid (e.g., mercury) heavier than the working fluid (e.g., water/oil).
2. Apply the principle: **pressure is the same at the same horizontal level within a connected static fluid of uniform density**.
3. Starting from the connection point, traverse through the working fluid (up or down) and through the manometric fluid to the open end.
4. Add pressure when moving **downward**, subtract when moving **upward**.

### General Equation for Simple U-tube Manometer:
With point A connected to the manometer, working fluid of density $\rho_1$, manometric fluid of density $\rho_2$ (where $\rho_2 > \rho_1$), and the manometer reading showing a mercury level difference of $h$ with the open end having an air column of height $y$ above mercury:

$$P_A = \rho_2 g h - \rho_1 g y$$

(For an open-end simple manometer where the right limb is open to atmosphere and mercury is on the right side lower, the standard form is shown in Section 3.)

---

## 2.4 Operating Logic of a Differential Manometer

### U-tube Differential Manometer (Heavier fluid):

For two points A and B with pressures $P_A$ and $P_B$, working fluid density $\rho_1$, manometric fluid (heavier) density $\rho_2$, mercury level difference $h$, with both limbs connected to points A and B at the same elevation:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_2 - \rho_1) \cdot g \cdot h$$

This gives the **pressure difference** directly.

### Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer (Lighter fluid):

When the manometric fluid is **lighter** than the working fluid (e.g., oil on top of water, or air for gases):

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_1 - \rho_2) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Note the sign reversal because the geometry flips.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Device | Configuration | Key Formula | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Piezometer** | Single vertical open tube | $P = \rho g h$ | Measure gauge pressure of liquids at low-to-moderate values |
| **Simple Manometer** | U-tube with heavy fluid (mercury) | $P_A = \rho_2 g h - \rho_1 g y + P_{atm}$ | Measure pressure of any liquid including high pressures |
| **U-tube Differential Manometer** | Both limbs connected, heavy fluid in U | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_2 - \rho_1) g h$ | Measure pressure difference in pipelines |
| **Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer** | Both limbs connected, light fluid/air in inverted U | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g h$ | Measure small pressure differences in liquids / flows in gas lines |
| **Hydrostatic Equation** | General principle | $dP/dz = -\rho g$ | Foundation of all manometer derivations |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the formulas above, $h$ represents the **difference in mercury (or manometric fluid) levels** between the two limbs of the U-tube — NOT the height of the column above the connection point. This distinction is frequently tested.

### Where this is used in real engineering:
- **Hydraulic engineering**: Measuring flow rates through Venturimeters and orifice meters (using differential manometers).
- **Pipe flow analysis**: Determining head loss and friction factor in pipelines.
- **Boiler and pressure vessel testing**: Using manometers to verify gauge pressures.
- **Aerospace**: Pitot-static tubes use differential manometer principles for airspeed measurement.
- **Civil engineering**: Monitoring piezometric head in groundwater wells and dams.

---

## 2.6 Key Assumptions in Manometer Theory

1. **Static fluid assumption** — the fluid inside the manometer tubing must be at rest (no acceleration).
2. **Constant density** — fluids are assumed incompressible (true for liquids under typical pressures).
3. **No air pockets** — the connecting tubes must be completely filled with working fluid (no gas bubbles).
4. **Isothermal conditions** — temperature effects on density are neglected.
5. **Manometric fluid is immiscible** with the working fluid and has a sharp, well-defined meniscus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Piezometer Pressure Equation

### Setup:
A vertical piezometer tube is connected at point X in a pipe carrying water. The water rises to a height $h$ in the tube above point X. The tube is open to atmosphere at the top.

### Derivation:

We apply the hydrostatic equation along the liquid column from point X up to the free surface in the piezometer tube.

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

Integrating from point X (where pressure is $P_X$) to the free surface (where pressure is $P_{atm}$):

$$\int_{P_X}^{P_{atm}} dP = -\int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dz$$

$$P_{atm} - P_X = -\rho g h$$

$$P_X - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{P_X = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

The gauge pressure at X is:

$$P_{X,\text{gauge}} = P_X - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

### Interpretation:
The piezometer gives us the **gauge pressure** as the product of specific weight and height. Every 1 m of water column corresponds to **9.81 kPa** of gauge pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Simple U-Tube Manometer Equation

### Setup:
A simple U-tube manometer contains mercury ($\rho_{Hg}$) at the bottom. Point A is in a pipe carrying oil (density $\rho_{oil}$). The left limb is connected to A, the right limb is open to atmosphere. Let:
- $y$ = height of oil column above the mercury level on the left side
- $h$ = difference in mercury levels (right side is higher by $h$, since oil is lighter than mercury)
- The right limb is open to atmosphere with negligible gas column.

### Derivation:

We trace pressures starting from point A, going up the left limb through oil, down to the lower mercury level, across to the same level on the right limb, and up through mercury to the open end.

**Step 1:** Pressure at point A (this is what we want):

$$P_A$$

**Step 2:** Move UP through the oil column by height $y$ to reach the left mercury surface:

$$P_{\text{left Hg surface}} = P_A - \rho_{oil} g y$$

(Pressure decreases as we go up.)

**Step 3:** Move DOWN through mercury by height $h$ to reach the right mercury surface level (which is the same as the left surface level after accounting for the difference):

Actually, since the right mercury surface is **higher** by $h$, the left mercury surface is **lower**. Let us instead move from left mercury surface **DOWN** to the level of right mercury surface — this is a height of 0 if we choose reference cleverly.

Let me redefine for clarity:
- Let the **lower mercury level** (left side) be our datum.
- The right mercury level is at height $h$ above this datum.

**Recomputed derivation:**

**Step 1:** $P_A$ at point A in pipe.

**Step 2:** Move UP through oil of height $y_1$ to reach the lower (left) mercury surface:

$$P_{\text{left Hg surf}} = P_A - \rho_{oil} g y_1$$

**Step 3:** The right mercury surface is at the same horizontal level as a point inside the right limb mercury column. Pressure at this same level in a connected static fluid of uniform density is **equal**. So at the level of the lower mercury surface (left), the pressure in the right limb is:

$$P_{\text{at left Hg level, right side}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

(Atmospheric pressure plus the weight of mercury column of height $h$ above this level on the right side.)

**Step 4:** Equating the two expressions (same level, same fluid — mercury — on both sides at the lower interface):

$$P_A - \rho_{oil} g y_1 = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $P_A$:

$$\boxed{P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h + \rho_{oil} g y_1}$$

### General Simple Manometer Formula:
For a connection point A with working fluid density $\rho_1$ (oil/water/etc.) and manometric fluid (heavier) density $\rho_2$:

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_2 g h + \rho_1 g y_1$$

If the right limb is **closed and evacuated** (a barometer-like setup), then $P_{atm}$ is replaced by 0 (vacuum), giving absolute pressure.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: U-Tube Differential Manometer

### Setup:
Points A and B are at the **same horizontal elevation** in a horizontal pipe carrying fluid of density $\rho_1$. A U-tube differential manometer is connected below the pipe, containing mercury ($\rho_2 = \rho_{Hg} > \rho_1$). Let $h$ be the difference in mercury levels (mercury on the side of lower pressure is pushed higher).

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Trace from point A downward through working fluid to the higher mercury level (say, on side B, since pressure at B is lower — mercury rises on the low-pressure side):

Let the height of working fluid between A and the mercury surface on A's side be $y$. Since A and B are at the same elevation, both limbs have the same geometry of working fluid connection.

**Step 2:** Pressure at the higher mercury surface (let's say on side B):

$$P_{\text{top Hg, B}} = P_B + \rho_1 g y$$

**Step 3:** Pressure at the lower mercury surface (on side A) — this is at a level $h$ BELOW the higher surface:

$$P_{\text{bottom Hg, A}} = P_{\text{top Hg, B}} + \rho_2 g h = P_B + \rho_1 g y + \rho_2 g h$$

**Step 4:** Pressure at the lower mercury surface can also be reached by going from A:

$$P_{\text{bottom Hg, A}} = P_A + \rho_1 g y$$

(Going down from A through working fluid by height $y$ adds pressure.)

**Step 5:** Equating the two expressions:

$$P_A + \rho_1 g y = P_B + \rho_1 g y + \rho_2 g h$$

**Step 6:** Simplifying (the $\rho_1 g y$ terms cancel — beautifully elegant):

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_2 g h$$

**Step 7:** But this assumes the working fluid below the manometer is **only on one side** — in reality, both limbs have working fluid. The rigorous derivation gives:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_2 - \rho_1) g h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A very common mistake is to write $P_A - P_B = \rho_2 g h$ without subtracting $\rho_1 g h$. The term $(\rho_2 - \rho_1)$ arises because the working fluid column on both sides partially cancels out — only the *difference* between the working fluid and the manometric fluid contributes to the differential reading. The full rigorous derivation is shown in Section 3.5 below.

### Interpretation:
- If $P_A > P_B$, mercury is pushed **down on side A** and **up on side B**, so $h$ is measured as the height difference with side B mercury level higher.
- The pressure difference is proportional to the **difference in densities** between the manometric fluid and the working fluid, multiplied by $g$ and $h$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer

### Setup:
An inverted U-tube contains a **lighter** fluid (e.g., oil with density $\rho_2 < \rho_1$, the working fluid density). The inverted U is connected at the top of the pipe to points A and B at the same elevation. The lighter fluid sits in the inverted portion of the U.

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Trace from A upward through working fluid by height $y$ to reach the lower oil-mercury interface (working fluid pushes oil up).

**Step 2:** Continue through the lighter fluid (oil) across the inverted U. The oil column height in the inverted U is $h$ (this is the difference in oil levels).

**Step 3:** Move down on side B through the working fluid by height $y$ to reach point B.

**Step 4:** Pressure balance (starting from A):

$$P_A - \rho_1 g y - \rho_2 g h - \rho_1 g y + \rho_1 g y = P_B$$

Wait — let me redo this carefully.

Starting at A, going UP through working fluid by $y$ to the left oil surface:

$$P_{\text{left oil surf}} = P_A - \rho_1 g y$$

Going UP through oil column of height $h$ (inverted U) to the right oil surface (which is $h$ higher than left when $P_A > P_B$):

$$P_{\text{right oil surf}} = P_A - \rho_1 g y - \rho_2 g h$$

Going DOWN through working fluid by $y$ to point B:

$$P_B = P_A - \rho_1 g y - \rho_2 g h + \rho_1 g y$$

$$P_B = P_A - \rho_2 g h$$

**Step 5:** Solving:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_2 g h$$

For the general case with both limbs having working fluid columns up to the oil interfaces:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_1 - \rho_2) g h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For inverted U-tube, the formula has $(\rho_1 - \rho_2)$ — note the order! Because the working fluid is now the denser one, the density difference reverses.

---

## 3.5 Rigorous Derivation: U-Tube Differential Manometer (Full Form)

To address the issue raised in Section 3.3, let me derive the **complete** formula showing both $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ contributions explicitly.

### Setup:
- Point A at elevation $z_A$, pressure $P_A$
- Point B at elevation $z_B$, pressure $P_B$
- Working fluid (above manometric fluid) density $\rho_1$
- Manometric fluid (in U-tube) density $\rho_2$
- Heights of working fluid columns in left and right limbs: $h_1$ (from A to left Hg surface) and $h_2$ (from B to right Hg surface)
- Difference in mercury levels: $h_m$ (with mercury higher on the side of lower pressure)

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Pressure at left mercury surface (going down from A):

$$P_{\text{left Hg surf}} = P_A + \rho_1 g h_1$$

**Step 2:** Pressure at right mercury surface (going down from B):

$$P_{\text{right Hg surf}} = P_B + \rho_1 g h_2$$

**Step 3:** The two mercury surfaces are at different elevations. If the right mercury surface is **higher** than the left by $h_m$, then at the level of the left mercury surface, the pressure in the right limb is:

$$P_{\text{at left level, right limb}} = P_{\text{right Hg surf}} + \rho_2 g h_m$$

**Step 4:** But at the same horizontal level in the **same connected static fluid** (mercury), the pressure must be equal:

$$P_{\text{left Hg surf}} = P_{\text{at left level, right limb}}$$

$$P_A + \rho_1 g h_1 = P_B + \rho_1 g h_2 + \rho_2 g h_m$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the pressure difference:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_1 g (h_2 - h_1) + \rho_2 g h_m$$

Now, observe the geometry. If we choose the left mercury surface as the lower one (meaning $h_m$ is the height of right mercury surface above left), and if points A and B are at the **same elevation**, then:

$$z_A = z_B \implies h_1 + \text{(left Hg level)} = h_2 + \text{(right Hg level)}$$

This gives:

$$h_1 - h_2 = \text{(right Hg level)} - \text{(left Hg level)} = h_m$$

So:

$$h_2 - h_1 = -h_m$$

Substituting:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_1 g (-h_m) + \rho_2 g h_m = (\rho_2 - \rho_1) g h_m$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_2 - \rho_1) g h}$$

This confirms the result. The $(\rho_2 - \rho_1)$ term is NOT an approximation — it is the exact answer when A and B are at the same elevation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If A and B are at **different elevations**, you cannot simply use this formula. You must either:
> 1. Use the full equation $P_A - P_B = \rho_1 g (h_2 - h_1) + \rho_2 g h_m$ with appropriate height terms, OR
> 2. Convert pressures to **piezometric head** $P/\rho_1 g + z$ before applying the differential manometer formula.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Differential Manometer

**Problem:** Two pressure taps are made 1.5 m apart in a horizontal water pipe. A differential manometer using mercury is connected across these taps. The mercury level difference is observed to be 200 mm. Find the pressure difference between the two taps in (a) $\text{N/m}^2$, (b) $\text{m}$ of water column.

**Given:**
- $\rho_1 = \rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_2 = \rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

**(a) Pressure difference in $\text{N/m}^2$:**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_2 - \rho_1) g h$$

$$P_A - P_B = (13{,}600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.2$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.2$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12{,}600 \times 1.962$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = 24{,}721.2 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 24.72 \text{ kPa}}$$

**(b) Pressure difference in $\text{m}$ of water column:**

$$h_{water} = \frac{P_A - P_B}{\rho_{water} \cdot g}$$

$$h_{water} = \frac{24{,}721.2}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{water} = \frac{24{,}721.2}{9810}$$

$$\boxed{h_{water} = 2.52 \text{ m of water}}$$

**Verification by another method:**
$$h_{water} = h \times \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) = 0.2 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.2 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a piezometer. Mention two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

A piezometer is a simple device used to measure the gauge pressure of a liquid at a point in a pipe or container. It consists of a single vertical, open-ended transparent tube (usually glass) connected to the point of measurement. The liquid rises in the tube to a height $h$ such that the hydrostatic pressure balance is satisfied:

$$P = \rho g h$$

**Limitations:**
1. A piezometer cannot measure the pressure of **gases** because gases have very low density and would require an impractically tall tube to show a measurable rise.
2. It cannot effectively measure **negative gauge pressure** (vacuum/suction), as the liquid column would simply fall back into the pipe.
3. It cannot measure **large pressures** because that would require impractically tall tubes (1 MPa needs ~102 m of water column).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a simple manometer and a differential manometer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Manometer | Differential Manometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Measures pressure at a **single point** (gauge or absolute) | Measures the **difference** in pressure between two points |
| **Connections** | One end connected to the point, other end open/closed | Both ends connected to two points of interest |
