| Vertical rectangle, top at free surface, width $b$, height $d$ | $\dfrac{\rho g b d^2}{2}$ | $\dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\dfrac{2d}{3}$ |
| Vertical rectangle, top at depth $h_1$, height $d$, width $b$ | $\rho g b d \left(h_1 + \dfrac{d}{2}\right)$ | $h_1 + \dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ with $\theta = 90°$ |
| Inclined rectangle, immersed at angle $\theta$ | $\rho g A \bar{h}$ | $\bar{y} \sin\theta$ | $\bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Centre of Pressure Determination Logic

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **decision flow** a KTU student should follow when solving any Centre of Pressure problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submerged Plane Surface Problem] --> B{Is surface horizontal?}
    B -- YES --> C[h* equals h equals bar h]
    B -- NO --> D{Identify Geometry}
    D --> E[Rectangular Section]
    D --> F[Circular Section]
    D --> G[Triangular Section]
    D --> H[Trapezoidal Section]
    E --> I[Compute A and bar h]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute I_G from Standard Table]
    J --> K[Determine Theta]
    K --> L[Apply h* equals bar h plus I_G sin squared theta over A bar h]
    L --> M[Compute Total Force F equals rho g A bar h]
    M --> N[Report h* and F as Final Answer]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Hydrostatic Thrust on an Immersed Surface

This block diagram maps the **physics-to-mathematics chain** of computing Centre of Pressure, suitable for revision and exam preparation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputData[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        A1[Liquid specific weight gamma]
        A2[Surface area A]
        A3[Depth of centroid bar h]
        A4[Moment of inertia I_G]
        A5[Inclination angle theta]
    end

    subgraph Processing[COMPUTATIONAL CORE]
        B1[Total Force F equals gamma A bar h]
        B2[Pressure shift term I_G sin squared theta over A bar h]
        B3[h* equals bar h plus pressure shift term]
    end

    subgraph Output[ENGINEERING OUTPUT]
        C1[Resultant Hydrostatic Thrust F in Newtons]
        C2[Centre of Pressure depth h* in metres]
        C3[Line of action for stability analysis]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A1 --> C1
    B1 --> C1

    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    B1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description — Pressure Distribution on a Vertical Wall

For students visualizing the underlying physics (no Mermaid equivalent for physical drawings), the conceptual schematic is as follows:

> A vertical rectangular wall of height $d$ and width $b$ has its top edge at the free liquid surface. The pressure at the top is **zero** ($P = 0$). The pressure at the bottom is **maximum**: $P_{bottom} = \rho g d$. The pressure distribution forms a **right-angled triangle**, with the hypotenuse slanting from zero at the top to $P_{bottom}$ at the bottom. The area of this pressure triangle (per unit width) represents the total force per unit width:
> 
> $$\frac{F}{b} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \rho g d = \frac{\rho g d^2}{2}$$
> 
> The **centroid of this triangular pressure distribution** lies at one-third of the height from the base, which corresponds to **two-thirds of the depth from the free surface** — this is the Centre of Pressure $h^{*} = \dfrac{2d}{3}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

Define the term **"Centre of Pressure"** for a submerged plane surface. Under what condition does it coincide with the centroid of the surface?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Centre of Pressure** is the point on a submerged plane surface at which the resultant hydrostatic pressure force is considered to act. It is the point where the line of action of the total hydrostatic thrust intersects the surface.
>
> **Condition for coincidence with centroid:** When the plane surface is **horizontal** (i.e., lying in a plane of constant depth), the pressure is uniform across the entire surface. In this case, the resultant force acts at the centroid of the area, so the Centre of Pressure **coincides exactly with the Centroid**.
>
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition — 2 marks, Condition — 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

A vertical rectangular gate of width 2 m and height 3 m is installed with its top edge at the free surface of water. Calculate the depth of the Centre of Pressure from the free surface.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Width $b = 2 \, \text{m}$, Height $d = 3 \, \text{m}$
- Top edge at free surface, vertical ($\theta = 90°$)

**Depth of Centre of Pressure (from standard formula):**

$$h^{*} = \frac{2d}{3} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3} = 2 \, \text{m}$$

**Therefore, the Centre of Pressure lies at a depth of 2 m from the free surface.**

**Verification using the general formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{h} &= \frac{d}{2} = 1.5 \, \text{m} \\
I_G &= \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 27}{12} = 4.5 \, \text{m}^4 \\
A &= b \cdot d = 2 \times 3 = 6 \, \text{m}^2 \\
h^{*} &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} = 1.5 + \frac{4.5 \times 1}{6 \times 1.5} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2 \, \text{m} \, \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Formula identification — 1 mark, Calculation — 1.5 marks, Answer with units — 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

(a) **Derive the expression for the depth of the Centre of Pressure for an inclined plane surface immersed in a static liquid.** Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

(b) A vertical rectangular gate of width 1.5 m and height 2 m is hinged at the top and has its bottom edge 3 m below the free water surface. Determine:
   - (i) The magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic thrust on the gate. **(3 Marks)**
   - (ii) The depth of the Centre of Pressure from the free surface. **(4 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.2 (Derivation 2)** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key steps to present in the exam:

1. Draw the inclined plane surface submerged at angle $\theta$ with horizontal free surface. Mark the intersection point $O$, the elemental strip at distance $y$ from $O$.
2. Write $h = y \sin\theta$, $dF = \gamma y \sin\theta \, dA$, and integrate to get $F = \gamma A \bar{y} \sin\theta = \rho g A \bar{h}$.
3. Take moments about $O$: $M_O = \int y \, dF = \gamma \sin\theta \cdot I_O$.
4. Equate: $F \cdot y^{*} = M_O$, giving $y^{*} = \dfrac{I_O}{A \bar{y}}$.
5. Apply Parallel Axis Theorem $I_O = I_G + A \bar{y}^2$.
6. Final result: $y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{y}}$, and $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$.

**Assumptions to state:**
- Liquid is static and incompressible.
- Surface is plane (not curved).
- Pressure varies linearly with depth (hydrostatic law).
- Atmospheric pressure acts on both sides (gauge pressure considered).

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Width of gate: $b = 1.5 \, \text{m}$
- Height of gate: $d = 2 \, \text{m}$
- Depth of top edge from free surface: $h_1 = 3 \, \text{m}$
- Vertical orientation: $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$
- Specific weight of water: $\gamma = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute area of gate:**

$$A = b \times d = 1.5 \times 2 = 3 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute depth of centroid from free surface:**

$$\bar{h} = h_1 + \frac{d}{2} = 3 + \frac{2}{2} = 3 + 1 = 4 \, \text{m}$$

**Part (b)(i) — Resultant Hydrostatic Thrust (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho g A \bar{h} = \gamma A \bar{h} \\
  &= 9810 \times 3 \times 4 \\
  &= 117720 \, \text{N} \\
  &= 117.72 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Depth of Centre of Pressure (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia about centroidal axis:**

$$I_G = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 2^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 8}{12} = \frac{12}{12} = 1 \, \text{m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Apply Centre of Pressure formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{*} &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} \\
      &= 4 + \frac{1 \times 1}{3 \times 4} \\
      &= 4 + \frac{1}{12} \\
      &= 4 + 0.0833 \\
      &= 4.0833 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the depth of the Centre of Pressure from the free water surface is $h^{*} = 4.083 \, \text{m}$.**

**Marks Distribution:** Area and centroid — 1 mark, Force computation — 2 marks, $I_G$ — 1 mark, $h^{*}$ formula application — 2 marks, Final answer — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

(a) With a neat sketch, explain the concept of **Total Pressure Force** and **Centre of Pressure** for a plane surface immersed in a static liquid. **(7 Marks)**

(b) A circular gate of diameter 1.5 m is mounted vertically with its centre at a depth of 4 m below the free water surface. Determine:
   - (i) The total hydrostatic thrust acting on the gate. **(3 Marks)**
   - (ii) The position of the Centre of Pressure measured from the free surface. **(4 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation with Sketch (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 1** and **Section 4.3** of these notes for the sketch and physical reasoning. Key points to present:

1. **Sketch:** Inclined plane surface submerged in liquid, with pressure distribution diagram (triangular/linear).
2. **Total Pressure Force (F):** Resultant of the non-uniform pressure distribution acting on the plane surface. $F = \rho g A \bar{h}$ where $\bar{h}$ is the vertical depth of the centroid.
3. **Centre of Pressure (h*):** Point of application of $F$ on the plane surface. It lies below the centroid for vertical/inclined surfaces and coincides with the centroid for horizontal surfaces.
4. **Significance:** Used for stability checks (dams, gates), hinge design, etc.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of circular gate: $D = 1.5 \, \text{m}$
- Radius: $R = 0.75 \, \text{m}$
- Depth of centre of gate (centroid): $\bar{h} = 4 \, \text{m}$
- Vertical orientation: $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$

**Step 1 — Compute area of circular gate:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.25}{4} = 1.767 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute moment of inertia of circular section about centroidal axis:**

$$I_G = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625}{64} = 0.2485 \, \text{m}^4$$

**Part (b)(i) — Total Hydrostatic Thrust (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho g A \bar{h} = \gamma A \bar{h} \\
  &= 9810 \times 1.767 \times 4 \\
  &= 69339 \, \text{N} \\
  &\approx 69.34 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Position of Centre of Pressure (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{*} &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} \\
      &= 4 + \frac{0.2485 \times 1}{1.767 \times 4} \\
      &= 4 + \frac{0.2485}{7.068} \\
      &= 4 + 0.03516 \\
      &= 4.035 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the Centre of Pressure is located at a depth of $h^{*} = 4.035 \, \text{m}$ below the free surface.**

**Marks Distribution:** Area — 1 mark, $I_G$ — 1 mark, $F$ computation — 2 marks, $h^{*}$ formula — 2 marks, Final answer — 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks on Centre of Pressure Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert angle:** Students frequently confuse the angle $\theta$ as measured from the vertical instead of from the horizontal free surface. **Always verify** whether your $\theta$ is "inclination to horizontal" or "inclination to vertical" before substituting $\sin\theta$.
>
> 2. **Mixing $y^{*}$ and $h^{*}$:** $y^{*}$ is the distance measured **along the inclined plane** from $O$, while $h^{*}$ is the **vertical depth** from the free surface. They are related by $h^{*} = y^{*} \sin\theta$. Examiners will deduct marks if units and geometry are confused.
>
> 3. **Using wrong moment of inertia:** For a triangle, students often write $I_G = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ (which is for a rectangle) instead of $I_G = \dfrac{bd^3}{36}$.
>
> 4. **Including atmospheric pressure:** Unless explicitly asked, work in **gauge pressure** terms. Adding 101.325 kPa atmospheric pressure without justification will mark the answer wrong.
>
> 5. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** KTU examiners specifically allocate **at least 1–2 marks** for stating assumptions in derivations. Always list: static liquid, plane surface, linear pressure variation, incompressibility.
>
> 6. **Unit errors:** Forgetting that $\rho g$ has units of $\text{N/m}^3$ (specific weight) and that the final force must be in **Newtons (N)** or **kiloNewtons (kN)**. Numerical answers without units lose 0.5 mark.
>
> 7. **Not using parallel axis theorem explicitly:** If you write $I_O$ instead of converting to $I_G$, you must justify or you may not receive full credit for the simplification step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Centre of Pressure:**
>
> - **Definition:** Centre of Pressure is the point on a submerged plane surface where the resultant hydrostatic thrust acts.
> - **Total Pressure Force:** $F = \rho g A \bar{h} = \gamma A \bar{h}$, where $\bar{h}$ is the vertical depth of the **centroid**.
> - **Depth of Centre of Pressure:** $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}$.
> - **Along Inclined Plane from intersection O:** $y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{y}}$.
> - **Always $y^{*} > \bar{y}$** for non-horizontal surfaces — Centre of Pressure lies below the centroid.
> - **Horizontal Surface Exception:** $h^{*} = \bar{h}$ (centroid and centre of pressure coincide).
> - **Vertical Rectangle (top at free surface):** $h^{*} = \dfrac{2d}{3}$ and $F = \dfrac{\rho g b d^2}{2}$.
> - **Standard Moments of Inertia to memorize:**
>   - Rectangle: $\dfrac{bd^3}{12}$
>   - Triangle: $\dfrac{bd^3}{36}$
>   - Circle: $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$
>   - Semicircle (about diameter): $\dfrac{\pi R^4}{8} - \dfrac{8R^4}{9\pi}$
> - **Parallel Axis Theorem (essential tool):** $I_O = I_G + A \bar{y}^2$.
> - **Physical constants:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$, $\gamma_{Hg} \approx 133416 \, \text{N/m}^3$.
> - **Always state assumptions** in derivations: static liquid, plane surface, linear pressure variation, incompressibility, gauge pressure (unless specified otherwise).
> - **Real-world applications:** dam design, sluice gates, lock gates, submarine hulls, aircraft control surfaces, tainter gates.
> - **Examiner's red flags:** Wrong moment of inertia, $\theta$ convention confusion, missing units, skipping assumptions, mixing $y^{*}$ and $h^{*}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy and Metacentre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy and Metacentre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Buoyancy?

**Buoyancy** is the upward force exerted by a fluid (liquid or gas) that opposes the weight of an object fully or partially immersed in it. This upward force is called the **Buoyant Force** (or **Upthrust**). The science of buoyancy is formally known as **Hydrostatics** when dealing with liquids and **Aerostatics** when dealing with gases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Buoyancy is the resultant upward vertical force experienced by a body when wholly or partially submerged in a fluid, equal in magnitude to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

### 🔑 Archimedes' Principle (287–212 BCE)

The mathematical and physical foundation of buoyancy is built on **Archimedes' Principle**, which states:

> *"When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid at rest, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

Where:
- $F_B$ = Buoyant Force (in Newtons, **N**)
- $\rho_f$ = Mass density of the fluid (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $V_{disp}$ = Volume of fluid displaced (in **m³**)

---

## 1.2 What is the Metacentre?

When a floating body (like a ship or a buoy) is tilted by a small angle from its equilibrium position, the buoyant force shifts its line of action. The point at which the new line of action of the buoyant force intersects the original vertical centerline of the body is called the **Metacentre**, denoted by the letter **M**.

The metacentre is a *virtual* point — it does not physically exist on the body. It is a geometric construction that helps engineers determine the rotational stability of floating vessels.

### 🔑 Metacentric Height (GM)

The distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of the body and the **Metacentre (M)** is known as the **Metacentric Height**, denoted as **GM**. This is the single most important parameter in naval architecture and ship design.

$$GM = BM - BG$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> The sign and magnitude of **GM** determines whether a floating body is in **Stable**, **Unstable**, or **Neutral** equilibrium. Questions on metacentre are guaranteed in every KTU ESE paper, often for 7 marks under Module 1.

---

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy 🌊

### 🍎 The Floating Apple Analogy

Imagine dropping an apple into a bucket of water:
- The apple **sinks** initially because its weight is greater than the buoyant force acting on it (Apple is denser than water).
- A **wooden log** floats because the buoyant force equals the log's weight at the waterline.
- A **steel ship** floats because, despite steel being denser than water, the ship is hollow — its *average* density (including the air-filled hull) is less than water.

Now imagine **tilting** the wooden log slightly:
- If it returns to its upright position → **Stable Equilibrium**
- If it tips over completely → **Unstable Equilibrium**
- If it stays tilted at the new angle → **Neutral Equilibrium**

The **Metacentre** is the mathematical "anchor point" that determines which of these three scenarios will occur.

### 🚢 Real Engineering Applications

| Application | Why Buoyancy/Metacentre Matters |
|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Ship Design** | Ensures vessels don't capsize in rough seas |
| **Submarines** | Controlled flooding of ballast tanks for diving/surfacing |
| **Hot Air Balloons** | Buoyancy of hot air lifts the balloon |
| **Hydrometers** | Float at different depths based on fluid density |
| **Offshore Platforms** | Stability against wave-induced tilting |
| **Life Jackets** | Low density material provides buoyant force = body weight |

---

## 1.4 Foundational Terminology

Before diving deeper, here are the **standard KTU terms** you must memorize:

> [!NOTE]
> **Center of Buoyancy (B):** The centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the submerged body. It is the point through which the buoyant force is assumed to act vertically upward.
>
> **Center of Gravity (G):** The point through which the entire weight of the body acts vertically downward.
>
> **Metacentre (M):** The point where the line of action of the buoyant force (after tilting) intersects the original vertical axis of the body.
>
> **Metacentric Radius (BM):** The distance from the Center of Buoyancy B to the Metacentre M.
>
> **Metacentric Height (GM):** The distance from the Center of Gravity G to the Metacentre M.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Buoyancy

The concept of buoyancy arises directly from the fundamental principle of fluid statics — **Pascal's Law** combined with the **hydrostatic pressure variation** with depth.

### 🔬 The Physics Behind Buoyancy

Consider a rectangular block submerged in a fluid. The fluid exerts pressure on all surfaces of the block. The pressure on the **bottom face** is greater than on the **top face** because pressure increases with depth according to:

$$P = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where $h$ is the depth below the free surface. This **pressure difference** between the bottom and top faces creates a net upward force — this *is* the buoyant force.

### 📜 Archimedes' Principle — Formal Proof Logic

1. Consider an arbitrary volume $V$ of fluid in equilibrium.
2. The weight of this fluid volume acts downward through its centroid.
3. The surrounding fluid exerts pressure on every part of this volume's surface.
5. By equilibrium, the net upward force from pressure equals the weight of the fluid.
6. If we replace this fluid volume with a solid body of identical shape, the pressure distribution remains unchanged (the fluid doesn't "know" if it's pressing on fluid or solid).
7. Therefore, the buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced.

## 2.2 Theory of Metacentre — Step-by-Step Logic

When a floating body tilts by a small angle $\theta$ about a horizontal axis:

### **Step 1: Shift in Center of Buoyancy**
The shape of the displaced fluid changes. The new center of buoyancy moves from $B$ to $B_1$.

### **Step 2: New Line of Action**
The buoyant force now acts vertically upward through $B_1$.

### **Step 3: Intersection Point**
The vertical line through $B_1$ (new line of action of $F_B$) intersects the original vertical axis through $B$ at point **M**.

### **Step 4: Stability Coupling**
The metacentre M, the new center of buoyancy $B_1$, and the center of gravity G together determine the **restoring moment** (or **capsizing moment**) acting on the tilted body.

## 2.3 Conditions of Equilibrium

The stability of a floating body is determined entirely by the relative position of **G**, **B**, and **M**:

| Condition | Relationship | Physical Behavior |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Stable Equilibrium** | $GM > 0$ (M above G) | Body returns to original position after tilting |
| **Unstable Equilibrium** | $GM < 0$ (M below G) | Body capsizes (tips over completely) |
| **Neutral Equilibrium** | $GM = 0$ (M coincides with G) | Body remains in tilted position |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:**
> Examiners frequently test the **three conditions of equilibrium** with a simple diagram showing the positions of G, B, and M. Always draw the diagram and label all three points for full marks.

## 2.4 Oscillation Period of a Floating Body

For a floating body undergoing small angular oscillations, the time period is given by:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$$

Where $k$ is the **radius of gyration** of the body about its longitudinal axis through G.

## 2.5 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Archimedes' Principle (Buoyant Force) | **N** |
| 2 | $W_{fluid} = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Weight of fluid displaced | **N** |
| 3 | $F_B = W_{body}$ | Condition for floating equilibrium | **N** |
| 4 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}$ | Metacentric Radius | **m** |
| 5 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Metacentric Height | **m** |
| 6 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ | Oscillation time period | **s** |
| 7 | $M_{restoring} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring moment | **N·m** |
| 8 | $\rho_{body} = \dfrac{m}{V_{total}}$ | Average density of floating body | **kg/m³** |
| 9 | $\rho_{body} < \rho_{fluid}$ | Floating condition | **kg/m³** |
| 10 | $\rho_{body} > \rho_{fluid}$ | Sinking condition | **kg/m³** |

**Where:**
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of tilting (**m⁴**)
- $V_{disp}$ = Displaced volume of fluid (**m³**)
- $W$ = Weight of the body (**N**)
- $k$ = Radius of gyration (**m**)
- $\theta$ = Angle of tilt (**radians** for small angles)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:**
> In the formula $BM = I / V_{disp}$, the $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the **waterline plane (plan area)**, NOT the volume moment of inertia. Many students confuse these two. Always use the waterline cross-section!

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

### 🚢 Naval Architecture
The metacentric height GM directly determines a ship's **roll period** — how fast it rocks side-to-side. Cargo ships typically require $GM > 0.15$ m for stability. Warships and submarines have variable ballast systems to dynamically adjust GM.

### 🛢️ Offshore Engineering
Semi-submersible oil rigs use metacentric height calculations to remain stable in extreme ocean conditions. A higher GM means a stiffer platform but uncomfortable for crew.

### 🌊 Civil Engineering
Buoyancy calculations are critical in designing **submerged pipelines**, **underground water tanks**, **caissons for bridge construction**, and **dam foundations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)

### Setup
Consider a cylindrical body of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$, fully submerged in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ with its top face at depth $h_1$ from the free surface.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Pressure on the top face (depth $h_1$):
$$P_{top} = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1$$

**Step 2:** Pressure on the bottom face (depth $h_1 + h$):
$$P_{bottom} = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 + h)$$

**Step 3:** Net upward force (Pressure difference × Area):
$$F_B = (P_{bottom} - P_{top}) \cdot A$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the pressures:
$$F_B = \left[\rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_1 + h) - \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_1\right] \cdot A$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:
$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h \cdot A$$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $h \cdot A = V_{disp}$ (displaced volume):
$$F_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

This is the **Archimedes' Principle equation**, now derived from first principles. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Metacentric Radius (BM)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Module 1.

### Setup
A floating body of weight $W$ and displaced volume $\nabla$ (or $V_{disp}$) tilts by a small angle $\theta$. The center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$.

**Geometric definitions:**
- $O$ = Center of rotation (intersection of axis of tilt with centerline)
- $OB = $ original distance from O to B
- $OB_1 = $ new distance from O to $B_1$
- $BB_1 = $ shift in center of buoyancy

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider an elementary strip of the waterline area at radius $r$ from the axis of tilt $O$.

The volume of fluid added on one side and removed on the other side creates a wedge.

**Step 2:** The elementary wedge volume $dV$ at radius $r$ and angular width $r \cdot d\phi$:

$$dV = r \cdot d\phi \cdot r\theta \cdot \frac{r}{2} = \frac{r^3 \cdot \theta \cdot d\phi}{2}$$

*Explanation:* The wedge has a triangular cross-section with height $r\theta$ at radius $r$ and width $r \cdot d\phi$, giving area $\frac{1}{2} \cdot r \cdot r\theta$, then multiplied by $r \cdot d\phi$ gives the volume.

**Step 3:** The total volume transferred from one side to the other (the "wedge" volume $V_w$):

$$V_w = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{r^3 \cdot \theta}{2} \cdot d\phi \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{\theta}{2} \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{r^3 \cdot d\phi}{2}$$

Wait — for a more rigorous derivation, consider two wedges: one added wedge and one removed wedge. By symmetry:

$$V_{wedge} = \int_{added side} \frac{1}{2} \cdot r \cdot (r\theta) \cdot r \cdot d\phi = \int \frac{r^3 \theta}{2} d\phi$$

**Step 4:** Taking moments about the axis O for the wedge on the "added" side (the volume on one side has a first moment about O):

$$V_{wedge} \cdot \overline{r} = \int_{added} \frac{r^3 \theta}{2} \cdot r \cdot d\phi = \frac{\theta}{2} \int_{added} r^3 \cdot d\phi$$

Wait, more rigorously, the moment of the wedge volume about O is:

$$\text{Moment} = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{r^3 \theta}{2} \cdot r \cdot d\phi = \frac{\theta}{2} \int_0^{2\pi} r^4 \cdot d\phi$$

But we only integrate over the "wedge" region (half the plane, since the other side is being removed).

Actually, the standard derivation uses the fact that by symmetry, the removed wedge has equal and opposite moment. The shift $BB_1$ is given by:

$$BB_1 = \frac{\text{Moment of added wedge about O} - \text{Moment of removed wedge}}{V_{disp}}$$

**Step 5:** Using the fact that the second moment of area of the waterline plane about O is:
$$I = \int_A r^2 \, dA = \int_0^{2\pi} r^2 \cdot r \, d\phi \, dr$$

For the wedge analysis, the relevant integral becomes:

$$I = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

**Step 6:** The shift in the center of buoyancy due to tilting:

By taking moments about the axis O of the wedge volumes, and using the principle of virtual work / buoyancy balance:

$$BB_1 = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{disp}}$$

*Explanation:* The horizontal shift of B is proportional to the second moment of area of the waterline plane and the tilt angle, inversely proportional to the displaced volume.

**Step 7:** Now, from the geometry of the metacentre:

In triangle $OBB_1$ (or more precisely, triangle formed by the vertical through original B and vertical through $B_1$, intersected at M):

$$\tan\theta = \frac{BB_1}{BM}$$

For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$:

$$BM = \frac{BB_1}{\theta}$$

**Step 8:** Substituting the expression for $BB_1$:

$$BM = \frac{I \cdot \theta / V_{disp}}{\theta}$$

**Step 9:** Cancel $\theta$:

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}}}$$

This is the **Metacentric Radius formula** — fully derived. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem Statement
A rectangular pontoon of dimensions $6 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}$ floats in seawater ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$) with a draft of $1.5 \text{ m}$. Calculate:
1. The metacentric radius BM
2. The metacentric height GM, given BG = 0.4 m
3. Determine the stability condition

### Given Data
- Length $L = 6 \text{ m}$
- Breadth $B = 4 \text{ m}$
- Draft $d = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Fluid density $\rho_f = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $BG = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Solution

**Part (i): Metacentric Radius BM**

Displaced volume:
$$V_{disp} = L \times B \times d = 6 \times 4 \times 1.5 = 36 \text{ m}^3$$

Second moment of area of waterline plane (rectangle about longitudinal axis):
$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 64}{12} = 32 \text{ m}^4$$

Therefore:
$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} = \frac{32}{36} = 0.889 \text{ m}$$

**Part (ii): Metacentric Height GM**

$$GM = BM - BG = 0.889 - 0.4 = 0.489 \text{ m}$$

**Part (iii): Stability Condition**

Since $GM = +0.489 \text{ m} > 0$, the body is in **STABLE EQUILIBRIUM**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state units explicitly. State "Stable Equilibrium" or "Unstable Equilibrium" as the final conclusion. Examiners award the final mark for this statement.

### Bonus: Restoring Moment Calculation

If the pontoon tilts by $\theta = 5°$:

$$M_{restoring} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

Weight of pontoon:
$$W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 36 = 362{,}001 \text{ N}$$

Restoring moment:
$$M_{restoring} = 362{,}001 \times 0.489 \times \sin(5°)$$
$$M_{restoring} = 362{,}001 \times 0.489 \times 0.0872$$
$$M_{restoring} = 15{,}435.6 \text{ N·m}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability Conditions at a Glance

| Position of M relative to G | GM Value | Stability | Restoring Couple |
|----------------------------|----------|-----------|------------------|
| M is **above** G | $GM > 0$ | Stable | $W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (acts to restore) |
| M is **below** G | $GM < 0$ | Unstable | Capsizes |
| M **coincides** with G | $GM = 0$ | Neutral | Zero couple — no rotation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Buoyancy → Metacentre → Stability

This Mermaid diagram shows the conceptual hierarchy and logical flow of how buoyancy leads to the concept of the metacentre and stability analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Submersion] --> B[Pressure Difference Exists]
    B --> C[Net Upward Force Generated]
    C --> D[Archimedes Principle FB = rho g V]
    D --> E[Center of Buoyancy B Identified]
    E --> F[Body Tilts by angle theta]
    F --> G[Buoyancy Force Shifts to B1]
    G --> H[New Line of Action Through B1]
    H --> I[Intersection with Original Axis = Metacentre M]
    I --> J[Distance GM = BM minus BG]
    J --> K{GM Positive Negative or Zero}
    K -->|GM greater than 0| L[STABLE EQUILIBRIUM]
    K -->|GM less than 0| M[UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM]
    K -->|GM equals 0| N[NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM]
    L --> O[Restoring Moment W times GM times sin theta]
    M --> P[Capsizing Tendency]
    N --> Q[No Restoring or Capsizing]
```

## 4.3 Force and Moment Diagram on a Tilted Floating Body

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Vertical Axis through G and B] --> B[Body Tilts by small angle theta]
    B --> C[New Center of Buoyancy B1 appears]

    D --> E[Weight W acts downward through G]
    E --> F[Vertical line through B1 intersects original axis at M]
```

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🎯 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State and explain Archimedes' Principle. Derive the expression for the buoyant force experienced by a body submerged in a fluid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):**
Archimedes' Principle states that when a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid at rest, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Consider a cylindrical body of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ submerged in a fluid of density $\rho_f$. Let $h_1$ be the depth of the top face from the free surface.

Pressure on top face: $P_{top} = \rho_f g h_1$
Pressure on bottom face: $P_{bottom} = \rho_f g (h_1 + h)$
Net upward force (Buoyant Force):
$$F_B = (P_{bottom} - P_{top}) \cdot A = \rho_f g h \cdot A = \rho_f g V_{disp}$$

Thus, buoyant force equals the weight of fluid displaced. ✓

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Define the terms: (i) Center of Buoyancy (ii) Metacentre (iii) Metacentric Height. State the condition for stable equilibrium of a floating body.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Center of Buoyancy (1 mark):**
The Center of Buoyancy (B) is the centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the submerged body. It is the point through which the buoyant force acts vertically upward.

**(ii) Metacentre (1 mark):**
The Metacentre (M) is the point at which the line of action of the buoyant force (when the body is tilted) intersects the original vertical axis (centerline) of the body.

**(iii) Condition for Stable Equilibrium (1 mark):**
A floating body is in stable equilibrium when the metacentre M lies **above** the center of gravity G, i.e., $GM > 0$ (Metacentric height is positive).

---

## 🎯 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]

**(a)** A solid cylinder of diameter $0.5 \text{ m}$ and height $1.2 \text{ m}$ is fully immersed in water ($\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Calculate: (i) the buoyant force acting on the cylinder, and (ii) the apparent weight of the cylinder if its actual mass is $180 \text{ kg}$. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the metacentric radius (BM) of a floating body. Explain with a neat sketch how the metacentre is located when the body tilts by a small angle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$ → Radius $r = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Height $H = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Mass $m = 180 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (i): Buoyant Force (3 marks)**

Volume of cylinder:
$$V = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times (0.25)^2 \times 1.2$$
$$V = \pi \times 0.0625 \times 1.2 = 0.2356 \text{ m}^3$$

Buoyant force:
$$F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V$$
$$F_B = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.2356$$
$$\boxed{F_B = 2311.4 \text{ N}}$$

**Part (ii): Apparent Weight (4 marks)**

Actual weight of cylinder:
$$W_{actual} = m \cdot g = 180 \times 9.81 = 1765.8 \text{ N}$$

Apparent weight (subtracting buoyant force):
$$W_{apparent} = W_{actual} - F_B$$
$$W_{apparent} = 1765.8 - 2311.4$$
$$\boxed{W_{apparent} = -545.6 \text{ N}}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative apparent weight indicates that the cylinder **floats** (the buoyant force exceeds the actual weight). The cylinder would need to be partially immersed to be in equilibrium. ✓

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Derivation of BM (5 marks):**

When a floating body tilts by a small angle $\theta$, the center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B_1$.

Consider the elementary wedge of fluid added on one side and removed on the other side. For an elementary strip at radius $r$ from the axis of rotation:

Wedge volume:
$$dV = \frac{1}{2} \cdot r \cdot (r\theta) \cdot r \, d\phi = \frac{r^3 \theta}{2} d\phi$$

Taking moments about the axis of tilt for the entire wedge:
$$\text{Moment} = \int \frac{r^3 \theta}{2} \cdot r \, d\phi = \frac{\theta}{2} \int r^3 d\phi$$

After integration over the waterline area, the shift in center of buoyancy is:
$$BB_1 = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{disp}}$$

Where $I = \int r^2 dA$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane.

From geometry:
$$\tan\theta = \frac{BB_1}{BM}$$

For small angles:
$$BM = \frac{BB_1}{\theta} = \frac{I \cdot \theta / V_{disp}}{\theta}$$

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}}}$$

**Sketch Description (2 marks):**

The sketch should show:
- The original vertical centerline through G and B
- The body tilted at angle $\theta$
- New center of buoyancy $B_1$
- The vertical line through $B_1$ intersecting the original centerline at M
- Labels for G, B, $B_1$, M, BG, BM, GM, and $\theta$

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Modified]

**(a)** A rectangular pontoon has dimensions $8 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$ and weighs $350 \text{ kN}$. It floats in seawater of density $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate: (i) the draft of the pontoon, (ii) the depth of the center of buoyancy below the water surface, and (iii) the metacentric height if BG = 0.6 m. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the three conditions of equilibrium of a floating body with neat diagrams. Discuss the significance of metacentric height in ship design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Given:**
- Length $L = 8 \text{ m}$, Breadth $B = 4 \text{ m}$, Height $H = 3 \text{ m}$
- Weight $W = 350 \text{ kN} = 350{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $BG = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (i): Draft Calculation (3 marks)**

For floating equilibrium: Buoyant force = Weight
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} = W$$

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot d) = W$$

$$1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 4 \times d = 350{,}000$$

$$321{,}672 \cdot d = 350{,}000$$

$$\boxed{d = 1.088 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (ii): Depth of Center of Buoyancy (2 marks)**

For a rectangular pontoon, the center of buoyancy lies at the centroid of the submerged volume:
$$\overline{h}_B = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{1.088}{2}$$
$$\boxed{\overline{h}_B = 0.544 \text{ m below water surface}}$$

**Part (iii): Metacentric Height (2 marks)**

Displaced volume:
$$V_{disp} = 8 \times 4 \times 1.088 = 34.82 \text{ m}^3$$

Second moment of waterline area:
$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 64}{12} = 42.67 \text{ m}^4$$

Metacentric radius:
$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} = \frac{42.67}{34.82} = 1.225 \text{ m}$$

Metacentric height:
$$GM = BM - BG = 1.225 - 0.6$$
$$\boxed{GM = 0.625 \text{ m}}$$

Since $GM > 0$, the pontoon is in **stable equilibrium**. ✓

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Three Conditions of Equilibrium (4 marks):**

**Case 1: Stable Equilibrium (GM > 0)**
When the metacentre M is located above the center of gravity G, the buoyant force and weight form a couple that tends to restore the body to its original upright position.

**Case 2: Unstable Equilibrium (GM < 0)**
When M is below G, the couple formed by the buoyant force and weight tends to tilt the body further, causing it to capsize.

**Case 3: Neutral Equilibrium (GM = 0)**
When M coincides exactly with G, there is no restoring or capsizing couple. The body remains at whatever angle it is tilted to.

**Diagrams (Required for full marks):**
- Show three separate sketches
- Mark positions of G, B, M in each
- Indicate direction of buoyant force and weight
- Show direction of the resulting couple

**Significance in Ship Design (3 marks):**

The metacentric height GM is **critical** in ship design for the following reasons:

1. **Stability Against Capsizing:** A positive GM ensures the ship returns to upright after a tilt caused by waves. Warships and passenger vessels require positive GM.

2. **Roll Period:** The rolling period of a ship is given by $T = 2\pi\sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$. A larger GM means faster (stiffer) rolling, which can be uncomfortable for passengers. Cargo ships prefer moderate GM.

3. **Cargo Loading:** As cargo is loaded, G shifts upward, reducing GM. Naval architects must balance stability with operational comfort.

4. **Free Surface Effect:** Partially filled liquid cargo tanks (like fuel or ballast) effectively lower G, affecting metacentric height.

5. **Recommended Values:** Cargo ships typically have $GM = 0.15$ to $0.50 \text{ m}$. Warships have higher GM for combat stability.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (2–3 marks lost):** Students often forget to convert kN to N or cm to m. Always use **SI base units** throughout the calculation.
>
> 2. **Wrong I Formula (Up to 4 marks lost):** For a rectangle, $I = LB^3/12$ where $B$ is the dimension **perpendicular** to the axis of tilting. If tilting about the longitudinal axis, $B$ = breadth. Don't confuse length and breadth.
>
> 3. **Misinterpreted GM Sign (3 marks lost):** Always explicitly state whether GM is positive, negative, or zero, AND what it means for stability. Examiners want both the number AND the conclusion.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Sketch (2 marks lost):** In metacentre derivation questions, the **neat sketch is mandatory**. Without it, expect to lose 1–2 marks even if the derivation is perfect.
>
> 5. **Confusing BG with GB:** Some textbooks use $\overline{GB}$ instead of $\overline{BG}$. The KTU convention is $GM = BM - BG$ where BG is the **distance** (always positive).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **Buoyant Force** = $\rho_f g V_{disp}$ (Archimedes' Principle)
- **Center of Buoyancy (B)** = Centroid of displaced fluid volume
- **Metacentre (M)** = Intersection point of new and old buoyancy force lines of action
- **Metacentric Height (GM)** = BM − BG
- **Metacentric Radius (BM)** = $I / V_{disp}$

### 🎯 Stability Rules to Memorize
- **$GM > 0$** → Stable Equilibrium (M above G)
- **$GM < 0$** → Unstable Equilibrium (M below G)
- **$GM = 0$** → Neutral Equilibrium (M coincides with G)
- **Restoring Moment** = $W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$
- **Oscillation Period** = $T = 2\pi\sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$

### ⚡ Rapid-Fire Facts for KTU Exam
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $\rho_{mercury} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (use 9.81 unless specified otherwise)
- For a rectangle tilting about longitudinal axis: $I = LB^3/12$
- For a rectangle tilting about transverse axis: $I = BL^3/12$
- For a circle of radius R: $I = \pi R^4 / 4$

### 🧠 Common Engineering Applications
- Ship stability (positive GM mandatory)
- Submarine ballast systems
- Floating dry docks
- Pontoon bridges
- Offshore oil platforms

### 🚨 Top Pitfalls to Avoid
1. Never forget to subtract $F_B$ from weight for apparent weight
2. Always check the direction of tilt before choosing the I formula
3. Always draw the diagram with proper labels (G, B, M, $\theta$, $BB_1$, $BM$)
4. Mention the stability condition explicitly at the end of every problem
5. Watch for the **free surface effect** in tanks — it effectively reduces GM

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stability of Immersed and Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Stability of a Floating or Immersed Body** refers to the ability of the body to return to its original equilibrium position (or to a new equilibrium position) after being slightly disturbed by an external force or moment, such as wind, wave action, or a small tilt.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, three types of equilibrium are recognized for floating/immersed bodies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Types (KTU 2024 Module 1 Core Definition)**
> 1. **Stable Equilibrium** — The body, on being displaced, returns to its original position. The restoring moment acts in the direction opposite to the disturbance. (Example: A ball resting inside a bowl.)
> 2. **Unstable Equilibrium** — The body, on being displaced, moves further away from its original position. The overturning moment aids the disturbance. (Example: A ball balanced on top of a hill.)
> 3. **Neutral Equilibrium** — The body, on being displaced, remains in the new position without any restoring or overturning moment. (Example: A ball on a perfectly flat surface.)

## 1.2 Key Technical Terms

- **Centre of Gravity (G):** The point through which the weight of the body is considered to act vertically downward.
- **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** The centroid of the displaced fluid volume, through which the buoyant force acts vertically upward.
- **Metacentre (M):** The point about which a floating body, when slightly tilted, oscillates. It is the intersection of the vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy and the original vertical axis of the body.
- **Metacentric Height (GM):** The distance between the centre of gravity (G) and the metacentre (M). This is the single most important parameter that determines stability.
- **Righting Couple / Righting Moment:** The couple produced by the weight of the body acting downward through G and the buoyant force acting upward through B' (new centre of buoyancy), which tends to restore the body to its original position.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bowl, Hill, and Floor" Picture

Imagine three identical balls in three scenarios:

| Scenario | Real-Life Analogy | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Stable** | Ball inside a bowl — it always rolls back to the bottom. | A ship floating upright in calm water. |
| **Unstable** | Ball balanced on the peak of a hill — any push sends it down. | A fully submerged body whose centre of gravity lies *above* its centre of buoyancy. |
| **Neutral** | Ball on a perfectly flat glass floor — it can rest anywhere. | A uniformly dense sphere fully submerged (theoretically neutral for pure rotation). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water (standard reference): **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **γ = 9810 N/m³**

## 1.4 Stability of a Fully Submerged Body (Quick Concept)

A fully submerged body is in **stable equilibrium** if its **centre of gravity (G) lies *below* the centre of buoyancy (B)**. If G lies above B, it is in **unstable equilibrium**. If G and B coincide, it is in **neutral equilibrium**.

This is the direct analogue of the "ball in a bowl" picture — the heavier point (G) acts like a pendulum that prefers to hang lowest.

## 1.5 Stability of a Floating Body (Quick Concept)

For a floating body, stability is a more complex problem because the shape of the submerged portion (and hence the position of B) changes when the body tilts. The metacentre (M) is therefore introduced, and the criterion becomes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion for Floating Bodies:**
> - **Stable:** Metacentre M lies *above* Centre of Gravity G  →  **GM > 0**
> - **Unstable:** Metacentre M lies *below* Centre of Gravity G  →  **GM < 0**
> - **Neutral:** Metacentre M coincides with Centre of Gravity G  →  **GM = 0**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stability of a Fully Submerged Body — Step-by-Step Logic

Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape, suspended in a static fluid.

**Step 1 — Initial Configuration:**
- Weight **W** acts vertically downward through G.
- Buoyant force **F_B = ρ · g · V** acts vertically upward through B (centroid of the displaced volume).

**Step 2 — Condition for Equilibrium:**
- For translational equilibrium: W = F_B  →  ρ_body · V · g = ρ_fluid · V · g  →  ρ_body = ρ_fluid.
- For rotational equilibrium: The line of action of W and F_B must coincide, i.e., G and B must lie on the same vertical line.

**Step 3 — Tilt the Body by a Small Angle θ:**
- For a fully submerged body, the shape of the displaced volume does **not change** with rotation.
- Therefore, the position of B remains fixed *relative to the body*.
- However, the *relative positions* of G and B (as seen from the vertical) change.

**Step 4 — Moment Analysis:**
- If **G is below B**: The weight W and buoyant force F_B form a **restoring couple** that brings the body back to its original position. → **Stable.**
- If **G is above B**: The couple formed is an **overturning couple** that pushes the body further away. → **Unstable.**
- If **G coincides with B**: No couple is formed regardless of θ. → **Neutral.**

## 2.2 Stability of a Floating Body — Detailed Analysis

For a floating body, the situation is fundamentally different because the **submerged shape changes** when the body tilts.

**Step 1 — Floating Equilibrium (Un-tilted State):**
- The body floats with submerged volume **V**, displacing weight W = ρ · g · V.
- B is the centroid of this submerged wedge, located on the vertical centreline.
- G is on the same vertical line as B.

**Step 2 — Tilt by a Small Angle θ:**
- One side dips deeper; the other side rises. A wedge of volume **ΔV** is submerged on one side, and an equal wedge of volume ΔV emerges on the other side.
- The new centre of buoyancy B' is the centroid of the *new* submerged volume.
- B' is shifted *horizontally* from B by a small distance, and lies to the side of the original B.

**Step 3 — Locating the Metacentre:**
- Draw a vertical line upward from B'.
- This line intersects the original vertical centreline of the body at a point called the **Metacentre (M)**.
- For small angles of tilt, M remains essentially fixed in position.

**Step 4 — Righting Couple Formation:**
- Weight W acts downward through G (which has not moved relative to the body).
- Buoyant force F_B acts upward through B'.
- The horizontal offset between G and B' produces a **couple** with moment arm = horizontal distance.
- The **Righting Moment (R.M.) = W × (Horizontal offset between G and B') = W × GM × sin θ**.
- For small θ: sin θ ≈ θ (in radians)  →  **R.M. ≈ W × GM × θ**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Code)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | $BM$ = distance B to M; $I$ = 2nd moment of area of waterline plane about axis of tilt; $V$ = submerged volume | m, m⁴, m³ | Floating body (small tilt) |
| 2 | $GM = BM - BG$ (if G above B) | $GM$ = metacentric height; $BG$ = vertical distance B to G | m | Floating body |
| 3 | $GM = BM + BG$ (if G below B) | Sign convention: B to G upward is positive | m | Floating body (rare) |
| 4 | $\overline{GM} = \dfrac{I}{V} - \overline{BG}$ | Bar indicates signed distances; $B$ is reference | m | General floating body |
| 5 | $R.M. = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Righting moment; $W$ = weight; $\theta$ = tilt angle | N·m | Floating body, any θ |
| 6 | $R.M. = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$ (small θ) | Approximation using $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ | N·m | Small tilt only |
| 7 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}$ | Time period of oscillation; $k$ = radius of gyration of body about axis of tilt | s | Floating body oscillation |
| 8 | $\text{Stability} \iff GM > 0$ | Metacentre above G | dimensionless check | Stability criterion |
| 9 | $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | Buoyant force = weight of displaced fluid | N | Archimedes' principle |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ (rectangle) | 2nd moment of waterline area, axis along L | m⁴ | Rectangular pontoon/float |
| 11 | $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ (circle) | 2nd moment of waterline area, circular section | m⁴ | Circular float |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of the Metacentre Concept:**
> - Naval architects use GM to design ships, submarines, and offshore platforms.
> - A high GM → "stiff" ship (returns quickly, may roll uncomfortably).
> - A low GM → "tender" ship (rolls slowly and widely, but cargo may shift).
> - Negative GM → capsize risk.
> - The IMO and classification societies (e.g., Lloyd's Register) prescribe minimum GM values for commercial vessels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Radius: $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$

This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 1 for floating body stability. Every numerical problem on floating stability uses this formula.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

- A floating body of arbitrary waterline shape is tilted through a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis passing through the centre of flotation (the centroid of the waterline area).
- The original centre of buoyancy is $B$, the new centre of buoyancy is $B'$, and $M$ is the metacentre.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Analysis

When the body tilts, consider an elementary strip of the waterline plane at horizontal distance $x$ from the axis of tilt, of width $dx$ and length $L$ (perpendicular to the tilt).

- **Volume submerged on one side** (wedge $W_1$): $\delta V_1 = x \cdot \theta \cdot L \cdot dx$ (geometrically, this is the volume of the wedge on the dipped side).
- **Volume emerged on the other side** (wedge $W_2$): $\delta V_2 = x \cdot \theta \cdot L \cdot dx$ (equal in magnitude by geometry, on the other side at distance $x$).

### 3.1.3 Shift of Centre of Buoyancy

The horizontal shift of the centre of buoyancy from $B$ to $B'$ is given by the principle of moments:

$$B B' = \dfrac{\text{Moment of wedge volumes about original vertical axis}}{\text{Total submerged volume}}$$

The moment of the elementary wedge about the axis of tilt is:

$$\delta M_{wedge} = (x \cdot \theta \cdot L \cdot dx) \cdot x = \theta \cdot L \cdot x^2 \cdot dx$$

Total moment of all such wedge elements, integrated across the waterline plane:

$$\text{Total Moment} = \theta \cdot L \int x^2 \, dx = \theta \cdot L \cdot I_{xx}$$

But recall that the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of tilt is:

$$I = L \int x^2 \, dx$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Total Moment} = \theta \cdot I$$

### 3.1.4 Final Derivation of $BM$

The horizontal shift $BB'$ is:

$$B B' = \dfrac{\theta \cdot I}{V}$$

From the geometry of the metacentre (intersection of vertical through $B'$ with the original axis), the triangle $B B' M$ is a right triangle with the angle at $B$ equal to $\theta$, and $BM$ is the hypotenuse along the body axis (which, for small angles, is essentially vertical).

For small $\theta$:

$$\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = \dfrac{B B'}{B M}$$

Solving for $BM$:

$$B M = \dfrac{B B'}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{B B'}{\theta} = \dfrac{I}{V}$$

$$\boxed{\,B M = \dfrac{I}{V}\,}$$

This is the **Metacentric Radius** formula. The metacentric height is then:

$$G M = B M - B G = \dfrac{I}{V} - B G$$

If $G$ lies *below* $B$, then $BG$ is taken as **negative**, and:

$$G M = \dfrac{I}{V} + \vert B G \vert$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Time Period of Oscillation of a Floating Body

When a floating body is tilted and released, it oscillates about the metacentre $M$ under the action of the righting moment.

### 3.2.1 Equation of Motion

Let $\theta$ be the instantaneous angular displacement. The restoring (righting) moment is:

$$M_{restoring} = -W \cdot G M \cdot \sin\theta$$

By Newton's second law for rotation:

$$I_{body} \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot G M \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small oscillations, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:

$$I_{body} \cdot \ddot{\theta} + W \cdot G M \cdot \theta = 0$$

### 3.2.2 Standard SHM Form

This is the equation of **Simple Harmonic Motion**:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \left(\dfrac{W \cdot G M}{I_{body}}\right) \theta = 0$$

The angular frequency is:

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{W \cdot G M}{I_{body}}$$

### 3.2.3 Substitute $I_{body} = m k^2$ and $W = mg$

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{m g \cdot G M}{m k^2} = \dfrac{g \cdot G M}{k^2}$$

The time period is:

$$\boxed{\,T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot G M}}\,}$$

where $k$ is the **radius of gyration** of the body about the axis of oscillation.

## 3.3 Complete Analytical Matrix — Stability of Fully Submerged vs Floating Bodies

| Property | Fully Submerged Body | Floating Body |
|---|---|---|
| Volume of displaced fluid | $V$ = full volume of body (constant) | $V$ = submerged volume only (depends on load) |
| Position of $B$ on tilting | Fixed relative to body | **Shifts** with tilt |
| Need to define metacentre? | **No** | **Yes** — $M$ is the key |
| Stability criterion | $G$ below $B$ | $M$ above $G$  i.e., $GM > 0$ |
| Restoring moment | $W \cdot (GB) \cdot \sin\theta$ | $W \cdot (GM) \cdot \sin\theta$ |
| Formula for stability | $GB \cdot \sin\theta > 0$ (G below B) | $GM > 0$ |
| Time period formula | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k_B^2}{g \cdot GB}}$ | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ |
| Practical example | Submarine in fully dived state | Cargo ship at surface |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration — Sign Convention Walkthrough

**Problem Setup:** A rectangular pontoon of length 6 m, width 4 m, and height 2 m weighs 200 kN. Its centre of gravity is 0.8 m above the base. It floats in water. Find the metacentric height.

**Step 1:** Displaced volume at equilibrium:
$$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho g} = \dfrac{200{,}000}{9810} = 20.387 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Submerged depth $h$:
$$V = L \times B \times h \Rightarrow h = \dfrac{V}{L \cdot B} = \dfrac{20.387}{6 \times 4} = 0.849 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Position of $B$ (centroid of submerged depth) above base:
$$\overline{OB} = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{0.849}{2} = 0.4245 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Position of $G$ above base = 0.8 m. So:
$$\overline{BG} = 0.8 - 0.4245 = 0.3755 \text{ m} \quad (G \text{ above } B)$$

**Step 5:** Second moment of area of waterline plane (rectangle, tilt about longitudinal axis of length $L$):
$$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{6 \times 4^3}{12} = \dfrac{384}{12} = 32 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 6:** Metacentric radius:
$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{32}{20.387} = 1.570 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Metacentric height:
$$\boxed{\,\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG} = 1.570 - 0.3755 = 1.1945 \text{ m}\,}$$

Since $GM > 0$, the pontoon is in **stable equilibrium**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stability Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Body in Fluid] --> B{Submerged or Floating?}
    B -->|Fully Submerged| C[Compare G and B]
    B -->|Floating| D[Compute I, V, BG]
    C --> C1{G below B?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[STABLE]
    C1 -->|No| C3{G above B?}
    C3 -->|Yes| C4[UNSTABLE]
    C3 -->|No| C5[NEUTRAL]
    D --> E[Calculate BM = I / V]
    E --> F[Calculate GM = BM - BG]
    F --> G{GM greater than 0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[STABLE Floating Body]
    G -->|No| I{GM less than 0?}
    I -->|Yes| J[UNSTABLE Floating Body]
    I -->|No| K[NEUTRAL Floating Body]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Righting Couple Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Vertical Axis] --> B[Centre of Buoyancy B]
    A --> C[Centre of Gravity G]
    A --> D[Metacentre M]
    B -->|Shift on tilt| E[New Centre of Buoyancy B prime]
    E -->|Vertical upward line| D
    C -->|Weight W acts down| F[Restoring Couple]
    E -->|Buoyant Force acts up| F
    F -->|GM sin theta moment arm| G[Righting Moment]
    G -->|Tends to restore| H[Stable Oscillation]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Geometry of a Tilted Floating Body (Textual Schematic)

```
                  Original Vertical
                        |
                        |      New Vertical
                        |         |     /
                        |         |    /  θ
                        |    M    |   /
                        |    .    |  /
                        |    :    | /
                  G     |         .B'
                  .     |        /|
                  :     |       / |
                  :     |      /  |  
                  :     |     /   |  δV emerged
        __________:_____|____/____|________________  NEW WATERLINE
                  :     |   /     |
                  :     |  / δV submerged
                  :     | /       |
                  :     |/        |
                  B     |         |
                  .     |         |
                  :     |         |
        __________:_____|_________|________________  OLD WATERLINE
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The body is rotated by a small angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis.
- $M$ = Metacentre (intersection point).
- $B$ = original centre of buoyancy.
- $B'$ = new centre of buoyancy.
- $G$ = centre of gravity.
- The wedge labelled "$\delta V$ submerged" is the volume that dipped in.
- The wedge labelled "$\delta V$ emerged" is the volume that came out (equal volume by geometry).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1]

**Define the following terms with respect to floating body stability:**
(a) Centre of buoyancy
(b) Metacentre
(c) Metacentric height

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

(a) **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** The point through which the resultant buoyant force (or upthrust) is considered to act on a submerged or floating body. It is the centroid of the displaced fluid volume.

(b) **Metacentre (M):** It is the point about which a floating body, when slightly tilted, starts oscillating. Mathematically, it is the point of intersection of the vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy (when the body is tilted) and the original vertical axis of the body.

(c) **Metacentric Height (GM):** It is the distance between the centre of gravity (G) of the body and the metacentre (M). It is the key parameter that determines whether a floating body is in stable, unstable, or neutral equilibrium. The condition for stability is **GM > 0**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q2]

**A solid cylinder of relative density 0.7 is fully submerged in water with its axis vertical. Comment on its stability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Given: Relative density of cylinder $\rho_r = 0.7$ < 1.0 → the cylinder would tend to float, but it is held fully submerged.
- The weight $W$ acts through the **centre of gravity G** (geometric centre of the cylinder, since it is uniform).
- The buoyant force $F_B$ acts through the **centre of buoyancy B**, which is also the geometric centre (centroid of displaced volume = centroid of cylinder).
- Therefore, **G and B coincide** for a uniform vertical cylinder. 
- Since $G$ and $B$ are at the same point, the body is in **neutral equilibrium**. Any tilt does not produce a restoring or overturning couple because the offset $GB = 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Note:**
> Many students answer "unstable" because the body is "trying to float up." This is wrong. Stability is a question of the *couple* formed on tilting, not the vertical motion. A body in neutral vertical motion can still be neutrally stable rotationally. Marks are deducted for confusing translation and rotation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q3]

**(a)** A rectangular pontoon of length $L = 8$ m, breadth $B = 3$ m, and height $H = 1.5$ m weighs $W = 110$ kN. Its centre of gravity is at $0.6$ m from the base. Find the metacentric height when the pontoon floats in water. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the physical significance of the metacentre for a floating body. Derive the relation $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V}$ where $I$ is the second moment of the waterline area and $V$ is the displaced volume.  **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Displaced Volume [1 Mark]**

$$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{110 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} = 11.213 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Submerged Depth [1 Mark]**

$$h = \dfrac{V}{L \times B} = \dfrac{11.213}{8 \times 3} = 0.4672 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Position of Centre of Buoyancy from Base [1 Mark]**

$$\overline{OB} = \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{0.4672}{2} = 0.2336 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Distance BG [1 Mark]**

$$\overline{BG} = 0.6 - 0.2336 = 0.3664 \text{ m} \quad (G \text{ lies above } B)$$

**Step 5 — Second Moment of Waterline Area [1 Mark]**

For tilt about the longitudinal axis (length $L$), the breadth $B$ is the dimension perpendicular to the tilt:

$$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 3^3}{12} = \dfrac{216}{12} = 18 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 6 — Metacentric Radius BM [1 Mark]**

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{18}{11.213} = 1.605 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Metacentric Height GM [1 Mark]**

$$\boxed{\,\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG} = 1.605 - 0.3664 = 1.239 \text{ m}\,}$$

Since $GM > 0$, the pontoon is **stable**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Physical Significance of the Metacentre [3 Marks]:**

When a floating body is given a small angular displacement $\theta$ (tilt) about a longitudinal axis, the centre of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$ (since the submerged shape changes). The vertical line through $B'$ meets the original vertical axis of the body at a fixed point $M$ called the metacentre. The body then oscillates about $M$. The metacentric height $GM$ determines:
- Whether the body returns to its original position (stable, $GM > 0$).
- Whether it capsizes (unstable, $GM < 0$).
- Whether it stays where tilted (neutral, $GM = 0$).

**Derivation of $BM = I/V$ [4 Marks]:**

When the body tilts by small angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis through the centre of flotation:

1. **Wedge Volumes:** A small wedge of volume $\delta V_1 = x \cdot \theta \cdot L \cdot dx$ is submerged on one side, and an equal wedge emerges on the other side.

2. **Moment of Wedge:** The moment of $\delta V_1$ about the axis of tilt is $(x \cdot \theta \cdot L \cdot dx) \cdot x = \theta \cdot L \cdot x^2 \, dx$.

3. **Total Moment:** Integrating over the entire waterline plane:
$$\text{Total Moment} = \theta \cdot L \int x^2 \, dx = \theta \cdot I$$

4. **Shift of B:** The horizontal shift of the centre of buoyancy:
$$B B' = \dfrac{\theta \cdot I}{V}$$

5. **Geometry of Metacentre:** In the right triangle $B B' M$, for small $\theta$, $\tan\theta \approx \theta = BB'/BM$. Substituting:
$$\theta = \dfrac{\theta \cdot I / V}{B M} \Rightarrow B M = \dfrac{I}{V}$$

$$\boxed{\,\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V}\,}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q4]

**(a)** Define the three types of equilibrium for a floating body. A solid cylinder of diameter 1.0 m and height 1.5 m is floating in water with its axis vertical. The specific gravity of the cylinder material is 0.6. Determine: (i) the depth of immersion, (ii) whether the equilibrium is stable, unstable, or neutral.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What do you understand by the time period of oscillation of a floating body? Derive the expression for the time period of a floating body undergoing small angular oscillations.  **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Three Types of Equilibrium [3 Marks]:**

1. **Stable Equilibrium:** The body returns to its original position after a small disturbance (positive righting moment exists). The metacentre M is *above* the centre of gravity G ($GM > 0$).
2. **Unstable Equilibrium:** The body moves further away from its original position after disturbance. M is *below* G ($GM < 0$).
3. **Neutral Equilibrium:** The body remains in any displaced position with no couple. M *coincides* with G ($GM = 0$).

**Numerical Solution [4 Marks]:**

Volume of cylinder:
$$V_{cyl} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 1^2 \cdot 1.5 = 1.178 \text{ m}^3$$

Submerged volume at equilibrium (weight = buoyant force):
$$V_{sub} = S \cdot V_{cyl} = 0.6 \times 1.178 = 0.707 \text{ m}^3$$

Submerged depth:
$$h = \dfrac{V_{sub}}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2} = \dfrac{0.707}{0.7854} = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

Position of $B$ from base = $h/2 = 0.45$ m.
Position of $G$ from base = $H/2 = 0.75$ m (since cylinder is uniform).

$$\overline{BG} = 0.75 - 0.45 = 0.30 \text{ m} \quad (G \text{ above } B)$$

For a circular waterline:
$$I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1^4}{64} = 0.0491 \text{ m}^4$$

$$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} = \dfrac{0.0491}{0.707} = 0.0694 \text{ m}$$

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG} = 0.0694 - 0.30 = -0.2306 \text{ m}$$

Since $GM < 0$, the equilibrium is **unstable**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Definition [2 Marks]:**
The time period of oscillation $T$ of a floating body is the time taken by the body to complete one full oscillation (roll or pitch) about the metacentre when slightly displaced from its equilibrium position and released.

**Derivation [5 Marks]:**

When the body is tilted by an angle $\theta$, the righting moment is:
$$M_R = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small $\theta$, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:
$$M_R = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

Equation of motion (Newton's rotational law):
$$I_{body} \cdot \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \theta$$

With $I_{body} = m k^2$ and $W = mg$:
$$m k^2 \ddot{\theta} + m g \overline{GM} \cdot \theta = 0$$

$$\ddot{\theta} + \left(\dfrac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}\right) \theta = 0$$

This is the standard SHM equation with angular frequency:
$$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot \overline{GM}}{k^2}}$$

The time period is:
$$\boxed{\,T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot \overline{GM}}}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $GM$:** Students often write $GM = BM + BG$ even when G is above B. The correct formula when G is *above* B is $GM = BM - BG$. If G is below B, then $GM = BM + BG$. (1 to 2 marks lost.)
> 2. **Wrong second moment of area:** For a rectangle tilting about its long axis, use $I = LB^3/12$ (breadth cubed). For tilt about the short axis, $I = BL^3/12$ (length cubed). Confusion of these costs full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units of $\theta$:** In $R.M. = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$, $\theta$ must be in **radians** (not degrees) when using the small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.
> 4. **Mixing up $I$ (waterline area) and $I$ (mass moment of inertia):** The $I$ in $BM = I/V$ is the **second moment of area of the waterline plane**, *not* the mass moment of inertia. Mass moment of inertia appears only in the time period formula.
> 5. **Stability criterion confusion:** Writing "stable if $BM > BG$" without explicitly computing and showing $GM > 0$ is incomplete. Always show the numerical $GM$ value and conclude with the sign.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Archimedes' Principle** is the foundation: $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$.
- **Centre of Gravity (G):** Weight acts here, downward. Position depends on mass distribution.
- **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** Buoyant force acts here, upward. For a *fully submerged* body, B is the centroid of the body. For a *floating* body, B is the centroid of the submerged volume.
- **Metacentre (M):** Found only for floating bodies. The point where the body oscillates upon a small tilt.
- **Metacentric Radius:** $BM = I/V$ — derivable only for small angles of tilt.
- **Metacentric Height:** $GM = BM - BG$ if G is above B; $GM = BM + BG$ if G is below B.
- **Stability of Submerged Body:** Stable if G is below B. (No metacentre needed.)
- **Stability of Floating Body:** Stable if $GM > 0$ (M is above G). Unstable if $GM < 0$. Neutral if $GM = 0$.
- **Righting Moment:** $R.M. = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$. For small $\theta$, $R.M. \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ (θ in radians).
- **Time Period of Oscillation:** $T = 2\pi \sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$, where $k$ is the radius of gyration of the body about the axis of oscillation.
- **Typical I formulas:** Rectangle → $LB^3/12$ (tilt about L); Circle → $\pi D^4/64$.
- **Submerged Depth for Floating Body:** $h = W / (\rho g \cdot A_{plan})$, where $A_{plan}$ is the plan area of the body at the waterline.
- **Always state the sign of GM** in your final answer and explicitly comment on whether the body is stable, unstable, or neutrally stable.
- **Unit Check:** $I$ is in m⁴, $V$ in m³ → $BM$ in m ✓. $W$ in N, $GM$ in m, $\theta$ in rad → $R.M.$ in N·m ✓.
- **Common trick in KTU questions:** "A body is given a small angular displacement and released. Find the time period." This requires $T = 2\pi \sqrt{k^2/(g \cdot GM)}$, not just the stability condition.
- **Real-world numbers:** Ship GM is usually 0.05 to 0.5 m (small positive value for comfortable rolling). Pontoons can have GM > 1 m (stiff).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Description of Fluid Motion - Types of Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Description of Fluid Motion - Types of Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Description of Fluid Motion — The Two Fundamental Approaches

In continuum mechanics, the motion of a fluid can be described mathematically using **one of two complementary reference frames**. These are known as the **Lagrangian Description** and the **Eulerian Description**, named after the celebrated mathematicians **Joseph-Louis Lagrange** and **Leonhard Euler**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to clearly distinguish between Lagrangian and Eulerian approaches, identify flow types, and recognize streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines as visual representations of fluid motion.

### 1.1.1 Lagrangian Description (Particle-Following Approach)

The **Lagrangian description** traces the journey of *individual fluid particles* through space and time. Each particle is identified at the initial instant, and its position, velocity, and acceleration are recorded as functions of time.

Mathematically, the position vector of a fluid particle is expressed as:

$$\vec{r} = \vec{r}\left(\vec{a}, t\right)$$

where $\vec{a}$ is the **label vector** that uniquely identifies the particle (typically its initial position at time $t = t_0$), and $t$ is the time.

### 1.1.2 Eulerian Description (Field-Based Approach)

The **Eulerian description** observes fluid motion at *fixed points in space* (control points) and records how flow properties (velocity, pressure, density) change at those points with time. This is the approach used almost universally in engineering practice because the field variables become **functions of position and time**:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}\left(\vec{x}, t\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Distinction:** The Lagrangian approach asks *"Where did this particle go?"* while the Eulerian approach asks *"What is the velocity at this fixed point right now?"*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The River and the Bridge

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - **Lagrangian View** → Imagine throwing a **leaf into a river** and tracking it with a drone. You are following a *specific particle* as it drifts downstream, swirling around rocks, and eventually leaves your view. The leaf is your "labeled particle," and you record its trajectory.
> - **Eulerian View** → Now stand on a **fixed bridge over the river** with a current meter. You measure the velocity of water passing *beneath you* at a particular location. You don't care *which* water molecules are passing; you only measure the *field property* at that fixed station.
>
> Engineers almost always use the **Eulerian approach** because it is impractical to tag and track billions of molecules, but easy to install sensors at strategic locations (think wind tunnels, weather stations, or pipe flow meters).

## 1.3 Types of Flows — The Classification Framework

Fluid flows can be classified along **multiple independent axes** based on different physical characteristics. A complete description of a flow situation usually combines several of these classifications.

| # | Classification Axis | Two Opposite Categories |
|---|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Time dependence | Steady vs Unsteady |
| 2 | Space variation | Uniform vs Non-uniform |
| 3 | Flow regime | Laminar vs Turbulent |
| 4 | Density behavior | Compressible vs Incompressible |
| 5 | Vorticity | Rotational vs Irrotational |
| 6 | Dimensionality | 1-D, 2-D, or 3-D flow |
| 7 | Geometry | Internal vs External flow |

> [!NOTE]
> The same physical flow can simultaneously be, for example, **steady, uniform, laminar, incompressible, and 2-D**. These are not mutually exclusive labels — they are independent attributes.
