- ⭐ **Path Line:** Trajectory of a **single particle** over time → use $t$ as parameter.
- ⭐ **Streak Line:** Locus of **all particles that passed through a fixed point** → use past time $\tau$ as parameter.
- ⭐ **Key Equation (streamline):** $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$
- ⭐ **Key Equation (path line):** $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = u(x,y,z,t)$
- ⭐ **Key Equation (streak line):** Parametric in $\tau$ with fixed $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ and current time $t$.
- ⭐ **Coincidence condition:** In **steady flow** ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$), all three lines are identical.
- ⭐ **Non-coincidence condition:** In **unsteady flow**, streamlines, path lines, and streak lines generally differ.
- ⭐ **Special case (counter-intuitive):** Even in some unsteady flows, streamline and path line may coincide if velocity *direction* is time-independent (only magnitude scales). Verify by direct substitution.
- ⭐ **Streak line range:** Always specify the **range of $\tau$** in your answer (e.g., $0 \leq \tau \leq 1$).
- ⭐ **Common error:** Don't confuse the *form* $dy/dx = v/u$ (streamline) with the *integration* $dy/dx = v/u$ using $t$ as the running variable (path line).
- ⭐ **KTU board tip:** Always include the words *"snapshot of the flow"* and *"time-history"* in 3-mark answers to score full marks.
- ⭐ **Visualization:** Streamlines = field lines; Path lines = individual particle trails; Streak lines = dye injection ribbons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Function and Velocity Potential Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stream Function and Velocity Potential Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stream Function (ψ) — The "Flow Map" of a Fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **stream function (ψ)** is a scalar function of position and time in a two-dimensional flow field such that the partial derivative of ψ with respect to any direction gives the velocity component perpendicular to that direction (with appropriate sign convention).

Mathematically, for a 2D flow in the $x\text{-}y$ plane:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Lines of constant ψ (called **streamlines**) are everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity vector. ψ itself is constant along a streamline, meaning the difference ψ₂ − ψ₁ between two streamlines physically represents the **volumetric flow rate (per unit depth)** crossing between them.

**A constant of the stream function $\psi$ defines a streamline.**

## 1.2 Velocity Potential Function (φ) — The "Pressure Map" of a Fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **velocity potential function (φ)** is a scalar function of position and time in a flow field such that the negative gradient of φ at any point equals the velocity vector at that point.

In Cartesian coordinates:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}, \quad w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** φ exists **only when the flow is irrotational** (i.e., vorticity ω = 0). Surfaces of constant φ are called **equipotential surfaces**, and they are always **perpendicular to streamlines**.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Hiking Trail" Picture

Imagine you are hiking on a mountain:

| Concept | Hiking Analogy | Fluid Mechanics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Contour lines (constant height) | Equipotential lines (φ = const) | Lines of equal potential |
| Steepest descent path | Perpendicular to contours | Streamlines (ψ = const) |
| Height map (elevation) | Velocity potential φ | Scalar field |
| Trail map (paths) | Stream function ψ | Scalar field |
| Slope of terrain | Velocity magnitude | Speed of fluid |

Just as contour lines never cross (a single point has only one height), **streamlines never intersect in steady flow** because the velocity at any point is unique.

## 1.4 Existence Conditions — When Can We Use ψ and φ?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two foundational theorems from Vector Calculus:**
> 1. A stream function ψ **always exists** for a 2D incompressible flow (continuity is automatically satisfied).
> 2. A velocity potential φ **exists if and only if** the flow is **irrotational** (curl $\vec{V} = 0$).

These two simple facts drive the entire Module 2 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuity Equation — Why ψ Exists

For a 2D, incompressible flow, the continuity equation in Cartesian form is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Substituting $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Hence, **defining ψ automatically satisfies continuity** in 2D incompressible flow.

## 2.2 The Irrotationality Condition — Why φ Exists

For a 2D flow, the z-component of vorticity is:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

For φ to exist, we require $\omega_z = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)$$

This is a statement that φ is a well-defined scalar function (the cross-partials are equal), which is true as long as the flow is irrotational.

## 2.3 Cauchy–Riemann Equations — The Heart of the Module

When **both** ψ and φ exist (2D, incompressible, irrotational flow), the velocity components can be written using either function. Equating them:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Rearranging gives the **Cauchy–Riemann (C–R) equations**:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The C–R equations imply that φ and ψ are **harmonic conjugates**, satisfying **Laplace's equation**:
> $$\nabla^2 \phi = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0$$
> This is enormously powerful in **groundwater flow**, **aerofoil design**, and **electrochemical analogy** (Hele-Shaw apparatus), where the flow is analogous to current flow or heat conduction in a solid.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition | Valid When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Definition of stream function | 2D incompressible |
| 2 | $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$, $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$ | Definition of velocity potential | 2D irrotational |
| 3 | $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ | Continuity equation | Incompressible 2D |
| 4 | $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$ | Irrotationality condition | Irrotational 2D |
| 5 | $\partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $\partial\phi/\partial y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Cauchy–Riemann equations | 2D, incompressible, irrotational |
| 6 | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ and $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | Laplace equations | 2D, incompressible, irrotational |
| 7 | $d\psi = u\,dy - v\,dx$ | Differential form of ψ | 2D incompressible |
| 8 | $d\phi = u\,dx + v\,dy$ | Differential form of φ | 2D irrotational |
| 9 | $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ (per unit depth) | Volumetric flow rate between streamlines | 2D incompressible |
| 10 | $F(z) = \phi + i\psi$ | Complex potential function | 2D, incompressible, irrotational |
| 11 | $u_r = (1/r)\partial\psi/\partial\theta$, $u_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r$ | ψ in polar coordinates | 2D incompressible (polar) |
| 12 | $u_r = \partial\phi/\partial r$, $u_\theta = (1/r)\partial\phi/\partial\theta$ | φ in polar coordinates | 2D irrotational (polar) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight for KTU Exams:** If you are given u and v, the *first* step is to check continuity and irrotationality. Then you can:
> - Integrate u with respect to x to get φ (plus a function of y).
> - Integrate u with respect to y to get ψ (plus a function of x).
> - Apply C–R equations to find the unknown functions of integration.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerofoil Theory:** In 2D potential flow around an aerofoil, ψ = 0 on the body surface is the no-penetration boundary condition.
- **Groundwater Hydrology:** Darcy flow in confined aquifers obeys Laplace's equation; flow nets (orthogonal ψ and φ lines) are designed using φ and ψ.
- **Flow Meters:** Hele-Shaw apparatus uses viscous flow between parallel plates, where the pressure field is mathematically equivalent to φ.
- **Hydraulic Structure Design:** Seepage analysis beneath dams and weirs uses flow nets built from ψ and φ lines.
- **Vortex Analysis:** Free and forced vortices are most elegantly described using ψ and φ in polar coordinates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From (u, v) to Stream Function ψ

**Given:** A 2D, incompressible velocity field $u(x,y)$ and $v(x,y)$.

**Step 1 — Define a differential of ψ.** Since ψ is constant along a streamline, the streamline equation is $dx/u = dy/v$, which can be rearranged as:

$$u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$$

This suggests defining:

$$d\psi = u\,dy - v\,dx$$

**Step 2 — Verify exactness.** For dψ to be an exact differential, the cross-partials must match:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(u) = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-v) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

This is precisely the **continuity equation**, which holds for incompressible flow. Therefore, dψ is exact and ψ exists.

**Step 3 — Express in partial derivatives.** Comparing $d\psi = u\,dy - v\,dx$ with $d\psi = (\partial\psi/\partial x)dx + (\partial\psi/\partial y)dy$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -v, \quad \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = u}$$

> This is the **fundamental stream function relation**.

## 3.2 Derivation: From (u, v) to Velocity Potential φ

**Given:** A 2D, irrotational velocity field $u$ and $v$.

**Step 1 — Define a differential of φ.** The line integral of velocity along a path is the change in potential:

$$d\phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy = u\,dx + v\,dy$$

**Step 2 — Verify exactness.** For dφ to be exact:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

This is precisely the **irrotationality condition**. Therefore, φ exists.

**Step 3 — Express in partial derivatives:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = u, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = v}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Uniform Flow

**Velocity field:** $u = U$ (constant), $v = 0$.

### Part A — Stream Function ψ

**Step 1.** Use the relations $\partial\psi/\partial x = -v = 0$ and $\partial\psi/\partial y = u = U$.

**Step 2.** Integrate the second relation with respect to y:

$$\psi = \int U\,dy = Uy + f(x)$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate this expression with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = f'(x) = -v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(x) = C$$

**Step 4.** Taking $C = 0$ for simplicity:

$$\boxed{\psi = Uy}$$

**Verification:** Streamlines are $Uy = \text{const}$, i.e., $y = \text{const}$ — horizontal lines, which is correct for uniform flow along the x-axis.

### Part B — Velocity Potential φ

**Step 1.** Use $\partial\phi/\partial x = u = U$ and $\partial\phi/\partial y = v = 0$.

**Step 2.** Integrate with respect to x:

$$\phi = Ux + g(y)$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate with respect to y:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = g'(y) = v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(y) = C$$

**Step 4.** Hence:

$$\boxed{\phi = Ux}$$

**Verification:** Equipotential lines are $Ux = \text{const}$, i.e., $x = \text{const}$ — vertical lines perpendicular to streamlines.

### Part C — Check Cauchy–Riemann Equations

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = U = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = 0 = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \checkmark$$

> Both C–R equations are satisfied — the flow is a valid potential flow.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Source/Sink Flow in Polar Coordinates

**Given:** Radial velocity $v_r = (m)/(2\pi r)$, tangential velocity $v_\theta = 0$, where $m$ is the source strength (m³/s per unit depth).

### Part A — Stream Function

**Step 1.** Use the polar definitions:

$$v_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \quad v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

**Step 2.** From $v_r = m/(2\pi r)$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = \frac{m}{2\pi r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = \frac{m}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3.** Integrate with respect to θ:

$$\psi = \frac{m}{2\pi}\theta + f(r)$$

**Step 4.** Use $v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r = 0$:

$$f'(r) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(r) = C$$

**Step 5.** With $C = 0$:

$$\boxed{\psi = \frac{m}{2\pi}\theta}$$

> **Streamlines are radial lines** emanating from the origin (constant θ = streamline).

### Part B — Velocity Potential

**Step 1.** Use polar definitions:

$$v_r = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r}, \quad v_\theta = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 2.** From $v_r$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = \frac{m}{2\pi r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \frac{m}{2\pi}\ln(r) + h(\theta)$$

**Step 3.** From $v_\theta = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = \frac{h'(\theta)}{r} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h(\theta) = C$$

**Step 4.** Hence:

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{m}{2\pi}\ln(r)}$$

> **Equipotential lines are circles** ($r = \text{const}$), perpendicular to radial streamlines.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Free Vortex Flow

**Given:** $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = K/r$ (where $K$ is vortex strength).

### Stream Function

**Step 1.** $v_r = (1/r)\partial\psi/\partial\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \partial\psi/\partial\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \psi = f(r)$.

**Step 2.** $v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r = K/r$:

$$f'(r) = -\frac{K}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(r) = -K\ln(r) + C$$

**Step 3.** With $C = 0$:

$$\boxed{\psi = -K\ln(r)}$$

> **Streamlines are circles** (r = const) for a free vortex.

### Velocity Potential

**Step 1.** $v_r = \partial\phi/\partial r = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = h(\theta)$.

**Step 2.** $v_\theta = (1/r)\partial\phi/\partial\theta = K/r$:

$$h'(\theta) = K \quad \Rightarrow \quad h(\theta) = K\theta + C$$

**Step 3.** With $C = 0$:

$$\boxed{\phi = K\theta}$$

> **Equipotential lines are radial lines** (θ = const) for a free vortex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Striking Duality:** A source has $\psi \propto \theta$ and $\phi \propto \ln(r)$. A free vortex has $\psi \propto \ln(r)$ and $\phi \propto \theta$. The roles of ψ and φ are **swapped** — this is the **duality principle** of 2D potential flow, beloved by KTU examiners.

## 3.6 Worked Example 4: Finding ψ and φ from a Given Velocity Field

**Given:** $u = 2xy$, $v = x^2 - y^2$.

**Step 1 — Check Continuity:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2y + (-2y) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is incompressible, so ψ exists.

**Step 2 — Check Irrotationality:**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2x - 2x = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is irrotational, so φ exists. This is a **2D potential flow**.

**Step 3 — Find φ:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2xy \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = x^2 y + g(y)$$

Differentiate with respect to y:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = x^2 + g'(y) = v = x^2 - y^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g'(y) = -y^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(y) = -\frac{y^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = x^2 y - \frac{y^3}{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Find ψ:**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = u = 2xy \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = xy^2 + f(x)$$

Differentiate with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = y^2 + f'(x) = -v = -(x^2 - y^2) = y^2 - x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f'(x) = -x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(x) = -\frac{x^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\psi = xy^2 - \frac{x^3}{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify C–R Equations:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2xy = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = x^2 - y^2 = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -(-x^2) = x^2 \quad \text{(Check: need } x^2 \text{, got } x^2 - y^2 \text{)}$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = y^2 - x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = x^2 - y^2 = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} \quad \checkmark$$

Both C–R equations are satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marking Insight:** The third step of "verify C–R equations" is the **most-skipped step** in exam answers. Examiners award 1 to 2 marks explicitly for this verification. **Do not skip it.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Decision Logic for ψ and φ Existence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Velocity Field u, v] --> B{Is Flow 2D and Incompressible?}
    B -- No --> C[Stream Function not applicable in 2D form]
    B -- Yes --> D[Stream Function psi always exists]
    D --> E{Is Flow Irrotational?}
    E -- No --> F[Velocity Potential phi does NOT exist]
    E -- Yes --> G[Velocity Potential phi exists]
    G --> H[Apply Cauchy Riemann Equations]
    H --> I[Both phi and psi satisfy Laplace Equation]
    I --> J[Build Flow Net psi and phi Lines Orthogonal]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture: Relationship Between ψ, φ, and (u, v)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        VEL[Velocity Components u, v]
    end

    subgraph Compute[Computation Layer]
        PSI[Stream Function psi]
        PHI[Velocity Potential phi]
        CR[Cauchy Riemann Check]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        STR[Streamlines psi equals const]
        EQU[Equipotential Lines phi equals const]
        NET[Orthogonal Flow Net]
        Q[Volumetric Flow Rate Q equals psi2 minus psi1]
    end

    VEL --> PSI
    VEL --> PHI
    PSI --> CR
    PHI --> CR
    CR --> NET
    PSI --> STR
    PHI --> EQU
    PSI --> Q
    NET --> Q
```

## 4.3 Source/Sink Flow Net — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SourceField[Source Flow Geometry]
        O[Center at Origin] --> S1[Streamline theta equals 0 degrees]
        O --> S2[Streamline theta equals 45 degrees]
        O --> S3[Streamline theta equals 90 degrees]
        O --> S4[Streamline theta equals 135 degrees]
        O --> S5[Streamline theta equals 180 degrees]
    end

    subgraph Equipot[Equipotential Circles]
        E1[phi equals const at r equals 1]
        E2[phi equals const at r equals 2]
        E3[phi equals const at r equals 3]
    end

    S1 -. orthogonal to .-> E1
    S2 -. orthogonal to .-> E2
    S3 -. orthogonal to .-> E3
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: ψ vs. φ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is being represented?} --> A1[Stream Function psi]
    Q1 --> A2[Velocity Potential phi]

    A1 --> B1[Always exists for 2D incompressible flow]
    A2 --> B2[Exists only if flow is irrotational]

    A1 --> C1[Lines of constant psi are streamlines]
    A2 --> C2[Lines of constant phi are equipotential lines]

    A1 --> D1[psi2 minus psi1 equals volumetric flow rate per unit depth]
    A2 --> D2[No direct flow rate meaning]

    A1 --> E1[Streamlines do not cross in steady flow]
    A2 --> E2[Equipotential lines do not cross if flow is potential]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Solving a Standard KTU Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Read u, v from problem] --> P2[Step 2: Check continuity]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Check irrotationality]
    P3 --> P4{Does phi exist?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Step 4: Integrate u w.r.t. x to get phi]
    P4 -- No --> P5b[Step 4: Stop here, only psi exists]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5: Use dv/dy to find g of y]
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: Integrate v w.r.t. y to get psi]
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: Use du/dx to find f of x]
    P8 --> P9[Step 8: Verify Cauchy Riemann equations]
    P9 --> P10[Step 9: Conclude with final psi and phi expressions]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

Define stream function and velocity potential function. State the conditions for their existence in a 2D flow field.

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> **Stream Function (ψ):** It is a scalar function of position and time in a 2D flow such that the velocity components are given by $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. It always exists for 2D incompressible flow. **(1 Mark)**

> **Velocity Potential (φ):** It is a scalar function such that $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ and $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$. It exists if and only if the flow is irrotational, i.e., $\partial v/\partial x = \partial u/\partial y$. **(1 Mark)**

> **Conditions:**
> - ψ exists when flow is 2D and incompressible (continuity satisfied).
> - φ exists when flow is 2D and irrotational (vorticity = 0).
> - When both exist, they satisfy Cauchy–Riemann equations and Laplace's equation. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

For a 2D incompressible, irrotational flow, prove that the stream function ψ satisfies Laplace's equation.

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

For a 2D flow, the velocity components in terms of ψ are:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

For the flow to be irrotational, $\partial v/\partial x = \partial u/\partial y$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right)$$

$$-\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0}$$

This is Laplace's equation. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

(a) **Derive the Cauchy–Riemann equations for a 2D incompressible, irrotational flow.** (7 Marks)

(b) **For the velocity field $u = 2xy$ and $v = x^2 - y^2$, find the stream function and velocity potential function. Verify that the flow is a potential flow.** (7 Marks)

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Cauchy–Riemann Derivation (7 Marks)

**Given:** 2D, incompressible, irrotational flow. Hence, both ψ and φ exist.

For a 2D flow, velocity components can be expressed using either ψ or φ:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Rearranging (1) and (2) gives the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Differentiating C–R equations and using equality of cross-partials:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = -\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x}$$

Adding these:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

Similarly, $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$. Hence both φ and ψ are **harmonic functions**. **(7 Marks — full derivation + Laplace)**

#### Part (b) — Finding ψ and φ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Check Continuity:** $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 2y + (-2y) = 0$ ✓ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Check Irrotationality:** $\partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 2x - 2x = 0$ ✓ **(1 Mark)**

Since both continuity and irrotationality hold, **both ψ and φ exist**.

**Step 3 — Find φ:** From $\partial\phi/\partial x = 2xy$:

$$\phi = \int 2xy\,dx = x^2 y + g(y)$$

From $\partial\phi/\partial y = x^2 + g'(y) = v = x^2 - y^2$:

$$g'(y) = -y^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(y) = -\frac{y^3}{3} + C_1$$

$$\boxed{\phi = x^2 y - \frac{y^3}{3}}$$ **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 — Find ψ:** From $\partial\psi/\partial y = u = 2xy$:

$$\psi = \int 2xy\,dy = xy^2 + f(x)$$

From $\partial\psi/\partial x = y^2 + f'(x) = -v = -(x^2 - y^2) = y^2 - x^2$:

$$f'(x) = -x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(x) = -\frac{x^3}{3} + C_2$$

$$\boxed{\psi = xy^2 - \frac{x^3}{3}}$$ **(2 Marks)**

**Step 5 — Verify C–R:** 
$\partial\phi/\partial x = 2xy = \partial\psi/\partial y$ ✓ and $\partial\phi/\partial y = x^2 - y^2 = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ ✓ **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

(a) **Explain the physical significance of stream function and velocity potential function. Show that the difference in the values of stream function between two streamlines gives the volumetric flow rate between them.** (7 Marks)

(b) **A source of strength $m$ = 10 m²/s is located at the origin in a 2D flow. Find the stream function and velocity potential. Also determine the radial distance at which the radial velocity equals 2 m/s.** (7 Marks)

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Physical Significance (7 Marks)

**Stream Function (ψ):**
- It is constant along a streamline, so it serves as a **label** for streamlines. **(1 Mark)**
- The **physical significance** is that the difference in ψ values between two streamlines equals the **volumetric flow rate (per unit depth)** passing between them. **(1 Mark)**

**Proof:** Consider two streamlines ψ₁ and ψ₂. The flow rate across any curve from point A on ψ₁ to point B on ψ₂ is:

$$Q = \int_A^B (u\,dy - v\,dx) = \int_A^B d\psi = \psi_B - \psi_A = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

Hence, $Q$ per unit depth $= \psi_2 - \psi_1$. **(3 Marks)**

**Velocity Potential (φ):**
- It exists only for irrotational flow.
- Surfaces of constant φ are called **equipotential surfaces**, and they are always perpendicular to streamlines.
- It has no direct flow-rate interpretation but is the basis of potential flow theory used in aerofoil design and groundwater flow. **(2 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Source Flow Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $m = 10$ m²/s (per unit depth), source at origin.

**Step 1 — Recall standard source flow formulas:**

$$\psi = \frac{m}{2\pi}\theta, \quad \phi = \frac{m}{2\pi}\ln(r), \quad v_r = \frac{m}{2\pi r}, \quad v_\theta = 0$$ **(2 Marks)**

**Step 2 — Substitute $m = 10$:**

$$\boxed{\psi = \frac{10}{2\pi}\theta = \frac{5\theta}{\pi}}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{10}{2\pi}\ln(r) = \frac{5}{\pi}\ln(r)}$$ **(2 Marks)**

**Step 3 — Find $r$ for $v_r = 2$ m/s:**

$$v_r = \frac{m}{2\pi r} = \frac{10}{2\pi r} = 2$$

$$r = \frac{10}{4\pi} = \frac{5}{2\pi} \approx 0.796 \text{ m}$$ **(3 Marks)**

$$\boxed{r \approx 0.796 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the "check continuity" and "check irrotationality" steps** — Examiners allocate 1 to 2 marks for these explicit checks. If you write ψ and φ without justifying their existence, you lose marks.
> 2. **Sign error in stream function relation:** Many students write $v = +\partial\psi/\partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. This single sign error propagates and gives the wrong streamline pattern.
> 3. **Forgetting the function of integration** ($f(x)$ or $g(y)$): A common error is to write $\phi = x^2 y$ without including the $g(y)$ term. This makes the verification of C–R fail.
> 4. **Mixing Cartesian and polar relations:** A typical KTU question gives polar velocity components. Writing $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ in such a question is a **sure 2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Not verifying C–R equations at the end:** A full-mark answer always ends with C–R verification. Skipping it loses 1 mark even when the integration is correct.
> 6. **Forgetting the constant of integration** when stating the final answer: Always mention that the constant has been set to zero by choosing a reference streamline/potential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Stream Function and Velocity Potential:**

- **Stream function ψ** exists for **2D incompressible flow** (continuity satisfied).
- **Velocity potential φ** exists for **irrotational flow** (vorticity = 0).
- **Velocity relations (Cartesian):** $u = \partial\psi/\partial y = \partial\phi/\partial x$; $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x = \partial\phi/\partial y$.
- **Velocity relations (Polar):** $v_r = (1/r)\partial\psi/\partial\theta = \partial\phi/\partial r$; $v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r = (1/r)\partial\phi/\partial\theta$.
- **Cauchy–Riemann equations:** $\partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $\partial\phi/\partial y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.
- **Laplace equations:** $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ and $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ for potential flow.
- **Lines of constant ψ** are **streamlines**; **lines of constant φ** are **equipotential lines**.
- **Streamlines and equipotential lines are mutually orthogonal** in 2D potential flow.
- **Volumetric flow rate per unit depth** between two streamlines equals $\psi_2 - \psi_1$.
- **Duality principle:** Source/sink has streamlines radial and equipotentials circular; free vortex has streamlines circular and equipotentials radial.
- **Complex potential:** $F(z) = \phi + i\psi$ is analytic in 2D potential flow.
- **Standard stream functions:** Uniform flow $\psi = Uy$; Source $\psi = (m/2\pi)\theta$; Free vortex $\psi = -K\ln r$.
- **Standard velocity potentials:** Uniform flow $\phi = Ux$; Source $\phi = (m/2\pi)\ln r$; Free vortex $\phi = K\theta$.
- **Always verify** continuity, irrotationality, and C–R equations at the end of any problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Net
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Net

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Net (KTU 2024 Official Definition):** A *flow net* is a graphical representation of the path taken by water particles (flow lines) and the lines of equal hydraulic head (equipotential lines) in a two-dimensional, steady-state, incompressible seepage flow through a porous medium. It is a pictorial solution of **Laplace's equation** of continuity.

For KTU examinations, the flow net is treated as a fundamental tool in **Seepage Analysis** under hydraulic structures such as **weirs, dams, sheet piles, and coffer dams**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology of a Flow Net

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Flow Line (Streamline)** | The actual path that a water particle follows during seepage. No water crosses a flow line. |
| **Equipotential Line** | A line joining points of equal total hydraulic head (piezometric head). |
| **Flow Channel** | The strip of soil bounded between two adjacent flow lines. |
| **Equipotential Drop** | The strip bounded between two adjacent equipotential lines. |
| **Mesh (Element)** | The curvilinear quadrilateral formed by two flow lines and two equipotential lines. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hiking Topo-Map**
> Imagine a hilly terrain viewed from a helicopter. The closed **contour lines** on a map join points of equal *elevation* (head). The **stream paths** join the highest peak to the lowest valley. A **flow net is essentially a topo-map of underground water** flowing under a dam.
> - The *contour lines* = **Equipotential lines** (equal hydraulic head).
> - The *valley streams* = **Flow lines** (path of water particles).
> - Just as contour lines never cross, two equipotential lines never cross, and just as a stream always crosses contour lines at **right angles**, the flow lines cross equipotential lines at right angles.

### 1.4 Governing Physical Constants

- The hydraulic head loss in the flow net is measured against the reference datum.
- The flow quantity depends on the **coefficient of permeability (k)** of the soil — a property measured in **cm/s** or **m/day**.
- Acceleration due to gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²** is not directly used in the flow net itself but is implicit in the head terms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Equation — Laplace Equation

For 2-D, steady, incompressible, irrotational flow through a porous medium:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} h}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} h}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$$

This is the **Laplace Equation** in terms of hydraulic head *h*. A flow net is the *pictorial* solution of this equation.

### 2.2 Theoretical Properties of a Flow Net (High-Yield for KTU)

A perfectly drawn flow net must satisfy the following properties — these are favorite **2-mark and 7-mark** exam questions:

1. **Orthogonality:** Flow lines and equipotential lines intersect at **right angles** everywhere.
2. **Constant Seepage per Channel:** The quantity of water *q* flowing through every flow channel is the **same**.
3. **Constant Head Drop per Drop:** The head loss along every equipotential drop Δh is the **same**.
4. **Curvilinear Squares (Preferred):** The mesh elements are *curvilinear squares* — meaning the average width of a mesh equals its average length. This is the standard KTU convention.
5. **Boundaries as Special Lines:**
   - Impervious boundary (e.g., base of a dam) is itself a **flow line**.
   - The upstream and downstream water surfaces are **equipotential lines**.
6. **Refraction Law:** When a flow line crosses a soil interface of different permeability, it **refracts** (bends) according to:
   $$\frac{\tan \alpha_{1}}{\tan \alpha_{2}} = \frac{k_{1}}{k_{2}}$$
7. **Density of Net:** The spacing between lines is inversely proportional to the hydraulic gradient. Closer lines = steeper gradient = higher velocity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they appear in 95% of KTU Flow Net numerical problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description of Variables |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Seepage Discharge (Q)** | $q = k \cdot H \cdot \dfrac{N_{f}}{N_{d}}$ | $k$ = permeability, $H$ = total head, $N_f$ = number of flow channels, $N_d$ = number of equipotential drops |
| 2 | **Head Loss per Drop (Δh)** | $\Delta h = \dfrac{H}{N_{d}}$ | Equal head drop between consecutive equipotential lines |
| 3 | **Pressure Head at a Point** | $h_{p} = H - \Delta h \cdot N_{p}$ | $N_p$ = number of drops from upstream to the point |
| 4 | **Exit Gradient ($G_e$)** | $G_{e} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta s} = \dfrac{H}{N_{d} \cdot \Delta s}$ | $\Delta s$ = length of the last flow element at the exit |
| 5 | **Critical Exit Gradient (Boiling)** | $G_{e} = (G_{s} - 1) \cdot (1 - n)$ | $G_s$ = specific gravity of soil, $n$ = porosity (often $n \approx 0.30$ for quick check) |
| 6 | **Factor of Safety against Piping** | $F_{s} = \dfrac{\text{Critical Gradient}}{\text{Actual Exit Gradient}}$ | $F_s \geq 4$ to $5$ is desirable per IS code |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Dams (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar):** Determines *uplift pressure* on the dam base, critical for stability against overturning and sliding.
- **Sheet Pile Cofferdams:** Computes the *seepage quantity* that must be pumped out during construction.
- **Earthen Dams (Embankment Dams):** Locates the *phreatic line* (top seepage line) to design the downstream drainage blanket.
- **Spillways and Weirs:** Avoids *piping* failure at the downstream toe by checking the exit gradient.
- **Foundation Engineering:** Estimates *seepage loss* under impervious aprons and *heaving* of excavation pits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Laplace Equation (Foundational)

Consider a 2-D element of soil of size $dx \times dy$ through which water seeps. The flow is governed by **Darcy's Law**:

$$v_{x} = -k \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad v_{y} = -k \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}$$

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)**
For steady, incompressible seepage, the net volume of water entering the element must equal the net volume leaving it. Equating the inflows and outflows along the *x* and *y* axes:

$$\left(v_{x} - \frac{\partial v_{x}}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{2}\right) dy - \left(v_{x} + \frac{\partial v_{x}}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx}{2}\right) dy = 0$$

$$\left(v_{y} - \frac{\partial v_{y}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{2}\right) dx - \left(v_{y} + \frac{\partial v_{y}}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{dy}{2}\right) dx = 0$$

**Step 2 — Simplification**
Both expressions simplify to:

$$\frac{\partial v_{x}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_{y}}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substituting Darcy's Law**
Since $k$ is constant for a homogeneous soil:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-k \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Final Result**
Factoring out $-k$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} h}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} h}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$$

This is the **Laplace Equation** — a second-order partial differential equation whose graphical solution is the **Flow Net**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Seepage Discharge Equation

Consider a single flow channel of width *b* and length *L* between two adjacent equipotential lines. The hydraulic gradient across this strip is:

$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{H / N_{d}}{L}$$

By Darcy's Law, the discharge through this strip is:

$$\Delta q = k \cdot i \cdot A = k \cdot \frac{H}{N_{d} \cdot L} \cdot (b \cdot 1)$$

Since the mesh is a **curvilinear square**, $L = b$. Therefore:

$$\Delta q = k \cdot \frac{H}{N_{d}}$$

The total seepage discharge across the full flow domain, where there are $N_{f}$ such flow channels, becomes:

$$q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_{f}}{N_{d}}$$

This is the **most heavily tested KTU equation** in any flow net numerical.

### 3.3 Derivation of Exit Gradient

At the downstream exit (downstream toe of a weir / sheet pile), the flow lines converge most tightly. The hydraulic gradient in the last flow element is maximum. The **exit gradient** is defined as the hydraulic gradient at this point:

$$G_{e} = \frac{\text{Head drop across last element}}{\text{Length of last element}}$$

$$G_{e} = \frac{\Delta h}{\Delta s} = \frac{H / N_{d}}{\Delta s}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Model Style)

> **[Numerical]** A sheet pile is driven to a depth of **6 m** in a homogeneous soil bed of permeability **k = 4 × 10⁻⁵ cm/s**. The water head on the upstream side is **5 m** and the downstream side is **0.5 m** above the bed level. A flow net drawn for the geometry gives **N_f = 4** flow channels and **N_d = 12** equipotential drops. The length of the last flow element at the exit is **0.6 m**. Calculate the seepage discharge per meter length of the pile and the exit gradient.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Head**
$$H = 5 - 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Head Loss per Drop**
$$\Delta h = \frac{H}{N_{d}} = \frac{4.5}{12} = 0.375 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Seepage Discharge per unit length**
Permeability must be in **m/s**:
$$k = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}$$

$$q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_{f}}{N_{d}} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.5 \times \frac{4}{12}$$

$$q = 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.5 \times 0.3333 = 6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s per m}$$

**Step 4 — Exit Gradient**
$$G_{e} = \frac{H}{N_{d} \cdot \Delta s} = \frac{4.5}{12 \times 0.6} = \frac{4.5}{7.2} = 0.625$$

**Step 5 — Safety against Piping** (Quick check)
Assuming $G_s = 2.65$ and $n = 0.30$:
$$G_{e,\text{critical}} = (2.65 - 1)(1 - 0.30) = 1.155$$

$$F_{s} = \frac{1.155}{0.625} = 1.848$$

This is **less than 4**, meaning the design is **unsafe against piping** — the sheet pile depth must be increased.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic of a Typical Sheet Pile Flow Net (KTU Standard Diagram)

The following Mermaid block describes the functional architecture and flow logic of seepage under a sheet pile, the most commonly drawn flow net in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upstream Water Level H1] -->|Equipotential Line 1| B[Top of Sheet Pile]
    B -->|Flow Line 1 turns down| C[Tip of Sheet Pile Depth D]
    C -->|Flow Line continues| D[Downstream Water Level H2]
    D -->|Equipotential Last Line| E[Downstream Bed]
    C -->|High Gradient Zone| F[Exit Point Toe]
    F -->|Maximum Velocity| G[Critical Exit Gradient Ge]
    G -->|If Ge Greater than Gcr| H[Boiling Failure Piping]
    G -->|If Ge Less than Gcr| I[Safe Structure]
    A -->|Seepage q flows through Nf Channels| J[Seepage Discharge Q]
    D -->|Head Drop H minus delta h| K[Uplift Pressure at Base]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Flow Net Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Geometry Soil k H1 H2] --> B[Draw Boundary Conditions]
    B --> C[Sketch Initial Flow Lines]
    C --> D[Sketch Equipotential Lines]
    D --> E{Orthogonal Curvilinear Squares?}
    E -->|No| F[Adjust and Redraw]
    F --> C
    E -->|Yes| G[Count Nf and Nd]
    G --> H[Compute Q Ge Uplift]
    H --> I[Safety Check vs Critical]
    I --> J[Final Design Validation]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Components of a Flow Net

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Boundaries
        B1[Impervious Base: Acts as Flow Line]
        B2[Upstream Bed: Equipotential Line]
        B3[Downstream Bed: Equipotential Line]
    end
    subgraph Lines
        L1[Flow Lines curves]
        L2[Equipotential Lines curves]
    end
    subgraph Property
        P1[Right Angle Intersection]
        P2[Equal Discharge per Channel]
        P3[Equal Head Drop per Strip]
    end
    L1 --> P1
    L2 --> P1
    L1 --> P2
    L2 --> P3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a flow net. List any **four** essential properties of a flow net.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A flow net is a graphical network consisting of two sets of curves — **flow lines** and **equipotential lines** — that intersect at right angles, representing the two-dimensional steady seepage flow of water through a porous medium.
The four essential properties are:
1. Flow lines and equipotential lines intersect at right angles to each other.
2. The quantity of seepage water passing through each flow channel is constant.
3. The head loss between any two adjacent equipotential lines (an equipotential drop) is the same.
4. The flow net elements are curvilinear squares (for a homogeneous soil).
**(1 + 0.5 × 4 = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between a **flow line** and an **equipotential line** in a flow net.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Flow Line | Equipotential Line |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Path traced by a water particle | Line joining points of equal hydraulic head |
| Crossing | No two flow lines cross | No two equipotential lines cross |
| Orientation | Tangent to the seepage velocity vector | Perpendicular to the velocity vector |
| Boundary | Impervious boundary acts as a flow line | Upstream and downstream surfaces act as equipotential lines |
**(6 distinguishing points × 0.5 = 3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Flow Net Numerical**
> - Students commonly **forget to convert k to consistent units (m/s)**. If k is in cm/s, multiply by $10^{-2}$ to get m/s. A unit error = **−2 marks minimum**.
> - Many students confuse $N_{f}$ (flow channels) with the number of *flow lines*. **Number of channels = Number of flow lines − 1**.
> - Same for $N_{d}$: **Number of drops = Number of equipotential lines − 1**.
> - In *exit gradient* problems, ensure $\Delta s$ is the length of the *last* flow element at the toe, not an average length.
> - Final answer must include the **Factor of Safety** check to score full marks (the 14-mark question almost always demands it).

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

A concrete weir is founded on a permeable soil of permeability **k = 2 × 10⁻⁴ cm/s**. The upstream and downstream water depths are **8 m** and **1.5 m** respectively. A sheet pile of length **7 m** is provided at the upstream end. A flow net constructed for this weir has **N_f = 3** flow channels and **N_d = 10** equipotential drops. The length of the last flow element at the exit is **0.5 m**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the seepage discharge per metre length of the weir. Also compute the head loss per drop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the exit gradient and the factor of safety against piping. Assume specific gravity of soil = 2.70 and porosity = 0.32.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

*Step 1:* Total head
$$H = 8 - 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ m}$$

*Step 2:* Permeability in SI units
$$k = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm/s} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 3:* Head loss per equipotential drop
$$\Delta h = \frac{H}{N_{d}} = \frac{6.5}{10} = 0.65 \text{ m}$$

*Step 4:* Seepage discharge per unit length
$$q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_{f}}{N_{d}} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.5 \times \frac{3}{10}$$

$$q = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.5 \times 0.3 = 3.9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s per m}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

*Step 5:* Exit gradient
$$G_{e} = \frac{H}{N_{d} \cdot \Delta s} = \frac{6.5}{10 \times 0.5} = \frac{6.5}{5} = 1.30$$

*Step 6:* Critical exit gradient
$$G_{e,\text{critical}} = (G_{s} - 1)(1 - n) = (2.70 - 1)(1 - 0.32)$$

$$G_{e,\text{critical}} = 1.70 \times 0.68 = 1.156$$

*Step 7:* Factor of safety
$$F_{s} = \frac{G_{e,\text{critical}}}{G_{e}} = \frac{1.156}{1.30} = 0.889$$

**Conclusion:** $F_{s} < 1$ — the design is **unsafe against piping**; the sheet pile depth must be increased to reduce the exit gradient.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

A flow net was drawn for seepage flow under a concrete dam founded on sandy soil with **k = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m/s**. The dam retains water to a height of **10 m** on the upstream side, and the downstream water level is **2 m** above the base. The flow net revealed **N_f = 5** flow channels and **N_d = 16** equipotential drops. The length of the last flow element at the downstream toe is **0.4 m**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the seepage loss per metre run of the dam per day and the head loss per drop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the uplift pressure head at a point located **6 equipotential drops** downstream from the upstream bed. Also, find the exit gradient. Comment on the safety of the structure.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

*Step 1:* Total head loss
$$H = 10 - 2 = 8 \text{ m}$$

*Step 2:* Head loss per drop
$$\Delta h = \frac{H}{N_{d}} = \frac{8}{16} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

*Step 3:* Seepage discharge per unit length per second
$$q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_{f}}{N_{d}} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \times \frac{5}{16}$$

$$q = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \times 0.3125 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s per m}$$

*Step 4:* Converting to per day
$$q_{\text{day}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 = 10.8 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{day per m}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

*Step 5:* Pressure head at the point (6 drops downstream)
$$h_{p} = H - \Delta h \cdot N_{p} = 8 - 0.5 \times 6 = 8 - 3 = 5 \text{ m}$$

*Step 6:* Exit gradient
$$G_{e} = \frac{H}{N_{d} \cdot \Delta s} = \frac{8}{16 \times 0.4} = \frac{8}{6.4} = 1.25$$

*Step 7:* Safety check (assuming $G_s = 2.65$, $n = 0.30$)
$$G_{e,\text{critical}} = (2.65 - 1)(1 - 0.30) = 1.65 \times 0.70 = 1.155$$

$$F_{s} = \frac{1.155}{1.25} = 0.924$$

**Conclusion:** Since $F_{s} < 1$, the structure is **unsafe against piping** at the exit — a longer sheet pile or a downstream filter blanket is required.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Principle:** A flow net is the **graphical solution of Laplace's equation** $\nabla^{2} h = 0$ for 2-D steady seepage.
- **Two Lines:** Flow lines (seepage path) and Equipotential lines (equal head). They **always cross at 90°**.
- **The "Magic Formula"** $q = k H (N_f / N_d)$ is the single most important equation — practice variations of this for 14-mark questions.
- **Units Vigilance:** Always convert *k* to **m/s** before substituting; *H* in **m**; answer for *q* will be in **m³/s per m** length.
- **Counting Tip:** Channels = Lines − 1; Drops = Lines − 1. Beginners frequently make an off-by-one error here.
- **Curvilinear Squares:** Standard convention — the *width* of a mesh element should equal its *length*.
- **Impervious Boundary = Flow Line:** The base of the dam and the tip of the sheet pile act as flow lines.
- **Phreatic Line:** For earth dams, the top flow line is called the phreatic line — used to design the **drainage filter**.
- **Piping / Boiling:** Occurs at the downstream exit. Always check **Exit Gradient < Critical Gradient** for safety.
- **Factor of Safety $F_s$ ≥ 4–5** is the IS-code recommendation. If lower, the design must be revised.
- **Laplace Equation Origin:** Derived from continuity + Darcy's Law. Memorize the derivation — it is a guaranteed 4-mark sub-question.
- **Refraction at Soil Interface:** Flow lines **bend** when crossing soils of different permeability — $\tan \alpha_1 / \tan \alpha_2 = k_1 / k_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Dynamics: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fluid Dynamics: Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** in fluid mechanics is the mathematical statement of the **conservation of mass** applied to a flowing fluid. It expresses that, for a control volume (or stream tube), the net rate of mass entering must equal the net rate of mass leaving plus the rate of accumulation of mass within the volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> The continuity equation is a fundamental conservation principle stating that the **mass flow rate** (ρAV) of a fluid across every cross-section of a stream tube remains constant for *steady flow*. In its most simplified form, for an **incompressible fluid** (ρ = constant), the volumetric flow rate (Q = AV) is constant:
> $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}$$

**Syllabus Keywords (for 2-mark definitions):**
- Streamline, Stream tube, Steady flow
- Path line, Streak line, Incompressible flow
- Continuity equation in **3-D Cartesian** form and **1-D** form

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Intuition

Imagine you are watering your garden using a **flexible hose**.

- When you press your thumb over the nozzle (reducing the **cross-sectional area A**), the water **shoots out faster** (velocity **V increases**).
- The *amount of water* passing per second remains the same — it is just squeezed through a smaller opening.
- Mathematically: if A goes down by half, V must go up by **two times** to keep the flow rate constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples of the Continuity Equation:**
> - **Venturi meter**: Pipe narrows → velocity rises → pressure drops (used in carburettors, aircraft fuel systems).
> - **Aircraft wings**: Air flowing over the curved upper surface has a smaller "effective cross-section" → higher velocity → lower pressure (generating lift).
> - **Blood flow in arteries**: Arteries narrow (vasoconstriction) → blood velocity increases sharply.
> - **Rocket nozzle**: Converging-diverging shape modulates exhaust velocity using continuity.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units (SI) |
|:------:|:---------|:-------------------|
| **ρ** | Density of fluid | **kg/m³** |
| **A** | Cross-sectional area | **m²** |
| **V** | Velocity of flow | **m/s** |
| **Q** | Volumetric flow rate | **m³/s** |
| **ṁ** | Mass flow rate | **kg/s** |
| **g** | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — "Why" Mass Must Be Conserved

The continuity equation is a direct consequence of two foundational pillars:

1. **Conservation of Mass** (an empirical law — matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a flow field, ignoring nuclear reactions).
2. **Reynolds Transport Theorem** — a mathematical tool that links system-based conservation to a fixed *control volume* in space.

### 2.1.1 Logical Flow Steps

- **Step 1:** Consider a **stream tube** (an imaginary tube bounded by streamlines) of fluid. No fluid crosses the streamlines — it acts as a "flow pipe."
- **Step 2:** Let section 1 (inlet) have area **A₁** and velocity **V₁**, and section 2 (outlet) have area **A₂** and velocity **V₂**.
- **Step 3:** In a small time **dt**, the mass entering at section 1 = ρ₁A₁V₁dt, and the mass leaving at section 2 = ρ₂A₂V₂dt.
- **Step 4:** For **steady, incompressible flow** with no accumulation, mass in = mass out.
- **Step 5:** Simplify to obtain the 1-D continuity equation.

---

## 2.2 Differential Form of the Continuity Equation

For a general, **3-D, compressible, unsteady** flow, the continuity equation in vector notation is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

Expanded in **Cartesian coordinates (x, y, z)**:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

Where **u, v, w** are the velocity components along the x, y, z axes respectively.

For **incompressible flow** (ρ = constant), the equation simplifies to the elegant form:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The divergence of the velocity field is zero for an incompressible fluid. This means there are **no sources or sinks** — the fluid neither expands nor contracts within the control volume.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — Memorize this for KTU 2024 ESE and Internal Assessments**

| S.No. | Form of Continuity Equation | Governing Condition | Standard Formula |
|:-----:|:----------------------------|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | 1-D Steady, Incompressible (Uniform) | ρ = const, A varies | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ |
| 2 | 1-D Steady, Compressible (Uniform) | ρ varies, A varies | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ |
| 3 | 1-D Unsteady, Incompressible (Variable Area) | ρ = const, A(x,t), V(x,t) | $\frac{\partial A}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (AV)}{\partial x} = 0$ |
| 4 | 3-D Cartesian (General) | Compressible, Unsteady | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ |
| 5 | 3-D Vector Form (General) | Compressible, Unsteady | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ |
| 6 | Incompressible, Vector Form | ρ = const | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ |
| 7 | Cylindrical Coordinates (r, θ, z) | Compressible, Unsteady | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r \rho v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ |
| 8 | Mass Flow Rate Definition | General | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ (kg/s) |
| 9 | Volumetric Flow Rate Definition | Incompressible | $Q = A V$ (m³/s) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline Design**: Continuity governs how much fluid can be transported; the diameter of pipes in oil refineries is selected using Q = AV.
- **Open Channel Flow**: A wider river section means slower flow, an effect routinely observed in nature and used in **spillway design** for dams.
- **Turbomachinery**: In pumps, turbines, and compressors, the continuity equation is used along with **Euler's turbomachinery equation** to find work done.
- **Blood Flow Modelling (Biomedical Engineering)**: Used to model arterial narrowing (stenosis) and design stents.
- **Aerospace**: Rocket and jet engine nozzle design relies fundamentally on continuity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 1-D Continuity Equation for Steady, Incompressible Flow

### Step 1: Set Up the Stream Tube

Consider a stream tube (control volume) between two cross-sections, **Section 1** and **Section 2**, of cross-sectional areas **A₁** and **A₂** respectively. Let the average velocities at these sections be **V₁** and **V₂**, and the densities be **ρ₁** and **ρ₂**.

### Step 2: Compute Mass Entering in Time dt

In a small time interval **dt**, the volume of fluid crossing Section 1 is:

$$dV_1 = A_1 \cdot V_1 \cdot dt$$

The mass entering at Section 1 is therefore:

$$dm_1 = \rho_1 \cdot dV_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, dt$$

### Step 3: Compute Mass Leaving in Time dt

Similarly, the mass leaving at Section 2 in time **dt** is:

$$dm_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, dt$$

### Step 4: Apply Conservation of Mass (Steady Flow Assumption)

For **steady flow**, there is no accumulation of mass within the control volume. Therefore:

$$dm_1 = dm_2$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, dt = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, dt$$

### Step 5: Cancel the Time Differential

The term **dt** appears on both sides and can be cancelled:

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

### Step 6: Apply the Incompressibility Condition

For an **incompressible fluid**, ρ₁ = ρ₂ = ρ. Cancelling ρ:

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant}}$$

Where **Q** is the **volumetric flow rate** in m³/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — General 3-D Continuity Equation in Cartesian Coordinates

### Step 1: Consider an Infinitesimal Control Volume

Consider a small rectangular parallelepiped (box) of dimensions **dx, dy, dz** centred at point **(x, y, z)**. Let the velocity components be **u, v, w** in the **x, y, z** directions respectively, and let the density be **ρ(x, y, z, t)**.

### Step 2: Mass Flow Rate Entering the x-Face (at x)

Mass flux entering through the left x-face (area = dy·dz):

$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = \rho u \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 3: Mass Flow Rate Leaving the x-Face (at x + dx)

Using Taylor series expansion for ρu at x + dx:

$$\rho u \Big\vert_{x+dx} = \rho u \Big\vert_{x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx$$

Therefore, the mass leaving through the right x-face is:

$$\dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} = \left[ \rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy \, dz$$

### Step 4: Net Mass Accumulation in the x-Direction

$$\text{Net outflow}_x = \dot{m}_{x,\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{x,\text{in}} = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 5: Repeat for y and z Directions

By symmetry, the net mass outflow in the y-direction is:

$$\text{Net outflow}_y = \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} dy \, dx \, dz$$

And in the z-direction:

$$\text{Net outflow}_z = \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} dz \, dx \, dy$$

### Step 6: Total Net Outflow

Summing all three contributions:

$$\text{Total net outflow} = \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 7: Apply Conservation of Mass

The net outflow must equal the **rate of decrease of mass** within the control volume:

$$\text{Total net outflow} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \left( \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz \right)$$

### Step 8: Simplify

The volume element **dx dy dz** is constant and can be cancelled from both sides:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

Rearranging gives the **general 3-D continuity equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

This is the KTU 2024 *board-exam gold standard* form.

### Step 9: Incompressible Special Case

If ρ is constant in space and time, then ∂ρ/∂t = 0, and all terms containing ρ inside the derivatives can be factored out:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right) = 0$$

Since ρ ≠ 0:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0}$$

This is the **continuity equation for incompressible flow** in Cartesian coordinates.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 1-D Unsteady Continuity Equation (Variable Area Pipe)

This form is heavily tested in KTU Module 2 for problems involving **filling/emptying tanks** or **varying cross-section pipes**.

Consider a 1-D pipe where both the cross-sectional area **A(x, t)** and velocity **V(x, t)** vary along the length **x** and time **t**. The control volume is bounded by sections at **x** and **x + dx**.

### Mass Inflow at x (time dt):

$$dm_{\text{in}} = \rho A V \, dt \Big\vert_{x}$$

### Mass Outflow at x + dx (time dt):

Using Taylor series:

$$dm_{\text{out}} = \rho A V \, dt \Big\vert_{x} + \frac{\partial (\rho A V)}{\partial x} dx \, dt$$

### Net Mass Accumulation in CV:

The net change in mass within the control volume is the rate of change of mass in the volume **A·dx**:

$$\frac{\partial m}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} \, dx$$

### Apply Conservation of Mass:

Mass flow in − Mass flow out = Rate of mass accumulation:

$$\rho A V \, dt - \left[ \rho A V + \frac{\partial (\rho A V)}{\partial x} dx \right] dt = \frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} \, dx \, dt$$

### Simplify and Cancel dt:

$$-\frac{\partial (\rho A V)}{\partial x} dx = \frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} dx$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial (\rho A)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho A V)}{\partial x} = 0}$$

For an **incompressible fluid** (ρ = constant), ρ cancels:

$$\frac{\partial A}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (A V)}{\partial x} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form most commonly applied to problems where a tank is filling or a flexible pipe is deforming.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Forms

| Parameter | Steady Flow | Unsteady Flow |
|:----------|:------------|:--------------|
| **General Compressible** | $\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ |
| **Incompressible (ρ = const)** | $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Same (no time term since ρ is constant) |
| **1-D Simplification** | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\frac{\partial A}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (AV)}{\partial x} = 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Continuity Concept Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fluid Inlet\nSection 1: A1, V1, rho1"] --> B["Stream Tube\nControl Volume"]
    B --> C["Fluid Outlet\nSection 2: A2, V2, rho2"]
    D["Conservation\nof Mass Law"] --> E["Steady Flow?"]
    E -- "Yes" --> F["No Accumulation\nm_in = m_out"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Accumulation Allowed\npartial rho / partial t not equal to 0"]
    F --> H["rho1 A1 V1 = rho2 A2 V2"]
    G --> I["General 3D Form\npartial rho/partial t + div rho V = 0"]
    H --> J["Incompressible?\nrho = constant"]
    J -- "Yes" --> K["A1 V1 = A2 V2 = Q"]
    J -- "No" --> L["rho1 A1 V1 = rho2 A2 V2"]
    I --> M["Incompressible?\nrho = constant"]
    M -- "Yes" --> N["div V = 0"]
    M -- "No" --> O["Full Compressible\nContinuity Equation"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mass Conservation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CV["Control Volume / Stream Tube"]
        A1["Inlet A1\nVelocity V1\nDensity rho1"] --> M["Mass Balance Node\n(Summation)"]
        M --> A2["Outlet A2\nVelocity V2\nDensity rho2"]
        M --> ACC["Accumulation\npartial m / partial t"]
    end
    A1 -->|"Mass Inflow\nrho1 A1 V1"| M
    M -->|"Mass Outflow\nrho2 A2 V2"| A2
    M -->|"Net Storage\nd m by d t"| ACC
    style CV fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style ACC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399
```

## 4.3 Physical Picture — Stream Tube Schematic (Textual Representation)

```
    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                  STREAM TUBE PICTURE                  │
    │                                                        │
    │   ρ₁, V₁              ρ₂, V₂                          │
    │    →→→                →→→                              │
    │   ╔═══╗ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╔═══╗                              │
    │   ║   ║ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ║   ║                              │
    │   ║ A₁║ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ║ A₂║                              │
    │   ║   ║ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ║   ║                              │
    │   ╚═══╝ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╚═══╝                              │
    │   Section 1           Section 2                         │
    │  (Inlet)             (Outlet)                           │
    │                                                        │
    │  For Steady, Incompressible Flow:                      │
    │       A₁ × V₁ = A₂ × V₂                                │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a Venturi meter, A₂ < A₁ → V₂ > V₁. This rise in velocity is what causes the pressure drop (governed by **Bernoulli's equation**, Module 2 next topic). The continuity equation is the *kinematic* half of the story; Bernoulli supplies the *dynamic* half.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Application Map of Continuity Equation

```mermaid
    VenturiMeter

```
