<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the continuity equation for a 1-D steady, incompressible flow. Explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The continuity equation for a 1-D, steady, incompressible flow is:
> $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant (Q)}$$
>
> where A is the cross-sectional area and V is the average velocity at any section of the stream tube.
>
> **Physical Significance:** It is a direct statement of the **conservation of mass**. For a steady flow with no sources or sinks, the *volumetric flow rate* passing through every cross-section of a stream tube must be identical. As the cross-section decreases, the velocity must increase proportionally to deliver the same volume of fluid per second.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Write the general 3-D continuity equation in Cartesian coordinates. What does it reduce to for an incompressible fluid?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The general 3-D continuity equation in Cartesian coordinates is:
> $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$
>
> For an **incompressible fluid** (ρ = constant in time and space), this reduces to:
> $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$
>
> This states that the **divergence of the velocity field is zero** — the fluid is neither expanding nor contracting.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the continuity equation in **3-D Cartesian coordinates** for a compressible fluid. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pipe of diameter **300 mm** at section 1 contracts to a diameter of **150 mm** at section 2. If the velocity of water at section 1 is **2 m/s**, determine:
- (i) The velocity at section 2
- (ii) The mass flow rate at section 1
- (iii) The volumetric flow rate in **litres per second**

Assume ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-A:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):

Refer to **Section 3.2 (Derivation 2)** of these notes.

**Key Steps to Write in the Exam (1 Mark Each):**

1. Consider an elementary control volume of dimensions **dx, dy, dz** at point (x, y, z).
2. Write expressions for mass flux entering and leaving each face (e.g., x-faces: ρu and ρu + ∂(ρu)/∂x · dx).
3. Compute net mass outflow in **x, y, z** directions using Taylor series.
4. Sum the contributions to get the total net outflow.
5. Equate it to the rate of decrease of mass within the CV: −∂(ρ)/∂t · dx·dy·dz.
6. Simplify to obtain the final form: ∂ρ/∂t + ∂(ρu)/∂x + ∂(ρv)/∂y + ∂(ρw)/∂z = 0.
7. State the **incompressible special case** clearly: ∂u/∂x + ∂v/∂y + ∂w/∂z = 0.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):

**Given:**
- D₁ = 300 mm = **0.3 m**, D₂ = 150 mm = **0.15 m**
- V₁ = **2 m/s**, ρ = **1000 kg/m³**

**Step 1: Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.3)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Continuity (Incompressible) to Find V₂**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.0707 \times 2}{0.01767}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 8.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Mass Flow Rate at Section 1**

$$\dot{m}_1 = \rho A_1 V_1 = 1000 \times 0.0707 \times 2$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_1 = 141.4 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Step 4: Volumetric Flow Rate in L/s**

$$Q = A_1 V_1 = 0.0707 \times 2 = 0.1414 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$\boxed{Q = 141.4 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Verification (using Section 2):** Q = A₂ V₂ = 0.01767 × 8 = 0.1414 m³/s ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the **1-D continuity equation for steady incompressible flow** through a stream tube. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Air flows steadily through a duct that has a cross-section of **0.5 m²** at the inlet and **0.1 m²** at the outlet. At the inlet, the velocity is **10 m/s** and the density is **1.2 kg/m³**. At the outlet, the density is **0.9 kg/m³**. Determine:
- (i) The mass flow rate
- (ii) The outlet velocity
- (iii) Whether the flow can be treated as incompressible. Justify. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-B:

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)** of these notes.

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
- Flow is **steady** (∂/∂t = 0).
- Fluid is **incompressible** (ρ = constant).
- Flow is **1-D** (uniform velocity at each cross-section).
- Stream tube has **no sources or sinks** (no fluid crosses the bounding streamlines).

**Derivation Steps (1 Mark Each):**
1. Define control volume between sections 1 and 2.
2. Express mass entering in time dt: dm₁ = ρA₁V₁dt.
3. Express mass leaving in time dt: dm₂ = ρA₂V₂dt.
4. Apply conservation of mass (steady flow): dm₁ = dm₂.
5. Cancel dt and ρ to get A₁V₁ = A₂V₂.
6. Conclude Q = AV = constant.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):

**Given:**
- A₁ = 0.5 m², A₂ = 0.1 m²
- V₁ = 10 m/s, ρ₁ = 1.2 kg/m³
- ρ₂ = 0.9 kg/m³

**Step 1: Mass Flow Rate (Compressible Continuity)**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = 1.2 \times 0.5 \times 10$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 6.0 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Step 2: Outlet Velocity**

From continuity for compressible flow: ρ₁A₁V₁ = ρ₂A₂V₂

$$V_2 = \frac{\rho_1 A_1 V_1}{\rho_2 A_2} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.5 \times 10}{0.9 \times 0.1}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{6.0}{0.09}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 66.67 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Incompressibility Justification (2 Marks)**

The change in density is:

$$\Delta \rho = 1.2 - 0.9 = 0.3 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_1} = \frac{0.3}{1.2} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Verdict:** The density change exceeds **5%**, so the flow **CANNOT** be treated as incompressible. The compressible form of the continuity equation must be used.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Time Derivative in Unsteady Flow:** Many students write A₁V₁ = A₂V₂ for unsteady problems and lose 3-4 marks. Always check if the problem states "steady" first.
> 2. **Mixing Up Compressible vs Incompressible:** If density is given as different at the two sections, you **MUST** use ρ₁A₁V₁ = ρ₂A₂V₂. Cancelling ρ (saying "water is incompressible") loses full marks when the problem is about air.
> 3. **Not Converting Units:** Velocities in cm/s, areas in cm², diameters in mm — failing to convert to SI units (m, s, kg) is a guaranteed **1-mark** deduction in numerical problems.
> 4. **Missing the "Justify" Part:** In part (b) of KTU questions, examiners often include a "comment" or "justify" sub-part worth 1-2 marks. Skipping it loses easy marks.
> 5. **Omitting Assumptions:** In derivation questions, listing assumptions is worth **1 mark minimum**. Never start the derivation without writing "Assumptions:" first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Continuity Equation**
> - **Conservation of mass** is the only physical law used; the equation is *kinematic* (does not involve forces).
> - **1-D Steady Incompressible:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ — most basic form.
> - **1-D Steady Compressible:** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}$ — use this when densities differ.
> - **3-D General Form (Cartesian):** $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$.
> - **3-D Incompressible Form:** $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \Rightarrow \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
> - **Unsteady 1-D (Variable Area):** $\frac{\partial A}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (AV)}{\partial x} = 0$ — used for filling tanks and deforming pipes.
> - **Units to remember:** Q in m³/s, ṁ in kg/s, ρ in kg/m³, A in m², V in m/s.
> - **Continuity is independent of Bernoulli** — both must be satisfied simultaneously for ideal flow problems.
> - **Stream tube** = imaginary tube bounded by streamlines; no fluid crosses its boundary.
> - **Practical cutoff for incompressibility:** density variation < **5%** allows use of A₁V₁ = A₂V₂.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's and Bernoulli's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Euler's and Bernoulli's Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Euler's Equation of Motion — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Equation of Motion** is the fundamental differential equation that governs the steady, inviscid (frictionless), and incompressible flow of a fluid along a streamline. It expresses Newton's Second Law of Motion applied to a fluid element subjected to pressure, gravitational, and inertial forces.

Mathematically, Euler's equation along a streamline (say, the *s*-direction) is expressed as:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + g \frac{dz}{ds} + V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$$

Where:
- $p$ = Static pressure exerted on the fluid element (**N/m²**)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $V$ = Velocity of flow along the streamline (**m/s**)
- $z$ = Elevation head above a chosen datum (**m**)
- $s$ = Distance measured along the streamline (**m**)

### 1.2 Bernoulli's Equation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bernoulli's Equation** is the celebrated **energy conservation equation** for an ideal (inviscid, incompressible, steady) fluid flow. It is obtained by integrating Euler's equation along a streamline. It states that the total mechanical energy (pressure energy + kinetic energy + potential energy) per unit weight of a fluid particle remains **constant** along a streamline.

The standard form of Bernoulli's equation is:

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Think of a ball rolling down a smooth, frictionless hill.**

- At the **top** of the hill, the ball is slow (low kinetic energy) but high up (high potential energy).
- As it **rolls downhill**, it speeds up — potential energy is converted into kinetic energy. The same happens to fluid: as it descends through a pipe, pressure energy is converted into velocity energy.
- **Pressure**, in fluid terms, is like the "push" of the fluid — just like you feel more "pushed" when water from a narrow shower head hits you harder (high velocity + low pressure region).

**Euler's equation** is the *raw differential statement*: "Hey, these three forces (pressure, gravity, inertia) are perfectly balanced on every tiny fluid droplet."
**Bernoulli's equation** is the *integrated, friendlier cousin*: "Add them all up over a distance, and the total stays the same."

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumptions for Both Equations:**
> - Fluid is **inviscid** (zero viscosity, $\mu = 0$)
> - Fluid is **incompressible** (constant density, $\rho = \text{const}$)
> - Flow is **steady** ($\frac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0$)
> - Flow is along a **streamline** (or irrotational for full validity)
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard gravity)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Euler's Equation — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline:

**Step 1: Identify Forces Acting on the Fluid Element**

- **Pressure Force** (upstream face): $+p \cdot dA$
- **Pressure Force** (downstream face): $-(p + dp) \cdot dA$
- **Gravity Force** (vertical component): $-\rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot \cos\theta$ (which equals $-\rho g dz$ since $\cos\theta \cdot ds = dz$)

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law (F = ma)**

The net force equals mass × acceleration along the streamline. For steady flow, acceleration is $V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$.

$$\sum F = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 3: Equate and Simplify**

The $dA$ and $ds$ terms cancel (after dividing both sides by $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$), yielding Euler's equation in its differential form.

**Step 4: Why Is This Equation Important?**

- It is the **mother equation** of all ideal-fluid flow analyses.
- It is used to derive Bernoulli's equation (integration along a streamline).
- It is the foundation for analyzing flow through nozzles, venturimeters, pitot tubes, and airfoils.

### 2.2 Bernoulli's Equation — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

**Step 1: Integrate Euler's Equation**

Multiply Euler's equation by $ds$ and integrate between two points (1) and (2) along the same streamline:

$$\int_{1}^{2} \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{1}^{2} V \, dV + g \int_{1}^{2} dz = 0$$

**Step 2: Evaluate Each Integral**

- For incompressible flow: $\int \frac{dp}{\rho} = \frac{p}{\rho}$
- $\int V \, dV = \frac{V^{2}}{2}$
- $\int g \, dz = gz$

**Step 3: Arrive at the Final Form**

$$\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = \text{Constant}$$

Dividing throughout by $g$ gives the **head form** (in meters of fluid):

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The three terms in Bernoulli's equation are universally known as:
> - $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ = **Pressure Head** (m)
> - $\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$ = **Velocity Head / Kinetic Head** (m)
> - $z$ = **Elevation Head / Potential Head** (m)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euler's Equation (Streamline) | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$ | N/m³ | Different form of Euler along streamline |
| Euler's Equation (Divided by $\rho g$) | $\frac{1}{\rho g}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + \frac{V}{g}\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$ | Dimensionless | Integration step towards Bernoulli |
| Bernoulli (Energy Form, J/kg) | $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = C$ | J/kg | Energy conservation calculation |
| Bernoulli (Head Form, m) | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = C$ | m | Practical hydraulic problems |
| Bernoulli (Two Points) | $\frac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \frac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2}$ | m | Most exam problems |
| Discharge Form (Continuity) | $A_{1} V_{1} = A_{2} V_{2} = Q$ | m³/s | Coupled with Bernoulli |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Venturimeter**: Flow rate measurement in pipes (uses Bernoulli + Continuity).
- **Pitot Tube**: Aircraft airspeed measurement (uses Bernoulli).
- **Orifice Meter**: Discharge measurement from tanks.
- **Aerofoil Lift**: Aircraft wing lift generation (pressure difference due to velocity difference).
- **Dam Spillways**: Energy conversion design from potential to kinetic.
- **Turbomachine Analysis**: Governing equation for pumps and turbines (Euler's turbomachinery equation is built upon Bernoulli's principles).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Euler's Equation

**Setup:** Consider a small cylindrical fluid element aligned along a streamline. Let the cross-sectional area be $dA$, the length along the streamline be $ds$, and let the streamline make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal datum.

**Step 1: Mass of the Fluid Element**

The volume of the cylindrical element is $dA \cdot ds$. Multiplying by density $\rho$ gives the mass:

$$m = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$$

**Step 2: Forces on the Fluid Element**

*(a) Pressure Force on the Upstream Face (Face 1):*
Acting in the direction of flow (positive s-direction):

$$F_{p1} = p \cdot dA$$

*(b) Pressure Force on the Downstream Face (Face 2):*
Acting opposite to the direction of flow. The pressure at this face is $(p + dp)$:

$$F_{p2} = -(p + dp) \cdot dA$$

*(c) Gravity Force Component along Streamline:*
The weight of the element is $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot g$, acting vertically downward. Its component along the streamline is $-\rho g \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot \cos\theta$. Since $\cos\theta = \frac{dz}{ds}$, this becomes:

$$F_{g} = -\rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz$$

> **Logic Note:** The vertical projection $dz$ is positive when moving upward against gravity, so the gravity component along the streamline is negative (it opposes upward motion).

**Step 3: Net Force on the Element**

$$F_{net} = F_{p1} + F_{p2} + F_{g}$$

$$F_{net} = p \cdot dA - (p + dp) \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz$$

$$F_{net} = -dp \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz$$

**Step 4: Acceleration of the Fluid Element**

For steady flow along a streamline, the fluid velocity $V$ is a function of position $s$. The convective acceleration along the streamline is:

$$a_{s} = V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

**Step 5: Apply Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$)**

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a_{s}$$

$$-dp \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = (\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds) \cdot \left( V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} \right)$$

**Step 6: Simplify the Equation**

Divide both sides by $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$:

$$-\frac{dp}{\rho \cdot ds} - g \frac{dz}{ds} = V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

**This is Euler's Equation of Motion along a streamline.**

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation

**Step 1: Start with Euler's Equation**

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

**Step 2: Multiply Throughout by $ds$**

$$\frac{dp}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$$

> **Logic Note:** Since $V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} ds = V \, dV$ (chain rule of calculus).

**Step 3: Integrate Between Points 1 and 2 Along the Streamline**

$$\int_{p_{1}}^{p_{2}} \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} V \, dV + \int_{z_{1}}^{z_{2}} g \, dz = 0$$

**Step 4: Apply the Incompressibility Assumption**

For an incompressible fluid, $\rho = \text{constant}$, so it can be taken out of the integral:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} \int_{p_{1}}^{p_{2}} dp = \frac{p_{2} - p_{1}}{\rho}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate Each Integral**

$$\frac{p_{2} - p_{1}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}{2} + g(z_{2} - z_{1}) = 0$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to Group State 1 and State 2 Terms**

$$\frac{p_{1}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} + g z_{1} = \frac{p_{2}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2} + g z_{2}$$

**Step 7: State the General Bernoulli Equation**

For any point in the flow field:

$$\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = \text{Constant}$$

**Step 8: Convert to Head Form (Divide by $g$)**

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

> **Physical Interpretation of Each Term (in J/kg form):**
> - $\frac{p}{\rho}$ = Flow work / Pressure energy per unit mass (**J/kg**)
> - $\frac{V^{2}}{2}$ = Kinetic energy per unit mass (**J/kg**)
> - $gz$ = Potential energy per unit mass (**J/kg**)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Euler vs Bernoulli Comparison

| Feature | Euler's Equation | Bernoulli's Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Nature** | Differential equation | Integrated algebraic equation |
| **Variables** | Pressure gradient, velocity gradient, elevation gradient | Pressure, velocity, elevation (point values) |
| **Form** | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = 0$ | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = C$ |
| **Utility** | Foundation; basis for deriving others | Direct problem-solving tool |
| **Information Provided** | Local force balance at a point | Total energy at any two points |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fluid Element Force Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **force balance architecture** on a differential fluid element along a streamline, which is the conceptual foundation of Euler's equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fluid Element dA × ds] --> B[Identify Three Forces]
    B --> C[Pressure Force Upstream: +p·dA]
    B --> D[Pressure Force Downstream: -(p+dp)·dA]
    B --> E[Gravity Component: -ρ·g·dz·dA]
    C --> F[Sum of Forces: Net Force]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Newton's 2nd Law: F = ma]
    G --> H[Acceleration = V ∂V/∂s]
    H --> I[Substitute and Simplify]
    I --> J[Euler's Equation Obtained]
    J --> K[Multiply by ds and Integrate]
    K --> L[Bernoulli's Equation Obtained]
    L --> M[Apply to Real Systems: Venturi, Pitot, Orifice]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart — From Assumptions to Final Bernoulli Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Assumption 1: Inviscid Fluid μ=0] --> Q1[Combine All Assumptions]
    P2[Assumption 2: Incompressible ρ=const] --> Q1
    P3[Assumption 3: Steady Flow ∂/∂t=0] --> Q1
    P4[Assumption 4: Along Streamline] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Apply Newton's 2nd Law to Fluid Element]
    R1 --> R2[Derive Euler's Differential Equation]
    R2 --> R3[Multiply by ds and Integrate]
    R3 --> R4[Evaluate Integrals]
    R4 --> R5[Final Bernoulli's Equation]
    R5 --> S1[Pressure Head: p/ρg]
    R5 --> S2[Velocity Head: V²/2g]
    R5 --> S3[Elevation Head: z]
    S1 --> T[Total Head = Constant]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
```

### 4.3 Real-World Application Map (Engineering Systems)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BE[Bernoulli's Equation] --> APP1[Venturimeter: Flow Measurement]
    BE --> APP2[Pitot Tube: Aircraft Speed]
    BE --> APP3[Orifice Meter: Tank Discharge]
    BE --> APP4[Aerofoil: Aircraft Lift]
    BE --> APP5[Spillway Design: Dam Hydraulics]
    BE --> APP6[Turbomachines: Pumps and Turbines]
    
    APP1 --> SUB1[Pipe Inlet, Throat, Outlet]
    APP2 --> SUB2[Stagnation + Static Points]
    APP3 --> SUB3[Tank Surface + Jet]
    APP4 --> SUB4[Upper and Lower Wing Surface]
    APP5 --> SUB5[Crest and Tail Water]
    APP6 --> SUB6[Impeller Inlet and Outlet]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
*State Bernoulli's equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. Explain the physical significance of each term in the equation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bernoulli's equation states that for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline, the sum of the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head remains constant.

$$\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term:**

1. **$\frac{p}{\rho g}$ — Pressure Head:** Represents the pressure energy per unit weight of the fluid. It indicates the height of a fluid column that would produce the existing static pressure.

2. **$\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$ — Velocity Head (Kinetic Head):** Represents the kinetic energy per unit weight of the fluid. It is the vertical distance through which the fluid must fall freely to acquire the existing velocity $V$.

3. **$z$ — Elevation Head (Potential Head):** Represents the potential energy per unit weight of the fluid due to its position above a chosen datum.

The equation essentially states the **conservation of mechanical energy** for an ideal fluid in motion.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]:**
*List the assumptions made in the derivation of Euler's equation of motion.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The assumptions in the derivation of Euler's equation of motion are:

1. **Inviscid Fluid:** The fluid has zero viscosity ($\mu = 0$), meaning no shear stresses due to fluid friction.

2. **Incompressible Fluid:** The fluid density $\rho$ remains constant throughout the flow.

3. **Steady Flow:** The flow parameters do not change with time ($\frac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0$).

4. **Flow Along a Streamline:** The equation is valid along a single streamline (or for irrotational flow, anywhere in the field).

5. **No Additional Body Forces:** Apart from gravity, no other body forces (such as electromagnetic forces) act on the fluid.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**

**(a)** Derive Euler's equation of motion for a fluid element moving along a streamline. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from Euler's equation, derive Bernoulli's equation. Hence, state its three different forms with proper units. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Euler's Equation (7 Marks):**

*Assumptions:*
- Fluid is inviscid, incompressible, and the flow is steady.
- Flow is along a streamline.

*Derivation Steps:*

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ along a streamline making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.

The forces acting along the streamline are:

$$F_{p1} = p \cdot dA \quad \text{(upstream pressure force, positive)}$$

$$F_{p2} = -(p + dp) \cdot dA \quad \text{(downstream pressure force, negative)}$$

$$F_{g} = -\rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz \quad \text{(gravity component along streamline)}$$

Net force:

$$F_{net} = -dp \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz$$

Mass of the element: $m = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$

Convective acceleration along streamline: $a_{s} = V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$

Applying Newton's Second Law:

$$-dp \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Dividing by $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + g\frac{dz}{ds} = 0} \quad \text{(Euler's Equation)}$$

**(b) Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (7 Marks):**

Multiplying Euler's equation by $ds$:

$$\frac{dp}{\rho} + V \, dV + g \, dz = 0$$

Integrating between points 1 and 2:

$$\int_{1}^{2} \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{1}^{2} V \, dV + \int_{1}^{2} g \, dz = 0$$

Since $\rho$ is constant (incompressible flow):

$$\frac{p_{2} - p_{1}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}{2} + g(z_{2} - z_{1}) = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{p_{1}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} + g z_{1} = \frac{p_{2}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2} + g z_{2}$$

**Three Forms of Bernoulli's Equation:**

| Form | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Energy per unit mass | $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2} + gz = C$ | J/kg |
| Energy per unit weight (Head form) | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = C$ | m |
| Energy per unit volume | $p + \frac{\rho V^{2}}{2} + \rho g z = C$ | N/m² (Pa) |

---

**Question B [KTU University Exam - June 2024]: (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the assumptions and limitations of Bernoulli's equation. Why is Bernoulli's equation not directly applicable to real fluid flow systems? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter **300 mm** at section 1 narrows to a diameter of **150 mm** at section 2. If the pressure at section 1 is **120 kPa** and at section 2 is **100 kPa**, determine the flow rate through the pipe. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Assumptions and Limitations of Bernoulli's Equation (7 Marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. The fluid is **ideal** (inviscid and incompressible).
2. The flow is **steady**.
3. The flow is **along a streamline** (or irrotational for full validity).
4. **No energy addition or extraction** by pumps, turbines, or machinery between the two sections.
5. **No friction losses** in the flow.

*Limitations (Why It Fails for Real Fluids):*

1. **Viscosity:** Real fluids have viscosity, leading to frictional losses (head loss $h_L$), which Bernoulli's equation ignores. To apply it to real systems, a head loss term must be added:
$$\frac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} = \frac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{L}$$

2. **Compressibility:** Bernoulli's equation assumes constant $\rho$. For high-speed gas flow (Mach > 0.3), compressibility effects become significant.

3. **Turbulence:** In turbulent flow, eddies cause additional energy dissipation.

4. **Unsteady Flow:** The standard Bernoulli's equation does not apply to time-varying flows.

5. **Rotational Flow:** Strictly applies only along a streamline; for full field validity, the flow must be irrotational.

**(b) Numerical Problem — Flow Rate Through a Pipe (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- $D_{1} = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$, so $A_{1} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^{2} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $D_{2} = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, so $A_{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^{2} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $p_{1} = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $p_{2} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $z_{1} = z_{2}$ (horizontal pipe)
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

*Step 1: Apply Continuity Equation*

$$A_{1} V_{1} = A_{2} V_{2}$$

$$V_{2} = \frac{A_{1}}{A_{2}} V_{1} = \frac{0.0707}{0.01767} V_{1} = 4 V_{1}$$

*Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation*

Since $z_{1} = z_{2}$:

$$\frac{p_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{p_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g}$$

$$\frac{p_{1} - p_{2}}{\rho g} = \frac{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}{2g}$$

$$\frac{120{,}000 - 100{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{(4V_{1})^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$\frac{20{,}000}{9810} = \frac{16 V_{1}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}{19.62}$$

$$2.039 = \frac{15 V_{1}^{2}}{19.62}$$

$$V_{1}^{2} = \frac{2.039 \times 19.62}{15} = 2.667$$

$$V_{1} = 1.633 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 3: Calculate Flow Rate*

$$Q = A_{1} \cdot V_{1} = 0.0707 \times 1.633 = 0.1155 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.1155 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \text{ (or 115.5 L/s)}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions explicitly** — A full-mark answer on Euler/Bernoulli derivation MUST begin with the assumptions block. Omitting this costs at least 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign convention errors** — The downstream pressure force is NEGATIVE because it acts opposite to the assumed flow direction. Reversing signs leads to a wrong final equation.
> 3. **Confusing Euler's form with Bernoulli's form** — Euler is DIFFERENTIAL ($\frac{\partial p}{\partial s}$); Bernoulli is INTEGRATED ($p$, $V$, $z$ as point values).
> 4. **Mixing head form with energy form** — Students often write $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^{2}}{2}$ without $gz$ and lose marks for incomplete terms.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors in numericals** — Always convert mm to m, kPa to Pa BEFORE substituting into equations.
> 6. **Missing the continuity equation** — In numericals involving different cross-sections, you MUST apply both Bernoulli AND continuity. Skipping continuity = losing 2-3 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting $z_{1} = z_{2}$ simplification** — For horizontal pipes, the elevation terms cancel, which simplifies calculations significantly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Euler's and Bernoulli's Equations**

**Core Conceptual Points:**

- ✅ Euler's equation is the **differential** form of momentum conservation for an inviscid, incompressible fluid.
- ✅ Bernoulli's equation is the **integrated** form of Euler's equation, representing **energy conservation** along a streamline.
- ✅ Both equations require the fluid to be **ideal** (inviscid + incompressible) and the flow to be **steady**.

**Key Mathematical Forms to Memorize:**

- ✅ Euler's Equation: $\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + g\frac{dz}{ds} = 0$
- ✅ Bernoulli's Equation (Energy/Head): $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$

**Three Heads (Term-by-Term Meaning):**

- ✅ **Pressure Head** ($\frac{p}{\rho g}$): Energy due to fluid pressure, in meters of fluid.
- ✅ **Velocity Head** ($\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$): Energy due to motion, in meters of fluid.
- ✅ **Elevation Head** ($z$): Energy due to height above datum, in meters of fluid.

**Assumptions Checklist (Always State Before Solving):**

- ✅ Inviscid fluid (no shear stress)
- ✅ Incompressible (constant $\rho$)
- ✅ Steady flow (no time variation)
- ✅ Along a streamline
- ✅ No external work/pump/turbine between sections (for Bernoulli)
- ✅ No frictional losses (for Bernoulli)

**Real-World Applications to Remember:**

- ✅ Venturimeter, Pitot tube, Orifice meter (flow measurement)
- ✅ Aerofoil lift, spillway design, turbomachinery analysis

**Problem-Solving Sequence for KTU Numericals:**

- ✅ Step 1: List givens, identify fluid (assume water unless stated).
- ✅ Step 2: Write Bernoulli's equation between the two points of interest.
- ✅ Step 3: Apply continuity equation if cross-sections differ.
- ✅ Step 4: Solve simultaneously for unknowns.
- ✅ Step 5: Verify units and physical reasonableness of the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring Instruments - Pitot Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measuring Instruments - Pitot Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pitot Tube** is a differential pressure flow measurement device used to calculate the local fluid velocity at a specific point within a flow stream. It operates on the principle of converting the **kinetic energy (dynamic head)** of a moving fluid into a measurable **pressure head (stagnation pressure)** at a stagnation point where the flow is brought to rest.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCSFT303)**, the Pitot tube is classified under *Flow Measurement Devices* in the category of **Velocity Measurement Instruments**, as opposed to discharge measurement devices like venturimeters, orifices, and notches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Definition)**
> - **Static Pressure ($P$)** : The pressure exerted by the fluid when it is in true (undisturbed) motion. It is the thermodynamic pressure of the flowing fluid.
> - **Stagnation Pressure ($P_0$)** : The total pressure at a point where the fluid velocity is brought to rest isentropically (reversibly and without friction). $P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$.
> - **Dynamic Pressure ($\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$)** : The kinetic energy per unit volume of the fluid. It is the difference between stagnation and static pressure.
> - **Pitot-Static Tube** : A combination of a Pitot tube and a static pressure tap in a single instrument.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "River Pebble" Intuition)

Imagine you are standing on the bank of a fast-flowing river and you want to measure how fast the water is moving at the surface.

- **Hold a hollow L-shaped tube** in the water, with the **opening facing upstream (into the current)**. The water rushes into the tube and rises to a certain height because the moving water has *kinetic energy* which gets converted into *pressure energy* (potential energy of the column). The faster the flow, the higher the water rises.
- **Now, take another tube** with its opening **perpendicular to the flow** (facing sideways), so water simply glides past it. This measures only the "background" pressure of the river — the **static pressure**.
- **The difference in heights** between these two tubes tells you *only* the part of pressure caused by the river's motion — that is, the **dynamic pressure**, from which the velocity can be calculated.

A Pitot tube is essentially this pair of tubes used in engineering to measure the velocity of a fluid in a pipe, duct, or open channel — without obstructing the main flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note for KTU Answers** : The Pitot tube was invented by French hydraulic engineer **Henri Pitot in 1732** to measure the flow velocity of the Seine River. This is a frequently asked 1-mark or 2-mark question in KTU university exams.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity** : $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ (standard value used unless otherwise specified)
- **Density of water** : $\rho_{water} = 1000 \; kg/m^3$
- **Specific weight of water** : $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \; N/m^3$
- **Density of mercury** : $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \; kg/m^3$ (used as manometric fluid)
- **Mercury specific gravity** : $S_{Hg} = 13.6$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Underlying Physics

A Pitot tube works on the intersection of **two fundamental fluid mechanics principles**:

1. **Continuity Principle** : When the tube opening is directly aligned with the streamline (pointing upstream), the oncoming fluid is forced to decelerate to zero velocity exactly at the entrance. This creates a *stagnation point*.
2. **Bernoulli's Principle (Energy Conservation)** : As the velocity drops to zero, the kinetic energy does not vanish — it is converted entirely into pressure energy. This pressure rises above the normal static pressure of the flow.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Flow

- **Step 1 : Identification of Two Measurement Points**
  - *Point 1* : Located upstream of the tube opening, in the undisturbed flow region. Here, fluid has pressure $P_1$ (static pressure) and velocity $V_1$ (the velocity we wish to measure).
  - *Point 2* : Located precisely at the mouth of the Pitot tube. Here, the fluid is brought to rest isentropically, so $V_2 = 0$, and the pressure rises to $P_2$ (stagnation pressure).

- **Step 2 : Application of Bernoulli's Equation**
  - Assumptions: **Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline flow** with no external work or heat transfer.

- **Step 3 : Pressure Difference Calculation**
  - The difference $(P_2 - P_1)$ represents the pressure rise purely due to conversion of kinetic energy to pressure energy.

- **Step 4 : Velocity Determination**
  - Solve Bernoulli's equation for $V_1$ in terms of the measurable pressure difference.

- **Step 5 : Manometric Substitution (For Practical Use)**
  - Since $(P_2 - P_1)$ is small, a differential U-tube manometer is typically used. Substitute the manometric head $h$ into the velocity formula.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme — Master These.**

| S. No. | Formula | Description / KTU Use Case | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Stagnation pressure relation (ideal Pitot) | $Pa$ or $N/m^2$ |
| 2 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$ | Velocity in terms of pressure difference (ideal) | $m/s$ |
| 3 | $V = \sqrt{2g\,h}$ | Velocity using piezometric head difference | $m/s$ |
| 4 | $V = \sqrt{2g\,h\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)}$ | Velocity using U-tube manometer with heavier fluid | $m/s$ |
| 5 | $V = C_v \sqrt{2g\,h}$ | Velocity with Pitot tube coefficient $C_v$ (real Pitot) | $m/s$ |
| 6 | $P_0 - P = \rho g h$ | Dynamic pressure in terms of head | $Pa$ |
| 7 | $h = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Velocity head (numerically equals dynamic head) | $m$ of fluid |
| 8 | $V = C_v \sqrt{2g\,h\left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}$ | General form for any manometric fluid | $m/s$ |
| 9 | $Q = A \cdot V_{avg}$ | For finding discharge if area $A$ known | $m^3/s$ |
| 10 | $V_{avg} = 0.84\,V_{max}$ | For turbulent flow in pipes (useful in numerical) | $m/s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams** : "SP + DP = TP" — **S**tatic Pressure + **D**ynamic Pressure = **T**otal (Stagnation) Pressure. The Pitot tube is essentially a device that isolates the **DP** by measuring TP and SP separately.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aviation Industry** : The Pitot-static system is the primary airspeed indicator for aircraft. The pitot tube measures total air pressure while static ports measure ambient atmospheric pressure. The difference gives dynamic pressure, which is calibrated to display Indicated Airspeed (IAS).
- **Hydraulic Engineering** : Used in large penstocks and water supply mains to measure instantaneous flow velocity profiles.
- **Wind Engineering** : Cup anemometers are often paired with pitot-static probes for precise wind tunnel measurements.
- **Automotive Testing** : Used in wind tunnels for testing car aerodynamics and drag coefficients.
- **Industrial Process Lines** : For non-intrusive velocity profiling in chemical and oil-gas pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Pitot Tube over Venturimeter?** The Pitot tube measures velocity at a **single point** (a local measurement), whereas venturimeters, orifice meters, and notches measure the **total discharge** of a flow. Pitot tubes cause **minimal pressure loss** (≈ 1–2% of venturimeter loss) because the flow is not significantly obstructed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity from Pressure Difference (Ideal Pitot Tube)

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Problem

Consider a fluid flowing in a pipe or open stream with a uniform velocity $V$ at a point. A Pitot tube is inserted into the flow with its opening facing directly upstream along the streamline.

Let us define two key points along the same streamline:

- **Point 1** : Located at distance upstream, in the *undisturbed* region of the flow.
  - Pressure = $P_1$ (static pressure)
  - Velocity = $V_1$ (this is the velocity we want to measure)
  - Elevation = $z_1$

- **Point 2** : Located exactly at the mouth/opening of the Pitot tube (stagnation point).
  - Pressure = $P_2$ (stagnation pressure)
  - Velocity = $V_2 = 0$ (fluid is brought to rest isentropically)
  - Elevation = $z_2$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

We apply **Bernoulli's equation** along the streamline connecting Point 1 and Point 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 \\
&\text{(Bernoulli's equation between Point 1 and Point 2)} \\
&\text{Since the Pitot tube is small, elevation difference is negligible: } z_1 = z_2 \\
&\text{Substituting } V_2 = 0 : \quad \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0 + z_1 \\
&\text{Cancelling } z_1 \text{ on both sides :} \quad \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} \\
&\text{Subtracting } \frac{P_1}{\rho g} \text{ from both sides :} \quad \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} \\
&\text{Multiplying both sides by } 2g : \quad V_1^2 = \frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho} \\
&\text{Taking the square root :} \quad V_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}} \\
&\text{Or equivalently :} \quad V_1 = \sqrt{2g\left(\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g}\right)} = \sqrt{2gh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $h = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g}$ is the pressure head difference in metres of the flowing fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result** : $\boxed{V = \sqrt{2g\,h}}$ — This is the most frequently tested formula in KTU Module 2 university examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity Using a Differential U-tube Manometer

In practical engineering applications, the pressure difference $(P_2 - P_1)$ is small and difficult to measure directly with a pressure gauge. Therefore, a **differential U-tube manometer** containing a heavier fluid (typically mercury, or carbon tetrachloride for gases) is connected between the stagnation and static pressure taps.

### 3.2.1 Setup

- Let $h$ = manometric deflection of the heavier fluid (in metres).
- Let $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid (heavier).
- Let $\rho$ = density of the flowing fluid.
- Let $S_m = \rho_m / \rho_w$ = specific gravity of manometric fluid.
- Let $S = \rho / \rho_w$ = specific gravity of flowing fluid.

### 3.2.2 Pressure Balance Across the Manometer

Consider the U-tube manometer connected to the Pitot-static tube. Apply the principle of hydrostatic pressure balance from the high-pressure leg, across the manometric fluid, to the low-pressure leg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&P_2 - P_1 = (\rho_m - \rho)\,g\,h \\
&\text{(Pressure difference across a differential manometer)} \\
&\text{But we also know from Bernoulli's equation that :} \quad P_2 - P_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \\
&\text{Equating the two :} \quad \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = (\rho_m - \rho)\,g\,h \\
&\text{Solving for } V^2 : \quad V^2 = \frac{2(\rho_m - \rho)\,g\,h}{\rho} \\
&\text{Therefore :} \quad V = \sqrt{\frac{2(\rho_m - \rho)\,g\,h}{\rho}} \\
&\text{Introducing specific gravities } S_m = \rho_m/\rho_w \text{ and } S = \rho/\rho_w : \\
&\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho} = \frac{S_m - S}{S} = \left(\frac{S_m}{S} - 1\right) \\
&\text{Substituting :} \quad \boxed{V = \sqrt{2g\,h\left(\frac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exams** : When the manometric fluid is **mercury** and the flowing fluid is **water**, substitute $S_m = 13.6$ and $S = 1.0$ directly into the boxed formula above.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Incorporating the Pitot Tube Coefficient ($C_v$)

In real (practical) Pitot tubes, the flow is **not perfectly isentropic**. There are small losses due to viscosity, turbulence, and imperfect alignment with the streamlines. To account for this, a **coefficient of velocity** $C_v$ is introduced.

The general governing equation becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&V_{actual} = C_v \cdot V_{ideal} \\
&\text{Substituting the ideal result :} \quad V_{actual} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2g\,h\left(\frac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)} \\
&\text{Where } C_v \text{ is typically in the range } 0.95 \text{ to } 0.99 \text{ for well-designed Pitot-static tubes.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake** : Students often confuse $C_v$ (coefficient of velocity) with $C_d$ (coefficient of discharge). In Pitot tubes, only $C_v$ is used because the Pitot tube measures **velocity at a point**, not a **discharge**. There is **no $C_d$** for Pitot tubes in KTU syllabus.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem** : A Pitot-static tube is inserted in a pipe conveying water. The stagnation pressure and static pressure are connected to a differential mercury manometer which shows a deflection of $h = 12 \; cm$. Calculate the velocity of flow at the centre of the pipe. Take $S_{Hg} = 13.6$, $S_{water} = 1.0$, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$.

### Solution

**Given Data**
- Manometric deflection, $h = 12 \; cm = 0.12 \; m$
- Specific gravity of mercury, $S_m = 13.6$
- Specific gravity of water, $S = 1.0$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- Coefficient of velocity, $C_v = 1.0$ (assumed ideal unless stated otherwise)

**Formula to Use**

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2g\,h\left(\frac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)}
$$

**Step-by-Step Substitution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 1.0 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 \times \left(\frac{13.6}{1.0} - 1\right)} \\
&= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 \times (13.6 - 1)} \\
&= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 \times 12.6} \\
&= \sqrt{2.3544 \times 12.6} \\
&= \sqrt{29.6654} \\
&= 5.447 \; m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer** : $\boxed{V \approx 5.45 \; m/s}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pitot-Static Tube Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a Pitot-static tube, showing the flow of fluid, the stagnation mechanism, and the manometric pressure measurement loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flow Stream with Velocity V] --> B[Undisturbed Region Point 1]
    B -- Static Pressure P1 --> C[Pitot Tube Bend Curve]
    A --> D[Stagnation Point Point 2 at Tube Mouth]
    D -- Stagnation Pressure P0 --> C
    C --> E[Inner Tube - Measures Stagnation Pressure]
    C --> F[Outer Tube - Measures Static Pressure]
    E --> G[High Pressure Leg of Manometer]
    F --> H[Low Pressure Leg of Manometer]
    G --> I[Differential U-tube Manometer]
    H --> I
    I -- Deflection h --> J[Apply Bernoulli Equation]
    J --> K[Calculate Velocity V = sqrt 2gh]
    K --> L[Apply Specific Gravity Correction V = sqrt 2gh SmS minus 1]
    L --> M[Apply Coefficient of Velocity Cv]
    M --> N[Final Velocity V actual in m per s]
    N --> O[Optional Compute Discharge Q equals A times V]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Pressure Variation Along a Pitot Tube Streamline

This diagram illustrates the **physics of stagnation** — how static, dynamic, and stagnation pressures transform as fluid approaches the tube mouth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point 1 - Undisturbed Flow] -- Static Pressure P --> Q[Point 1.5 - Approaching Tube]
    Q -- Velocity Decreases --> R[Point 1.8 - Near Tube Mouth]
    R -- Dynamic Pressure Converts to Static --> S[Point 2 - Stagnation Point]
    S -- V equals 0 --> T[Pressure rises to P0 equals P plus half rho V squared]
    T --> U[This pressure is transmitted up the Pitot tube to the manometer]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input_Side
        A1[Main Flow Pipe] --> A2[Flow Velocity V to be Measured]
    end
    subgraph Measurement_Probe
        B1[Pitot Tube Stagnation Opening] -- Measures P0 --> B2[Central Inner Tube]
        B3[Static Pressure Holes on Sleeve] -- Measures P --> B4[Outer Annular Space]
    end
    subgraph Signal_Transmission
        B2 --> C1[High Pressure Connection Line]
        B4 --> C2[Low Pressure Connection Line]
        C1 --> D1[Left Limb of Manometer]
        C2 --> D2[Right Limb of Manometer]
    end
    subgraph Output_Stage
        D1 --> E1[Manometric Fluid Mercury Rises by h on Right Side]
        D2 --> E1
        E1 --> F1[Read Deflection h]
        F1 --> F2[Apply V equals Cv sqrt 2gh SmS minus 1]
        F2 --> F3[Final Velocity in m per s]
        F3 --> F4[Optional Discharge Q equals A times V]
    end
```

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Pitot Tube vs. Other Flow Devices

| Feature | Pitot Tube | Venturimeter | Orifice Meter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Measures** | Local velocity | Total discharge | Total discharge |
| **Pressure Loss** | Negligible | Low | Moderate |
| **Installation Cost** | Low | High | Low |
| **Range** | Point measurement | Full flow | Full flow |
| **KTU Weightage** | Moderate-High | Very High | Very High |
| **Coefficient Used** | $C_v$ only | $C_d$ | $C_d$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** : Define a Pitot tube and state the principle on which it works.

**Model Answer** :
A **Pitot tube** is a device used to measure the local velocity of fluid flow at a specific point in a stream. It works on the principle of conversion of **kinetic energy of the flowing fluid into pressure energy** by bringing the fluid to rest isentropically at a stagnation point. The resulting stagnation pressure is measured and compared with the static pressure of the undisturbed flow. The difference between the two gives the dynamic pressure, from which the velocity is calculated using $V = \sqrt{2gh}$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** : Differentiate between static pressure, dynamic pressure, and stagnation pressure.

**Model Answer** :

| Pressure Type | Definition | Value at Stagnation Point |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static Pressure ($P$)** | Pressure exerted by fluid in true motion; the thermodynamic pressure of the flowing fluid. | Equals stagnation pressure minus dynamic pressure |
| **Dynamic Pressure ($\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$)** | Pressure equivalent of the kinetic energy of the fluid per unit volume. | Zero (since $V = 0$) |
| **Stagnation Pressure ($P_0$)** | Pressure at a point where the fluid is brought to rest isentropically. | Maximum (sum of static + dynamic) |

**Key Equation** : $P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Full Descriptive

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** :

**(a) [7 Marks]** : Derive an expression for the velocity of flow at a point using a Pitot tube. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** : A Pitot tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. The difference of pressure head recorded is $15 \; cm$ of water. If the Pitot tube coefficient is $0.98$, determine the velocity of flow. Take $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Assumptions** :
1. The fluid is **incompressible** and flow is **steady**.
2. The flow is **frictionless** (inviscid) along the streamline considered.
3. The Pitot tube is aligned perfectly with the streamline (no angular deviation).
4. The elevation difference between the upstream point and stagnation point is **negligible**.

**Derivation** :

Consider two points along a streamline in the flow:
- **Point 1** : Undisturbed flow region upstream of the Pitot tube.
  - Pressure = $P_1$, Velocity = $V_1$, Elevation = $z_1$
- **Point 2** : Stagnation point at the mouth of the Pitot tube.
  - Pressure = $P_2$, Velocity = $V_2 = 0$, Elevation = $z_2$

Applying Bernoulli's equation between Point 1 and Point 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 \\
&\text{Since } V_2 = 0 \text{ and } z_1 = z_2 : \\
&\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} \\
&\frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} \\
&V_1^2 = \frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho} \\
&\boxed{V_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the pressure difference is expressed in terms of head $h$ of the flowing fluid:
$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2gh}}$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data** :
- Pressure head difference, $h = 15 \; cm = 0.15 \; m$
- Coefficient of Pitot tube, $C_v = 0.98$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$

**Formula** :

$$
V = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2g\,h}
$$

**Calculation** :

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15} \\
&= 0.98 \times \sqrt{2.943} \\
&= 0.98 \times 1.7155 \\
&= 1.681 \; m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer** : $\boxed{V = 1.681 \; m/s}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper]** :

**(a) [7 Marks]** : With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a Pitot-static tube. How is it different from a simple Pitot tube?

**(b) [7 Marks]** : A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of air in a duct. The static and stagnation pressures are connected to a differential water manometer which shows a reading of $3 \; mm$. The air density is $1.2 \; kg/m^3$ and water density is $1000 \; kg/m^3$. Calculate the air velocity. Take $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Pitot-Static Tube — Working Principle** :

A **Pitot-static tube** is a combination of two concentric tubes:
- **Inner Tube** : Has a small opening at the front, directly facing the flow. This measures the **stagnation pressure** ($P_0$) since the fluid is brought to rest at this opening.
- **Outer Tube** : Has small radial holes on the cylindrical surface, perpendicular to the flow direction. These holes measure the **static pressure** ($P$) of the undisturbed flow, since the flow glides tangentially past the holes without entering them.

Both pressures are transmitted separately to a **differential manometer**, which measures the pressure difference $(P_0 - P)$. This difference equals the dynamic pressure, which is then used to compute the local flow velocity using Bernoulli's equation.

**Differences between Simple Pitot Tube and Pitot-Static Tube** :

| Feature | Simple Pitot Tube | Pitot-Static Tube |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tubes Required** | One tube + a separate static tap | Single instrument with both tubes combined |
| **Pressure Measured** | Only stagnation pressure | Both static and stagnation pressures |
| **Construction** | Simple L-shaped tube | Concentric double tube with static holes |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (depends on separate tap alignment) | Higher (both measurements from same point) |
| **Practical Use** | Rare in modern applications | Standard in aviation and industry |

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data** :
- Manometric deflection, $h = 3 \; mm = 0.003 \; m$
- Density of air, $\rho_{air} = 1.2 \; kg/m^3$
- Density of water, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \; kg/m^3$
- Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- Coefficient of Pitot tube, $C_v = 1.0$ (assumed)

**Formula for velocity with manometric fluid** :

$$
V = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2g\,h\left(\frac{\rho_{water}}{\rho_{air}} - 1\right)}
$$

**Substitution** :

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 1.0 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.003 \times \left(\frac{1000}{1.2} - 1\right)} \\
&= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.003 \times (833.33 - 1)} \\
&= \sqrt{0.05886 \times 832.33} \\
&= \sqrt{48.99} \\
&\approx 6.999 \; m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer** : $\boxed{V \approx 7.0 \; m/s}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder** : Students often forget to convert **mm to m** for the manometric deflection $h$. This is the **#1 cause of wrong answers** in KTU Module 2 numericals. Always write $h$ in **metres** before substitution.
> 2. **Confusing $\rho$ and $\rho_m$** : In the formula $V = \sqrt{2gh(S_m/S - 1)}$, $\rho$ is the density of the **flowing fluid** and $\rho_m$ is the density of the **manometric fluid**. Reversing them gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_v$** : When the problem states "coefficient of Pitot tube", students often write $C_d$ (coefficient of discharge) instead. The correct notation is **$C_v$ only**.
> 4. **Neglecting Elevation Difference** : In derivations, students sometimes forget to write "$z_1 = z_2$" which is a critical step in Bernoulli's application. Examiners deduct marks for missing this.
> 5. **Static Pressure Tap Blockage** : In real applications, blocked static holes give wrong readings. This is a common viva question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pitot tube = Point velocity measurement** device, not a discharge measuring device.
- **Inventor** : Henri Pitot (1732), originally for the Seine River in France.
- **Working Principle** : Conversion of kinetic energy (dynamic head) into pressure energy (stagnation head).
- **Key Equation** : $P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — the **fundamentally tested** relation in KTU exams.
- **Most Used Formula** : $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh\left(\dfrac{S_m}{S} - 1\right)}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Bernoulli's Assumptions** : Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no external work.
- **Pitot-Static Tube** : Two concentric tubes — inner for stagnation, outer (with radial holes) for static pressure.
- **Coefficient of Velocity ($C_v$)** : Only $C_v$ is used for Pitot tubes; $C_d$ is **not** applicable.
- **Manometric Fluid** : Mercury ($S = 13.6$) for water/air measurements; CCl₄ for low-pressure gas systems.
- **Pressure Loss** : Negligible (≈ 1% only) — this is the **biggest advantage** of a Pitot tube over a venturimeter.
- **Application** : Aviation (airspeed indicator), hydraulic engineering, wind tunnels, industrial flow monitoring.
- **Sign Convention** : $P_0 > P$ always, so $h > 0$ in the manometer.
- **Average vs Maximum Velocity** : In turbulent flow, $V_{avg} \approx 0.84 \cdot V_{max}$ (important for discharge calculations).
- **Static Pressure Hole Location** : Must be at least 6–8 tube diameters downstream of the stagnation opening to avoid flow disturbance.
- **Real Pitot Coefficient** : For well-designed Pitot-static tubes, $C_v = 0.95$ to $0.99$; for simple L-tubes, $C_v \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$.
- **Quick Memory Hook** : "Pitot = Point, Piezometer = Pressure, Venturi = Volume" — keeps all three devices distinct in your mind.
- **KTU Board Tip** : Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** of the Pitot tube with both static and stagnation taps marked. Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for a well-labelled figure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orificemeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Orificemeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Orificemeter (Orifice Plate Meter):** A classical, primary flow-measuring device installed concentrically inside a pipeline. It consists of a thin, flat circular plate with a sharp-edged, centrally bored circular hole (the **orifice**), clamped between the pipe flanges. The device works on the principle of producing a measurable **pressure drop** as fluid is forced to converge through the smaller orifice opening, from which the volumetric/steady-state discharge through the pipe can be inferred using Bernoulli's equation and a calibrated **coefficient of discharge**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCSFT303 – Module 2: Flow Measurement), the orificemeter is grouped under the family of *obstruction-type flowmeters* and is treated as a fundamental diagnostic device in hydraulic engineering. It is mathematically analogous to a Venturimeter but is preferred in industrial practice for its **low cost**, **ease of fabrication**, and **easy replacement**.

The three primary geometric variants of orifice plates that a KTU student is expected to recognize are:

1. **Concentric Orifice Plate** – the standard plate; the orifice is concentric with the pipe axis. Used for clean liquids, gases, and steam in straight, unobstructed pipelines.
2. **Eccentric Orifice Plate** – the orifice center is shifted to the inner wall of the pipe. Used when the fluid carries suspended solids or slurries to prevent debris accumulation upstream of the plate.
3. **Segmental Orifice Plate** – the orifice is a segment of a circle, opening flush with the inner pipe wall. Also used for dirty fluids and slurries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Garden Hose Squeeze":** Imagine holding a garden hose and squeezing the opening with your thumb. The water sprays out faster, but the pressure inside the hose (just before the squeeze) shoots up, while the pressure right at the squeezed opening is nearly atmospheric. A similar phenomenon occurs in an orificemeter: the constriction at the orifice plate causes a **pressure drop** (technically, a *piezometric head* difference) that is directly related to how fast the fluid is moving. The orificemeter simply measures that pressure drop and translates it into flow rate.

**Why it works (the "Physical Why"):**
- According to the **continuity equation**, when the cross-section is suddenly reduced, the velocity must increase.
- By **Bernoulli's principle**, an increase in velocity must be accompanied by a decrease in static pressure.
- The larger the flow rate, the bigger the velocity jump, and therefore the larger the pressure drop.
- This pressure drop is measured using a **differential manometer** (typically a U-tube containing mercury for liquids, or a special manometer for gases) connected at two tapping points — one upstream (before the plate) and one downstream (at the **vena contracta**, where the jet is narrowest).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term — Vena Contracta:** The point in the flow, slightly downstream of the orifice, where the jet area is minimum and the streamlines become parallel. The ratio of the jet area at the vena contracta to the orifice area is called the **coefficient of contraction (Cc)**. KTU examinations frequently test this definition.

## 1.3 Important Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (standard value used universally in KTU numericals)
- **Specific weight of mercury (S_Hg):** **13.6 × 10³ N/m³** (for U-tube manometer calculations)
- **Specific weight of water (S_w):** **9.81 × 10³ N/m³** at standard conditions
- **Standard pipe tapping locations:** as per ISO 5167 — *flange taps*, *D and D/2 taps*, or *corner taps*
- **Orifice diameter (d)** is typically **0.3 to 0.8 times** the pipe diameter (D) to ensure a stable vena contracta.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The flow phenomenon through an orificemeter can be broken into **five logical stages**:

1. **Approach Flow (Upstream of the Plate):**
   The fluid approaches the orifice plate with a uniform velocity $V_1$ across the full pipe cross-section $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$. The static pressure at the upstream tapping point (section 1–1) is $p_1$.

2. **Convergence Through the Orifice:**
   The fluid streamlines converge as they squeeze through the smaller orifice area $a = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$. Due to the inertia of the fluid, the streamlines do not make a sharp 90° turn; instead, they curve smoothly, continuing to contract for a short distance downstream of the plate.

3. **Formation of the Vena Contracta (Section 2–2):**
   At a distance of approximately **0.5 d to 0.7 d** downstream of the orifice, the jet reaches its minimum cross-sectional area $a_c = C_c \cdot a$. The fluid velocity here, $V_2$, is at its maximum, and the static pressure $p_2$ is at its minimum (the lowest pressure in the entire system).

4. **Pressure Recovery (Downstream Recovery):**
   Beyond the vena contracta, the streamlines diverge again to fill the pipe, and the jet expands back to the full pipe area. The velocity decreases, and the pressure partially recovers. However, due to frictional and turbulent losses, the recovered pressure is **always less** than the upstream pressure $p_1$. The net unrecovered head is called the **head loss** $h_L$.

5. **Flow Rate Determination:**
   A differential manometer measures $(p_1 - p_2)$, and the discharge $Q$ is calculated using the discharge equation derived from Bernoulli's principle, modified by a **coefficient of discharge (Cd)** to account for real-fluid effects.

## 2.2 Three Critical Coefficients — The "Holy Trinity" of Orifice Theory

A KTU answer book is considered incomplete if these three coefficients are not properly defined:

- **Coefficient of Velocity (Cv):** The ratio of the actual velocity at the vena contracta to the theoretical (ideal) velocity. It accounts for friction losses.
  $$C_v = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

- **Coefficient of Contraction (Cc):** The ratio of the area of the jet at the vena contracta to the area of the orifice.
  $$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a} = \frac{\text{Vena contracta area}}{\text{Orifice area}}$$

- **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** The combined effect of $C_v$ and $C_c$, representing the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge.
  $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$

Typical values for a sharp-edged, concentric orifice plate:
- $C_c \approx 0.61$ to **0.65**
- $C_v \approx 0.95$ to **0.98**
- $C_d \approx 0.60$ to **0.65**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Theoretical velocity (Torricelli-derived) | $V_{\text{th}} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | $H$ = piezometric head difference |
| 2 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{\text{th}} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Ideal (frictionless) case |
| 3 | Actual discharge | $Q_{\text{act}} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | **Master equation** — most used |
| 4 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | Combined efficiency factor |
| 5 | Differential manometer head | $H = \frac{x \cdot (S_m/S_f - 1)}{1 - (d_o^4 / D^4)}$ | m of fluid | For U-tube with specific gravity $S_m$ |
| 6 | Manometer deflection formula | $H = x \left( \frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1 \right)$ | m of fluid | Approximation (ignores $\beta^4$ term) |
| 7 | Diameter ratio | $\beta = d / D$ | dimensionless | Usually $0.3 \le \beta \le 0.8$ |
| 8 | Area ratio | $m = a/A = (d/D)^2 = \beta^2$ | dimensionless | Used in velocity-of-approach correction |
| 9 | Head loss (approx.) | $h_L = \dfrac{H}{C_d^2} (1 - C_d^2) \cdot \dfrac{1}{1}$ | m | Simplified form: $h_L \approx H \cdot (1 - C_d^2)$ |
| 10 | Vena contracta area | $a_c = C_c \cdot a = C_c \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | m² | Geometric definition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above table, $x$ = manometer reading (m), $S_m$ = specific gravity of manometric fluid, $S_f$ = specific gravity of flowing fluid, $D$ = pipe diameter, $d$ = orifice diameter, $d_o$ = diameter of pressure tappings, $a$ = orifice area, $A$ = pipe area.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Industries:** Orifice plates are the **workhorse** of custody transfer flow measurement, used in metering stations across Kerala's Cochin Refineries and the IOCs.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Used in raw water intake lines, filter backwash lines, and chemical dosing lines.
- **HVAC Systems:** Measures chilled water and condenser water flow in large commercial buildings.
- **Steam and Boiler Feedwater:** When used with steam, the manometric fluid is replaced by a special arrangement since mercury-vapor contamination is hazardous.
- **KTU Industrial Visit Context:** In Kerala, you may encounter orifice meters in the **Idukki hydroelectric power house** flow-monitoring systems and in the **Kerala Water Authority** distribution networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Discharge Equation — The Complete KTU Standard

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 2 of PCSFT303. Master this and you will secure full marks on the orificemeter sub-question.

### **Setup and Assumptions:**

- Steady, incompressible, inviscid (theoretical) flow.
- Flow is along a streamline from point (1) — just upstream of the orifice plate — to point (2) — at the vena contracta.
- Sections 1 and 2 are taken where the streamlines are straight and parallel, satisfying the conditions for Bernoulli's equation.
- Let $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$ be the pipe area, $a_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$ be the orifice area, and $V_1$, $V_2$ be the corresponding velocities.
- Let $p_1$, $p_2$ be the static pressures, and $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal pipe).

### **Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Section (1) and Section (2)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the pipe is horizontal, $z_1 = z_2$. Cancelling these terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} &= \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging to isolate the head difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} &= \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining the **piezometric head difference** as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g}
\end{aligned}
$$

We get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 2: Apply the Continuity Equation**

For an incompressible fluid, mass in = mass out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 \cdot V_1 &= a \cdot V_{\text{th}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $a$ is the orifice area and $V_{\text{th}}$ is the theoretical velocity at the orifice (assuming no contraction). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= V_{\text{th}} \cdot \frac{a}{A_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3: Solve for the Theoretical Velocity**

Substituting the continuity relation into the Bernoulli-derived head difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{V_{\text{th}}^2 - V_{\text{th}}^2 \cdot (a/A_1)^2}{2g} \\[6pt]
2gH &= V_{\text{th}}^2 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{a}{A_1}\right)^2 \right] \\[6pt]
V_{\text{th}}^2 &= \frac{2gH}{1 - (a/A_1)^2} \\[6pt]
V_{\text{th}} &= \sqrt{ \frac{2gH}{1 - (a/A_1)^2} }
\end{aligned}
$$

If we **neglect the velocity of approach** (i.e., assume $a/A_1 \to 0$, valid for small orifices relative to pipe size), this simplifies to the famous **Torricelli result**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{th}} &= \sqrt{2gH}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4: Compute the Theoretical Discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{th}} &= a \cdot V_{\text{th}} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5: Apply Empirical Correction Factors to Get Actual Discharge**

In real flow:
- The fluid does not achieve the full theoretical velocity due to friction → multiply by $C_v$.
- The jet contracts downstream of the orifice, so the effective discharge area is $C_c \cdot a$ → multiply by $C_c$.
- Hence, the **actual discharge** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{act}} &= (C_c \cdot a) \cdot (C_v \cdot V_{\text{th}}) \\[6pt]
Q_{\text{act}} &= C_c \cdot C_v \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH} \\[6pt]
Q_{\text{act}} &= C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_d &= C_c \cdot C_v
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master discharge equation for an orificemeter** and is the answer the KTU examiner expects.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Template

The following matrix summarizes the standard approach a KTU student should use when solving an orificemeter numerical:

| Step | What to Do | Key Formula |
|------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Identify the flowing fluid and its specific gravity $S_f$ | $S_f = \rho_f / \rho_w$ |
| 2 | Identify the manometric fluid and its specific gravity $S_m$ | $S_m$ for mercury = 13.6 |
| 3 | Convert the manometer reading $x$ to piezometric head $H$ | $H = x(S_m/S_f - 1)$ |
| 4 | Compute the orifice area $a$ | $a = (\pi/4) \cdot d^2$ |
| 5 | Plug into master equation | $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ |
| 6 | Optional: compute the head loss | $h_L = H(1 - C_d^2)$ |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Model):** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 200 mm fitted with a sharp-edged concentric orifice of diameter 100 mm. A differential U-tube mercury manometer connected across the orifice shows a deflection of 250 mm. The coefficient of discharge is 0.62. Calculate the discharge of water in litres per second.

**Given:**
- $D = 200$ mm $= 0.20$ m
- $d = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- $x = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m
- $C_d = 0.62$
- $S_m = 13.6$ (mercury)
- $S_f = 1.0$ (water)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Piezometric head difference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= x \left( \frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1 \right) = 0.25 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.25 \times 12.6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 3.15 \text{ m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Orifice area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH} = 0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.15} \\[6pt]
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.15} &= \sqrt{61.803} = 7.862 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
Q &= 0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.862 \\[6pt]
Q &= 38.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 38.27 \text{ L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The discharge is approximately **38.27 L/s**.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Orificemeter System

The diagram below illustrates the flow of fluid, pressure differential extraction, and the manometric readout chain of an orificemeter system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PIPE INLET\nUpstream Section 1-1\nDiameter D, Area A1"]
    node2["ORIFICE PLATE\nSharp Edged\nOrifice dia d, Area a"]
    node3["VENA CONTRACTA\nSection 2-2\nMin jet area ac = Cc x a"]
    node4["PRESSURE RECOVERY\nDownstream Section\nPressure partially recovered"]
    node5["PIPE OUTLET\nFull bore restored"]
    node6["UPSTREAM TAPPING\nPressure p1 measured"]
    node7["DOWNSTREAM TAPPING\nPressure p2 measured"]
    node8["U-TUBE MANOMETER\nMercury column\nDeflection x"]
    node9["MANOMETER READOUT\nH computed = x (Sm/Sf -1)"]
    node10["DISCHARGE COMPUTATION\nQ = Cd x a x sqrt 2gH"]

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5
    node6 -. p1 .-> node1
    node7 -. p2 .-> node3
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Pressure-Energy Conversion Logic

The diagram below shows the *cause-and-effect* sequence as the fluid traverses the orificemeter, mapping how static pressure converts to kinetic energy and back.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stageA["STAGE 1: APPROACH FLOW\nStatic Pressure p1 HIGH\nVelocity V1 LOW\nTotal Energy = Constant"]
    stageB["STAGE 2: CONVERGENCE\nCross section SHRINKS\nVelocity RISES\nStatic Pressure FALLS"]
    stageC["STAGE 3: VENA CONTRACTA\nMinimum Area ac\nVelocity V2 MAXIMUM\nStatic Pressure p2 MINIMUM"]
    stageD["STAGE 4: EXPANSION\nCross section ENLARGES\nVelocity DROPS\nStatic Pressure RISES but BELOW p1"]
    stageE["STAGE 5: PIPE RECOVERY\nLost head = hL\nEnergy equation:\nEnergy_in = Energy_out + hL"]

    stageA --> stageB --> stageC --> stageD --> stageE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Orifice vs. Nozzle vs. Venturi

This comparative diagram helps the student understand how the orificemeter differs from its close relatives — a frequently asked question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FAMILY ["FLOW OBSTRUCTION METERS"]
        direction TB
        ORIFICE["ORIFICEMETER\nCd = 0.60 to 0.65\nHead loss = HIGH\nCost = LOW\nAccuracy = MODERATE"]
        NOZZLE["FLOW NOZZLE\nCd = 0.93 to 0.98\nHead loss = MODERATE\nCost = MODERATE\nAccuracy = HIGH"]
        VENTURI["VENTURIMETER\nCd = 0.95 to 0.99\nHead loss = LOW\nCost = HIGH\nAccuracy = VERY HIGH"]
    end
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic of the Orifice Assembly

For situations where Mermaid rendering is limited, the following inline schematic describes the physical arrangement:

```
   ←——— Direction of Flow ———→
   
   Pipe Wall  |======================| Pipe Wall
              |                      |
              |    p1 (UPSTREAM)     |  ← Tapping Point (1)
              |                      |
              |   ┌──────────────┐   |  
              |   │  ORIFICE     │   |  ← Sharp-edged Plate
              |   │   (dia d)    │   |
              |   └──────────────┘   |
              |         ↓            |
              |     Jet Contracts    |  ← Vena Contracta (sec 2-2)
              |       (ac < a)       |
              |                      |  ← Tapping Point (2)
              |    p2 (DOWNSTREAM)   |
              |                      |
   Pipe Wall  |======================| Pipe Wall
   
   U-Tube Manometer Below:
   (mercury)     |  |          |  |
                |  |  Hg level|  |
   water ——>    |  |__ x __|  |  ←<— water ——>
                |             |
              Section 1     Section 2
```
