# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) for an orificemeter and state its typical range of values.**

**Model Answer:**

The **coefficient of discharge** of an orificemeter is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge of fluid flowing through the orifice to the theoretical discharge that would occur under ideal (frictionless, no-contraction) conditions. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = C_c \cdot C_v
\end{aligned}
$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity, since it is a ratio of two discharges having the same units. The typical range of $C_d$ for a sharp-edged concentric orifice plate is **0.60 to 0.65**. Lower values of $C_d$ indicate greater losses and contraction; higher values indicate the orifice behaves closer to an ideal frictionless device.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is vena contracta? Why does it form?**

**Model Answer:**

The **vena contracta** is the section in the flow downstream of an orifice plate where the cross-sectional area of the fluid jet is **minimum** and the streamlines become essentially straight and parallel. At this section, the static pressure is at its lowest value in the entire system, and the velocity is at its maximum.

**Why it forms:** As the fluid streamlines approach the orifice, they cannot make a sharp 90° turn at the edge of the orifice (due to the fluid's inertia and the curvature of the streamlines). They continue to converge for a short distance beyond the plate until the inward radial momentum is fully converted to axial momentum. Beyond this point, the jet begins to expand again to fill the pipe. The minimum cross-section, the **vena contracta**, is typically located at a distance of **0.5 d to 0.7 d** downstream of the orifice for a sharp-edged plate.

The ratio of vena contracta area to orifice area is defined as the **coefficient of contraction**, $C_c$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently forget to convert the manometer reading $x$ (in mm of mercury) into the piezometric head $H$ (in metres of flowing fluid) using the factor $(S_m/S_f - 1)$. This single error costs **3 to 4 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Many students use the wrong coefficient — confusing $C_c$ with $C_d$. The master equation uses $C_d$, not $C_c$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** When the problem says "diameter of the pipe is 200 mm," many students forget to convert to metres before substituting. Always use **SI units** (m, m², m/s).
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In multi-part questions, students often skip the **head loss** sub-part. Examiner expects the final formula $h_L = H(1 - C_d^2)$ to be derived, not just stated.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In velocity-of-approach correction questions, students forget the term $(1 - \beta^4)$ in the denominator. This term **must** be included whenever the orifice-to-pipe diameter ratio is significant.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

#### (a) Derive the expression for the discharge through an orificemeter, clearly defining all coefficients used. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.*

**Starting point — Bernoulli's equation between upstream section (1) and vena contracta section (2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

For a horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$. Substituting $H = (p_1 - p_2)/\rho g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Applying continuity:** $A_1 V_1 = a V_{\text{th}}$, so $V_1 = V_{\text{th}} (a/A_1)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H = \frac{V_{\text{th}}^2 - V_{\text{th}}^2 (a/A_1)^2}{2g} = \frac{V_{\text{th}}^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{a}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical velocity (neglecting velocity of approach):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{th}} = \sqrt{2gH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical discharge:** $Q_{\text{th}} = a \cdot V_{\text{th}} = a \sqrt{2gH}$.

**Applying empirical coefficients:** The actual jet area is $C_c \cdot a$ and the actual velocity is $C_v \cdot V_{\text{th}}$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{act}} = C_c \cdot a \cdot C_v \cdot \sqrt{2gH} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- **$C_c$** = Coefficient of Contraction = (Vena contracta area) / (Orifice area) = $a_c / a$
- **$C_v$** = Coefficient of Velocity = (Actual velocity at vena contracta) / (Theoretical velocity) = $V_2 / V_{\text{th}}$
- **$C_d$** = Coefficient of Discharge = $C_c \cdot C_v$ ≈ 0.62 for a standard concentric sharp-edged orifice plate

#### (b) A horizontal pipe of 200 mm diameter carries oil (specific gravity 0.85) with a flow rate of 60 litres/second. A sharp-edged orifice of 100 mm diameter is installed. A differential mercury manometer shows a reading of 220 mm. Find: (i) the coefficient of discharge of the orifice, and (ii) the head loss due to the orifice. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 200$ mm $= 0.20$ m, so $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.03142$ m²
- $d = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m, so $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $Q = 60$ L/s $= 0.060$ m³/s
- $x = 220$ mm $= 0.220$ m
- $S_m = 13.6$ (mercury), $S_f = 0.85$ (oil)

**Step 1 — Piezometric head difference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= x \left( \frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1 \right) = 0.220 \times \left( \frac{13.6}{0.85} - 1 \right) = 0.220 \times (16.0 - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= 0.220 \times 15.0 = 3.30 \text{ m of oil}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient of discharge:**

Using the master equation $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.060 &= C_d \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.30} \\[6pt]
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.30} &= \sqrt{64.746} = 8.046 \text{ m/s} \\[6pt]
0.060 &= C_d \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.046 \\[6pt]
0.060 &= C_d \times 0.06319 \\[6pt]
C_d &= \frac{0.060}{0.06319} = 0.9495
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** This unusually high $C_d$ suggests the problem may intend the use of rounded inputs or a different fluid. With cleaner numbers, $C_d$ typically falls in the 0.60–0.65 range. For a real KTU exam, the answer is **$C_d \approx 0.95$** as computed.

**Step 3 — Head loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_L &= H \left( 1 - C_d^2 \right) = 3.30 \times (1 - 0.9016) = 3.30 \times 0.0984
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_L &\approx 0.325 \text{ m of oil}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $C_d \approx 0.95$, (ii) $h_L \approx 0.325$ m of oil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a sharp-edged concentric orifice meter. State the locations of pressure tappings. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

A sharp-edged concentric orifice meter consists of the following components:

1. **Orifice Plate:** A thin circular disc (typically 6 mm to 12 mm thick) made of stainless steel, with a precisely machined sharp-edged concentric hole. The downstream edge of the hole is **square and sharp** to ensure that the boundary layer separates cleanly at the edge, producing a stable vena contracta.

2. **Pipe Flanges:** The orifice plate is clamped between two standard pipe flanges in the pipeline. The plate is held in place by bolts and gaskets to prevent leakage around the edges.

3. **Pressure Tappings:** Two small holes (typically 6 mm to 12 mm diameter) are drilled into the pipe wall — one upstream and one downstream of the orifice plate — to extract static pressure. According to **IS 2952 / ISO 5167**, the standard tapping locations are:
   - **Corner Taps:** Flush with the orifice plate faces.
   - **D and D/2 Taps:** 1 pipe diameter upstream, 0.5 pipe diameter downstream.
   - **Flange Taps:** About 25 mm (1 inch) from the orifice plate faces, on either side.

4. **Differential Manometer:** A U-tube manometer (or a more sophisticated differential pressure transducer in modern systems) is connected across the two tapping points to measure the pressure difference $(p_1 - p_2)$.

**Working Principle:**

When fluid flows through the pipe, the orifice plate forces the streamlines to converge. As the cross-section shrinks from $A_1$ to $a$, the velocity increases (continuity), and the static pressure decreases (Bernoulli). The maximum pressure drop occurs at the vena contracta. A differential manometer measures this pressure drop, which is then related to the discharge via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Compare the orificemeter with a venturimeter on the basis of head loss, cost, $C_d$, and applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Orificemeter | Venturimeter |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Discharge Coefficient ($C_d$)** | 0.60 to 0.65 | 0.95 to 0.99 |
| **Head Loss** | High (significant unrecovered pressure) | Very low (gradual recovery in diverging section) |
| **Cost** | Low — only a thin plate to fabricate | High — requires precision-machined converging and diverging sections |
| **Space Required** | Compact (plate thickness ~10 mm) | Bulky (total length 5 to 10 times pipe diameter) |
| **Maintenance** | Easy to inspect, clean, and replace | Difficult to maintain internal surfaces |
| **Suitability for Dirty Fluids** | Limited (eccentric/segmental plates can handle slurries) | Poor — converging section chokes easily |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (±1 to ±2%) | High (±0.5 to ±1%) |
| **Primary Applications** | Oil & gas custody transfer, water treatment, steam lines | High-precision lab work, large water pipelines, calibration standards |

**Conclusion:** The orificemeter is **economical and compact**, making it the most widely used flow meter in industry. The venturimeter, while more expensive, offers **superior accuracy and minimal head loss**, making it the preferred choice for large water-conveyance systems, hydroelectric installations, and applications where pumping cost is a major concern.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Orificemeter (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** A thin, sharp-edged circular plate with a concentric hole, installed in a pipeline to measure flow rate by creating a measurable pressure drop.

- **Master Discharge Equation:**
  $$Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$
  where $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$.

- **Three Critical Coefficients:**
  - $C_c$ (Coefficient of Contraction) — area ratio at vena contracta. Typical value: **0.61–0.65**.
  - $C_v$ (Coefficient of Velocity) — velocity ratio. Typical value: **0.95–0.98**.
  - $C_d$ (Coefficient of Discharge) — discharge ratio. Typical value: **0.60–0.65**.

- **Vena Contracta:** The point of minimum jet cross-section, located at a distance of **0.5 d to 0.7 d** downstream of the orifice.

- **Manometer Head Formula (U-tube Mercury Manometer):**
  $$H = x \left( \frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1 \right)$$
  - For mercury + water: $H = 12.6 \cdot x$
  - For mercury + oil (SG 0.85): $H \approx 15.0 \cdot x$

- **Standard Tappings (ISO 5167):** Corner, D-D/2, and Flange taps.

- **Three Plate Types:** **Concentric** (clean fluids), **Eccentric** (slurries), **Segmental** (dirty fluids with high solid content).

- **Head Loss due to Orifice:**
  $$h_L = H(1 - C_d^2)$$
  This is the **unrecovered head** that adds to the pumping cost.

- **Velocity of Approach Correction:** When $\beta = d/D$ is significant, the term $(1 - \beta^4)$ must appear in the denominator of the velocity equation.

- **Comparison Anchors:**
  - Orificemeter: $C_d$ low, $h_L$ high, cost low.
  - Venturimeter: $C_d$ high, $h_L$ low, cost high.

- **KTU-Most-Asked Sub-Topics (Memorize These):**
  1. Derivation of $Q = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$.
  2. Numerical on finding $Q$, $C_d$, or $H$ from given data.
  3. Head loss calculation.
  4. Comparison: Orifice vs Nozzle vs Venturi.
  5. Brief notes on the three plate types.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Always convert mm to m and use **g = 9.81 m/s²** (not 10). The manometric head $H$ is in **metres of the flowing fluid**, not in metres of mercury.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Venturimeter** is a horizontal (or inclined) differential pressure flow measuring device specifically engineered to determine the **discharge (rate of flow)** of an incompressible fluid flowing through a closed conduit or pipe. It operates on the classical principle of **Bernoulli's Equation** combined with the **Continuity Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "A Venturimeter is a device used for measuring the rate of flow of a fluid flowing through a closed pipe. It consists of three sections: a short converging part (convergent cone/entry), a cylindrical throat, and a gradually expanding part (divergent cone/exit)."

The fluid enters through the larger cross-section (Inlet), accelerates through the **convergent cone** (angle 19° to 23°, ideally **21°**), passes through the narrow **throat**, and then decelerates through the **divergent cone** (angle 5° to 15°, ideally **7°**) before discharging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU frequently tests the relationship between convergent angle (21°) and divergent angle (7°). The asymmetric geometry is intentional to **minimize frictional head loss**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a wide, multi-lane highway where cars are cruising at a steady pace. Suddenly, the highway narrows down to a single-lane tunnel due to construction. What happens?

1. **Cars must accelerate** to squeeze through the narrow tunnel (smaller cross-section → higher velocity).
2. As they speed up, the **pressure between cars drops** (think: less push from neighboring vehicles).
3. After exiting the tunnel, the highway widens back, cars slow down, and pressure normalizes.

A **Venturimeter works exactly like this tunnel**:
- The wide highway = **Inlet (upstream pipe)** of larger diameter $D_1$.
- The narrow tunnel = **Throat** of smaller diameter $D_2$.
- The cars = **Fluid particles** of density $\rho$.
- The speed (velocity) is determined by measuring the **pressure difference** between the wide section and the narrow tunnel using a **differential manometer (U-tube mercury manometer)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just make the throat a sudden contraction?** A sudden contraction would cause **turbulence, separation, and significant energy loss** (like cars crashing at the tunnel entrance). The smooth 21° convergent cone prevents this, making the Venturimeter one of the **most accurate** flow meters.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Metric Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 4°C) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of mercury (manometric fluid) | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13600 kg/m³** |
| Standard convergent cone half-angle | — | **21°** |
| Standard divergent cone half-angle | — | **7°** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Math

The Venturimeter exploits **two fundamental fluid mechanics laws** operating in tandem:

### Law 1: Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an incompressible fluid in a steady, streamlined flow:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

- **Why?** Mass cannot disappear. If the cross-section shrinks, velocity **must** rise proportionally.
- **How?** This gives us the relationship between inlet velocity $V_1$ and throat velocity $V_2$.

### Law 2: Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)
For an ideal fluid (no friction) along a streamline:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

- **Why?** Energy transforms — kinetic energy (velocity head) increases as pressure energy (pressure head) decreases.
- **How?** By measuring the pressure drop $(P_1 - P_2)$, we can deduce the velocity at the throat.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Insight:** For a **horizontal** Venturimeter (the most common KTU problem), $z_1 = z_2$, and the elevation head cancels out. Always verify pipe orientation before solving!

## 2.2 Real-World Utility of a Venturimeter

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| Water Treatment Plants | Measures flow in large distribution mains |
| Hydroelectric Power | Quantifies discharge through penstocks |
| Oil & Gas | Measures crude oil flow in pipelines |
| Chemical Processing | Monitors flow of acids, solvents in reactors |
| HVAC Systems | Air flow measurement in ducts |
| Irrigation Canals | Pipe-borne agricultural water supply metering |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the single most tested element on Venturimeters in KTU exams.**

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|--------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| Continuity Equation | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Mass conservation |
| Bernoulli's Equation (horizontal) | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | Energy conservation |
| Theoretical Velocity at Throat | $V_{2,th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right)}}$ | Ideal case velocity |
| Head Difference (manometer) | $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho} - 1\right)$ | U-tube mercury manometer reading |
| **Actual Discharge (Master Formula)** | $\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}}$ | **THE most tested equation** |
| Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ | Without $C_d$ correction |
| Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Ratio of actual to theoretical |
| Coefficient of Contraction | $C_c = \dfrac{A_{actual,throat}}{A_{throat}}$ | Area ratio |
| Coefficient of Velocity | $C_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$ | Velocity ratio |
| Relation between Coefficients | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Fundamental identity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Venturimeter Discharge Equation

### Step 1: Setup and Notation

Consider a horizontal Venturimeter with:
- Section 1: Inlet (upstream) with cross-sectional area $A_1$, diameter $D_1$, pressure $P_1$, velocity $V_1$
- Section 2: Throat with cross-sectional area $A_2$, diameter $D_2$, pressure $P_2$, velocity $V_2$

The Venturimeter is connected to a **differential U-tube manometer** containing mercury (density $\rho_{Hg}$), showing a deflection of $x$ meters.

> **Conversion Logic:** We will apply both governing equations, eliminate $V_1$ and $V_2$ to find discharge $Q$.

### Step 2: Apply the Continuity Equation

By conservation of mass for incompressible flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 V_1 &= A_2 V_2 \\
V_1 &= \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We express $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$ because $V_2$ will be our primary unknown (velocity at the throat).

### Step 3: Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Horizontal Venturimeter, $z_1 = z_2$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $z_1 = z_2$, the elevation heads cancel out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} &= \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This step eliminates gravity work terms because there is no vertical displacement.

### Step 4: Rearrange for Pressure Difference

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} - \frac{P_2}{\rho g} &= \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{V_1^2}{2g} \\
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} &= \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} \\
2g \cdot \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} &= V_2^2 - V_1^2 \\
\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} &= V_2^2 - V_1^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We isolate the velocity-squared difference, which is the key driver of the discharge.

### Step 5: Substitute Continuity Equation into Bernoulli Result

Replace $V_1$ with $\dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} &= V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \\
\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} &= V_2^2 - \frac{A_2^2 V_2^2}{A_1^2} \\
\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} &= V_2^2 \left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Factoring out $V_2^2$ consolidates the velocity term into a single unknown.

### Step 6: Solve for Theoretical Velocity at Throat $V_{2,th}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2^2 &= \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)} \\
V_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Taking the square root gives the velocity magnitude (we discard the negative root as unphysical).

### Step 7: Convert Pressure Head Difference to Manometer Head $h$

The pressure difference $P_1 - P_2$ is measured by a differential mercury manometer. By manometer principles (using **Paçal's Law**):

$$
P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho) \cdot g \cdot x
$$

Where $x$ is the manometer deflection in meters. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2(\rho_{Hg} - \rho) g x}{\rho \left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 g x (\rho_{Hg}/\rho - 1)}{\left(1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}\right)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho} - 1\right)$ be the equivalent head of the flowing fluid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We have expressed the throat velocity purely in terms of measurable quantities: areas, gravity, and equivalent head.

### Step 8: Calculate Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= A_2 \cdot V_2 \\
Q_{th} &= A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rationalizing the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $A_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh \cdot A_1^2}{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \\
Q_{th} &= \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The rationalized form is preferred because it allows cancellation of dimensions cleanly and matches standard KTU textbook notation.

### Step 9: Apply the Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$

Real fluids lose energy due to friction, turbulence, and vena contracta effects. The actual discharge is always **less than** theoretical. We introduce $C_d$:

$$
\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}}
$$

Where:
- $C_d$ is the **Coefficient of Discharge** (typically **0.95 to 0.98** for Venturimeters)
- $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_1^2$
- $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_2^2$
- $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho} - 1\right)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative diameter form** (commonly used in KTU problems):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= C_d \cdot \frac{\frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^2 (D_1^4 - D_2^4)}} \\
Q_{act} &= \frac{C_d \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 D_2^2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{D_1^4 - D_2^4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Problem Workflow

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Identify $D_1$, $D_2$, fluid density $\rho$, manometer fluid density $\rho_m$ | Inputs catalogued |
| 2 | Compute $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4}$ and $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4}$ | Areas in m² |
| 3 | Read manometer deflection $x$ (in m or mm — convert!) | $x$ in meters |
| 4 | Calculate equivalent head $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ | $h$ in meters |
| 5 | Apply $Q_{act} = C_d \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ | Discharge in m³/s |
| 6 | Convert to L/s or L/min if required | Final answer |
