# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Boundary Layer Separation** is a phenomenon in fluid dynamics wherein the boundary layer of a fluid flowing over a solid surface detaches or "separates" from the surface, creating a region of reverse flow or stagnant fluid between the main stream and the surface. Mathematically, it is defined as the point where the velocity gradient normal to the surface becomes zero, i.e.,

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

and beyond which the velocity profile shows a reversal of flow direction (backflow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Separation is the condition when the shear stress at the wall becomes zero ($\tau_w = 0$), accompanied by a reversal of flow near the surface. This marks the end of the attached boundary layer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a small toy boat against a slow-moving current in a river. As long as the river flows smoothly, the boat glides along the surface. But if you push the boat into a region where the current is moving *against* its direction (like near a dam or a sudden widening of the river), the water near the boat's hull gets "stuck" — it can no longer follow the boat's curve. A swirling, turbulent region of dead water forms behind the obstruction. This is exactly what happens in **boundary layer separation**: the fluid near the wall cannot "push hard enough" against an increasing pressure to stay attached to the surface, so it peels away.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Critical Reynolds Number for transition:** $Re_{cr} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ (for flat plate, turbulent transition)
- **Displacement Thickness:** $\delta^{*}$ (m)
- **Momentum Thickness:** $\theta$ (m)
- **Shape Factor:** $H = \frac{\delta^{*}}{\theta}$ (dimensionless)
- **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_w$ in **N/m²** or **Pa**

> [!NOTE]
> A **laminar boundary layer** separates much more easily than a **turbulent boundary layer** because turbulent eddies carry high-momentum fluid toward the wall, energizing the near-wall flow and allowing it to overcome adverse pressure gradients.

## 1.4 Causes of Boundary Layer Separation

The primary driver of boundary layer separation is the **Adverse Pressure Gradient (APG)**, denoted mathematically as:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$$

This condition occurs whenever the flow encounters a divergent passage (e.g., diffuser, diverging nozzle, rear portion of an airfoil, sudden expansion).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid:** **F = P/A** (Favorable gradient: dP/dx < 0, Velocity increases). **A = P/A** (Adverse gradient: dP/dx > 0, Velocity decreases, leading to separation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Separation (Step-by-Step Logic)

The separation process unfolds in the following structured sequence:

1. **Fluid enters with kinetic energy:** Near the leading edge, the fluid has high momentum and easily follows the surface curvature.
2. **Adverse pressure gradient encountered:** As the surface curves away from the flow direction, the pressure rises in the direction of flow ($\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$).
3. **Near-wall fluid decelerates first:** Due to viscosity, the fluid in the immediate vicinity of the wall has lower velocity (and hence lower kinetic energy) than the free-stream fluid.
4. **Pressure work overcomes kinetic energy:** The rising pressure does work *against* the motion of the slow-moving near-wall fluid, decelerating it further.
5. **Zero velocity point reached:** At a critical location (the **separation point**), the wall shear stress drops to zero:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

6. **Flow reversal begins:** Beyond this point, the near-wall fluid actually moves *backward*, creating a recirculation zone.
7. **Wake formation:** The detached shear layer rolls up into vortices, forming a turbulent wake.

## 2.2 Why Separation Happens — The Physics

The momentum equation in the boundary layer (Prandtl's form) gives the key insight:

$$\rho u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \rho v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

- The term $-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$ represents the favorable pressure force (when $\frac{dp}{dx} < 0$).
- When $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$, the pressure force acts **opposite to flow direction**, decelerating it.
- The viscous term $\mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$ further retards the fluid near the wall.
- Combined effect: velocity at the wall drops to zero, then reverses.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **SI Units** |
|:------------|:----------------|:-------------:|
| $\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$ | Wall shear stress (Newton's law of viscosity) | Pa or N/m² |
| $\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$ | Condition for boundary layer separation | s⁻¹ |
| $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$ | Adverse pressure gradient (necessary for separation) | Pa/m |
| $\delta^{*}(x) = \int_0^{\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy$ | Displacement thickness | m |
| $\theta(x) = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{u}{U}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy$ | Momentum thickness | m |
| $H = \frac{\delta^{*}}{\theta}$ | Shape factor (H > 2.5 → separated flow) | dimensionless |
| $C_f = \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^2}$ | Skin friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| $C_D = C_{D,\text{form}} + C_{D,\text{friction}}$ | Total drag = form drag + skin friction | dimensionless |
| $\frac{\delta}{x} = \frac{5.0}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Turbulent boundary layer thickness (flat plate) | m |
| $\frac{\delta}{x} = \frac{5.5}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Laminar boundary layer thickness (Blasius) | m |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **Shape Factor H** is a favorite KTU question. Remember: **Laminar BL:** $H \approx 2.59$, **Turbulent BL:** $H \approx 1.3$–$1.5$, **Separated flow:** $H > 2.5$ (and rising rapidly).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Separation on aircraft wings causes stall, a critical safety issue at high angles of attack.
- **Civil Engineering:** Separation behind bridge piers causes scour and vortex shedding, leading to structural failure.
- **Hydraulic Machines:** Separation in draft tubes of Francis turbines reduces efficiency and causes vibration.
- **Automotive:** Rear-window separation creates a low-pressure dirty zone covered in dust and raindrops.
- **Sports Engineering:** Golf ball dimples trip the boundary layer to turbulent, reducing separation and decreasing drag.
- **Wind Engineering:** Skyscrapers are designed with tapered shapes to delay separation and reduce wind loads.

## 2.5 Methods to Control / Delay Separation

| **Method** | **Working Principle** | **Application** |
|:-----------|:----------------------|:----------------|
| **Streamlining** | Maintains FPG longer; reduces APG region | Airfoils, car bodies, submarine hulls |
| **Suction** | Removes low-momentum fluid near wall, replacing with higher-momentum fluid | Aircraft wing boundary layer control, wind tunnels |
| **Blowing (Tangential Jet)** | Injects high-energy fluid into the near-wall region | Jet flaps, circulation control wings |
| **Vortex Generators (VG)** | Small fins create streamwise vortices, mixing high-momentum fluid toward the wall | Aircraft wings (turbulators), HVAC ducts |
| **Surface Roughness / Dimples** | Promotes early transition to turbulent BL (which resists separation longer) | Golf balls, dimpled turbine blades |
| **Boundary Layer Fences** | Block spanwise cross-flow, preventing separation in 3D flows | Swept wings |
| **Polymers/Additives (Toms Effect)** | Drag reduction in turbulent pipe flow | Oil pipelines, firefighting hoses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Turbulent BL resists separation better:** In turbulent flow, the intense cross-stream mixing (Reynolds stresses) constantly transports high-momentum fluid from the outer layer to the wall, energizing the near-wall flow. This extra kinetic energy allows the boundary layer to "fight" against the adverse pressure gradient for a longer distance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Boundary Layer Momentum Integral Equation (Von Kármán)

This derivation is **essential for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions**.

### Step 1: Start with the 2D Steady Incompressible Continuity Equation

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

### Step 2: Start with the 2D Steady Incompressible x-Momentum Equation

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Boundary Layer Approximation (Prandtl's Assumption)

- Pressure is constant across the boundary layer (normal to surface): $p(x,y) \approx p(x)$
- Therefore, from Bernoulli's equation applied to the free stream:

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -U\frac{dU}{dx}$$

Substitute this into the momentum equation:

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = U\frac{dU}{dx} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 4: Rearrange the Momentum Equation

Write the left side as a derivative of a product using continuity:

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(u^2) - u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

A more direct route is to use the identity:

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial (u^2)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (uv)}{\partial y}$$

This is derived by applying the product rule and substituting continuity.

### Step 5: Rewrite the Momentum Equation in Conservative Form

Using the identity from Step 4:

$$\frac{\partial (u^2)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (uv)}{\partial y} = U\frac{dU}{dx} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Now integrate this equation with respect to $y$ from $0$ to $\delta$ (the boundary layer edge).

### Step 6: Integrate Term by Term

**Integrating the LHS:**

$$\int_0^{\delta} \frac{\partial (u^2)}{\partial x}\, dy + \int_0^{\delta} \frac{\partial (uv)}{\partial y}\, dy$$

For the second integral, at $y = 0$: $v = 0$ (no-penetration); at $y = \delta$: $u = U$, so $uv = U v_{\delta}$.

Using Leibniz rule for the first integral:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta} u^2\, dy - u_{\delta}^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx} + U v_{\delta} = \int_0^{\delta} U\frac{dU}{dx}\, dy + \nu \left[\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right]_0^{\delta}$$

### Step 7: Apply Boundary Conditions

- At $y = \delta$: $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$ (velocity merges smoothly with free stream)
- At $y = 0$: $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\tau_w}{\mu}$ (definition of wall shear stress)
- $u_{\delta} = U$ (at the boundary layer edge)

### Step 8: Substitute to Get the Momentum Integral Equation

The result, after applying continuity in integrated form ($U\frac{d\delta}{dx} = v_{\delta}$), is the **Von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}}$$

where:
- $\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy$ is the **momentum thickness**
- $H = \frac{\delta^{*}}{\theta}$ is the **shape factor**
- $\frac{dU}{dx} < 0$: Favorable Pressure Gradient (FPG)
- $\frac{dU}{dx} > 0$: Adverse Pressure Gradient (APG)

### Step 9: Physical Interpretation (Critical for KTU)

The wall shear stress is the sum of two terms:
1. **$\frac{d\theta}{dx}$** — Rate of momentum thickness growth.
2. **$(2+H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$** — Effect of pressure gradient.

> When $\frac{dU}{dx} > 0$ (APG), the second term is positive, so $\tau_w$ decreases. If the APG is strong enough, $\tau_w \to 0$ — **this is the separation point**.

## 3.2 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Air flows over a flat plate at $U_\infty = 10$ m/s. At a station $x = 0.5$ m, the boundary layer has a linear velocity profile:

$$u(y) = U_\infty \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)$$

with $\delta = 0.01$ m. Find: (a) displacement thickness, (b) momentum thickness, (c) shape factor, (d) wall shear stress, (e) skin friction coefficient. Take $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu_{air} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s.

### Part (a): Displacement Thickness

$$\delta^{*} = \int_0^{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \int_0^{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right) dy = \left[y - \frac{y^2}{2\delta}\right]_0^{\delta} = \delta - \frac{\delta}{2} = \frac{\delta}{2}$$

$$\delta^{*} = \frac{0.01}{2} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

### Part (b): Momentum Thickness

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \int_0^{\delta} \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)\left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right) dy$$

Let $n = y/\delta$, $dy = \delta\, dn$:

$$\theta = \delta \int_0^{1} n(1-n)\, dn = \delta \left[\frac{n^2}{2} - \frac{n^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \delta \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\delta}{6}$$

$$\theta = \frac{0.01}{6} = 0.00167 \text{ m} \approx 1.67 \text{ mm}$$

### Part (c): Shape Factor

$$H = \frac{\delta^{*}}{\theta} = \frac{\delta/2}{\delta/6} = 3$$

> [!NOTE]
> $H = 3$ for a linear (triangular) velocity profile. Note that this is **greater than 2.5**, indicating the flow profile is very full and is at the verge of separation in adverse pressure gradient conditions.

### Part (d): Wall Shear Stress

The velocity profile: $u(y) = U_\infty \cdot \frac{y}{\delta}$, so $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{U_\infty}{\delta}$.

$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = \mu \cdot \frac{U_\infty}{\delta} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 10}{0.01} = 0.018 \text{ Pa}$$

### Part (e): Skin Friction Coefficient

$$C_f = \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.018}{1.2 \times 10^2} = \frac{0.036}{120} = 3 \times 10^{-4}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison:** For Blasius laminar BL, $C_f = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$. Compute $Re_x = \frac{\rho U x}{\mu} = \frac{1.2 \times 10 \times 0.5}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.33 \times 10^5$. So $C_{f,\text{Blasius}} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{3.33 \times 10^5}} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{-3}$. The linear profile gives a *lower* $C_f$ because the actual BL profile is fuller (more curved), as you can see from the H = 3 vs. H = 2.59 for Blasius.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Boundary Layer Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Approaches Solid Surface] --> B{Laminar or Turbulent BL Forms}
    B --> C{Adverse Pressure Gradient Present}
    C -- No --> D[BL Stays Attached]
    C -- Yes --> E[Near-Wall Fluid Decelerates]
    E --> F{Is APG Strong Enough}
    F -- No --> G[BL Remains Attached with Mild Thinning]
    F -- Yes --> H[Wall Shear Stress Tau w decreases]
    H --> I{Tau w reaches zero}
    I -- No --> J[BL Thickens and Recirculation Begins]
    I -- Yes --> K[SEPARATION POINT IDENTIFIED]
    K --> L[Reverse Flow Region Forms]
    L --> M[Vortex Shedding and Wake Turbulence]
    M --> N[Increased Form Drag and Pressure Loss]
    N --> O[Engineering Consequences Stall Scour Vibration]
    O --> P{Control Measures}
    P --> P1[Streamlining]
    P --> P2[Suction and Blowing]
    P --> P3[Vortex Generators]
    P --> P4[Surface Roughness Tripping]
    P --> P5[Boundary Layer Fences]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Velocity Profile Evolution at Separation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Velocity Profile UPSTREAM of Separation] -->|FPG Healthy BL| B[Profile with positive gradient at wall]
    B -->|APG Develops| C[Profile with reduced gradient at wall]
    C -->|APG Stronger| D[Profile with INFLECTION POINT inside BL]
    D -->|Critical APG| E[Wall Gradient becomes ZERO]
    E -->|Beyond Critical| F[Profile with REVERSE FLOW near wall]
    F --> G[FULL SEPARATION with Recirculation Bubble]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define boundary layer separation. State the conditions under which it occurs.**

**Model Answer:**

**Boundary layer separation** is the phenomenon in which the boundary layer detaches from the solid surface, creating a region of reverse flow adjacent to the wall.

**Conditions for separation:**

1. **Existence of an adverse pressure gradient:** $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$ (pressure increases in the direction of flow).
2. **Sufficiently strong adverse pressure gradient:** The pressure rise must be large enough to overcome the kinetic energy of the near-wall fluid.
3. **Wall shear stress becomes zero:** $\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$ at the separation point.
4. **Flow deceleration:** The streamwise velocity $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} < 0$ near the wall (retarded flow).
5. **Inflection point** in the velocity profile: The curvature of the velocity profile changes sign, i.e., $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0$ inside the boundary layer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "adverse pressure gradient" and stop. To get **full 3 marks**, KTU examiners expect you to **also mention the wall shear stress condition** and at least one of the geometric profile conditions (e.g., inflection point).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a laminar and a turbulent boundary layer with respect to separation.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **Laminar BL** | **Turbulent BL** |
|:-------------|:---------------|:-----------------|
| Shape factor $H$ | $\approx 2.59$ | $\approx 1.3$–$1.5$ |
| Velocity profile | Smooth, parabolic-like | Fuller, with steep gradient at wall |
| Wall shear stress $\tau_w$ | Lower (linear gradient) | Higher (steep gradient) |
| Resistance to separation | **Low** — separates easily | **High** — delays separation |
| Energy near wall | Low | High (due to turbulent mixing) |
| Critical APG required | Small | Large |

**Conclusion:** A turbulent boundary layer **delays separation** because vigorous mixing transports high-momentum fluid from the outer layer toward the wall, energizing the near-wall region and enabling it to overcome adverse pressure gradients. This is why **golf balls are dimpled** and **turbine blades are roughened** in certain regions — to trip the laminar BL to turbulent.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — KTU 2024 Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]`**

**(a)** Derive the Von Kármán momentum integral equation for a 2D steady incompressible boundary layer flow. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a linear velocity profile $u/U = y/\delta$ for a laminar boundary layer on a flat plate, derive expressions for displacement thickness, momentum thickness, and wall shear stress in terms of $\delta$ and free-stream velocity $U$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, 2D, incompressible flow.
2. Boundary layer approximation: $\delta \ll L$, so $\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \approx 0$.
3. Pressure across the boundary layer equals the free-stream pressure at any station: $p(x, y) \approx p(x)$.
4. No-slip at the wall: $u(0) = 0$, $v(0) = 0$.

**Derivation:**

Start with the x-momentum equation:

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Use Bernoulli's relation for the free stream: $\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dp}{dx} = -U\frac{dU}{dx}$. Substituting:

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = U\frac{dU}{dx} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Rearrange using product rule and continuity:

$$\frac{\partial (u^2)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (uv)}{\partial y} = U\frac{dU}{dx} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Integrate from $y = 0$ to $y = \delta$:

$$\int_0^{\delta} \frac{\partial (u^2)}{\partial x}\, dy + [uv]_0^{\delta} = \int_0^{\delta} U\frac{dU}{dx}\, dy + \nu \left[\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right]_0^{\delta}$$

Apply boundary conditions and use Leibniz rule. After algebraic simplification (as shown in Section 3):

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given: $\frac{u}{U} = \frac{y}{\delta}$, with $u(0) = 0$ and $u(\delta) = U$.

**Displacement Thickness:**

$$\delta^{*} = \int_0^{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy = \int_0^{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right) dy = \frac{\delta}{2}$$

**Momentum Thickness:**

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{y}{\delta}\left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right) dy = \frac{\delta}{6}$$

**Wall Shear Stress:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = \frac{U}{\delta}$$

$$\tau_w = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{\delta} = \frac{\mu U}{\delta}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternate Pattern]`**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the phenomenon of boundary layer separation over a curved surface. Discuss the role of adverse pressure gradient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laminar boundary layer separates from a curved surface at a point where the local free-stream velocity is $U = 25$ m/s and the local boundary layer thickness is $\delta = 8$ mm. If the velocity profile is approximated as $u/U = 2(y/\delta) - (y/\delta)^2$, calculate the wall shear stress, displacement thickness, momentum thickness, and shape factor. Take $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Phenomenon (3 marks):**
When a fluid flows over a curved surface with an adverse pressure gradient, the slow-moving fluid particles in the boundary layer lose momentum as they work against the rising pressure. At the **separation point**, the wall shear stress becomes zero, and beyond it, the flow reverses, forming a recirculation region. The detached shear layer creates a **wake** with intense mixing and energy loss.

**Role of Adverse Pressure Gradient (4 marks):**
- Governed by $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$.
- Pressure work is done **against** the flow direction, decelerating it.
- Near-wall fluid (low momentum) is most affected — it stops first.
- Bernoulli's equation confirms velocity decreases where pressure increases.
- Strong APG causes separation; weak APG only thins the BL.
- Diffusers, diverging nozzles, and rear of airfoils are classic locations.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given: $U = 25$ m/s, $\delta = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\frac{u}{U} = 2\eta - \eta^2$ where $\eta = y/\delta$.

**Step 1: Wall Shear Stress**

$$\frac{u}{U} = 2\eta - \eta^2 \implies u = U(2\eta - \eta^2) = U\left(\frac{2y}{\delta} - \frac{y^2}{\delta^2}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = U\left(\frac{2}{\delta} - \frac{2y}{\delta^2}\right) = \frac{2U}{\delta}\left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right)$$

At $y = 0$: $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{2U}{\delta} = \frac{2 \times 25}{0.008} = 6250 \text{ s}^{-1}$

$$\tau_w = \mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 6250 = 0.1125 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Displacement Thickness**

$$\delta^{*} = \int_0^{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy = \delta \int_0^1 (1 - 2\eta + \eta^2) d\eta = \delta \left[\eta - \eta^2 + \frac{\eta^3}{3}\right]_0^1$$

$$\delta^{*} = \delta \left(1 - 1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\delta}{3} = \frac{8}{3} = 2.667 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Momentum Thickness**

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy = \delta \int_0^1 (2\eta - \eta^2)(1 - 2\eta + \eta^2) d\eta$$

Expand: $(2\eta - \eta^2)(1 - 2\eta + \eta^2) = 2\eta - 4\eta^2 + 2\eta^3 - \eta^2 + 2\eta^3 - \eta^4 = 2\eta - 5\eta^2 + 4\eta^3 - \eta^4$

$$\theta = \delta \int_0^1 (2\eta - 5\eta^2 + 4\eta^3 - \eta^4) d\eta = \delta \left[\eta^2 - \frac{5\eta^3}{3} + \eta^4 - \frac{\eta^5}{5}\right]_0^1$$

$$\theta = \delta \left(1 - \frac{5}{3} + 1 - \frac{1}{5}\right) = \delta \left(2 - \frac{5}{3} - \frac{1}{5}\right) = \delta \left(\frac{30 - 25 - 3}{15}\right) = \frac{2\delta}{15}$$

$$\theta = \frac{2 \times 8}{15} = 1.067 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Shape Factor**

$$H = \frac{\delta^{*}}{\theta} = \frac{\delta/3}{2\delta/15} = \frac{15}{6} = 2.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> $H = 2.5$ indicates the profile is **at the verge of separation**. In practice, separation is imminent when $H \geq 2.5$, which is consistent with the problem's framing of "separates at this point."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\partial u / \partial y$:** Many students forget to differentiate properly and write $\partial u / \partial y = 2U/\delta$ but then fail to evaluate it at $y = 0$ correctly. The function is non-zero at the wall.
> 2. **Forgetting to substitute $U$:** Some students compute the integral of the *non-dimensional* profile but forget to multiply by $U$ when computing $\tau_w$.
> 3. **Wrong limits on integration:** Always integrate from $\eta = 0$ to $\eta = 1$, not from $0$ to $\delta$.
> 4. **Missing units:** Always write the units (Pa, mm) for each final answer. KTU deducts **½ mark** per missing unit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Definition of separation:** Point where wall shear stress is **zero** AND flow reversal occurs.
- ✅ **Master condition:** $\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$ at the separation point.
- ✅ **Necessary condition:** Adverse Pressure Gradient, $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$, i.e., flow in a **divergent passage**.
- ✅ **Velocity profile signature:** **Inflection point** appears inside the boundary layer ($\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0$ at some $0 < y < \delta$).
- ✅ **Shape Factor rule of thumb:** $H > 2.5$ → separated; $H \approx 2.59$ → laminar; $H \approx 1.3$–$1.5$ → turbulent; $H = 1$ → ideal (uniform).
- ✅ **Turbulent BL resists separation** better than laminar BL due to Reynolds-stress mixing.
- ✅ **Von Kármán momentum integral equation** is the master equation for integral boundary layer analysis:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$$

- ✅ **Control methods:** Streamlining, suction, blowing, vortex generators, surface roughness, fences, polymer additives.
- ✅ **Engineering examples to memorize:** Aircraft stall, golf ball drag reduction, bridge pier scour, draft tube losses in turbines, chimney downdraft.
- ✅ **Skin friction coefficient for flat plate laminar (Blasius):** $C_f = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$.
- ✅ **Boundary layer thickness formulas:** $\delta_{lam} = \frac{5.0x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$; $\delta_{turb} = \frac{0.37x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$.
- ✅ **Common velocity profiles for KTU problems:** Linear ($u/U = y/\delta$), Quadratic ($u/U = 2(y/\delta) - (y/\delta)^2$), Power-law, and Blasius.
- ✅ **When computing $\delta^{*}$, $\theta$, $\tau_w$ for a given profile:** Always express the profile in *dimensionless form* $u/U$ vs. $y/\delta$ first, then integrate.
- ✅ **Bernoulli-Pressure Coupling:** $\frac{dp}{dx} = -\rho U \frac{dU}{dx}$ — vital for relating velocity and pressure changes in BL analysis.
- ✅ **Frequent 3-mark question:** "Why does a turbulent BL resist separation better?" — Answer with **Reynolds-stress mixing** transferring high-momentum fluid toward the wall.
- ✅ **Frequent 14-mark question:** Derive Von Kármán's equation, or compute integral parameters for a given velocity profile — practice these two repeatedly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Jet on Fixed Vertical and Moving Vertical Flat Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Jet on Fixed Vertical and Moving Vertical Flat Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Impact of Jet on Fixed & Moving Vertical Flat Plates

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **impact of a jet** refers to the force generated when a high-velocity fluid jet strikes a stationary or moving surface. The magnitude of this force depends on the **rate of change of momentum** of the fluid jet as it is deflected by the surface, in accordance with **Newton's Second Law of Motion** (Impulse-Momentum Theorem).

The governing principle for this entire topic is:

$$F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V = \rho \, a \, V \times (V_{\text{initial}} - V_{\text{final}})$$

where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid (for water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ = **1000 kg/m³**)
- $a$ = cross-sectional area of the jet at the vena contracta (**m²**)
- $V$ = absolute velocity of the jet (**m/s**)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate per second (**kg/s**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Configurations Studied in Module 4:**
> 1. **Fixed Vertical Flat Plate** — Plate is stationary, jet strikes and is deflected 90°.
> 2. **Moving Vertical Flat Plate (Jet & Plate in same direction)** — Plate moves *away* from the jet source.
> 3. **Moving Vertical Flat Plate (Plate moving towards jet)** — Plate moves *into* the oncoming jet.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing still and someone throws a tennis ball at you at 30 km/h. You feel a certain "thump" on your chest. Now imagine you are **running away** from the person throwing the ball at the same speed they are throwing it. The relative speed of the ball to you becomes smaller, so the thump is *less forceful*. However, because you are *moving*, you actually absorb some **energy** from the thrower — you are being "pushed" forward as you run.

This is exactly how a **moving flat plate** behaves under a water jet:
- The **force** on the plate is governed by the **relative velocity** of the jet w.r.t. the plate.
- The **work done** (useful energy extracted) is Force × velocity of the plate.

A **firefighter's hose** held against a wall (fixed plate) feels the **maximum possible thrust** because all momentum is destroyed. A **ship's hull** moving with the waves (moving plate in same direction) experiences **less drag force** but can have its kinetic energy harnessed (this is the principle behind **Pelton wheel buckets** and **ship propulsion**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does a Jet Exert Force on a Plate?

When a fluid jet strikes a plate, the jet's velocity vector changes direction. According to **Newton's Second Law**, a change in momentum requires an external force. The plate provides this force, and by **Newton's Third Law**, the jet exerts an equal and opposite force on the plate.

For a smooth flat plate, we make the following standard assumptions (as per KTU 2024 evaluation key):

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Assumptions:**
> 1. The plate is **smooth** (frictionless) — friction between jet and plate is neglected.
> 2. The plate is **impervious** — no water passes through or splashes off the edges.
> 3. The jet **splits smoothly** along the plate surface with no loss of kinetic energy.
> 4. The pressure of the surrounding fluid (atmosphere) acts equally on all sides → its **net effect is zero** on the force calculation.
> 5. The jet velocity profile is **uniform** (idealized).

## 2.2 Three Cases — Conceptual Logic

**Case 1 — Fixed Plate:**
- Absolute velocity of jet before impact = $V$ (in horizontal direction)
- After impact, the jet runs *along* the plate surface. The component of velocity *normal* to the plate (i.e., the original direction of the jet) is reduced to **zero**.
- So the **change in velocity** in the original jet direction is simply $V - 0 = V$.
- Force = mass flow rate × change in velocity = $\rho a V \times V = \rho a V^2$.

**Case 2 — Plate Moving in the Same Direction as Jet (Velocity = $u$):**
- The plate runs *away* from the jet nozzle.
- The jet "catches up" to the plate at a *reduced* relative velocity.
- Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. plate = $V_r = V - u$.
- Mass flow rate striking the plate = $\rho a (V - u)$ (since the relative velocity determines how much mass hits the plate per second).
- Change in relative velocity = $V_r - 0 = V - u$.
- Force on plate = $\rho a (V - u)^2$.
- The plate moves a distance $u$ every second, so useful work done/sec = $F \times u$.

**Case 3 — Plate Moving Towards the Jet (Velocity = $u$):**
- The plate runs *into* the jet.
- Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. plate = $V_r = V + u$.
- Mass flow rate striking = $\rho a (V + u)$.
- Force = $\rho a (V + u)^2$.
- Work done/sec = $\rho a (V + u)^2 \times u$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Revision Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 equations — they appear in 90% of KTU ESE problems on this topic.**

| # | Configuration | Force on Plate ($F$) | Work Done per sec ($W$) | Condition for Max Work |
|---|---------------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Fixed Vertical Plate** | $F = \rho \, a \, V^{2}$ | $W = 0$ (plate does not move) | Not applicable |
| 2 | **Moving Plate (same dir.)** | $F = \rho \, a \, (V - u)^{2}$ | $W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V - u)^{2}$ | $u = V/3$ |
| 3 | **Moving Plate (opposite dir.)** | $F = \rho \, a \, (V + u)^{2}$ | $W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V + u)^{2}$ | No real maximum (monotonic) |
| 4 | **Max Work (Case 2)** | $F_{\max} = \dfrac{4}{9} \rho \, a \, V^{2}$ | $W_{\max} = \dfrac{4}{27} \rho \, a \, V^{3}$ | $u = V/3$ |
| 5 | **Efficiency (Case 2)** | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{W_{\max}}{KE_{\text{jet}}/sec} = \dfrac{4/27}{1/2}$ | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{8}{27} = 0.2963 = 29.63\%$ | At $u = V/3$ |
| 6 | **KE of Jet per second** | (N/A) | $KE/sec = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, a \, V^{3}$ | Input energy reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this is used in real engineering:**
> - **Pelton Wheel turbines** (hydropower) — buckets act as curved moving plates.
> - **Ship propulsion** (water-jet propulsion systems).
> - **Fire-fighting monitors** — design of reaction-force nozzles.
> - **Hydraulic ram pumps** — energy transfer from a moving water column.

## 2.4 The Critical Difference Between Force and Work

This is the most commonly confused point in KTU valuation:
- **Force** depends only on the *relative velocity* of the jet.
- **Work done** depends on both the *force* AND the *velocity of the plate*.
- A fixed plate experiences the **maximum force** but does **zero work** (it does not move).
- A moving plate experiences **less force** but can do **positive work** on external machinery.

This trade-off is what gives rise to the concept of **maximum efficiency** at $u = V/3$ for the case where the plate moves in the same direction as the jet.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Fixed Vertical Flat Plate

**Given:**
- A circular jet of diameter $d$ issues from a nozzle with velocity $V$.
- Cross-sectional area of jet: $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2}$.
- Mass density of water: $\rho$ (kg/m³).
- The jet strikes a flat plate held perpendicular to its axis and is brought to rest in the direction normal to the plate (the velocity is redirected along the plate surface).

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate of the jet per second:**

The mass of water striking the plate per second is the mass contained in a column of length $V$ (the distance the jet travels in 1 second) and cross-section $a$.

$$\dot{m} = \text{mass/sec} = \rho \times a \times V = \rho \, a \, V$$

**Step 2 — Initial and final velocities in the direction of the jet (x-direction):**

Before striking: the velocity in the x-direction is $V$ (the jet is moving toward the plate).
After striking: the jet runs along the plate. The component of velocity in the original x-direction is **zero** (all the velocity is now in the y-direction, along the plate).

$$V_{x,\text{initial}} = V$$
$$V_{x,\text{final}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Impulse-Momentum Theorem:**

The force exerted by the jet on the plate equals the rate of change of momentum of the jet in the x-direction.

$$F = \dot{m} \times \left( V_{x,\text{initial}} - V_{x,\text{final}} \right)$$

$$F = \rho \, a \, V \times (V - 0)$$

$$\boxed{F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$

**Step 4 — Work done by the jet on the fixed plate:**

Since the plate is fixed, it has no displacement. The work done by the jet on the plate is therefore **zero**. (The kinetic energy of the jet is *not* destroyed — it merely changes direction. The water still leaves the plate with kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2} \rho a V^{3}$ per second, but it is now directed parallel to the plate.)

$$W_{\text{fixed}} = F \times 0 = 0 \text{ J/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Students often write "Energy lost = Force × distance = $\rho a V^2 \times 0$". The correct interpretation is: the jet does **zero work** on a fixed plate because the plate does not move, even though the jet itself still has kinetic energy after deflection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force & Work on a Moving Vertical Flat Plate (Same Direction as Jet)

**Given:**
- Jet velocity = $V$ (m/s), cross-section = $a$, density = $\rho$.
- Plate moves *away* from the jet with velocity $u$ (where $u < V$, otherwise the jet can never catch the plate).
- Plate is smooth and impervious.

**Step 1 — Identify the velocities in the *absolute* (ground) reference frame:**

- Jet velocity (absolute): $V_{\text{jet}} = V$ (towards the plate).
- Plate velocity (absolute): $V_{\text{plate}} = u$ (in the same direction as the jet).

**Step 2 — Find the *relative* velocity of the jet with respect to the plate:**

$$V_{r} = V_{\text{jet}} - V_{\text{plate}} = V - u$$

This is the velocity at which the jet "catches up" to the plate. The mass of water striking the plate per second is determined by this relative velocity.

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate striking the plate per second:**

$$\dot{m}_{r} = \rho \, a \, V_{r} = \rho \, a \, (V - u)$$

**Step 4 — Change of velocity of the jet in the direction of motion (in the plate's reference frame):**

In the plate's reference frame, the jet arrives with velocity $V_r = V - u$ normal to the plate. After striking, the jet is deflected and runs along the plate. The normal component is reduced to **zero** in the plate's reference frame.

$$\Delta V = (V - u) - 0 = V - u$$

**Step 5 — Force exerted by the jet on the plate (using relative quantities):**

$$F = \dot{m}_{r} \times \Delta V = \rho \, a \, (V - u) \times (V - u)$$

$$\boxed{F = \rho \, a \, (V - u)^{2} \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$

**Step 6 — Work done by the jet on the plate per second:**

The plate moves a distance $u$ in one second. The work done per unit time is:

$$W = F \times u$$

$$\boxed{W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V - u)^{2} \quad \text{(Watts or J/s)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition for Maximum Work Done (Same-Direction Case)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU ESE Module 4.

**Step 1 — Set up the optimization problem:**

We have $W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V - u)^{2}$. Constants: $\rho, a, V$. Variable: $u$.

To find the maximum, differentiate $W$ with respect to $u$ and set it to zero.

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \, \frac{d}{du} \left[ u \, (V - u)^{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the product rule:**

Let $f(u) = u$ and $g(u) = (V - u)^{2}$.

$$f'(u) = 1$$
$$g'(u) = 2(V - u) \cdot (-1) = -2(V - u)$$

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \left[ f'(u) \cdot g(u) + f(u) \cdot g'(u) \right]$$

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \left[ (1)(V - u)^{2} + (u)\left( -2(V - u) \right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \left[ (V - u)^{2} - 2u(V - u) \right]$$

Factor out $(V - u)$:

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \, (V - u) \left[ (V - u) - 2u \right]$$

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \, (V - u)(V - 3u)$$

**Step 4 — Set the derivative to zero and solve:**

$$\rho \, a \, (V - u)(V - 3u) = 0$$

This gives two roots:
- $V - u = 0 \implies u = V$ → at this point, work done = $\rho a V (0)^2 = 0$ (trivial minimum).
- $V - 3u = 0 \implies u = V/3$ → this is the non-trivial maximum.

$$\boxed{u_{\text{optimum}} = \frac{V}{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Confirm it is a maximum (second derivative test or sign change):**

For $u < V/3$: both $(V-u)$ and $(V-3u)$ are positive → $dW/du > 0$ (W is increasing).
For $u > V/3$ (but $u < V$): $(V-u) > 0$ and $(V-3u) < 0$ → $dW/du < 0$ (W is decreasing).

Hence $u = V/3$ is a genuine **maximum**.

**Step 6 — Maximum force and maximum work done:**

Substitute $u = V/3$ into the force equation:

$$F_{\max} = \rho \, a \, \left( V - \frac{V}{3} \right)^{2} = \rho \, a \, \left( \frac{2V}{3} \right)^{2} = \frac{4}{9} \rho \, a \, V^{2}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\max} = \frac{4}{9} \rho \, a \, V^{2}}$$

Substitute $u = V/3$ into the work equation:

$$W_{\max} = \rho \, a \cdot \frac{V}{3} \cdot \left( V - \frac{V}{3} \right)^{2} = \rho \, a \cdot \frac{V}{3} \cdot \left( \frac{2V}{3} \right)^{2}$$

$$W_{\max} = \rho \, a \cdot \frac{V}{3} \cdot \frac{4V^{2}}{9} = \frac{4}{27} \rho \, a \, V^{3}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\max} = \frac{4}{27} \rho \, a \, V^{3}}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum efficiency:**

Kinetic energy supplied by the jet per second:

$$KE_{\text{jet}}/sec = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, a \, V \cdot V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, a \, V^{3}$$

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{W_{\max}}{KE_{\text{jet}}/sec} = \frac{\frac{4}{27} \rho \, a \, V^{3}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho \, a \, V^{3}} = \frac{4}{27} \times \frac{2}{1} = \frac{8}{27}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{8}{27} \approx 0.2963 = 29.63\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The 8/27 result is a *universal constant* for an ideal jet striking a single flat moving plate. It does NOT depend on $\rho, a,$ or $V$! This is why the answer to many KTU numericals always ends with "$\eta_{\max} = 29.63\%$".

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Force & Work on a Moving Vertical Flat Plate (Towards the Jet)

**Step 1 — Relative velocity (plate moving towards jet):**

The plate moves at velocity $u$ *towards* the jet source. The jet also moves at $V$ towards the plate. In the plate's reference frame, the relative velocity is the **sum**:

$$V_{r} = V + u$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate striking the plate:**

$$\dot{m}_{r} = \rho \, a \, (V + u)$$

**Step 3 — Force on the plate:**

The relative velocity normal to the plate is destroyed:

$$F = \dot{m}_{r} \times V_{r} = \rho \, a \, (V + u) \times (V + u)$$

$$\boxed{F = \rho \, a \, (V + u)^{2} \quad \text{(Newtons)}}$$

**Step 4 — Work done per second:**

The plate moves a distance $u$ per second (towards the jet source):

$$W = F \times u = \rho \, a \, u \, (V + u)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V + u)^{2} \quad \text{(Watts)}}$$

**Step 5 — Check for maximum work:**

Differentiate $W$ w.r.t. $u$:

$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \left[ (V + u)^{2} + u \cdot 2(V + u)(1) \right] = \rho \, a (V + u)(V + 3u)$$

Setting $dW/du = 0$:

- $V + u = 0 \implies u = -V$ (negative, physically not meaningful).
- $V + 3u = 0 \implies u = -V/3$ (negative, not meaningful).

Since $u$ must be positive (magnitude of plate velocity), $dW/du > 0$ for all $u > 0$. **Work done increases monotonically** with $u$ — no physical maximum efficiency exists for this configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** When a plate moves *into* a jet, increasing the plate's speed actually increases the work done without bound. However, in practice, very high $u$ leads to splashing and turbulence losses that this idealized model cannot capture. The textbook (KTU syllabus) treats this as a case with no maximum.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — Standard KTU Style

> **Problem:** A jet of water 75 mm in diameter strikes a fixed vertical plate with a velocity of 20 m/s. Calculate (a) the force on the plate, (b) the work done per second if the plate moves in the same direction as the jet with a velocity of 8 m/s, and (c) the maximum efficiency of the system.

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Jet velocity $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$
- Density of water $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Plate velocity $u = 8 \text{ m/s}$ (for part b)

**Solution:**

Area of jet:

$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.005625 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Part (a) — Force on fixed plate:**

$$F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^{2}$$

$$F = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 400$$

$$\boxed{F = 1767.15 \text{ N} \approx 1.767 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) — Work done per second:**

$$W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V - u)^{2} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times (20 - 8)^{2}$$

$$W = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 144$$

$$\boxed{W = 5089.5 \text{ W} \approx 5.09 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum efficiency:**

The maximum efficiency for the same-direction case is a constant:

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{8}{27} = 29.63\%}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Jet Impact Force Calculation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how a jet impact problem is solved, from the moment the jet is emitted to the final force/work calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nozzle Issues Jet] --> B[Jet Properties: V, a, rho]
    B --> C{Plate State}
    C --> D1[Fixed Plate - u = 0]
    C --> D2[Moving Plate - same dir - u lt V]
    C --> D3[Moving Plate - opposite dir - u toward jet]

    B --> E[Compute Relative Velocity Vr]

    D1 --> F1[Vr = V]
    D2 --> F2[Vr = V minus u]
    D3 --> F3[Vr = V plus u]

    F1 --> G1[Mass Flow Rate mdot = rho a Vr]
    F2 --> G2[Mass Flow Rate mdot = rho a Vr]
    F3 --> G3[Mass Flow Rate mdot = rho a Vr]

    G1 --> H1[Force F = rho a Vr squared]
    G2 --> H2[Force F = rho a Vr squared]
    G3 --> H3[Force F = rho a Vr squared]

    H1 --> I1[Work W = F times 0 = 0]
    H2 --> I2[Work W = F times u]
    H3 --> I3[Work W = F times u]

    I2 --> J{Optimize W w.r.t. u}
    J --> K[Condition: du dW = 0]
    K --> L[Result: u = V slash 3]
    L --> M[Efficiency eta max = 8 by 27 = 29.63 percent]

    H1 --> N[Final Output: F in Newtons]
    I2 --> N
    I3 --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Three Configurations Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CASE_A["CASE 1: FIXED PLATE"]
        A1[Jet V] -->|strikes| A2[Fixed Plate]
        A2 --> A3[Force F = rho a V squared]
        A2 --> A4[Work W = 0]
    end

    subgraph CASE_B["CASE 2: PLATE MOVES WITH JET"]
        B1[Jet V] -->|relative Vr = V minus u| B2[Moving Plate speed u]
        B2 --> B3[Force F = rho a V minus u squared]
        B2 --> B4[Work W = rho a u V minus u squared]
        B4 --> B5[Max Work at u = V slash 3]
    end

    subgraph CASE_C["CASE 3: PLATE MOVES TOWARDS JET"]
        C1[Jet V] -->|relative Vr = V plus u| C2[Moving Plate speed u]
        C2 --> C3[Force F = rho a V plus u squared]
        C2 --> C4[Work W = rho a u V plus u squared]
        C4 --> C5[No physical maximum]
    end
```

## 4.3 Vector Force-Balance Diagram (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE IMPACT - Jet in x direction"]
        V0[Velocity = V along x axis]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER IMPACT - Jet runs along plate"]
        Vx[Velocity component in x = 0]
        Vy[Velocity component in y = V]
    end

    BEFORE -->|Impact| AFTER
    Vx --> CALC[Delta Vx = V minus 0 = V]
    CALC --> FORCE[Force on plate F = mdot times V = rho a V squared]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **left column** of each subgraph represents the input (jet/relative velocity).
> - The **middle column** is the plate interaction.
> - The **right column** gives the output (force, work, efficiency).
> - Note how the *same* formula $F = \rho a V_r^2$ applies to all three cases — only the expression for $V_r$ differs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"State the impulse-momentum principle as applied to the impact of jets on a flat plate. Derive the expression for the force exerted by a jet on a fixed vertical flat plate."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Valuation-Ready):**

The **impulse-momentum theorem** states that the net force acting on a fluid stream is equal to the **rate of change of momentum** of the stream in a given direction.

$$F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V = \rho \, a \, V \times (V_{\text{initial}} - V_{\text{final}})$$

For a jet striking a **fixed vertical flat plate**:
- Initial velocity of jet in the direction of motion = $V$
- Final velocity of jet in the direction of motion (normal to plate) = $0$ (jet is deflected along the plate)
- Mass flow rate per second = $\rho a V$

$$\therefore F = \rho \, a \, V \times (V - 0) = \rho \, a \, V^{2}$$

$$\boxed{F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} \text{ N}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Work done = 0** since the plate is stationary.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define the terms 'relative velocity' and 'mass flow rate striking the plate' in the context of a jet striking a moving flat plate. Why is the relative velocity used in force calculations and not the absolute velocity of the jet?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

When a flat plate moves with velocity $u$ in the direction of a jet of velocity $V$:

**Relative velocity** $V_r$ is the velocity of the jet *as observed from the plate's reference frame*:
$$V_r = V - u \quad \text{(same direction case)} \quad \text{or} \quad V_r = V + u \quad \text{(opposite direction case)}$$

**Mass flow rate striking the plate per second** is the mass of fluid that actually contacts the plate per unit time, which is governed by the *relative* velocity:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \, a \, V_r$$

**Why use relative velocity?**
The force on the plate depends on the *change in momentum of the fluid relative to the plate*. In the plate's reference frame, the jet arrives at $V_r$ and leaves with zero component in the normal direction. Using the absolute velocity $V$ would over-predict the force and work output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is internally divided as **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE** of the two full-question choices (Choice 1 OR Choice 2).

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A jet of water of diameter **50 mm** moving with a velocity of **25 m/s** strikes a fixed vertical plate. Determine:
- (i) The force exerted by the jet on the plate.
- (ii) The pressure of the jet on the plate in kN/m². *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the same plate now moves in the *same direction* as the jet with a velocity of **10 m/s**, calculate:
- (i) The force exerted on the moving plate.
- (ii) The work done per second by the jet on the plate.
- (iii) The efficiency of the system. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Jet velocity $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Area of jet:**
$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^{2} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Force on fixed plate:**
$$F = \rho \, a \, V^{2} = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (25)^{2}$$
$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 625$$
$$\boxed{F = 1227.18 \text{ N} \approx 1.227 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure on the plate:**
$$\text{Pressure} = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area of jet at impact}} = \frac{F}{a}$$
$$P = \frac{1227.18}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.25 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/m}^{2}$$
$$\boxed{P = 625 \text{ kN/m}^{2} = 0.625 \text{ MPa}}$$

*(Note: $P = \rho V^2 = 1000 \times 625 = 6.25 \times 10^5$ N/m² — a quick cross-check.)*

---

**Part (b) — Given:** $u = 10 \text{ m/s}$ (same direction as jet).

**Step 1 — Force on moving plate:**
$$F = \rho \, a \, (V - u)^{2} = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (25 - 10)^{2}$$
$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 225$$
$$\boxed{F = 441.79 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2 — Work done per second:**
$$W = F \times u = 441.79 \times 10 = 4417.9 \text{ W}$$
$$\boxed{W = 4.418 \text{ kW}}$$

(Alternative computation: $W = \rho a u (V-u)^2 = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 225 = 4417.9$ W ✓)

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**
KE of jet per second:
$$KE/sec = \frac{1}{2} \rho a V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (25)^{3}$$
$$KE/sec = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 15625 = 15339.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{W}{KE/sec} = \frac{4417.9}{15339.8} = 0.2880 = 28.80\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 28.80\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The efficiency is *not* 29.63% here because the plate velocity $u = 10$ m/s is **not** equal to $V/3 = 25/3 = 8.33$ m/s. The maximum efficiency of 29.63% is only achieved at $u = V/3$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** A jet of water of diameter **75 mm** discharges from a nozzle with a velocity of **30 m/s** and strikes a vertical plate moving *away* from the jet with a velocity of **15 m/s**. Find:
- (i) The force exerted by the jet on the plate.
- (ii) The work done by the jet on the plate per second.
- (iii) The power lost in the formation of eddies and unrestrained expansion of the jet. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the condition for **maximum efficiency** when a jet strikes a moving vertical flat plate (same direction case). Show that the maximum efficiency is $\dfrac{8}{27} = 29.63\%$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Given:**
- $d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$, $V = 30 \text{ m/s}$, $u = 15 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$a = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^{2} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Force on plate:**
$$F = \rho \, a \, (V - u)^{2} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (30 - 15)^{2}$$
$$F = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 225$$
$$\boxed{F = 994.05 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3 — Work done per second:**
$$W = F \times u = 994.05 \times 15$$
$$\boxed{W = 14910.75 \text{ W} \approx 14.91 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Power lost in eddies:**

The total KE supplied by the jet per second:
$$KE/sec = \frac{1}{2} \rho a V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (30)^{3}$$
$$KE/sec = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.418 \times 27000 = 59643 \text{ W}$$

The jet, after striking, still has KE. In the absolute frame, the water leaves the plate with velocity equal to the plate velocity $u$ (since the relative velocity along the plate surface is conserved in magnitude). So the KE *leaving* per second:
$$KE_{\text{leaving}}/sec = \frac{1}{2} \rho a (V - u) \cdot u^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 \times 225$$
$$KE_{\text{leaving}}/sec = 7455.4 \text{ W}$$

Power lost in eddies/unrestrained expansion:
$$P_{\text{lost}} = KE/sec - W - KE_{\text{leaving}}/sec$$
$$P_{\text{lost}} = 59643 - 14910.75 - 7455.4$$
$$\boxed{P_{\text{lost}} = 37276.85 \text{ W} \approx 37.28 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of Maximum Efficiency:**

The work done per second when the plate moves in the same direction is:
$$W = \rho \, a \, u \, (V - u)^{2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $u$:
$$\frac{dW}{du} = \rho \, a \left[ (V - u)^{2} - 2u(V - u) \right] = \rho \, a \, (V - u)(V - 3u)$$

Setting $\dfrac{dW}{du} = 0$ for extremum:
- $V - u = 0 \implies u = V$ (trivial, gives $W = 0$)
- $V - 3u = 0 \implies \boxed{u = V/3}$ ← **Condition for maximum work**

Substituting $u = V/3$ into the work equation:
$$W_{\max} = \rho \, a \cdot \frac{V}{3} \cdot \left( \frac{2V}{3} \right)^{2} = \frac{4}{27} \rho \, a \, V^{3}$$

The kinetic energy of the jet per second:
$$KE/sec = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, a \, V^{3}$$

Therefore the maximum efficiency:
$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{W_{\max}}{KE/sec} = \frac{4/27}{1/2} = \frac{8}{27}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{8}{27} = 0.2963 = 29.63\%}$$

This result is a *constant* and is independent of the jet velocity, area, or fluid density.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up absolute and relative velocity.** The mass flow rate striking a *moving* plate is $\rho a (V-u)$ or $\rho a (V+u)$, **not** $\rho a V$. Using the absolute velocity $\rho a V$ is the **#1 mistake** in this module and will lose 4–5 marks per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the work term.** Writing force correctly but then claiming $W = F$ instead of $W = F \times u$ loses the second half of the marks in part-(b) numericals.
>
> 3. **Treating $u = V/3$ as a universal "best plate speed" for ALL cases.** It applies **only** to the same-direction moving plate. For the opposite-direction case, **no real maximum exists**; for the fixed plate, $u = 0$.
>
> 4. **Incorrect pressure calculation.** Pressure on a fixed plate is $P = F / a = \rho V^2$ (NOT $\rho V^2 / 2$ — that is dynamic pressure in Bernoulli's equation, which assumes no momentum destruction). The factor of ½ is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency.** Plate velocity $u$ must be in **m/s** (not cm/s) and area $a$ in **m²** (not mm² or cm²). A single unit slip = **−1 mark** as per KTU norms.
>
> 6. **Not stating the assumptions** (smooth plate, no friction, no splash). KTU evaluators explicitly look for the assumption statement in the first 2 lines of any derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Impact of Jet on Vertical Flat Plates**

### ⚡ Core Formulas (Must Memorize)

- ✅ Force on **fixed** plate: $F = \rho a V^2$
- ✅ Force on **moving (same dir.)** plate: $F = \rho a (V-u)^2$
- ✅ Force on **moving (towards jet)** plate: $F = \rho a (V+u)^2$
- ✅ Work done (fixed): $W = 0$
- ✅ Work done (moving same dir.): $W = \rho a u (V-u)^2$
- ✅ Work done (moving towards): $W = \rho a u (V+u)^2$
- ✅ Condition for max work: $u = V/3$ (same-direction case only)
- ✅ Max force (same-dir.): $F_{\max} = \dfrac{4}{9} \rho a V^2$
- ✅ Max work (same-dir.): $W_{\max} = \dfrac{4}{27} \rho a V^3$
- ✅ Max efficiency: $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{8}{27} = 29.63\%$

### ⚡ Critical Conceptual Points

- ✅ The **fixed plate** has the **maximum force** but **zero work**.
- ✅ The **moving plate** has **less force** but **positive work** (energy extracted).
- ✅ The **relative velocity** $(V_r)$ — not absolute velocity — governs both the mass flow rate striking the plate and the force on it.
- ✅ Work done is **force × plate velocity** (not force × jet velocity).
- ✅ The 8/27 efficiency is a **universal constant** for an ideal jet on a single smooth flat moving plate — independent of $\rho$, $a$, or $V$.

### ⚡ Engineering Applications (For 2-Mark Application Questions)

- ✅ Pelton wheel bucket design (curved plate equivalent).
- ✅ Ship water-jet propulsion.
- ✅ Fire monitor / firefighting nozzle thrust.
- ✅ Hydraulic ram pumps.
- ✅ Reaction turbines (impulse principle).

### ⚡ Common KTU Question Patterns

- ✅ **Derivation of $F = \rho a V^2$** for fixed plate (7–10 marks).
- ✅ **Numerical on force and work** for moving plate with given $V, u, d$ (7 marks).
- ✅ **Proof of $u = V/3$ and $\eta_{\max} = 8/27$** (7 marks).
- ✅ **Comparison of fixed vs moving plate** (3–5 marks conceptual).
- ✅ **Why use relative velocity and not absolute velocity** (2–3 marks conceptual).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Jet on Curved Vanes - Fixed and Moving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Jet on Curved Vanes - Fixed and Moving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact of Jet (KTU Terminology):** When a fluid jet issuing from a nozzle strikes a stationary or moving vane, there is a sudden change in the momentum of the jet. According to **Newton's Second Law of Motion**, this change in momentum per unit time produces a **dynamic force** on the vane. The analysis of this force, the resulting work done, and the efficiency of energy transfer forms the core study of "Impact of Jets on Curved Vanes."

A **Curved Vane** is a blade shaped along an arc (or smooth curve) such that the jet enters without shock, glides along the concave surface, and exits with a definite change in direction (typically deflected through an angle **θ**, where **θ = 180° − φ**, φ being the angle subtended by the vane at its center of curvature).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cricket Bat" Intuition

Imagine you hold a **cricket bat** and a ball is thrown at you:
- If you **stand still** (fixed vane), the ball just bounces off — you feel a jerk on your hands. Force exists, but no useful work is done over a distance.
- If you **swing the bat forward** (moving vane) while the ball strikes it, the ball is carried along — you not only feel a force but you also **do work** (force × displacement per second = power).

> [!NOTE]
> **The moving vane is exactly the principle behind the PELTON WHEEL of a hydroelectric power plant.** Water jet strikes moving buckets, water is deflected through nearly **180°**, and the wheel rotates producing mechanical power. The faster the vane moves (up to an optimum speed), the more work is done per second — but only up to a point.

### 1.3 Types of Curved Vanes in KTU Syllabus

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Curved Vane** | Vane is stationary; jet is deflected but no work is extracted continuously | Lab demonstration setup, fire hose nozzle |
| **Single Moving Curved Vane** | One vane moves with velocity `u`; jet strikes once | Conceptual study |
| **Series of Moving Vanes** | Multiple vanes mounted on a wheel (turbine buckets) | **Pelton Wheel** |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of water (ρ):** **1000 kg/m³** (or **ρw = 9810 N/m³** as specific weight)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Angles:** φ = angle of vane arc; θ = angle of deflection = **180° − φ**
- **Nozzle coefficient (Cd):** typically **0.95 to 0.98**
- **Vane coefficient (Kv):** coefficient to account for friction on vane surface, **0.85 to 0.98**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

#### Case A — Jet Striking a **Fixed** Curved Vane

**Step 1 — Identify initial velocity components:**
- Jet velocity = **V** (m/s)
- Area of jet = **a** (m²)
- Mass flow rate striking vane per second = **ρaV** (kg/s)
- Initial momentum per second = **ρaV²** (N) — along jet direction

**Step 2 — Apply impulse-momentum theorem:**
Force on the vane in the direction of jet = Rate of change of momentum
$$F_x = \text{Initial Momentum} - \text{Final Momentum (in x-direction)}$$

**Step 3 — Decompose exit velocity into components:**
If the jet is deflected through angle **θ**, the exit velocity (assuming no friction loss) still has magnitude **V**, but components are **V cos θ** (along original direction) and **V sin θ** (perpendicular).

**Step 4 — Summation of forces:**
- Force parallel to jet: $F_x = \rho a V^2 - \rho a V^2 \cos\theta = \rho a V^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$
- Force perpendicular to jet: $F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$
- **Resultant force**: $F_R = \rho a V^2 \sqrt{(1+\cos\theta)^2 + \sin^2\theta}$

#### Case B — Jet Striking a **Single Moving** Curved Vane

**Step 1 — Switch to the frame of reference of the vane:**
The vane moves with velocity **u** in the direction of the jet. From the vane's perspective, the jet approaches with a **relative velocity**:
$$V_r = V - u$$

**Step 2 — Apply impact-momentum in the moving frame:**
Force on vane (along motion) = $\rho a V_r^2 (1 + \cos\theta)$
*(Same formula as fixed vane, but with Vr replacing V)*

**Step 3 — Compute the work done per second (Power):**
$$P = F_x \times u = \rho a V_r^2 \cdot u \cdot (1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the kinetic energy supplied by jet per second:**
$$KE_{jet} = \frac{1}{2}(\rho a V) V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho a V^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute the efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{\text{Work done / sec}}{\text{KE of jet / sec}} = \frac{\rho a V_r^2 u (1+\cos\theta)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3} = \frac{2 V_r^2 u (1+\cos\theta)}{V^3}$$

Substituting $V_r = V - u$ and $\frac{V_r}{V} = k$ (velocity coefficient ratio):
$$\eta = 2 k^2 \cdot \frac{u}{V} \cdot (1+\cos\theta) = 2(1-k)^2 k (1+\cos\theta) \text{ where } u = kV$$

Wait — careful. Let $k = u/V$. Then $V_r = V(1-k)$, so $V_r^2 = V^2(1-k)^2$.
$$\eta = 2 \cdot V^2(1-k)^2 \cdot kV \cdot (1+\cos\theta) / V^3 = 2k(1-k)^2(1+\cos\theta)$$

**Step 6 — Maximize efficiency** by differentiating w.r.t. k and equating to zero:
$$\frac{d\eta}{dk} = 2(1+\cos\theta)[(1-k)^2 + k \cdot 2(1-k)(-1)] = 2(1+\cos\theta)(1-k)[(1-k) - 2k]$$
$$= 2(1+\cos\theta)(1-k)(1-3k) = 0$$

Since k ≠ 1 (trivial), **k = u/V = 1/3** for maximum efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum efficiency occurs when the vane velocity is exactly 1/3 of the jet velocity.**
> At this condition: $\eta_{max} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 (1+\cos\theta) = \frac{8}{27}(1+\cos\theta)$

For a **semicircular vane** (θ = 180°, φ = 180°, cos θ = −1, so 1+cosθ = 0):
- This naive analysis gives zero. **This is a contradiction!**
- The **resolution** in KTU problems: assume the jet strikes at one tip and leaves from the other tip (tangential entry/exit). In that case, the jet enters tangentially and leaves tangentially, and the work-done analysis uses the **tangential velocities** at the two tips relative to the vane.

#### Case C — Jet Striking a **Semicircular** Moving Vane (Tangential Entry/Exit)

When the jet is directed **tangentially** at the tip of a semicircular vane:
- **At inlet:** Jet direction relative to vane is tangential → angle of vane at inlet tip is **0°** (tangent to motion).
- **At outlet:** Jet leaves the other tip, also tangential → angle of vane at outlet is **0°**.
- Velocity triangles at both tips are identical.

Force on vane along its motion:
$$F_x = \rho a V_r^2$$

*(Since 1+cos180° = 0 in the formula, but using tangential entry correction: the exit velocity component in jet direction is −Vr, so Fx = ρaVr(Vr − (−Vr)) = 2ρaVr², and the work done per unit mass of fluid is Vr². )*

Work done/sec by jet on vane:
$$P = \rho a V_r^2 \cdot u$$

Kinetic energy supplied/sec:
$$KE = \frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{\rho a V_r^2 u}{\frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3} = \frac{2 V_r^2 u}{V^3} = 2k(1-k)^2$$

**Maximum efficiency** when k = 1/3:
$$\eta_{max} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{8}{27} \approx 0.296 = 29.6\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The maximum theoretical efficiency of a single curved vane (semicircular) is 8/27 ≈ 29.63%. This is a HIGH-YIELD number in KTU exams.**

#### Case D — Jet Striking **Series of Moving Vanes** (Pelton Wheel Concept)

In practice, the jet cannot strike the same vane continuously — it must strike the next vane after the vane has moved. Hence the vane must be **shorter** than the jet diameter.

**Ratio of vane length to jet diameter:**
$$\psi = \frac{\text{Length of vane arc}}{\text{Diameter of jet}}$$

**Mass of water actually striking per second:**
$$\dot{m}_{actual} = \rho \cdot a \cdot (V - u) \cdot \psi = \rho a V_r \psi$$

(For a single vane, ψ = 1; for series of vanes, ψ < 1.)

**Friction effect:**
A coefficient of friction **Cv** (or Kv) accounts for velocity loss along the curved surface:
$$V_{r2} = C_v \cdot V_{r1} = C_v \cdot V_r$$

where $C_v$ is typically 0.85 to 0.98 for smooth vanes.

**Work done per second (with friction):**
$$P = \dot{m}_{actual} \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}) \cdot u$$

For a semicircular vane with tangential entry/exit and friction:
$$P = \rho a V_r \psi \cdot u \cdot V_r(1 + C_v) = \rho a V_r^2 u \psi (1 + C_v)$$

Efficiency (with friction):
$$\eta = \frac{2 \psi (1+C_v) V_r^2 u}{V^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For Cv = 1 (no friction) and ψ = 1 (single vane):** This reduces back to $2V_r^2 u / V^3$ — exactly the same as Case C.

**Condition for maximum efficiency** (series of vanes with friction):
$$\frac{d\eta}{dk} = 0 \Rightarrow k = \frac{u}{V} = \frac{1}{1 + C_v}$$

So **u/V = 1/(1 + Cv)** for maximum efficiency. For Cv = 0.9, u/V = 1/1.9 ≈ 0.526.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print-Friendly Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass flow rate of jet | $\dot{m} = \rho a V$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Force on **Fixed** vane (∥ to jet) | $F_x = \rho a V^2(1+\cos\theta)$ | N |
| 3 | Force on **Fixed** vane (⊥ to jet) | $F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$ | N |
| 4 | Resultant force on fixed vane | $F_R = \rho a V^2 \sqrt{(1+\cos\theta)^2 + \sin^2\theta}$ | N |
| 5 | Relative velocity (moving vane) | $V_r = V - u$ | m/s |
| 6 | Force on **Moving** vane | $F_x = \rho a V_r^2(1+\cos\theta)$ | N |
| 7 | Work done/sec on moving vane | $P = \rho a V_r^2 u (1+\cos\theta)$ | W (J/s) |
| 8 | Work done per unit weight (general) | $W = \frac{V_r^2(1+\cos\theta)}{g} \cdot \frac{u}{V}$ | m·N/N = m |
| 9 | Work done per unit weight (semicircular) | $W = \frac{V_r^2}{g} \cdot \frac{u}{V} \cdot (1+C_v)$ | m |
| 10 | KE of jet per second | $KE = \frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3$ | W |
| 11 | Efficiency (general moving vane) | $\eta = \frac{2 V_r^2 u(1+\cos\theta)}{V^3}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Efficiency (semicircular, tangential) | $\eta = \frac{2 \psi (1+C_v) V_r^2 u}{V^3}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Maximum efficiency condition | $\dfrac{u}{V} = \dfrac{1}{1+C_v}$ | ratio |
| 14 | Maximum η (ideal, Cv=1, semicircular) | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{8}{27}$ ≈ 29.63 % | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Pelton Wheel Turbines** (Hydroelectric Power Plants): Direct application of curved vane impact.
- **Bucket-type impulse turbines** in mini-hydro plants (especially high-head, low-flow sites in Kerala like Idukki).
- **Fire-fighting monitors and ship propulsion** (ship's jet propulsion uses reversed principle — jet pushes ship forward).
- **Agricultural water sprinklers** (rotating arm struck by jet — lawn sprinklers work on moving vane principle).
- **Cutting tools using water jets** (the jet strikes a curved surface and the reaction is contained).
