The shaft work is then typically used to drive an electric generator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A hydraulic turbine is a prime mover that uses water as the working fluid to produce mechanical energy by exploiting the head difference between the reservoir/source and the tailrace. ### 1.2 Broad Classification of Hydraulic Turbines ``` HYDRAULIC TURBINES ├── IMPULSE TYPE (Pressure energy fully converted to KE before entry) │ └── Pelton Wheel │ └── REACTION TYPE (Pressure drop occurs in the runner itself) ├── Francis Turbine └── Kaplan / Propeller Turbine ``` ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel Story" Imagine three different "water wheels" placed in a stream: 1. **Pelton Wheel** → Picture a water tap with a **high-pressure jet** striking a row of spoons. The jet hits one spoon at a time and *pushes* it. The water loses **all pressure before hitting the wheel** — it's pure speed (kinetic energy). This is like throwing a tennis ball at a paddle. 2. **Francis Turbine** → Picture the wheel sitting **inside a pipe full of water**, where the pipe is swirling. The water flows *through* the curved blades. Both **pressure and velocity change** as it passes. It's like a ceiling fan where the air pushes *through* the blades while swirling. 3. **Kaplan Turbine** → Like the Francis, but the **blades can rotate** to adjust angle, like a boat propeller in a confined tunnel. The flow enters axially (parallel to shaft) and exits axially. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for Exams:** > - **Impulse Turbine:** Atmospheric pressure acts on the runner; all head → KE at nozzle → kinetic energy conversion. > - **Reaction Turbine:** Pressure drop occurs *across the runner*; both pressure and velocity change inside the runner passages. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit | |----------|--------|-------|------| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | | Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000** | kg/m³ | | Specific weight of water | $w = \rho g$ | **9810** | N/m³ | | Universal gas constant (not needed) | — | — | — | ### 1.5 Selection Based on Head & Specific Speed | Turbine Type | Head Range (m) | Specific Speed Range | |--------------|----------------|----------------------| | Pelton | High (> 300 m) | 10 – 70 (metric) | | Francis | Medium (30 – 300 m) | 60 – 400 (metric) | | Kaplan | Low (< 30 m) | 300 – 1000 (metric) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Specific Speed ($N_s$):** Defined as the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that would develop **unit power (1 kW)** under a **unit head (1 m)**. It is the most important index for **turbine selection** in KTU problems. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 THE PELTON WHEEL (Impulse Turbine) #### 2.1.1 Principle and Construction - Invented by **Lester A. Pelton (1880s)**. - **Working Principle:** A high-velocity water jet from a nozzle strikes a series of **bucket-shaped vanes** mounted on the periphery of a runner. The jet's kinetic energy is converted to rotational kinetic energy. - The jet strikes **tangentially**; the wheel rotates in a **vertical plane**. **Main Components:** 1. **Nozzle & Needle Assembly** – Controls flow rate and jet diameter. 2. **Runner with Buckets** – Splits the jet into two halves (to equalize side thrust). 3. **Casing** – Prevents water splashing; does *not* act as a pressure vessel. 4. **Brake Jet / Deflector** – Used for emergency stop without closing the main nozzle. 5. **Governor** – Controls needle movement to maintain constant speed. #### 2.1.2 Velocity Triangles for Pelton Wheel For a Pelton bucket (curved vane with exit angle $\beta$ from 165° to 170°): **At Inlet (relative to vane):** - $V_1$ = Absolute velocity of jet = $\sqrt{2gH}$ - $u$ = Blade velocity (tangential) - $V_{r1}$ = Relative velocity at inlet **At Outlet:** - $V_{r2} = k_f \cdot V_{r1}$ where $k_f$ = friction coefficient (typically 0.92 – 0.98) - $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta$ (small, ideally ≈ 0) - $V_{r2}$ makes angle $\beta$ with the direction of motion > [!NOTE] > In an **ideal frictionless** Pelton bucket, $V_{r2} = V_{r1}$ and $\beta = 180°$, meaning the jet is turned back on itself → all KE extracted. #### 2.1.3 Work Done by Pelton Wheel Work done per second per unit weight (Euler's equation): $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{V_{w1} u \pm V_{w2} u}{g}$$ For a single jet with negligible $V_{w2}$: $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{u}{g}\left(V_1 - u\right)\left(1 + k_f \cos\beta\right) \cdot V_1$$ Wait — more correctly (using $V_{w1} = V_1$): $$\boxed{\frac{W}{w \cdot Q} = \frac{u(V_1 - k_f V_{r1}\cos\beta - u)}{g}}$$ For **frictionless bucket with $\beta = 180°$**: $$\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)}{g}$$ #### 2.1.4 Efficiencies of Pelton Wheel | Efficiency | Definition | Formula | |------------|------------|---------| | Hydraulic ($\eta_h$) | Power in jet / Power at inlet | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k_f\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ | | Mechanical ($\eta_m$) | Power to shaft / Power from water | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Runner Power}}$ | | Overall ($\eta_o$) | Shaft Power / Power supplied | $\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_m$ | | Wheel efficiency (frictionless, single jet) | $\eta_w = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)}{V_1^2}$ | **Maximum = 50%** at $u = V_1/2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The maximum wheel efficiency of an ideal single-jet Pelton wheel is **50%**, and this occurs when the **bucket velocity is half the jet velocity** ($u = V_1/2$). --- ### 2.2 THE FRANCIS TURBINE (Reaction Turbine) #### 2.2.1 Principle and Construction - Invented by **James B. Francis (1849)**. - **Working Principle:** Water enters the runner **radially** (or mixed flow) under pressure. The flow is guided by **guide vanes (wicket gates)** and passes through the curved runner blades, where both **velocity and pressure decrease** — producing reaction force. **Main Components:** 1. **Spiral Casing** – Distributes water uniformly around the runner. 2. **Guide Vanes (Wicket Gates)** – Control flow rate and maintain radial entry. 3. **Stay Vanes** – Support guide vanes and convert tangential to radial flow. 4. **Runner** – Mixed-flow type; water enters radially, exits axially. 5. **Draft Tube** – Converts kinetic energy at exit to pressure energy (recovers head). > [!NOTE] > **Mixed Flow:** The Francis runner receives water **inward radially** at the outer periphery and discharges **axially downward** through the draft tube. This is why the shaft is **vertical**. #### 2.2.2 Velocity Triangles for Francis Turbine **At Inlet (Inlet Diameter $D_1$):** - $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ (blade velocity at inlet) - $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ - $V_{f1}$ = Velocity of flow at inlet = $K_f \sqrt{2gH}$ (where $K_f$ = flow ratio) - $V_{w1} = \sqrt{V_1^2 - V_{f1}^2}$ (whirl velocity at inlet) - Inlet vane angle $\theta$: $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1} - u_1}$ **At Outlet (Outlet Diameter $D_2$):** - $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ - $V_{f2}$ = Velocity of flow at outlet (equal to $V_{f1}$ if radial discharge assumed) - $V_{w2}$ = Whirl velocity at outlet (≈ 0 for minimum kinetic energy exit) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Assumption:** For maximum efficiency in Francis turbine, the **whirl at exit is zero** ($V_{w2} = 0$), ensuring no kinetic energy is wasted. #### 2.2.3 Work Done by Francis Turbine (Euler's Equation) $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ For **minimum kinetic energy exit** ($V_{w2} = 0$): $$\boxed{\frac{W}{w} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}}$$ **Per unit weight per unit time (work done per second per unit weight):** $$\boxed{\frac{W}{w} = \frac{u_1 V_{w1}}{g} = \frac{u_1 \sqrt{V_1^2 - V_{f1}^2}}{g}}$$ For **unit discharge ($Q = 1$)**: $$W = \rho Q \left[ V_{w1} u_1 \right] \quad \text{(per second)}$$ #### 2.2.4 Efficiencies of Francis Turbine | Efficiency | Formula | Remarks | |------------|---------|---------| | Hydraulic | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} u_1}{gH}$ | $H$ = total head on turbine | | Volumetric | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actually used}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}}$ | Accounts for leakage | | Mechanical | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Runner Power}}$ | Accounts for bearing/friction losses | | Overall | $\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | Most commonly asked in KTU | #### 2.2.5 Runner Vane Angle ($\phi$) $$\tan\phi = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1} - u_1}$$ This is the angle the runner vane makes with the tangent at inlet — required in design problems. --- ### 2.3 THE KAPLAN TURBINE (Axial-Flow Reaction Turbine) #### 2.3.1 Principle and Construction - Invented by **Viktor Kaplan (1913)**. - **Working Principle:** An **axial-flow** reaction turbine with **adjustable runner blades** and **adjustable guide vanes**. Water flows **parallel to the shaft axis**, and the runner resembles a ship's propeller enclosed in a tube. **Main Components:** 1. **Scroll Casing** – Same as Francis, distributes water uniformly. 2. **Guide Vane Mechanism** – Adjustable to vary flow. 3. **Runner with Adjustable Blades** – Can be rotated about their own axis (governed by servomotor). 4. **Draft Tube** – Essential to recover kinetic exit energy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Kaplan turbines are used for **low head (2 – 30 m)** and **high discharge** situations (e.g., run-of-river projects). The adjustable blades allow high efficiency over a wide range of operating conditions. #### 2.3.2 Velocity Triangles for Kaplan Turbine **At Inlet (Hub Tip $D_1$, Outer $D_0$):** - $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ - $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ - $V_{f1} = K_f \sqrt{2gH}$ - $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1} - u_1}$ **At Outlet:** - $V_{f2} = V_{f1}$ (continuity, ignoring losses) - $V_{w2} \approx 0$ (for minimum exit KE) - $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ (outlet diameter of hub) #### 2.3.3 Discharge Through Kaplan Turbine $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_0^2 - D_b^2\right) V_{f1}}$$ where $D_0$ = outer diameter, $D_b$ = hub diameter. #### 2.3.4 Work Done by Kaplan Turbine Same Euler equation as Francis: $$\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$ **Condition for maximum efficiency:** $$\boxed{u_1 = \frac{V_{w1}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{V_1^2 - V_{f1}^2}}{2}}$$ #### 2.3.5 Efficiencies — Same as Francis $\eta_h, \eta_v, \eta_m, \eta_o$ — all defined identically. #### 2.3.6 Draft Tube Theory The draft tube is a **diffuser** that: 1. Converts kinetic energy at runner exit → pressure energy (recovers $H_d$). 2. Allows the runner to be set **above tailwater level** (avoids cavitation, ease of maintenance). $$\boxed{H_d = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_3^2}{2g}}$$ where $V_2$ = velocity at draft tube inlet, $V_3$ = velocity at draft tube outlet. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable To | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------| | 1 | Jet Velocity | $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Pelton | | 2 | Blade Velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | All | | 3 | Work done per unit weight (Pelton, frictionless) | $\dfrac{W}{wQ} = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)}{g}$ | m | Pelton | | 4 | Max wheel efficiency | $\eta_{w,\max} = 0.50$ at $u = V_1/2$ | — | Pelton | | 5 | Hydraulic efficiency (Pelton) | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k_f\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ | — | Pelton | | 6 | Work done per unit weight (Francis/Kaplan) | $\dfrac{W}{wQ} = \dfrac{V_{w1}u_1}{g}$ | m | Francis, Kaplan | | 7 | Velocity of flow | $V_f = K_f\sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Francis, Kaplan | | 8 | Whirl velocity | $V_w = \sqrt{V_1^2 - V_f^2}$ | m/s | Francis, Kaplan | | 9 | Inlet vane angle | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{V_f}{V_w - u}$ | rad/deg | Francis, Kaplan | | 10 | Discharge (Kaplan) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_0^2 - D_b^2)V_f$ | m³/s | Kaplan | | 11 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | metric | All (selection) | | 12 | Draft Tube Head Recovery | $H_d = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_3^2}{2g}$ | m | Francis, Kaplan | | 13 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | All | | 14 | Power Output (Shaft) | $P = \eta_o \cdot \rho g Q H$ | W | All | | 15 | Power from Jet (Pelton) | $P_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}V_1^2$ | W | Pelton | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **hydroelectric power plant design**, penstock sizing, runner blade design (CFD validation), and **cavitation analysis** (Thoma's sigma criterion). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Work Done by a Single-Jet Pelton Wheel **Starting Point — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation:** $$\text{Work done per unit weight per second} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ For a Pelton wheel: - Water enters tangent to wheel: $V_{w1} = V_1$ (jet velocity) - Water exits nearly with the bucket: $V_{w2} \approx u_1 - V_{r2}\cos\beta$ - For symmetrically designed buckets: $V_{r1} = V_{r2} = V_1 - u_1$ (when jet strikes head-on) **Step 1 — Express inlet whirl:** $$V_{w1} = V_1$$ **Step 2 — Express outlet whirl (using velocity triangle):** $$V_{w2} = V_{r2} \cos\beta - u_1 = -k_f (V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta - u_1$$ where $k_f$ accounts for friction loss ($V_{r2} = k_f V_{r1}$). **Step 3 — Substitute into Euler's equation:** $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{V_1 u_1 - u_1[k_f(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta + u_1]}{g}$$ **Step 4 — Expand and simplify:** $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{V_1 u_1 - k_f u_1(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta - u_1^2}{g}$$ **Step 5 — Factor:** $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{u_1(V_1 - u_1) - k_f u_1(V_1 - u_1)\cos\beta}{g}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{W}{w} = \frac{u_1(V_1 - u_1)(1 + k_f \cos\beta)}{g}}$$ **Step 6 — Work done per unit weight per unit time (per unit discharge $Q$):** $$\boxed{\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{u_1(V_1 - u_1)(1 + k_f \cos\beta)}{g}}$$ **Step 7 — Special case: Frictionless + $\beta = 180°$:** With $k_f = 1$ and $\cos 180° = -1$: $$\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{u_1(V_1 - u_1)(1 - 1)}{g} \cdot (-1)$$ Wait, careful — when $\beta = 180°$, the jet is reversed. Recompute: $$\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{u_1(V_1 - u_1)(1 + \cos 180°)}{g} = \frac{u_1(V_1 - u_1)(1 - 1)}{g} = 0$$ This is wrong. The correct classical result requires the bucket to have the jet turning through $180°$ relative to the wheel, which means the **relative velocity at exit makes angle $\beta$ with direction of blade motion in opposite sense**. **Corrected Step 7 (classical derivation):** For ideal bucket with no friction and jet deflected through $180°$: $$\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 + V_{w2} u_1}{g} = \frac{V_1 u_1 + (V_1 - 2u_1)u_1}{g}$$ $$= \frac{2u_1 V_1 - 2u_1^2}{g} = \frac{2u_1(V_1 - u_1)}{g}$$ **Step 8 — Wheel efficiency = Power extracted / Power in jet:** $$\eta_w = \frac{\rho Q \cdot \frac{2u(V_1-u)}{g}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^2} = \frac{2u(V_1-u)}{V_1^2}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_w = 2\dfrac{u}{V_1}\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{V_1}\right)}$$ **Step 9 — Maximum wheel efficiency:** Let $x = u/V_1$: $$\eta_w = 2x(1 - x) = 2x - 2x^2$$ $$\frac{d\eta_w}{dx} = 2 - 4x = 0 \implies x = \frac{1}{2}$$ $$\therefore u = \frac{V_1}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_{w,\max} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 = 50\%$$ ✅ **Q.E.D. — Maximum efficiency of single-jet Pelton wheel = 50% at $u = V_1/2$.** --- ### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Work Done by Francis Turbine **Step 1 — Euler's equation for incompressible flow:** $$W = \dot{m}(V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2)$$ **Step 2 — For Francis turbine, choose sign convention (+ if opposite, − if same direction):** $$W = \rho Q (V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2)$$ **Step 3 — Apply boundary condition $V_{w2} = 0$ (minimum kinetic energy exit):** $$W = \rho Q V_{w1} u_1$$ **Step 4 — Convert to work per unit weight:** $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{\rho Q V_{w1} u_1}{\rho g} = \frac{Q V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$ **Step 5 — Per unit weight per unit time:** $$\boxed{\frac{W}{w \cdot \text{time}} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}}$$ **Step 6 — Express $V_{w1}$ in terms of head and flow ratio:** Given $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ and $V_{f1} = K_f \sqrt{2gH}$: $$V_{w1} = \sqrt{V_1^2 - V_{f1}^2} = \sqrt{2gH}\sqrt{1 - K_f^2}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute back:** $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{u_1 \sqrt{2gH}\sqrt{1 - K_f^2}}{g}$$ **Step 8 — Hydraulic efficiency:** $$\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{gH} = \frac{u_1 \sqrt{2gH}\sqrt{1-K_f^2}}{gH} = \frac{u_1 \sqrt{2}}{g^{1/2} H^{1/2}}\sqrt{1-K_f^2}$$ **Step 9 — Condition for max efficiency:** For given head, $\eta_h$ is maximum when $u_1$ is maximum (but constrained by $V_{w1} - u_1 > 0$). Optimal condition: $u_1 = V_{w1}/2$ → same as Pelton. --- ### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Specific Speed and Turbine Selection **Step 1 — Definition of specific speed:** $N_s$ is the speed of a geometrically similar turbine that produces 1 kW under 1 m head. **Step 2 — Dimensional analysis (omitted for brevity but standard result):** $$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}}$$ where $N$ in rpm, $P$ in kW, $H$ in metres. **Step 3 — Selection criteria:** | $N_s$ (metric) | Turbine Type | |----------------|--------------| | 10 – 35 | Single-jet Pelton | | 35 – 70 | Multi-jet Pelton | | 60 – 300 | Francis (slow/medium) | | 300 – 600 | Francis (fast) | | 300 – 1000 | Kaplan / Propeller | --- ### 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — PROBLEM-SOLVING FLOW ``` STEP 1 → Identify turbine type from data STEP 2 → Compute V₁ = √(2gH) STEP 3 → Compute u = πDN/60 STEP 4 → Compute V_f (if given K_f, else derive) STEP 5 → Construct velocity triangles STEP 6 → Apply Euler equation STEP 7 → Compute efficiency STEP 8 → Compute specific speed for verification ``` > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem Pattern:** Given head $H$, discharge $Q$, diameter $D$, speed $N$, find (a) runner vane angles, (b) work done, (c) efficiencies, (d) power. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hydraulic Turbine Family ```mermaid flowchart TB R1["Reservoir
High Head Source"] P1["Penstock
Conveys High Pressure Water"] NOZ["Nozzle Assembly
Converts PE to KE"] BUCK["Pelton Buckets
Impulse Force Transfer"] PELTON["Pelton Runner
Vertical Shaft"] GOV1["Governor
Needle Control"] S1["Generator
Electricity Output"] SC["Spiral Casing
Pressure Distribution"] GV["Guide Vanes
Flow Regulation"] FRUN["Francis Runner
Mixed Flow"] DT1["Draft Tube
Energy Recovery"] FRAN["Francis Generator"] SC2["Scroll Casing"] GV2["Adjustable Guide Vanes"] KRUN["Kaplan Runner
Adjustable Blades"] AB["Servomotor for
Blade Pitch Control"] DT2["Draft Tube"] KGEN["Kaplan Generator"] R1 --> P1 --> NOZ NOZ --> BUCK --> PELTON NOZ --> GOV1 PELTON --> S1 P1 --> SC --> GV --> FRUN --> DT1 --> FRAN GV -.regulation.-> GV P1 --> SC2 --> GV2 --> KRUN --> DT2 --> KGEN KRUN -.blade angle.-> AB GV2 -.flow control.-> GV2 ``` ### 4.2 Velocity Triangle Diagram — Pelton Wheel ```mermaid flowchart LR J["Jet V₁
absolute"] U["Blade velocity u
tangential"] VR1["Relative velocity V_r1
at entry"] VR2["Relative velocity V_r2
at exit, angle β"] W1["Whirl V_w1 = V₁"] W2["Whirl V_w2
residual"] J -->|"V₁ = V_w1"| W1 W1 -->|"minus u"| VR1 VR1 -->|"friction k_f"| VR2 VR2 -->|"minus u"| W2 U -.tangential direction.-> VR1 ``` ### 4.3 Velocity Triangle Diagram — Francis / Kaplan (Inlet Side) ```mermaid flowchart LR V1["V₁ absolute
√2gH"] UF["V_f flow velocity
radial / axial"] UW["V_w whirl velocity
tangential"] UU["u blade velocity
πDN/60"] ALPHA["Vane inlet angle α
tan α = V_f / V_w - u"] V1 -->|"resultant"| UF V1 --> UW UF --> ALPHA UW --> ALPHA UU -.tangent direction.-> ALPHA ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — All Three Turbines ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PELTON["PELTON WHEEL — Impulse"] P1["Head: High > 300m"] P2["Specific Speed: 10-70"] P3["Flow: Tangential Jet"] P4["No draft tube needed"] P5["Bucket angle: ~165°-170°"] end subgraph FRANCIS["FRANCIS TURBINE — Reaction"] F1["Head: Medium 30-300m"] F2["Specific Speed: 60-400"] F3["Flow: Mixed (Radial→Axial)"] F4["Draft tube essential"] F5["Inlet vane angle from V-triangle"] end subgraph KAPLAN["KAPLAN TURBINE — Axial Reaction"] K1["Head: Low < 30m"] K2["Specific Speed: 300-1000"] K3["Flow: Purely Axial"] K4["Adjustable blades + guide vanes"] K5["Highest efficiency range"] end PELTON -.selection boundary.-> FRANCIS FRANCIS -.selection boundary.-> KAPLAN ``` ### 4.5 Energy Conversion Flow Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR PE["Pressure Energy
at inlet"] KE1["Kinetic Energy
at nozzle exit"] KE2["Kinetic Energy
at bucket / runner"] ME["Mechanical Energy
at shaft"] ELE["Electrical Energy
at generator"] PE -->|"Nozzle
Conversion"| KE1 KE1 -->|"Impulse on buckets"| KE2 KE2 -->|"Euler work"| ME ME -->|"Generator"| ELE ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the principle of working of a Pelton wheel with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** The Pelton wheel is an **impulse-type hydraulic turbine** that operates on the principle of **impulse**. Water from a high-head reservoir is conveyed through a penstock to a **nozzle**, where all the pressure energy is converted into a high-velocity jet (kinetic energy). This jet strikes a series of **hemispherical/bucket-shaped vanes** mounted on the periphery of a circular runner. The jet is split into two equal halves by the central splitter of the bucket, deflected through an angle of nearly 165°–170°, and its momentum is transferred to the runner. This produces an impulsive force that rotates the wheel, and the mechanical energy is transmitted via the shaft to a generator. A **governor** controls the needle valve to regulate the flow, and a **brake jet/deflector** is provided for emergency shutdown. **Key Points:** - Impulse turbine → no pressure change across runner - Jet velocity $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$ - $u = V_1/2$ for maximum efficiency (50%) --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Differentiate between an impulse and a reaction turbine.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine | |---------|---------------|------------------| | Pressure change across runner | No (atmospheric) | Yes (drops) | | Energy at entry to runner | Pure KE | Both KE + PE | | Casing | Not pressure-tight | Pressure-tight | | Water contact with runner | Not full | Full (flow through passages) | | Examples | Pelton wheel | Francis, Kaplan | | Draft tube | Not required | Required | | Specific speed range | Low (10–70) | High (60–1000) | | Flow direction | Tangential | Radial/Axial/Mixed | --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **(a)** A Pelton wheel has a mean bucket speed of **15 m/s** with a jet velocity of **30 m/s**. The buckets deflect the jet through an angle of **165°**. The friction coefficient is **0.95**. Calculate: 1. Work done per unit weight of water per second. 2. Hydraulic efficiency. 3. Maximum wheel efficiency and corresponding bucket velocity. **(b)** The discharge through the wheel is **0.4 m³/s**. Find the power developed by the wheel and the specific speed if the head is **46 m**. --- ##### **Solution to Part (a) (7 marks):** **Given:** - $u = 15$ m/s - $V_1 = 30$ m/s - $\beta = 165°$ → $\cos 165° = -\cos 15° = -0.9659$ - $k_f = 0.95$ **Step 1 — Relative velocity at inlet:** $$V_{r1} = V_1 - u = 30 - 15 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Relative velocity at exit:** $$V_{r2} = k_f \cdot V_{r1} = 0.95 \times 15 = 14.25 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Whirl velocities:** $$V_{w1} = V_1 = 30 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta - u = 14.25 \times (-0.9659) - 15 = -13.764 - 15 = -28.764 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Work done per unit weight per second (Euler equation):** $$\frac{W}{wQ} = \frac{V_{w1} u + V_{w2} u}{g} = \frac{u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})}{g}$$ $$= \frac{15 \times (30 + (-28.764))}{9.81} = \frac{15 \times 1.236}{9.81} = \frac{18.54}{9.81}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{W}{wQ} = 1.89 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 5 — Hydraulic efficiency:** $$\eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k_f\cos\beta)}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 15 \times (1 + 0.95 \times (-0.9659))}{900}$$ $$= \frac{450 \times (1 - 0.9176)}{900} = \frac{450 \times 0.0824}{900} = \frac{37.08}{900}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_h = 0.0412 = 4.12\%}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum wheel efficiency (frictionless, $\beta = 180°$):** $$\eta_{w,\max} = 0.50 \text{ at } u = V_1/2 = 15 \text{ m/s}$$ ✓ Already satisfied here. --- ##### **Solution to Part (b) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Power developed by the wheel:** $$P = \rho Q \cdot \frac{W}{wQ} \cdot g = \rho Q \cdot V_{w1} u + \rho Q \cdot V_{w2} u$$ Equivalently: $$P = \dot{m} \cdot u(V_{w1} + V_{w2}) = \rho Q \cdot u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$ $$P = 1000 \times 0.4 \times 15 \times (30 - 28.764) = 400 \times 15 \times 1.236$$ $$P = 400 \times 18.54 = 7416 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P = 7.416 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 2 — Specific Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$ First, compute runner speed $N$. For a Pelton wheel, the bucket diameter $D$ is required. Use $u = \pi DN/60$. Assuming $D = 1.2$ m (standard): $$N = \frac{60 u}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 15}{\pi \times 1.2} = \frac{900}{3.7699} = 238.7 \text{ rpm}$$ Now: $$N_s = \frac{238.7 \times \sqrt{7.416}}{46^{1.25}}$$ $\sqrt{7.416} = 2.723$ $46^{1.25} = 46 \times 46^{0.25} = 46 \times 2.6056 = 119.86$ $$N_s = \frac{238.7 \times 2.723}{119.86} = \frac{650.0}{119.86}$$ $$\boxed{N_s \approx 5.42 \text{ (single-jet Pelton)}}$$ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **(a)** A Francis turbine has an **inlet diameter of 1.2 m** and **outlet diameter of 0.6 m**. The runner speed is **300 rpm**. The guide vane angle at inlet is **20°** and the velocity of flow is constant at **3 m/s**. Determine: 1. Inlet and outlet blade angles. 2. Work done per unit weight per second. 3. Runner power if discharge is **8 m³/s**. **(b)** If the head on the turbine is **20 m**, find the hydraulic, mechanical (assume $\eta_m = 0.92$), and overall efficiencies. Also compute the specific speed. --- ##### **Solution to Part (a) (7 marks):** **Given:** - $D_1 = 1.2$ m, $D_2 = 0.6$ m - $N = 300$ rpm - $\alpha = 20°$ (guide vane angle = flow angle at inlet) - $V_{f1} = V_{f2} = 3$ m/s **Step 1 — Blade velocities:** $$u_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 300}{60} = \frac{1130.97}{60} = 18.85 \text{ m/s}$$ $$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 300}{60} = 9.425 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity of flow and whirl at inlet:** Since $\alpha = 20°$ is the angle between $V_1$ and tangent: $$\tan\alpha = \frac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1}} \implies V_{w1} = \frac{V_{f1}}{\tan 20°} = \frac{3}{0.364} = 8.24 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Inlet blade angle $\theta_1$:** $$\tan\theta_1 = \frac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1} - u_1} = \frac{3}{8.24 - 18.85} = \frac{3}{-10.61} = -0.2828$$ $$\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}(-0.2828) = -15.81°$$ (Negative means blade is curved opposite to rotation direction — typical for reaction turbines.) $$\boxed{\theta_1 \approx -15.81° \text{ (or } 164.19° \text{ from positive tangent)}}$$ **Step 4 — Outlet blade angle $\theta_2$:** For minimum kinetic energy exit: $V_{w2} = 0$ $$\tan\theta_2 = \frac{V_{f2}}{u_2 - V_{w2}} = \frac{3}{9.425 - 0} = 0.3184$$ $$\boxed{\theta_2 = \tan^{-1}(0.3184) = 17.67°}$$ **Step 5 — Work done per unit weight per second:** $$\frac{W}{w} = \frac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{8.24 \times 18.85 - 0}{9.81}$$ $$= \frac{155.32}{9.81} = 15.83 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{W}{w} = 15.83 \text{ m of water}}$$ **Step 6 — Runner Power:** $$P_{runner} = \rho g Q \cdot \frac{W}{w} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 15.83$$ $$= 9810 \times 126.64 = 1.242 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{runner} = 1242 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ##### **Solution to Part (b) (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Hydraulic efficiency:** $$\eta_h = \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{gH} = \frac{8.24 \times 18.85}{9.81 \times 20} = \frac{155.32}{196.2}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_h = 0.7917 = 79.17\%}$$ **Step 2 — Mechanical efficiency (given):** $$\eta_m = 0.92 = 92\%$$ **Step 3 — Overall efficiency (assuming $\eta_v = 1$, i.e., ideal volumetric):** $$\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.7917 \times 1.0 \times 0.92 = 0.7284$$ $$\boxed{\eta_o = 72.84\%}$$ **Step 4 — Shaft Power:** $$P_{shaft} = \eta_o \cdot \rho g Q H = 0.7284 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 20$$ $$= 0.7284 \times 1{,}569{,}600 = 1{,}143{,}340 \text{ W} \approx 1143 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Specific Speed:** $$N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{300 \times \sqrt{1143}}{20^{1.25}}$$ $\sqrt{1143} = 33.81$ $20^{1.25} = 20 \times 20^{0.25} = 20 \times 2.1147 = 42.295$ $$N_s = \frac{300 \times 33.81}{42.295} = \frac{10143}{42.295}$$ $$\boxed{N_s \approx 239.8 \text{ (Francis range ✓)}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign error in Euler's equation:** Confusing the direction of $V_{w2}$ when the blade deflects water back. Use the convention: positive if opposite to blade motion. > 2. **Mixing up angles:** $\alpha$ = guide vane angle (flow angle); $\theta$ = runner vane angle. They are **different**! > 3. **Units in specific speed:** $N$ must be in rpm, $P$ in kW, $H$ in metres. Mixing units = zero. > 4. **Forgetting $\eta_v$:** When leakage is mentioned, $\eta_o = \eta_h \eta_v \eta_m$ — students often forget $\eta_v$. > 5. **Draft tube head recovery:** Many students forget to subtract draft tube loss or recover $H_d$ in net head calculation. > 6. **Maximum wheel efficiency:** Saying 100% or 60% — **correct answer is 50%** for single-jet frictionless Pelton. --- ### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST #### ✅ Pelton Wheel - [ ] Impulse turbine, no draft tube needed - [ ] Head range: **High (> 300 m)** - [ ] $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$, $u = \pi D N / 60$ - [ ] Optimal: $u = V_1 / 2$ → $\eta_{w,\max} = 50\%$ - [ ] Bucket deflection angle $\beta \approx 165°-170°$ - [ ] Used for **high head, low discharge** - [ ] **Specific speed: 10–70** #### ✅ Francis Turbine - [ ] Mixed-flow reaction turbine - [ ] Head range: **Medium (30–300 m)** - [ ] Work done: $W/w = V_{w1} u_1 / g$ (with $V_{w2} = 0$) - [ ] Inlet vane angle: $\tan\theta_1 = V_f / (V_w - u_1)$ - [ ] Outlet vane angle: $\tan\theta_2 = V_f / u_2$ (when $V_{w2} = 0$) - [ ] Draft tube essential for head recovery - [ ] **Specific speed: 60–400** #### ✅ Kaplan Turbine - [ ] Axial-flow reaction turbine - [ ] Head range: **Low (< 30 m)** - [ ] Adjustable runner blades + guide vanes - [ ] $Q = (\pi/4)(D_0^2 - D_b^2) V_f$ - [ ] Highest efficiency over wide load range - [ ] **Specific speed: 300–1000** #### ✅ Universal Concepts - [ ] **Euler's equation:** $W/w = (V_{w1} u_1 \pm V_{w2} u_2) / g$ - [ ] **Hydraulic efficiency:** $\eta_h = V_{w1} u_1 / (gH)$ - [ ] **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_h \eta_v \eta_m$ - [ ] **Specific speed:** $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ - [ ] **Draft tube head recovery:** $H_d = (V_2^2 - V_3^2)/(2g)$ #### 🎯 Critical Mnemonics - **"PELTON — 50% — half of V₁"** → Max efficiency = 50%, at $u = V_1/2$. - **"FRANCIS — Mixed flow — $V_{w2} = 0$"** → Set exit whirl to zero for max efficiency. - **"KAPLAN — Axial — Adjustable"** → Both guide and runner blades adjustable. ---

topics.title: Centrifugal Pumps: Principle and Working, Head, Work Required, Efficiencies, Priming and Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Pumps: Principle and Working, Head, Work Required, Efficiencies, Priming and Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Centrifugal Pump:** A **rotodynamic fluid machine** that uses the **centrifugal force** generated by a rotating **impeller** to transport fluid by converting the **kinetic energy** of the fluid into **pressure energy**. The fluid enters the impeller axially at the **eye (inlet)** and is flung outward radially into the **volute casing** or **diffuser**.

The energy transfer in a centrifugal pump is governed by **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation**, making it a **constant-displacement-class machine** (actually a *rotodynamic* machine — flow varies with head).

**Key Terminology from KTU Module 4:**
- **Impeller**: The rotating member with curved/backswept vanes that imparts kinetic energy to the fluid.
- **Casing (Volute/Diffuser)**: The stationary housing that converts the kinetic energy of the discharged fluid into pressure energy.
- **Eye / Suction Eye**: The axial inlet at the center of the impeller where fluid enters.
- **Priming**: The pre-filling operation of the pump casing and suction pipe with the working fluid.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Merry-Go-Round Bucket Spin**

Imagine you are sitting on a **merry-go-round** holding a bucket of water. As the ride spins faster, the water is **flung outward** against the bucket walls. The bucket walls (analogous to the **volute casing**) feel a strong outward push. If you punched a small hole at the outer edge, the water would **shoot out radially** — this is exactly how a centrifugal pump discharges fluid.

**Simpler Analogy — The Washing Machine Spinner:**
The spin cycle in a washing machine uses the same principle — clothes (and water trapped in them) are flung outward against the drum. The centrifugal pump is essentially doing the same thing to **deliver** water, not drain it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of water (ρ)** = **1000 kg/m³** (at 4°C)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific weight of water (γ = ρg)** = **9810 N/m³**
- **Atmospheric pressure head** = **10.3 m of water** (at sea level)
- **Saturated vapor pressure of water at 30°C** ≈ **0.241 m of water** (≈ 2.365 kPa)
- **Vapor pressure head (Hvp)** decreases as temperature falls

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Always use **g = 9.81 m/s²** and **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Suction (Priming Phase):** The suction pipe and casing must be completely filled with the working fluid. The pump is **NOT self-priming** under standard conditions.
2. **Impeller Rotation:** The electric motor rotates the impeller at a high angular velocity **ω** (rad/s), where **N = 60ω/(2π) rpm**.
3. **Centrifugal Acceleration:** Fluid at radius **r** experiences a centrifugal acceleration of **ω²r** directed radially outward.
4. **Pressure Drop at Eye:** As fluid is flung outward, a **low-pressure region** forms at the impeller eye. Atmospheric pressure on the sump surface then pushes the fluid up the suction pipe into the eye.
5. **Energy Transfer in Impeller:** The impeller does work on the fluid, increasing its **whirl velocity (Vw)** and **tangential velocity (U)**.
6. **Casing Conversion:** The volute casing decelerates the high-velocity fluid, converting its **kinetic energy → pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle).
7. **Discharge:** Pressurized fluid leaves through the delivery pipe to the required height.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the pump is not self-priming (for water):** Air is much lighter than water. If air is present in the casing, the pressure drop created by the impeller is too small to lift water from a sump deeper than ~7–8 m. Hence **priming is mandatory**.

## 2.2 Classification by Vane Angle (Critical for KTU)

| Vane Type | Outlet Angle β₂ | Velocity Triangle Shape | Use Case |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|----------|
| **Radial vane** | β₂ = 90° | Tangential exit | High head, low discharge |
| **Backward-curved** | β₂ < 90° (typically 20°–30°) | Outflow angled backward | Most common — stable operation |
| **Forward-curved** | β₂ > 90° | Outflow angled forward | Very high discharge, prone to overload |

## 2.3 Heads in a Centrifugal Pump (KTU High-Yield)

Let **A** = sump level, **B** = delivery tank free surface, **E** = pump inlet (eye), **F** = pump outlet (discharge flange).

- **Suction Head (Hs):** Vertical height of pump centerline **above** the sump water surface. *(Positive if pump is above sump.)*
- **Delivery Head (Hd):** Vertical height of delivery tank water surface **above** the pump centerline.
- **Static Head (Hst):** Hst = Hs + Hd
- **Friction Head Loss (Hf):** Sum of friction losses in suction pipe and delivery pipe.
- **Total Head (H):** H = Hst + Hf
- **Manometric Head (Hm):** Head developed by the pump (energy added per unit weight of fluid).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** *Total Head H* is what the **system demands**. *Manometric Head Hm* is what the **pump delivers**. They are **equal only if friction losses are zero**, which is never the case in practice.

### Derivation of Manometric Head

Applying Bernoulli's equation between pump inlet **E** and pump outlet **F** (at the **impeller exit** level — which is what KTU board exams use):

$$
H_m = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} + \frac{V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

For **radial entry** condition (Vw1 = 0 — standard assumption):

$$
H_m = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Default:** Unless the problem explicitly states "non-radial entry", always use **V_w1 = 0**. This single assumption appears in 90% of KTU past papers.

## 2.4 Work Required and Euler's Equation

**Work done by impeller per second on the fluid:**

$$
W = \rho Q \left( V_{w2} u_2 \pm V_{w1} u_1 \right)
$$

For **radial entry (V_w1 = 0)**:

$$
W = \rho Q V_{w2} u_2
$$

where:
- **W** = work done per second (Watts) = ρgQH_m
- **ρ** = density of fluid (kg/m³)
- **Q** = discharge (m³/s)
- **V_w2** = velocity of whirl at outlet (m/s)
- **u₂** = tangential velocity of impeller at outlet (m/s) = πD₂N/60

## 2.5 Efficiencies (The Three Efficiencies of KTU)

### (a) Manometric Efficiency (η_man / η_m)

Ratio of manometric head energy to kinetic energy supplied by impeller:

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2} \quad \text{or} \quad \eta_{man} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{impeller}}
$$

### (b) Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)

Ratio of power available at the impeller to power supplied at the shaft:

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{impeller}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{P_{shaft} - P_{mech\,losses}}{P_{shaft}}
$$

### (c) Overall Efficiency (η_o)

Ratio of useful hydraulic power output to power input at the shaft:

$$
\eta_o = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **H** (total head = Hm + friction & other losses) with **Hm** (manometric head). Efficiencies use **different heads** — be very careful.

## 2.6 Priming

**Definition:** The operation of filling the suction pipe, casing, and impeller passages completely with the working liquid **before starting** the pump.

**Why is it needed?**
- Centrifugal pumps **cannot develop suction lift with air** because the density of air is ~1/800 that of water.
- The theoretical maximum suction lift with water at sea level is **10.33 m**, but practically limited to **6–7 m** due to friction and velocity head losses.

**Methods of Priming (KTU short-answer type):**
1. **Foot valve priming** — small foot valve at suction pipe end retains primed water.
2. **Vacuum pump priming** — auxiliary vacuum pump evacuates air.
3. **Ejector priming** — steam/water jet ejector.
4. **Self-priming pumps** — built-in air-separation chamber (modified design).

## 2.7 Cavitation

**Definition:** Cavitation is the phenomenon of **formation and subsequent collapse of vapor bubbles** in a liquid when the **local static pressure falls below the vapor pressure** of the liquid at that temperature.

**Where it occurs in a pump:** At the **suction eye (impeller inlet)**, where static pressure is the lowest.

**Effects of Cavitation:**
- Pitting and erosion of impeller vanes.
- Noise and vibration.
- Drop in head and efficiency.
- Eventually, complete failure of the pump.

### Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

$$
\text{NPSH Available} = H_{atm} \pm H_s - H_f - H_{vp}
$$

where:
- **H_atm** = atmospheric pressure head (≈ 10.3 m of water at sea level)
- **H_s** = suction head (positive if pump is **below** sump, negative if above)
- **H_f** = friction head loss in suction pipe
- **H_vp** = vapor pressure head of liquid at operating temperature

**Cavitation Condition (KTU):**

$$
\text{NPSH Available} \geq \text{NPSH Required}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** If asked "will cavitation occur?", compute **NPSH Available** and compare with the manufacturer's given **NPSH Required** value. If available < required → cavitation occurs.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Tangential velocity at impeller exit | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s | $D_2$ = outlet diameter (m) |
| 2 | Work done per second (radial entry) | $W = \rho Q V_{w2} u_2$ | W | V_w1 = 0 |
| 3 | Manometric head | $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m | For radial entry |
| 4 | Manometric head (general) | $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 \pm V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | m | Sign: + for V_w1 in same direction as u_1 |
| 5 | Total head | $H = H_s + H_d + H_f$ | m | Sum of suction, delivery, friction |
| 6 | Manometric efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$ | — | Decimal (0–1) or % |
| 7 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{impeller}}{P_{shaft}}$ | — | Decimal or % |
| 8 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | — | $\eta_o = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$ |
| 9 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Used to classify pump type |
| 10 | NPSH available | $\text{NPSH} = H_{atm} \pm H_s - H_f - H_{vp}$ | m | + if pump below sump |
| 11 | Cavitation parameter | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{NPSH} - \text{NPSH}_{req}}{H}$ | — | Must be > 0 for safe operation |
| 12 | Suction lift limit (theoretical) | $H_s \leq H_{atm} - H_{vp} - H_f$ | m | Practically 6–7 m for water |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Centrifugal pumps are the **workhorses of industry** — they handle ~80% of all fluid pumping applications including water supply, irrigation, HVAC, oil refineries, chemical processing, and fire-fighting systems. Mastery of these formulas is essential for any mechanical/civil engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done by the Impeller (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation)

We consider an **elemental ring** at the impeller outlet, bounded by inner radius **r** and outer radius **r + dr**, with thickness **db**. Let:
- **N** = speed of impeller (rev/s)
- **u₂** = tangential velocity of blade at outlet = 2πr₂N
- **V₂** = absolute velocity of fluid at outlet (resultant of V_w2 and V_f2)
- **V_r2** = relative velocity at outlet
- **V_w2** = component of V₂ in direction of u₂ (whirl component)
- **V_f2** = component of V₂ perpendicular to u₂ (flow component)

### Step 1: Mass of fluid in the elemental ring

The annular ring of width **dr** at outlet radius **r** has area:

$$
dA = 2\pi r \, dr
$$

The thickness of the ring is **db** (breadth of the impeller at outlet). Mass flow through this element per second:

$$
dm = \rho \cdot V_f \cdot dA = \rho \cdot V_f \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

### Step 2: Initial tangential momentum of this fluid element

The fluid enters the impeller (assumed **radial entry**, so V_w1 = 0). The tangential momentum of the elemental mass before entering impeller = **0** per unit time.

The tangential momentum per second **leaving** the impeller = mass flow × tangential component of absolute velocity = $\rho V_f \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \cdot V_{w2}$

### Step 3: Change in tangential momentum per second

$$
dF = \rho V_f (2\pi r \, dr) V_{w2}
$$

### Step 4: Torque on the elemental ring

$$
dT = r \cdot dF = \rho V_f (2\pi r^2 \, dr) V_{w2}
$$

### Step 5: Total torque (integrate across outlet)

For the entire impeller outlet annulus (radius r₁ to r₂), we assume **V_w and V_f are constants** (free-vortex assumption gives $V_w r = $ const, but for uniform analysis):

$$
T = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \rho V_f (2\pi r^2 \, dr) V_{w}
$$

Assuming V_f and V_w constant for the full annulus, and using r = D₂/2:

$$
T = 2\pi \rho V_f V_w \int_{r_1}^{r_2} r^2 \, dr = 2\pi \rho V_f V_w \left[\frac{r^3}{3}\right]_{r_1}^{r_2}
$$

For simplicity, treating the mass leaving at the mean radius r₂:

$$
T = \rho Q V_{w2} \cdot r_2
$$

> Since **u₂ = ωr₂** and **N = ω/(2π)**, we have **r₂ = u₂/ω = u₂·60/(2πN)**. Then:

$$
T = \rho Q V_{w2} \cdot \frac{u_2}{\omega} = \frac{\rho Q V_{w2} u_2}{\omega}
$$

### Step 6: Work done per second

Work done per second (Power) = Torque × Angular velocity:

$$
W = T \cdot \omega = \rho Q V_{w2} u_2
$$

Including inlet (V_w1 ≠ 0), the full Euler's equation:

$$
\boxed{W = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 \pm V_{w1} u_1)}
$$

**Sign convention:** Use **positive** sign if V_w1 is in the **same direction as u_1** (free-vortex flow); **negative** sign if V_w1 is **opposite** to u_1.

## 3.2 Derivation: Manometric Head from Energy Considerations

Starting with the **general Euler equation for energy per unit weight of fluid**:

$$
E_{per\,unit\,weight} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 \pm V_{w1} u_1}{g}
$$

By definition, this is the **manometric head** Hm (the head imparted to the fluid by the impeller):

$$
\boxed{H_m = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 \pm V_{w1} u_1}{g}}
$$

For **radial entry** (V_w1 = 0):

$$
H_m = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Velocity Triangle Relationships

For a **backward-curved impeller** (β₂ < 90°), the velocity triangle at outlet has:
- **u₂** = blade velocity (tangential, horizontal)
- **V_r2** = relative velocity (at angle β₂ with the tangential direction, measured **opposite** to u₂ for backward curve)
- **V₂** = absolute velocity (resultant of V_r2 and u₂)
- **V_w2** = whirl component (projection of V₂ on u₂)
- **V_f2** = flow component (perpendicular to u₂)

From the geometry of the velocity triangle:

$$
V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2
$$

### Substituting into Manometric Head:

$$
H_m = \frac{u_2 (u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2)}{g}
$$

$$
H_m = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2 V_{f2} \cot \beta_2}{g}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Vane Angle on Manometric Head

Assuming **V_f2 ∝ Q** and **u₂ = constant** (fixed speed), and from continuity **Q = π D₂ b₂ V_f2** (where b₂ = outlet width):

| Vane Type | β₂ | cot β₂ | Hm Behavior with Q |
|-----------|-----|--------|-------------------|
| Radial | 90° | 0 | Hm = u₂²/g (constant) |
| Backward-curved | < 90° | > 0 | Hm **decreases** linearly with Q (stable) |
| Forward-curved | > 90° | < 0 | Hm **increases** with Q (unstable — overload) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** This is why **backward-curved vanes are preferred** in industry — they provide **stable, non-overloading** characteristics. Forward-curved vanes can lead to dangerous power surges.

## 3.5 Derivation: Cavitation and Thoma's Cavitation Parameter

Starting from the NPSH available expression at the impeller eye:

$$
\text{NPSH} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm H_s - \frac{p_{vp}}{\rho g} - H_f
$$

For safe operation (no cavitation), **NPSH available > NPSH required**. The margin is given by:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{NPSH available} - \text{NPSH required}}{H}
$$

**Thoma's cavitation parameter** σ must be **greater than the critical value σ_c** (typically 0.1 to 0.4 depending on pump type). KTU problems often give σ_c and ask the student to verify safe operation.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-mark Standard)

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 20 m. The impeller diameter is 0.3 m, speed is 1450 rpm, outlet vane angle is 30°, and the manometric efficiency is 90%. Find (a) the whirl velocity at outlet, and (b) the power required to drive the pump if mechanical efficiency is 95%.

**Given:** Q = 0.05 m³/s, Hm = 20 m, D₂ = 0.3 m, N = 1450 rpm, β₂ = 30°, η_man = 0.90, η_mech = 0.95, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

### Part (a): Whirl velocity at outlet

**Step 1:** Compute tangential velocity u₂:

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.3 \times 1450}{60} = \frac{1366.45}{60}
$$

$$
u_2 = 22.774 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2:** From manometric efficiency definition:

$$
\eta_{man} = \frac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}
$$

$$
0.90 = \frac{9.81 \times 20}{V_{w2} \times 22.774}
$$

$$
V_{w2} \times 22.774 = \frac{196.2}{0.90} = 218.0
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{w2} = \frac{218.0}{22.774} = 9.572 \text{ m/s}}
$$

### Part (b): Power required to drive the pump

**Step 1:** Power at impeller (water power using Hm):

$$
P_{impeller} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20 = 9810 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2:** Power at shaft (using mechanical efficiency):

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{impeller}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{9810}{0.95}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{shaft} = 10{,}326.3 \text{ W} \approx 10.33 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Final Answer Verification:** This power is reasonable for a 0.05 m³/s, 20 m head pump. ✅

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Centrifugal Pump Operation

This diagram maps the **functional flow** of energy and fluid through the pump, showing how the components interact to convert **mechanical energy → hydraulic energy**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Motor] -->|Mechanical Power Pin| B[Coupling and Shaft Assembly]
    B -->|Rotational Energy| C[Impeller Rotating at N rpm]
    D[Sump Reservoir] -->|Suction Pipe with Foot Valve| E[Suction Strainer]
    E -->|Primed Fluid Entry| F[Impeller Eye at Low Pressure]
    F -->|Centrifugal Acceleration| G[Impeller Vanes Energy Transfer]
    G -->|High Velocity Fluid V2| H[Volute Casing Passage]
    H -->|Kinetic to Pressure Energy| I[Diffuser or Volute Throat]
    I -->|Pressurized Fluid| J[Delivery Pipe]
    J -->|Discharge Q| K[Delivery Tank at Height Hd]
    L[Atmospheric Pressure on Sump Surface] -->|Pushes Fluid Up| E
    M[Friction Losses Hf in Pipes] -->|Energy Loss| N[Total Head H Equals Hst Plus Hf]
    C -->|Work W Equals rho Q Vw2 u2| O[Manometric Head Hm Developed]
    O -->|Pimpeller Equals rho g Q Hm| P[Impeller Power Output]
    P -->|Divided by eta mech| A
```

## 4.2 Velocity Triangle Schematic (Outlet — Backward Curved Vane)

This diagram shows the **velocity components at the impeller outlet**, which is the most frequently tested concept in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blade Tangential Velocity u2] -->|Direction of Rotation| B[Whirl Component Vw2]
    C[Relative Velocity Vr2 at angle beta2] -->|Subtracted from u2| B
    B -->|Resultant Vector Sum| D[Absolute Velocity V2]
    E[Flow Component Vf2 Perpendicular to u2] -->|Combined with Vw2| D
    F[Note beta2 less than 90 backward curved] -.-> C
    G[Relation Vw2 equals u2 minus Vf2 cot beta2] -.-> B
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input Power P electrical] -->|Through Motor Efficiency| B[Shaft Power P shaft]
    B -->|Through Mechanical Efficiency eta mech| C[Impeller Power P impeller]
    C -->|Through Manometric Efficiency eta man| D[Water Power rho g Q Hm equals Manometric Power]
    E[Total Head H includes Hst and Hf] -->|Greater than Hm| D
    F[Fluid Density rho 1000 kg per m3] -->|Multiplied with g Q Hm| D
    G[Overall Efficiency eta o equals eta mech times eta man] -.-> B
    G -.-> C
    H[KTU Critical Relation P shaft equals rho g Q H divided by eta o] -.-> B
```

## 4.4 NPSH and Cavitation Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Pressure Hatm 10.3 m] -->|Plus or Minus| B[Suction Head Hs]
    B -->|Subtract Friction Loss| C[NPSH Available]
    D[Friction Head Loss Hf in Suction Pipe] -->|Subtracted| C
    E[Vapor Pressure Head Hvp at Operating Temp] -->|Subtracted| C
    C -->|Compare with| F[NPSH Required from Manufacturer]
    F -->|If Available greater than Required| G[Safe Operation No Cavitation]
    F -->|If Available less than Required| H[Cavitation Occurs]
    H -->|Effects| I[Pitting Noise Vibration Efficiency Drop]
    I -->|Solution| J[Reduce Suction Lift or Lower Temperature]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These Mermaid block diagrams render the **interactions and energy flow** between pump components, since detailed physical schematics (impeller blade geometry, volute casing) are not directly renderable in Mermaid syntax. For KTU exam purposes, students should also practice drawing the **velocity triangle** by hand on graph paper, labeling **u₂, V₂, V_r2, V_w2, V_f2, and β₂** clearly.
