# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **manometric efficiency** of a centrifugal pump. State its typical range for modern pumps.

**Model Answer:**

> **Manometric efficiency (η_man)** is defined as the ratio of the **manometric head** (energy actually transferred to the fluid per unit weight) to the **head equivalent of the energy supplied by the impeller** at the impeller outlet.
>
> Mathematically: $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2} = \dfrac{\text{Manometric Power}}{\text{Impeller Power}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{impeller}}$
>
> **Typical range:** For modern centrifugal pumps, η_man lies between **0.85 to 0.95 (85% to 95%)**.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition 1 mark + Formula 1 mark + Typical range 1 mark = **3 marks** ✅

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q:** What is **cavitation**? What is its effect on the performance of a centrifugal pump?

**Model Answer:**

> **Cavitation** is the phenomenon of **formation and subsequent violent collapse of vapor bubbles** of the liquid in regions where the **local static pressure falls below the vapor pressure** of the liquid at the operating temperature.
>
> **Effects on pump performance:**
> 1. **Erosion and pitting** of the impeller vanes and casing surfaces.
> 2. **Noise and vibration** in the pump assembly.
> 3. **Drop in head** developed and **efficiency reduction**.
> 4. **Interruption of continuous flow** in severe cases.
> 5. **Reduction in discharge** capacity of the pump.
>
> **Preventive measure:** Ensure **NPSH Available ≥ NPSH Required** by reducing suction lift and friction losses.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition 1.5 marks + Effects 1 mark + Preventive measure 0.5 mark = **3 marks** ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Students must answer **either the full Question A OR the full Question B** (internal choice).

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A centrifugal pump has the following specifications:
- Discharge Q = 0.06 m³/s
- Head H = 30 m
- Impeller diameter at outlet D₂ = 0.4 m
- Width at outlet b₂ = 50 mm = 0.05 m
- Speed N = 1450 rpm
- Outlet vane angle β₂ = 30°
- Manometric efficiency η_man = 0.90
- Mechanical efficiency η_mech = 0.92

**Find:**
**(a)** The **whirl velocity (V_w2)**, **flow velocity (V_f2)**, and **absolute velocity (V_2)** at the outlet. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The **power required to drive the pump** and the **overall efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Velocity Components at Outlet

**Step 1: Tangential velocity u₂**

$$
u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1450}{60} = \frac{1822.12}{60} = 30.37 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Flow velocity V_f2 (from continuity)**

Discharge Q = πD₂b₂V_f2, so:

$$
V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \frac{0.06}{\pi \times 0.4 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.06}{0.0628}
$$

$$
V_{f2} = 0.955 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Whirl velocity V_w2 (from manometric efficiency)**

From $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$ and Hm = H (assume frictionless for manometric calc — given head is total, we use it as Hm here per KTU standard):

$$
V_{w2} = \frac{g H_m}{\eta_{man} \cdot u_2} = \frac{9.81 \times 30}{0.90 \times 30.37} = \frac{294.3}{27.33}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{w2} = 10.77 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 4: Absolute velocity V₂**

From velocity triangle: $V_2^2 = V_{w2}^2 + V_{f2}^2$

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{(10.77)^2 + (0.955)^2} = \sqrt{116.0 + 0.912} = \sqrt{116.91}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_2 = 10.81 \text{ m/s}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Power and Overall Efficiency

**Step 1: Water power (P_w)**

$$
P_w = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 \times 30 = 17{,}658 \text{ W} = 17.66 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Shaft power (P_shaft)**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_o} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{17{,}658}{0.90 \times 0.92} = \frac{17{,}658}{0.828}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{shaft} = 21{,}326 \text{ W} \approx 21.33 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 3: Overall efficiency**

$$
\eta_o = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.90 \times 0.92 = 0.828
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_o = 82.8\%}
$$

**Verification:** Power input × efficiency should equal water power: 21,326 × 0.828 = 17,658 W ✅

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q:** A centrifugal pump is installed with its centerline **5 m above** the sump water level. The suction pipe is 8 m long with friction factor such that friction head loss is 0.4 m. The water temperature is 30°C. The pump delivers 0.04 m³/s against a total head of 25 m. The atmospheric pressure is 10.1 m of water, and the manufacturer specifies NPSH required = 3.5 m.

**Find:**
**(a)** The **NPSH available** and check whether **cavitation will occur**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If cavitation occurs, suggest **two remedial measures** with calculations showing the effect. **(7 marks)**

*Note: Vapor pressure head of water at 30°C = 0.241 m of water.*

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — NPSH Calculation

**Given:**
- H_s = +5 m (pump **above** sump → negative for "above" convention)
- H_f = 0.4 m
- H_atm = 10.1 m of water
- H_vp = 0.241 m of water
- NPSH_required = 3.5 m

**Step 1: NPSH Available formula (pump above sump)**

$$
\text{NPSH}_{avail} = H_{atm} - H_s - H_f - H_{vp}
$$

$$
\text{NPSH}_{avail} = 10.1 - 5 - 0.4 - 0.241
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{NPSH}_{avail} = 4.459 \text{ m of water}}
$$

**Step 2: Cavitation check**

Compare: NPSH_available (4.459 m) **vs** NPSH_required (3.5 m)

Since **4.459 m > 3.5 m**, the pump has **sufficient margin** of **0.959 m**.

**Step 3: Thoma's cavitation parameter**

$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{NPSH}_{avail} - \text{NPSH}_{req}}{H} = \frac{4.459 - 3.5}{25} = \frac{0.959}{25} = 0.0384
$$

For a typical centrifugal pump, σ_critical ≈ 0.1. Since **σ = 0.0384 < 0.1**, the pump is operating **close to cavitation limits** and may experience **incipient cavitation**.

> **Conclusion:** The pump is operating within the NPSH margin but **very close to the cavitation threshold**. It is recommended to take precautionary measures.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Remedial Measures

**Measure 1: Lower the pump below sump level (negative suction head)**

If we lower the pump by 2 m (so H_s = -2 m, i.e., pump **below** sump):

$$
\text{NPSH}_{avail} = 10.1 - (-2) - 0.4 - 0.241 = 10.1 + 2 - 0.4 - 0.241
$$

$$
\text{NPSH}_{avail} = 11.459 \text{ m} \quad (\text{massive safety margin of 7.959 m!})
$$

**Result:** Cavitation completely eliminated. ✅

**Measure 2: Reduce friction losses in suction pipe**

Use a larger diameter pipe, halving H_f to 0.2 m (and keeping pump at 5 m above sump):

$$
\text{NPSH}_{avail} = 10.1 - 5 - 0.2 - 0.241 = 4.659 \text{ m}
$$

Margin increases to 4.659 − 3.5 = **1.159 m**, and σ = 0.0464 (still below 0.1, so this alone is not enough).

**Measure 3 (Bonus): Reduce water temperature**

Cool water from 30°C to 20°C: H_vp at 20°C ≈ 0.174 m. New NPSH:

$$
\text{NPSH}_{avail} = 10.1 - 5 - 0.4 - 0.174 = 4.526 \text{ m}
$$

Marginal improvement; not as effective as Measure 1.

**Best Recommendation:** **Relocate the pump below the sump water level** — this is the most effective engineering solution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks 🚨**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention for Suction Head (Hs):** This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU cavitation problems. If pump is **above** sump, use **NPSH = H_atm − H_s − H_f − H_vp**. If pump is **below** sump, use **NPSH = H_atm + H_s − H_f − H_vp** (note the sign flip). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for sign errors.
>
> 2. **Confusing H and Hm:** In efficiency formulas:
>    - η_man = g·H_m / (V_w2·u_2) → uses **manometric head**
>    - η_o = ρgQH / P_shaft → uses **total head** (with friction)
>
> 3. **Forgetting V_w1 = 0:** Always state "assuming radial entry, V_w1 = 0" at the start of the solution. Examiners look for this assumption statement.
>
> 4. **Units in Power:** Power output is in **Watts (W) or kW** — never in N/s or J/m. Convert carefully.
>
> 5. **NPSH Required vs Available:** Always clearly label which is "available" and which is "required" in your answer. Don't just write "NPSH" without subscript.
>
> 6. **Velocity Triangle Drawing:** KTU examiners give **2 marks** for a correctly drawn velocity triangle in many problems. Don't skip it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU Module 4 — Centrifugal Pumps: Final Revision Pointers**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- ✅ Centrifugal pump is a **rotodynamic machine** using **centrifugal force** for fluid transport.
- ✅ Fluid enters **axially at the eye** and discharges **radially at the periphery**.
- ✅ Energy conversion chain: **Mechanical → Kinetic (in impeller) → Pressure (in volute)**.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ✅ **u₂ = πD₂N/60** — blade velocity at outlet.
- ✅ **Hm = V_w2·u_2/g** — manometric head (radial entry).
- ✅ **W = ρQV_w2·u_2** — work done per second (power to fluid).
- ✅ **η_o = η_man × η_mech** — overall efficiency relation.
- ✅ **NPSH = H_atm ± H_s − H_f − H_vp** — cavitation parameter (sign: − if pump above sump).

### Classifications
- ✅ **Radial vane** (β₂ = 90°): constant head, low discharge.
- ✅ **Backward-curved** (β₂ < 90°): **most common**, stable, non-overloading.
- ✅ **Forward-curved** (β₂ > 90°): high discharge, prone to overload.

### Efficiencies Hierarchy (KTU order)
- ✅ η_man (0.85–0.95) → η_mech (0.90–0.95) → η_o = η_man × η_mech (0.75–0.90).

### Priming — Key Points
- ✅ Centrifugal pump is **not self-priming** for water — priming is mandatory.
- ✅ Practical suction lift limit: **6–7 m** (theoretical 10.33 m).
- ✅ Priming methods: foot valve, vacuum pump, ejector, self-priming design.

### Cavitation — Key Points
- ✅ Occurs at **impeller eye** (lowest pressure zone).
- ✅ Cavitation = local pressure < vapor pressure → vapor bubble formation + collapse.
- ✅ Effects: pitting, noise, vibration, efficiency drop.
- ✅ Prevention: ensure **NPSH_available > NPSH_required**, lower pump, reduce H_s and H_f.

### Specific Speed (For Classification, if asked)
- ✅ **N_s = N√Q / H^(3/4)** — used to choose pump type (centrifugal vs. axial).
- ✅ Low N_s → radial flow pump; high N_s → axial flow pump.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Sequence (Always Follow This Order)
1. ✅ **Calculate u₂** first.
2. ✅ **Calculate V_f2** from continuity (Q = πD₂b₂V_f2).
3. ✅ **Use η_man to find V_w2**.
4. ✅ **Compute Hm** from V_w2 and u₂.
5. ✅ **Compute P_impeller = ρgQHm**.
6. ✅ **Compute P_shaft = P_impeller / η_mech**.
7. ✅ **Compute η_o = η_man × η_mech**.

### Common Units to Remember
- ✅ Q in m³/s, N in rpm, D in m, ρ in kg/m³, g in m/s².
- ✅ Head in m of fluid (not kPa — convert only if asked).
- ✅ Power in W or kW (1 kW = 1000 W).

### Numerical Range Sanity Checks
- ✅ u₂ for water pumps is typically **10–50 m/s**.
- ✅ V_w2 is typically **0.5× to 0.9× of u₂**.
- ✅ V_f2 is much smaller than u₂ (typically **0.5–3 m/s**).
- ✅ If your answers are off by orders of magnitude, **recheck units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Pump: Principle and Working - Slip, Negative Slip, Work Required and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reciprocating Pump: Principle and Working - Slip, Negative Slip, Work Required and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocating Pump** is a **positive displacement pump** that moves a fixed quantity of fluid per stroke by means of a piston or plunger reciprocating inside a closely fitted cylinder, with fluid intake and discharge controlled by non-return (check) valves.

The pump converts the **rotational mechanical energy** of the prime mover into the **reciprocating (to-and-fro) motion** of the piston, which in turn pressurizes and discharges the fluid. Because it displaces a constant volume per cycle (independent of head, up to safe operating pressure), it is classified as a **positive displacement pump**.

### Key Subsystems
- **Cylinder** — houses the piston/plunger and fluid.
- **Piston (or Plunger)** — sealing element reciprocating inside the cylinder.
- **Suction Valve** — opens during intake stroke, closes during discharge stroke.
- **Delivery Valve** — opens during discharge stroke, closes during suction stroke.
- **Crank and Connecting Rod** — converts rotary motion into reciprocating motion.
- **Suction Pipe & Delivery Pipe** — convey fluid to and from the cylinder.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine using a **medical syringe** to draw in water and push it out:

- When you **pull the plunger back**, the inlet check-valve opens and water rushes into the barrel. (Suction Stroke)
- When you **push the plunger in**, the inlet valve snaps shut, the outlet valve opens, and water is forced out. (Delivery Stroke)

A reciprocating pump is essentially a **mechanically driven, high-capacity industrial syringe**, repeated thousands of times per minute by a crank-slider mechanism driven by an electric motor or engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world installations:** Reciprocating pumps are widely used in **oil wells (sucker-rod pumps), boiler feed systems, high-pressure cleaning jets, and metering chemicals**, where **high head at low discharge** is required.

## 1.3 Types of Reciprocating Pumps

| Classification | Variants | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By Action** | **Single-acting** | One suction + one delivery per revolution (per piston) |
| | **Double-acting** | Two suctions + two deliveries per revolution (per piston) |
| **By Cylinders** | **Simplex** | One cylinder |
| | **Duplex** | Two cylinders |
| | **Triplex** | Three cylinders (common in high-pressure washers) |
| **By Drive** | **Crank-driven** | Most common; uses crank-slider |
| | **Steam-driven** | Direct-acting steam pump (no crank) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Operational Cycle

The reciprocating pump operates in **two distinct strokes per cycle** (single-acting):

### Step 1 — Suction Stroke (Piston moves backward)
1. Piston retracts, increasing cylinder volume.
2. Pressure inside cylinder drops **below atmospheric pressure**.
3. **Suction valve opens**; atmospheric pressure pushes liquid up the suction pipe.
4. **Delivery valve remains closed** (held shut by higher delivery-pipe pressure).

### Step 2 — Delivery Stroke (Piston moves forward)
1. Piston advances, decreasing cylinder volume.
2. Pressure inside cylinder rises **above delivery pipe pressure**.
3. **Delivery valve opens**; liquid is forced out into the delivery pipe.
4. **Suction valve remains closed** (held shut by cylinder vacuum).

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous flow is NOT achieved** in a single-cylinder, single-acting pump. The discharge is **pulsating**. Smoothening devices like **air vessels** on suction and delivery sides are used in practice.

## 2.2 Slip in Reciprocating Pumps — The Core Concept

### 2.2.1 Definition of Slip
**Slip** is the **difference between the theoretical (calculated) discharge and the actual discharge** delivered by the pump.

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{\text{theoretical}} - Q_{\text{actual}}$$

Slip is **normally positive**, meaning $Q_{\text{actual}} < Q_{\text{theoretical}}$.

### 2.2.2 Causes of Slip
- **Delayed opening** of the suction and delivery valves (valve lag).
- **Leakage** of liquid past the piston (through worn piston rings or packing).
- **Leakage** through valves that never seat perfectly.
- **Compression and expansion** of trapped air/vapour in clearance volume.

### 2.2.3 Slip Coefficient and Coefficient of Discharge

$$\text{Slip coefficient} = C_s = \dfrac{\text{Slip}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = 1 - C_d$$

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Slip}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

## 2.3 Negative Slip

### 2.3.1 Concept
Under specific operating conditions, the **actual discharge can momentarily exceed the theoretical discharge**. This phenomenon is termed **Negative Slip**.

### 2.3.2 Cause — Inertia of Liquid Column
- At **high piston speeds**, the long column of liquid in the delivery pipe gains considerable **momentum (inertia)**.
- When the piston begins its **suction stroke**, the delivery valve does **not close immediately**.
- The momentum of the moving liquid column continues to push liquid out of the cylinder **into the delivery pipe**, even though the piston is now retracting.
- Only after the kinetic energy of the column is fully dissipated does the delivery valve finally close.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative slip is NOT desirable.** It indicates excessive piston speed, overly long delivery piping, or undersized air vessels. It wastes energy and causes water hammer.

Mathematically, during negative slip:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} > Q_{\text{theoretical}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Slip} < 0$$

$$C_d > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Slip coefficient} < 0$$

## 2.4 Work Required and Efficiency

### 2.4.1 Work Done Without Friction (Ideal Case)
For a pump raising liquid through static head **H**, with no friction:

$$W_{\text{ideal}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{\text{theoretical}} \cdot H$$

### 2.4.2 Work Done With Friction (Practical Case)
Accounting for frictional losses $h_s$ in suction pipe and $h_d$ in delivery pipe:

$$W_{\text{actual}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{\text{actual}} \cdot (H + h_s + h_d)$$

### 2.4.3 Overall Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{\text{actual}} \cdot H}{W_{\text{input}}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{water}}}{\text{Indicated Work}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{\text{actual}} \cdot H}{W_i}$$

$$\eta_{\text{volumetric}} = C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge (single-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $A$ = piston area $\text{m}^2$, $L$ = stroke length m, $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge (double-acting, no rod) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60}$ | Same as above |
| 3 | Theoretical Discharge (double-acting, with rod) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{(2A - a) L N}{60}$ | $a$ = piston rod area $\text{m}^2$ |
| 4 | Slip | $\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Slip}}{Q_{th}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Slip Coefficient | $C_s = 1 - C_d$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Ideal Work per second | $W_i = \rho g Q_{th} H$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Actual Work on water | $W_w = \rho g Q_{act} H$ | Watts (W) |
| 9 | Work against friction | $W_f = \rho g Q_{act} (h_s + h_d)$ | Watts (W) |
| 10 | Total Work input | $W_{in} = \rho g Q_{act} (H + h_s + h_d)$ | Watts (W) |
| 11 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = C_d$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{W_w}{W_i}$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{W_{input}}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Take water density** $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and **gravity** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ unless stated otherwise. KTU problems typically assume these values.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge — Single-Acting Pump

### Setup
Let:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston ($\text{m}^2$)
- $L$ = length of stroke (m)
- $N$ = speed of crank (rpm)
- $n$ = number of cylinders

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume displaced per stroke.**
In one stroke, the piston sweeps the full cylinder length once:

$$V_{\text{stroke}} = A \cdot L \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{stroke})$$

**Step 2 — Number of strokes per minute.**
For a single-acting pump, each revolution of the crank produces **one suction and one delivery stroke**, hence **one delivery stroke per revolution**:

$$\text{Delivery strokes per minute} = N$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge per minute.**

$$Q_{th,\min} = V_{\text{stroke}} \times N = A \cdot L \cdot N \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min})$$

**Step 4 — Convert to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$** (SI standard).

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Check:** Units verification: $A\,[\text{m}^2] \cdot L\,[\text{m}] \cdot N\,[\text{min}^{-1}] / 60\,[\text{s/min}] = \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Correct.

## 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge — Double-Acting Pump

### Setup
A double-acting pump discharges fluid **twice per revolution** (once from each side of the piston). If a piston rod of area $a$ passes through one end, that side displaces $(A-a) \cdot L$ per stroke.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume from the "blank" side (no rod):**

$$V_1 = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Volume from the "rod" side:**

$$V_2 = (A - a) \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Volume per revolution:**

$$V_{\text{rev}} = V_1 + V_2 = A L + (A - a) L = (2A - a) L$$

**Step 4 — Convert to discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Work Done per Second

### Ideal (No Friction) Case
**Step 1** — Mass of water lifted per second = $\rho \cdot Q_{th}$ (kg/s).

**Step 2** — Work done per unit mass against head H = $g \cdot H$ ($\text{J/kg}$).

**Step 3** — Power = Mass flow rate $\times$ work per unit mass:

$$W_i = \rho \cdot Q_{th} \cdot g \cdot H = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H \quad (\text{W})$$

### Practical (With Friction) Case
**Step 4** — When suction and delivery friction heads $h_s$, $h_d$ are considered, and actual discharge $Q_{act}$ is used:

$$W_{act} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot (H + h_s + h_d)$$

### Combined Efficiency Expression

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H}{W_{shaft}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Negative Slip Magnitude (Energy-Based)

The momentum approach for the delivery pipe column (length $L_d$, area $A_d$, velocity $v$, density $\rho$) is as follows.

**Step 1** — Mass of water column in delivery pipe:

$$m = \rho \cdot A_d \cdot L_d$$

**Step 2** — Velocity of water column at the start of suction stroke is roughly equal to mean piston delivery velocity:

$$v = \dfrac{A}{A_d} \cdot \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{A}{A_d} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \cdot r$$

**Step 3** — Kinetic energy (in J) to be dissipated before valve closure:

$$KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A_d L_d v^2$$

**Step 4** — This KE corresponds to an extra volume delivered as **negative slip**:

$$V_{\text{neg slip}} = A_d \cdot \Delta x = \text{(extra column length) moved out}$$

Solving for the *additional discharge*:

$$\Delta Q = A_d \cdot v \cdot \Delta t_{\text{closure}}$$

where $\Delta t_{\text{closure}}$ is the time taken for valve to seat after piston reversal. This is **empirically estimated**, not analytically fixed, since it depends on valve dynamics.

## 3.5 Analytical Mastery Matrix

| Concept | Key Insight | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| Single-acting $Q_{th}$ | Multiply by $N$ (not $2N$) | Using $2N$ for single-acting |
| Double-acting $Q_{th}$ | Use $(2A-a)$ when rod present | Forgetting rod area $a$ |
| Slip is normally positive | $Q_{act} < Q_{th}$ always in normal operation | Assuming negative slip is normal |
| Negative slip at high speed | Caused by inertia, not efficiency gain | Treating it as beneficial |
| Friction losses appear on both sides | $h_s$ on suction, $h_d$ on delivery | Only including one |
| Efficiency ratios are dimensionless | $\eta_v = C_d$, not percentage | Mixing ratios and percentages |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[Prime Mover Motor] --> CR[Crank and Flywheel]
    CR --> CRR[Connecting Rod]
    CRR --> PIST[Piston Plunger]
    PIST --> CYL[Cylinder Chamber]
    CYL --> SV[Suction Check Valve]
    SV --> SP[Suction Pipe from Reservoir]
    CYL --> DV[Delivery Check Valve]
    DV --> DP[Delivery Pipe to Tanker]
    CYL --> AV1[Suction Air Vessel]
    CYL --> AV2[Delivery Air Vessel]
    AV1 -.smoothens.-> SP
    AV2 -.smoothens.-> DP
```

## 4.2 State Machine of the Pumping Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SuctionStroke: Crank at TDC Top Dead Centre
    SuctionStroke --> DeliveryStroke: Piston at BDC Bottom Dead Centre
    DeliveryStroke --> SuctionStroke: Piston back at TDC
    SuctionStroke: Suction Valve OPEN
    SuctionStroke: Delivery Valve CLOSED
    SuctionStroke: Liquid enters cylinder
    DeliveryStroke: Suction Valve CLOSED
    DeliveryStroke: Delivery Valve OPEN
    DeliveryStroke: Liquid ejected to delivery
```

## 4.3 Indicator Diagram (P-V) — Ideal vs. Real

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDEAL[Ideal Indicator Diagram Rectangle]
        P1["P1 = 0 Suction Pressure"] --> P2["P2 = H plus Hs Total Delivery Pressure"]
        P2 --> P3["P3 = 0 Return"]
        P3 --> P1
    end
    subgraph REAL[Real Indicator Diagram Rounded with slopes]
        R1["Lower Curve Sa"] --> R2["Upper Curve Db with friction loops"]
        R2 --> R3["Hatched area = slip loss"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the indicator diagram:**
> - **Horizontal axis** = Volume in cylinder.
> - **Vertical axis** = Pressure (in cylinder).
> - **Rectangular shape** = ideal (no friction, no acceleration).
> - **Rounded corners** = valve lag and leakage (slip area).
> - **Sloped top/bottom** = acceleration head losses in long pipes.

## 4.4 Slip vs. Negative Slip Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pump operating] --> B{Piston Speed}
    B -->|Low to Moderate| C[Normal Operation]
    C --> D{Qact versus Qth}
    D -->|Qact less than Qth| E[Positive Slip]
    D -->|Qact equals Qth| F[Zero Slip Ideal]
    B -->|Very High Speed| G[Inertia Dominant]
    G --> H[Delivery valve stays open during early suction]
    H --> I[Extra water column delivered]
    I --> J[Qact greater than Qth]
    J --> K[Negative Slip]
    K --> L[Reduce speed, install air vessel]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define slip and negative slip in a reciprocating pump. Under what conditions does negative slip occur?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Slip** is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump:
> $$\text{Slip} = Q_{\text{theoretical}} - Q_{\text{actual}}$$
>
> **Negative slip** is said to occur when the actual discharge exceeds the theoretical discharge, i.e., $\text{Slip} < 0$.
>
> **Condition:** Negative slip occurs at **very high piston speeds** when the **inertia of the liquid column in the delivery pipe** prevents the delivery valve from closing immediately at the start of the suction stroke. The momentum of the moving liquid continues to push fluid out for a short duration, making $Q_{\text{actual}} > Q_{\text{theoretical}}$.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 300 mm. If it runs at 60 rpm, calculate the theoretical discharge.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Diameter $D = 150\,\text{mm} = 0.15\,\text{m}$, Stroke $L = 300\,\text{mm} = 0.3\,\text{m}$, $N = 60\,\text{rpm}$.

**Step 1** — Piston area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2** — Theoretical discharge:

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{0.01767 \times 0.3 \times 60}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 5.301 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 5.301\,\text{L/s}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a single-acting reciprocating pump. Derive the expression for its theoretical discharge.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 200 mm, stroke of 400 mm, and a piston rod diameter of 50 mm. It runs at 50 rpm and lifts water through a total head of 30 m including friction losses of 2 m. If the slip is 4%, calculate: (i) theoretical discharge, (ii) actual discharge, (iii) work done per second, and (iv) overall efficiency if the input power is 18 kW.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Working:**
A single-acting reciprocating pump consists of a piston reciprocating in a cylinder. The piston is connected to a crank via a connecting rod. There are two non-return valves — a **suction valve** at the bottom of the cylinder connected to the suction pipe, and a **delivery valve** at the top connected to the delivery pipe.

- **Suction stroke:** Piston moves away from the suction valve. Suction valve opens, delivery valve remains closed; water is drawn in.
- **Delivery stroke:** Piston moves toward the suction valve. Delivery valve opens, suction valve remains closed; water is forced out.

**Derivation** (already shown in §3.1):

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 200\,\text{mm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$, $d_{\text{rod}} = 50\,\text{mm} = 0.05\,\text{m}$
- $L = 400\,\text{mm} = 0.4\,\text{m}$, $N = 50\,\text{rpm}$
- Total head $H_t = 30\,\text{m}$, slip = 4 % = 0.04, $P_{\text{in}} = 18\,\text{kW}$.

**Step 1 — Piston area and rod area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142\,\text{m}^2$$

$$a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (double-acting):**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{(2A - a) L N}{60} = \dfrac{(2 \times 0.03142 - 0.001963) \times 0.4 \times 50}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{(0.06283 - 0.001963) \times 20}{60} = \dfrac{0.06087 \times 20}{60} = 0.02029\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Slip = 4% of theoretical:**

$$\text{Slip} = 0.04 \times 0.02029 = 8.115 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Actual discharge:**

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - \text{Slip} = 0.02029 - 8.115 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01948\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Work done per second by water:**

$$W_w = \rho g Q_{act} H_t = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01948 \times 30$$

$$W_w = 5732.6\,\text{W} \approx 5.733\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Overall efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \dfrac{W_w}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{5.733}{18} = 0.3185 = 31.85\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In double-acting pumps with a piston rod, students frequently use $2ALN/60$ (without subtracting rod area). This **overestimates discharge by $\approx 6\%$** here — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is meant by slip in a reciprocating pump? Discuss its causes. Explain negative slip with a neat sketch.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-acting reciprocating pump, running at 40 rpm, delivers 0.0075 m³/s. The bore and stroke are 200 mm and 350 mm respectively. Determine: (i) theoretical discharge, (ii) slip, (iii) coefficient of discharge, and (iv) slip as a percentage of theoretical discharge.

#### Model Solution to (a)

Slip, its causes and negative slip are explained in detail in §2.2 and §2.3 of these notes. Key points:

- **Slip** = $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$, caused by **valve lag, leakage past piston, leakage through valves**.
- **Negative slip** occurs at **high speeds** when the **inertia of the delivery column** keeps the delivery valve open during the early part of the suction stroke.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $N = 40\,\text{rpm}$, $Q_{act} = 0.0075\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $D = 0.2\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.35\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Piston area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (single-acting):**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} = \dfrac{0.03142 \times 0.35 \times 40}{60} = \dfrac{0.4399}{60} = 7.332 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Slip:**

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 7.332 \times 10^{-3} - 7.500 \times 10^{-3}$$

Wait — this gives a **negative slip**. Let us recheck: $7.332 < 7.500$. The problem implies $Q_{act}$ is the higher one (perhaps the pump exhibits negative slip). So:

$$\text{Negative slip} = Q_{act} - Q_{th} = 1.68 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7.332 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0229$$

**Step 5 — Percentage slip:**

$$\text{Percentage slip} = \dfrac{\text{Negative Slip}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.68 \times 10^{-4}}{7.332 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 2.29\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students assume slip is *always positive* and forcibly write $\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$. If the numerical result becomes negative, the correct interpretation is **negative slip** — and the percentage should be reported as a **negative** value. Writing positive slip with a magnitude error here costs **3 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist — Reciprocating Pump Slip & Efficiency**

- [x] Reciprocating pump is a **positive displacement** device (constant volume per cycle).
- [x] **Single-acting**: $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$; **Double-acting**: $Q_{th} = \dfrac{(2A - a)LN}{60}$.
- [x] **Slip** = $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$; usually **positive** (causes: valve lag, leakage, air binding).
- [x] **$C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$**; **$\eta_v = C_d$** (volumetric efficiency).
- [x] **Negative slip** arises at **high speed** due to **inertia of delivery column** keeping valve open.
- [x] Negative slip means $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ and $C_d > 1$.
- [x] **Work done per second** (water): $W = \rho g Q_{act} H$.
- [x] **Friction work**: $W_f = \rho g Q_{act}(h_s + h_d)$.
- [x] **Overall efficiency**: $\eta_o = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{W_{\text{shaft}}}$.
- [x] **Standard values**: $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.
- [x] **Air vessels** smooth pulsations and **reduce** negative slip.
- [x] **Indicator diagram** is the **P-V plot** of actual cylinder pressure vs. volume.
